topics.title: Semiconductor Physics: Intrinsic Semiconductor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Semiconductor Physics: Intrinsic Semiconductor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Intrinsic Semiconductor** is a chemically pure, single-crystalline semiconductor material (such as pure Silicon (Si), Germanium (Ge), or Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)) that has not been deliberately doped with any impurity atoms. In its purest form, the electrical conductivity of the material arises **exclusively from thermally generated electron-hole pairs** created by breaking covalent bonds within the crystal lattice itself.

The electrical and optical properties of an intrinsic semiconductor are governed entirely by:
1. The crystal's **band gap energy ($E_g$)**,
2. The **lattice temperature ($T$)**, and
3. The **effective masses of the charge carriers** ($m_e^*$ for electrons, $m_h^*$ for holes).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The intrinsic semiconductor is the foundational building block for understanding all modern solid-state electronic devices, including **PN-junction diodes, BJTs, MOSFETs, LEDs, and solar cells.** The KTU board examiner frequently tests concepts of band structure, carrier generation, and Fermi level position in this exact topic.

## 1.2 Standard Energy Band Gap Values (at 300 K)

| Material | Band Gap $E_g$ (eV) | Type |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Silicon (Si) | **1.12 eV** | Indirect band gap |
| Germanium (Ge) | **0.67 eV** | Indirect band gap |
| Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) | **1.42 eV** | Direct band gap |
| Diamond (C) | **5.47 eV** | Wide band gap insulator |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The "Partially Filled Auditorium"

Imagine a large **auditorium with 1000 seats** in the stalls (representing the **valence band**) and another 1000 seats in the balcony (representing the **conduction band**). Between them lies a **"staircase barrier"** (the **band gap, $E_g$**).

- **At 0 K (Absolute Zero):** Every seat in the stalls is occupied by an audience member (electron), and the balcony is completely empty. No one can move, and no electrical current flows. The material behaves as a perfect insulator.
- **At Room Temperature (300 K):** Some energetic audience members (electrons) absorb enough thermal energy to climb the staircase and occupy balcony seats. Each time an electron leaves a stall seat, it leaves behind a **"vacant chair" (a hole)** — essentially a positively charged empty position that neighboring electrons can shuffle into.
- The result: **one electron climbing up = one free electron in the conduction band + one mobile hole in the valence band.** This is the **electron-hole pair generation** process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** In an *intrinsic* semiconductor, the number of free electrons ($n$) in the conduction band **always equals** the number of holes ($p$) in the valence band. This is because every thermally excited electron automatically leaves behind exactly one hole.
> $$n = p = n_i \quad \text{(Intrinsic Condition)}$$

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants Used in This Topic

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value (SI) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $k_B$ | Boltzmann Constant | **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K** |
| $h$ | Planck's Constant | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s** |
| $q$ | Electronic Charge | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C** |
| $m_0$ | Free Electron Mass | **$9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Energy Band Picture of an Intrinsic Semiconductor

A single isolated atom possesses discrete energy levels. When $N$ atoms are brought together to form a crystal, the atomic wavefunctions overlap, causing each discrete level to **split into $N$ closely spaced sub-levels** — forming an **energy band**.

For an intrinsic semiconductor at $T > 0$ K, the energy band diagram has the following critical features:

1. **Valence Band (VB):** The lower energy band, completely filled with electrons at 0 K. Represents bonding covalent electron states.
2. **Conduction Band (CB):** The upper energy band, which is empty at 0 K. Represents anti-bonding or free electron states.
3. **Forbidden Energy Gap ($E_g$):** The energy region between the top of the valence band ($E_v$) and the bottom of the conduction band ($E_c$) where **no allowed electron states exist**.
4. **Fermi Level ($E_F$):** The hypothetical energy level at which the probability of occupation is exactly 50%. In an *intrinsic* semiconductor, $E_F$ lies very close to the **mid-gap**, i.e., $E_F \approx \frac{E_c + E_v}{2}$.

## 2.2 Carrier Concentration: Why and How

### Step 1: Thermal Generation of Electron-Hole Pairs
When a valence electron absorbs a thermal phonon of energy $\geq E_g$, it jumps from $E_v$ to $E_c$. This creates **one free electron** and **one hole** simultaneously. The rate of this process depends on temperature via the Boltzmann factor.

### Step 2: Density of Available States
The **Density of States (DOS)** describes how many available quantum states exist per unit energy per unit volume. The DOS is parabolic near the band edges:

$$g_c(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E - E_c} \quad \text{for } E \geq E_c$$

$$g_v(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_h^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E_v - E} \quad \text{for } E \leq E_v$$

### Step 3: Fermi-Dirac Occupation Probability
The probability that a state at energy $E$ is occupied by an electron is:

$$f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\left(\frac{E - E_F}{k_BT}\right)}$$

### Step 4: Net Carrier Concentration
The total carrier concentration is obtained by integrating the product of DOS and occupation probability over the respective band:

$$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g_c(E) \cdot f(E) \, dE$$

$$p = \int_{-\infty}^{E_v} g_v(E) \cdot [1 - f(E)] \, dE$$

### Step 5: Intrinsic Condition
For an intrinsic semiconductor, **charge neutrality** demands $n = p = n_i$. This single constraint, when combined with the band structure, fixes the position of $E_F$ and yields the famous **mass-action law**.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Instructions to Students:** This table is your exam-day lifeline. Memorize the physical meaning of every symbol and the conditions under which each equation is valid.

| # | Formula | Description / Condition |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $n = p = n_i$ | Intrinsic condition: free electrons = holes |
| 2 | $n_i^2 = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_BT}\right)$ | **Master Equation:** Intrinsic carrier concentration squared |
| 3 | $N_c = 2\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ | Effective DOS in the conduction band (units: m$^{-3}$) |
| 4 | $N_v = 2\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ | Effective DOS in the valence band (units: m$^{-3}$) |
| 5 | $n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{2k_BT}\right)$ | Temperature dependence of $n_i$ |
| 6 | $E_F = \dfrac{E_c + E_v}{2} + \dfrac{3}{4}k_BT \ln\left(\dfrac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)$ | Fermi level position in intrinsic semiconductor |
| 7 | $E_F \approx \dfrac{E_c + E_v}{2}$ | Approximation when $m_e^* \approx m_h^*$ (mid-gap) |
| 8 | $\sigma = n_i q (\mu_e + \mu_h)$ | Intrinsic electrical conductivity |
| 9 | $\sigma_i = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{2k_BT}\right)$ | Conductivity follows Arrhenius-type behavior |
| 10 | $\rho = \dfrac{1}{\sigma_i}$ | Intrinsic resistivity |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The intrinsic semiconductor model is the **mathematical foundation** for:
- **Photodetectors and Solar Cells:** Photon absorption generates $e^-$-$h^+$ pairs if $h\nu \geq E_g$. The resulting photocurrent is directly proportional to $n_i$ and the absorption coefficient.
- **Thermistors (NTC Type):** The strong exponential temperature dependence of $n_i$ (and hence $\sigma$) makes intrinsic semiconductors excellent temperature sensors.
- **High-Voltage Power Devices:** Materials with large $E_g$ (like SiC, GaN — used as intrinsic regions) can sustain high breakdown voltages.
- **Hall Effect Sensors:** Carrier concentration calibration in pure samples is based on the intrinsic $n_i$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Intrinsic Carrier Concentration $n_i$

We begin with the conduction band electron concentration. The general integral is:

$$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g_c(E) \cdot f(E) \, dE$$

**Step 1: Apply the Boltzmann Approximation.**
For an intrinsic semiconductor, $E_c - E_F \gg k_BT$ (because $E_F$ is deep inside the band gap). Therefore, the Fermi-Dirac function simplifies to the **Maxwell-Boltzmann tail**:

$$f(E) \approx \exp\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{k_BT}\right) \quad \text{when } E \gg E_F$$

**Step 2: Substitute the parabolic density of states near $E_c$.**

$$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E - E_c} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{k_BT}\right) dE$$

**Step 3: Perform a variable substitution to evaluate the integral.**
Let $u = \dfrac{E - E_c}{k_BT}$, so that $E - E_F = (E - E_c) + (E_c - E_F)$, and $dE = k_BT \, du$.

$$n = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_BT}\right) \int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{u \cdot k_BT} \cdot e^{-u} \cdot k_BT \, du$$

**Step 4: Use the standard Gamma integral.**
The integral $\int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/2} e^{-u} \, du = \Gamma(3/2) = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$.

$$n = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \cdot (k_BT)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_BT}\right)$$

**Step 5: Simplify to the canonical form.**

$$n = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_BT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_BT}\right)$$

This is the **electron density** in the conduction band, where we have defined the effective density of states:

$$N_c \equiv 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_BT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

Thus, in compact form:

$$n = N_c \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_BT}\right)$$

**Step 6: Apply the identical procedure for holes in the valence band.**

$$p = N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_v}{k_BT}\right)$$

where the effective density of states in the valence band is:

$$N_v \equiv 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_BT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

**Step 7: Impose the intrinsic condition $n = p = n_i$.**

$$N_c \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_BT}\right) = N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_v}{k_BT}\right)$$

**Step 8: Multiply $n \cdot p$ to eliminate $E_F$.**

$$np = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_v}{k_BT}\right) = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_BT}\right)$$

**Step 9: Apply $n = p = n_i$ to obtain the master equation.**

$$\boxed{n_i^2 = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_BT}\right)}$$

Or equivalently:

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2k_BT}\right)$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Position of the Fermi Level $E_F$

From **Step 7** of the previous derivation, set $n = p$:

$$N_c \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_BT}\right) = N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_v}{k_BT}\right)$$

Take the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$\ln N_c - \frac{E_c - E_F}{k_BT} = \ln N_v - \frac{E_F - E_v}{k_BT}$$

Rearrange to isolate $E_F$:

$$\frac{2E_F - E_c - E_v}{k_BT} = \ln N_c - \ln N_v = \ln\left(\frac{N_c}{N_v}\right)$$

Substitute the expressions for $N_c$ and $N_v$:

$$\frac{N_c}{N_v} = \left(\frac{m_e^*}{m_h^*}\right)^{3/2}$$

Therefore:

$$2E_F - E_c - E_v = k_BT \cdot \frac{3}{2} \ln\left(\frac{m_e^*}{m_h^*}\right)$$

$$\boxed{E_F = \frac{E_c + E_v}{2} + \frac{3}{4}k_BT \ln\left(\frac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- If $m_e^* = m_h^*$, the logarithmic term vanishes, and $E_F$ lies **exactly at the center of the band gap**.
- If $m_h^* > m_e^*$, the logarithm is positive, and $E_F$ shifts slightly **upward** (towards $E_c$).
- If $m_h^* < m_e^*$, $E_F$ shifts **downward** (towards $E_v$).

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Temperature Dependence of Intrinsic Conductivity

The electrical conductivity is given by $\sigma = n q \mu_e + p q \mu_h$. In an intrinsic semiconductor, $n = p = n_i$:

$$\sigma_i = n_i q (\mu_e + \mu_h)$$

Substitute the expression for $n_i$:

$$\sigma_i = q(\mu_e + \mu_h) \sqrt{N_c N_v} \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2k_BT}\right)$$

Group the weakly temperature-dependent prefactors into a constant $\sigma_0$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_i = \sigma_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2k_BT}\right)}$$

This Arrhenius-type equation shows that $\sigma_i$ is **exponentially sensitive** to both temperature and band gap. A plot of $\ln \sigma_i$ versus $1/T$ yields a straight line whose slope is $-E_g / (2k_B)$, providing an **experimental method to determine $E_g$**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Key Relations Quick Lookup

| Quantity | Expression | Physical Insight |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $n$ (electrons) | $N_c \exp[-(E_c - E_F)/k_BT]$ | Stronger as $E_F$ moves up |
| $p$ (holes) | $N_v \exp[-(E_F - E_v)/k_BT]$ | Stronger as $E_F$ moves down |
| $n_i^2$ | $N_c N_v \exp[-E_g/k_BT]$ | Independent of $E_F$ |
| $E_F$ position | $\frac{E_c + E_v}{2} + \frac{3}{4}k_BT \ln(m_h^*/m_e^*)$ | Near mid-gap |
| $\sigma_i$ | $\sigma_0 \exp[-E_g/(2k_BT)]$ | Exponential T-dependence |
| $n_i$ at 300 K (Si) | $\approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$ | Far less than atomic density |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Band Diagram of an Intrinsic Semiconductor (KTU Board Style)

The following mermaid block represents the standard **energy band diagram** that examiners expect students to draw during the exam. The diagram maps the relative positions of $E_c$, $E_F$, $E_v$, and the band gap $E_g$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CB["CONDUCTION BAND CB"]
        EcNode["Ec = Bottom of Conduction Band"]
    end
    subgraph FERMI["FERMI LEVEL POSITION"]
        EfNode["EF = Fermi Level - located near mid-gap"]
    end
    subgraph VB["VALENCE BAND VB"]
        EvNode["Ev = Top of Valence Band"]
    end
    EgArrow["Band Gap Energy Eg = Ec - Ev - typically 1.12 eV for Silicon at 300K"]
    GenProcess["Thermal Generation: Electrons jump from Ev to Ec creating electron-hole pairs"]
    
    EcNode -.-> EgArrow
    EgArrow -.-> EvNode
    EcNode -- "n electrons in CB" --> GenProcess
    EvNode -- "p holes left in VB" --> GenProcess
    GenProcess --> EfNode
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Electron-Hole Pair Generation and Recombination

This block diagram illustrates the **dynamic process** of carrier generation, equilibrium, and recombination that defines the steady-state behavior of an intrinsic semiconductor.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA["Thermal Energy kT from Lattice Vibrations"] --> procA["Phonon Absorption by Valence Electron"]
    procA --> procB["Electron Excitation: Ev to Ec"]
    procB --> outA["Free Electron in Conduction Band"]
    procB --> outB["Hole Created in Valence Band"]
    outA --> condA["Electron drifts and conducts current"]
    outB --> condB["Hole acts as positive charge carrier"]
    condA --> recomb["Recombination: Electron drops from Ec to Ev"]
    condB --> recomb
    recomb --> emitEnergy["Energy Released as Photon or Phonon"]
    emitEnergy --> inputA
    
    style inputA fill:#e1f5ff
    style procB fill:#fff4e1
    style recomb fill:#ffe1e1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** In thermal equilibrium, the rate of generation ($G$) and the rate of recombination ($R$) are exactly balanced. The steady-state carrier concentration $n_i$ is therefore **constant in time**, even though individual electrons and holes are continuously being created and destroyed.

## 4.3 Schematic Comparison: Conductor, Intrinsic Semiconductor, and Insulator

This block diagram contrasts the three classes of materials in terms of band structure. This is a frequent KTU question.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COND["CONDUCTOR METAL"]
        C1["Conduction Band Overlaps Valence Band"]
        C2["No Band Gap - electrons flow freely"]
    end
    subgraph INTR["INTRINSIC SEMICONDUCTOR"]
        I1["Ec at Top"]
        I2["Moderate Eg around 1 eV"]
        I3["Ev at Bottom"]
        I1 --- I2 --- I3
    end
    subgraph INSU["INSULATOR"]
        S1["Ec at Top"]
        S2["Very Large Eg greater than 5 eV"]
        S3["Ev at Bottom"]
        S1 --- S2 --- S3
    end
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exams:** The *only* quantitative difference between a semiconductor and an insulator is the **magnitude of the band gap**. At sufficiently high temperatures, even an insulator will eventually conduct. The boundary is not physically fundamental — it is purely defined by convention (typically $E_g \approx 3$ eV).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Q: Define an intrinsic semiconductor. Why is the Fermi level located at the center of the forbidden energy gap in an intrinsic semiconductor?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An intrinsic semiconductor is a pure, undoped semiconductor crystal in which the electrical properties are determined solely by the thermal generation of electron-hole pairs across the band gap $E_g$.

In such a material, the number of free electrons in the conduction band must equal the number of holes in the valence band, i.e., $n = p = n_i$. Setting the two carrier concentrations equal:

$$N_c \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_BT}\right) = N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_v}{k_BT}\right)$$

Solving for $E_F$ (assuming $N_c \approx N_v$, i.e., $m_e^* \approx m_h^*$):

$$E_F = \frac{E_c + E_v}{2}$$

This is the **mid-gap** position. Physically, the Fermi level adjusts itself so that the probability of finding an electron at energy $E_F$ produces exactly the same number of occupied states in the CB as unoccupied states in the VB, ensuring charge neutrality. **(3 Marks)**

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**Q: Distinguish between intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors with two key points.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Property | Intrinsic | Extrinsic |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Purity** | Chemically pure, no impurities added | Deliberately doped with trivalent/pentavalent atoms |
| **Carrier Origin** | $e^-$-$h^+$ pairs generated thermally across $E_g$ | Carriers supplied by dopant energy levels near $E_c$ or $E_v$ |
| **Conductivity** | Low, strongly temperature dependent | High, controlled by dopant concentration |
| **Fermi Level** | Near mid-gap | Shifts towards $E_c$ (n-type) or $E_v$ (p-type) |
| **Examples** | Pure Si, Ge, GaAs crystals | Si doped with P (n-type) or B (p-type) |

**(3 Marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$ of a semiconductor. Show clearly how it depends on the band gap energy and temperature.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For Silicon at 300 K, the following data is given: $E_g = 1.12$ eV, $m_e^* = 1.08 \, m_0$, $m_h^* = 0.56 \, m_0$, $m_0 = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg, $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s. Calculate (i) the effective density of states $N_c$ and $N_v$, and (ii) the intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$.

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation of $n_i$: [7 Marks]**

*Step 1:* The number of free electrons in the conduction band is obtained by integrating the density of states times the Fermi-Dirac occupation probability. With the substitution $E = E_c + \epsilon$ and using the Boltzmann approximation:

$$n = N_c \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_BT}\right)$$

where $N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_BT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$.

*Step 2:* By identical reasoning for holes in the valence band:

$$p = N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_v}{k_BT}\right)$$

where $N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_BT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$.

*Step 3:* Intrinsic condition: $n = p = n_i$. Multiplying $n \times p$ eliminates the Fermi level:

$$n_i^2 = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_v}{k_BT}\right) = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_BT}\right)$$

*Step 4:* Final expression:

$$\boxed{n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2k_BT}\right)}$$

This shows that $n_i$ is **exponentially sensitive** to both $E_g$ (larger gap → fewer carriers) and $T$ (higher temperature → more carriers). **(7 Marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation: [7 Marks]**

**(i) Effective Density of States:**

Substitute the values into the formula for $N_c$:

$$N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$

Compute the prefactor:
$$m_e^* k_B T = (1.08)(9.11 \times 10^{-31})(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)$$
$$= 4.07 \times 10^{-51} \text{ kg·J}$$

$$\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2} = \frac{2\pi (4.07 \times 10^{-51})}{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})^2} = \frac{2.557 \times 10^{-50}}{4.39 \times 10^{-67}} = 5.82 \times 10^{16} \text{ m}^{-2}$$

$$N_c = 2 \times (5.82 \times 10^{16})^{3/2} = 2 \times 1.405 \times 10^{25} = \mathbf{2.81 \times 10^{25} \text{ m}^{-3}}$$

Similarly for $N_v$ (using $m_h^* = 0.56 \, m_0$):

$$N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi (0.56) m_0 k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} = (0.56)^{3/2} \times N_c \text{ (recomputed)}$$

$$N_v = 0.419 \times 2.81 \times 10^{25} = \mathbf{1.18 \times 10^{25} \text{ m}^{-3}}$$

**(ii) Intrinsic Carrier Concentration:**

$$\frac{E_g}{2k_BT} = \frac{(1.12)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})}{2(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)} = \frac{1.794 \times 10^{-19}}{8.28 \times 10^{-21}} = 21.66$$

$$n_i = \sqrt{(2.81 \times 10^{25})(1.18 \times 10^{25})} \times e^{-21.66}$$

$$n_i = \sqrt{3.32 \times 10^{50}} \times (3.97 \times 10^{-10})$$

$$n_i = 1.82 \times 10^{25} \times 3.97 \times 10^{-10}$$

$$\boxed{n_i \approx 7.2 \times 10^{15} \text{ m}^{-3} = 7.2 \times 10^{9} \text{ cm}^{-3}}$$

*(Note: Standard textbook value for Si is $\approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ cm$^{-3}$. Slight discrepancy is due to rounding.)* **(7 Marks)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the formation of energy bands in solids. Discuss the position of the Fermi level in (i) conductors, (ii) intrinsic semiconductors, and (iii) insulators.

**(b) [7 Marks]** The electrical conductivity of an intrinsic semiconductor at 300 K is $10^{-6}$ S/m. If the electron and hole mobilities are 0.14 m²/(V·s) and 0.05 m²/(V·s) respectively, calculate the intrinsic carrier concentration. If the temperature is raised to 350 K, by what factor does the conductivity change, given $E_g = 1.1$ eV?

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Energy Band Formation and Fermi Levels: [7 Marks]**

*Energy Band Formation (Bloch's Theorem Insight):*
When $N$ isolated atoms are brought closer to form a crystal, the outermost electron wavefunctions begin to overlap. Pauli's exclusion principle forces each discrete atomic energy level to split into $N$ distinct but closely spaced sub-levels, forming a quasi-continuous **energy band**. The width of the band increases with the strength of inter-atomic overlap.

*Energy Band Diagram:* A typical KTU board diagram should show:
- A valence band completely filled at 0 K,
- A conduction band empty at 0 K,
- The forbidden gap of width $E_g$ in between.

*Position of the Fermi Level:*

| Material | Fermi Level Position | Reason |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Conductor** | Lies *inside* the conduction band (or within an overlapping band) | Partially filled band → free carriers even at 0 K |
| **Intrinsic Semiconductor** | Lies at the **center** of the forbidden gap | $n = p$ condition forces mid-gap position |
| **Insulator** | Lies at the center of a *very wide* gap ($E_g > 5$ eV) | Mid-gap, but gap is so large that $n_i \approx 0$ |

**(7 Marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation: [7 Marks]**

**(i) Intrinsic Carrier Concentration at 300 K:**

Using $\sigma_i = n_i q (\mu_e + \mu_h)$:

$$n_i = \frac{\sigma_i}{q(\mu_e + \mu_h)} = \frac{10^{-6}}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.14 + 0.05)}$$

$$n_i = \frac{10^{-6}}{(1.602 \times 10^{-19})(0.19)} = \frac{10^{-6}}{3.04 \times 10^{-20}}$$

$$\boxed{n_i \approx 3.29 \times 10^{13} \text{ m}^{-3}}$$

**(ii) Conductivity Ratio between 350 K and 300 K:**

The conductivity follows:

$$\sigma_i \propto \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{2k_BT}\right)$$

Therefore, the ratio is:

$$\frac{\sigma_{350}}{\sigma_{300}} = \exp\left[-\frac{E_g}{2k_B}\left(\frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{300}\right)\right]$$

Compute the exponent:
$$\frac{1}{350} - \frac{1}{300} = \frac{300 - 350}{350 \times 300} = \frac{-50}{105000} = -4.762 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$-\frac{E_g}{2k_B} = -\frac{(1.1)(1.602 \times 10^{-19})}{2(1.38 \times 10^{-23})} = -\frac{1.762 \times 10^{-19}}{2.76 \times 10^{-23}} = -6384 \text{ K}$$

$$\text{Exponent} = (-6384)(-4.762 \times 10^{-4}) = 3.04$$

$$\frac{\sigma_{350}}{\sigma_{300}} = e^{3.04} \approx \mathbf{20.9}$$

The conductivity **increases by a factor of about 21** when the temperature rises from 300 K to 350 K. This demonstrates the extreme temperature sensitivity of intrinsic semiconductors. **(7 Marks)**

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units of $N_c$ and $N_v$:** Students often compute $N_c$ but forget to express it in **m$^{-3}$** or **cm$^{-3}$**. A mismatch in units between $N_c$ and $n_i$ is the #1 cause of "wrong numerical answers with right method." Always state units explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing $E_g$ and $E_g/2$ in the exponent:** The conductivity uses $E_g/2$ in the exponent, while the $np$ product uses $E_g$. This is a frequent trap. Remember: **carrier concentration has $E_g/2$, while $np$ product has $E_g$**.
> 3. **Converting eV to Joules incorrectly:** When plugging $E_g$ into Boltzmann's exponential, you MUST convert eV to Joules by multiplying by $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$. Failing to do this gives absurdly wrong answers.
> 4. **Assuming $m_e^* = m_h^*$:** The effective masses are never exactly equal. In Si, $m_e^* \approx 0.26 \, m_0$ (with multiple valley contributions) and $m_h^* \approx 0.39 \, m_0$ (density-of-states averaged). Always use the values given in the problem.
> 5. **Forgetting the $T^{3/2}$ dependence:** The $\sqrt{N_c N_v}$ prefactor is **not constant** — it scales as $T^{3/2}$. Ignoring this leads to a 5–10% error, which examiners may deduct partial marks for.
> 6. **Fermi level approximation:** Writing $E_F = \frac{E_c + E_v}{2}$ is only valid if $m_e^* = m_h^*$. Otherwise, the full expression with the logarithmic correction must be used.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Summary — Print This for Last-Minute Revision:**

- **Intrinsic Semiconductor:** Pure crystal; carriers come only from thermal $E_g$ excitation; $n = p = n_i$.
- **Energy Band Gap Values (300 K):** Si = 1.12 eV, Ge = 0.67 eV, GaAs = 1.42 eV.
- **Master Equation (Law of Mass Action for Intrinsic):** $n_i^2 = N_c N_v \exp(-E_g/k_BT)$.
- **Effective DOS:** $N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_BT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$, similarly for $N_v$ with $m_h^*$.
- **Fermi Level Position:** $E_F = \frac{E_c + E_v}{2} + \frac{3}{4}k_BT \ln(m_h^*/m_e^*)$ — near mid-gap.
- **Intrinsic Conductivity:** $\sigma_i = \sigma_0 \exp(-E_g/2k_BT)$ — Arrhenius form.
- **Temperature Sensitivity:** A 50 K rise in $T$ can multiply $\sigma_i$ by 20× in Si — extremely strong $T$ dependence.
- **Mid-Gap Rule:** For an intrinsic semiconductor with equal effective masses, $E_F$ is exactly mid-gap; for unequal masses, $E_F$ shifts slightly towards the band with the **larger effective mass**.
- **Hall Effect Sign in Intrinsic:** Both electrons and holes contribute, often leading to ambiguous sign of Hall coefficient at high temperatures — a classic KTU conceptual question.
- **Common Confusion to Avoid:** The $E_g$ in the **conductivity** exponent is divided by 2; the $E_g$ in the **$np$ product** is undivided. Memorize this distinction.
- **KTU Exam Tip:** Always draw the energy band diagram with $E_c$, $E_F$, and $E_v$ clearly marked. Label $E_g$ between $E_c$ and $E_v$. A neat diagram often earns partial credit even if the calculation has a small error.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of Density of Electrons in Conduction Band and Density of Holes in Valence Band
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Derivation of Density of Electrons in Conduction Band and Density of Holes in Valence Band # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In semiconductor physics, the **density of electrons in the conduction band** ($n$) and the **density of holes in the valence band** ($p$) refer to the number of free charge carriers available per unit volume of a semiconductor material for electrical conduction at a given absolute temperature $T$. Mathematically, the carrier concentrations are obtained by integrating the product of the **Density of States (DOS)** function and the **Fermi-Dirac probability distribution function** over the respective energy bands. For a non-degenerate (lightly doped) semiconductor in thermal equilibrium, these reduce to the classical Maxwell-Boltzmann forms: $$n = N_c \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$ $$p = N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_v}{k_B T}\right)$$ where $E_c$, $E_v$, and $E_F$ are the conduction band minimum, valence band maximum, and Fermi energy, respectively. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This is a **high-yield derivation topic** for KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE). It directly maps to **Course Outcome CO1** (Apply the principles of physics in electrical engineering applications) and tests cognitive levels **Apply (L3)** and **Analyze (L4)** of Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **multi-storey parking garage** (the semiconductor crystal lattice) with two specific floors of interest: - **Ground Floor (Valence Band):** All parking slots are filled with cars (electrons bound in covalent bonds). These cars cannot move because every slot is occupied. - **Top Floor (Conduction Band):** This floor is completely empty. A car here is free to move and contribute to traffic (electrical current). - **The Forbidden Gap ($E_g$):** The closed shutter between the floors. - **Thermal Excitation (Heat):** The car driver who has enough energy to "jump" from the ground floor to the top floor. - **The Empty Slot (Hole):** When a car leaves the ground floor, the vacant spot behaves like a positively charged particle that can be filled by an adjacent car — appearing to move. **Plain English Summary:** - **Electron density ($n$):** How many cars are currently driving on the top floor. - **Hole density ($p$):** How many empty parking slots exist on the ground floor. > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constants** (memorize these for numerical problems): > - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** $= 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ **eV/K** > - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s** > - Reduced Planck's constant: $\hbar = h / 2\pi = 1.0546 \times 10^{-34}$ **J·s** > - Thermal voltage at 300 K: $V_T = k_B T / q \approx 0.0259$ **V** --- ## 1.3 Why This Derivation Matters in Electrical Engineering The carrier density equations are the **foundation** for understanding: - **pn-junction diodes and BJTs** (current-voltage characteristics depend on $n$ and $p$) - **MOSFETs and CMOS logic gates** (threshold voltage tuning requires controlling carrier densities) - **Photodetectors and solar cells** (photocurrent generation) - **Hall Effect sensors** (carrier concentration measurement) - **Intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductor behavior** (Fermi level shifts) # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Foundational Concepts Required Before the derivation, three pillars must be established: ### Pillar 1: The Fermi-Dirac Distribution Function $f(E)$ This probability function tells us the likelihood that an available quantum state at energy $E$ is occupied by an electron **at thermal equilibrium**: $$f(E) = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\left(\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)}$$ **Behavioural limits (critical for derivation):** - When $E - E_F \gg k_B T$: $f(E) \to \exp(-(E - E_F)/k_B T)$ → **Boltzmann Approximation** (used in derivation) - When $E - E_F \ll k_B T$: $f(E) \to 1$ (state is fully occupied) - At $E = E_F$: $f(E) = 1/2$ (the definition of Fermi level) ### Pillar 2: Density of States $g(E)$ The number of available quantum states per unit volume per unit energy interval at energy $E$. The 3D free-electron derivation yields: $$g(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2} \left(\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E}$$ This is valid for an electron near the **edge of a band** when measured relative to the band edge. ### Pillar 3: Effective Mass $m^*$ Electrons in a crystal lattice behave as if they have a modified inertial mass called **effective mass**, which captures the effect of the periodic potential. For our derivation: - Electron effective mass in conduction band: $m_e^*$ - Hole effective mass in valence band: $m_h^*$ --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of the Derivation The derivation follows a structured 5-step framework: 1. **Identify available states** in conduction band → write $g_c(E)$ measured from $E_c$. 2. **Identify occupancy probability** → use Fermi-Dirac function. 3. **Form the integrand** $g_c(E) \cdot f(E)$ — number of filled states per unit volume. 4. **Apply Boltzmann approximation** (justified when $E_c - E_F \gg k_B T$, i.e., non-degenerate semiconductor). 5. **Evaluate the integral** and recognize the result as a standard Gaussian-type form giving the effective density of states $N_c$. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Justification for Boltzmann Approximation:** For an **intrinsic or lightly doped semiconductor at room temperature**, the Fermi level lies deep inside the band gap (typically $> 3k_BT$ from either band edge). Hence $E - E_F \gg k_B T$ in the conduction band, and the exponential in the denominator dominates, allowing the simplification. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Physical Meaning** | |---|---|---|---| | Density of states (3D, free electron) | $g(E) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\dfrac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}\sqrt{E}$ | $\text{m}^{-3}\,\text{J}^{-1}$ | Available quantum states per unit volume per unit energy | | Fermi-Dirac distribution | $f(E) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp\left(\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)}$ | dimensionless | Probability of state occupancy | | Boltzmann approximation | $f(E) \approx \exp\left(-\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$ | dimensionless | Valid for $E - E_F \gg k_B T$ | | Effective DOS in conduction band | $N_c = 2\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ | $\text{m}^{-3}$ | Equivalent DOS at conduction band edge | | Effective DOS in valence band | $N_v = 2\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$ | $\text{m}^{-3}$ | Equivalent DOS at valence band edge | | Electron density (conduction band) | $n = N_c \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$ | $\text{m}^{-3}$ | Free electrons per unit volume | | Hole density (valence band) | $p = N_v \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_F - E_v}{k_B T}\right)$ | $\text{m}^{-3}$ | Free holes per unit volume | | Intrinsic carrier concentration | $n_i^2 = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$ | $\text{m}^{-6}$ | Law of mass action | | Intrinsic Fermi level | $E_i = \dfrac{E_c + E_v}{2} + \dfrac{3}{4}k_B T \ln\left(\dfrac{m_h^*}{m_e^*}\right)$ | eV | Fermi level of intrinsic semiconductor | | np product (mass action law) | $np = n_i^2$ | $\text{m}^{-6}$ | Fundamental equilibrium relation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility:** The expression $n = N_c \exp(-(E_c - E_F)/k_B T)$ is the **master equation** used in device physics simulators (TCAD tools like Sentaurus and ATLAS) to initialize carrier profiles in semiconductors. The $T^{3/2}$ dependence of $N_c$ and $N_v$ explains the **strong temperature sensitivity** of semiconductor devices. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation Part A: Density of States in 3D Conduction Band ### Step A1: Setting up the Schrödinger equation Consider a free electron confined in a 3D crystal volume $V = L^3$ with periodic boundary conditions. The time-independent Schrödinger equation is: $$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e^*}\nabla^2 \psi(\vec{r}) = E \psi(\vec{r})$$ The solution is a plane wave: $$\psi(\vec{r}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{V}} \exp(i\vec{k} \cdot \vec{r})$$ ### Step A2: Applying periodic boundary conditions Using the Born–von Karman condition that $\psi$ must be periodic over length $L$: $$\psi(x + L, y, z) = \psi(x, y, z)$$ This gives the constraint on wave vector components: $$k_x = \frac{2\pi n_x}{L}, \quad k_y = \frac{2\pi n_y}{L}, \quad k_z = \frac{2\pi n_z}{L}$$ where $n_x, n_y, n_z$ are integers (positive, negative, or zero). ### Step A3: Energy-wavevector relationship Substituting the plane wave into the Schrödinger equation yields the parabolic energy-momentum relation: $$E = \frac{\hbar^2 k^2}{2m_e^*} = \frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e^*}(k_x^2 + k_y^2 + k_z^2)$$ ### Step A4: Counting states in k-space In **k-space**, each allowed state occupies a volume of $(2\pi/L)^3 = 8\pi^3 / V$. Therefore, the **number of states per unit volume in k-space** (i.e., per unit real-space volume) is: $$\text{Density of states in k-space} = \frac{1}{8\pi^3 / V} \cdot \frac{1}{V} = \frac{1}{8\pi^3}$$ ### Step A5: Counting states with magnitude of k between k and k+dk The number of states in a spherical shell of radius $k$ and thickness $dk$ in k-space is: $$\text{Number of states} = \frac{1}{8\pi^3} \times 4\pi k^2 \, dk = \frac{k^2 \, dk}{2\pi^2}$$ Multiplying by 2 to account for **electron spin** (two spin states $\uparrow$ and $\downarrow$ per orbital state): $$\text{Number of states} = \frac{k^2 \, dk}{\pi^2}$$ ### Step A6: Converting k to E From the dispersion relation, $E = \hbar^2 k^2 / (2m_e^*)$, we solve for $k$: $$k = \sqrt{\frac{2m_e^* E}{\hbar^2}}$$ Differentiating with respect to $E$: $$\frac{dk}{dE} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2 E}} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{E}} = \frac{1}{2\hbar}\sqrt{\frac{2m_e^*}{E}}$$ ### Step A7: Number of states with energy between E and E+dE The number of states per unit volume in the energy interval $dE$ is $g(E) \, dE$: $$g(E) \, dE = \frac{k^2}{\pi^2} \, dk = \frac{k^2}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{dk}{dE} \, dE$$ Substituting $k^2 = 2m_e^* E / \hbar^2$ and $dk/dE$: $$g(E) = \frac{1}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{2m_e^* E}{\hbar^2} \cdot \frac{1}{2\hbar}\sqrt{\frac{2m_e^*}{E}}$$ $$g(E) = \frac{1}{\pi^2} \cdot \frac{m_e^*}{\hbar^3} \cdot \sqrt{2m_e^* E}$$ $$\boxed{g(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E}}$$ This is the density of states per unit volume per unit energy, measured from the **bottom of the conduction band** (so $E \geq 0$ in this convention). --- ## 3.2 Derivation Part B: Density of Electrons in the Conduction Band ### Step B1: The general integral The number of electrons per unit volume in the conduction band is obtained by integrating the **product** of (number of available states) and (probability that each state is occupied) over all energies in the conduction band: $$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g_c(E) \, f(E) \, dE$$ > **Physical interpretation:** $g_c(E) \, dE$ = number of states between $E$ and $E + dE$; $f(E)$ = fraction of those states that are actually filled. The product gives the **actual number of electrons** in that energy range. ### Step B2: Substitute the density of states Using the DOS expression derived in Part A, with energy measured from $E_c$ (so we replace $E$ by $E - E_c$): $$g_c(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_c}$$ Therefore: $$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_c} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \exp\left(\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)} \, dE$$ ### Step B3: Apply the Boltzmann approximation For a non-degenerate semiconductor where $E - E_F \gg k_B T$ throughout the conduction band (the Fermi level lies several $k_BT$ below $E_c$): $$\frac{1}{1 + \exp\left(\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)} \approx \exp\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$ This is valid because $\exp(x) \gg 1$ when $x = (E - E_F)/k_BT \gg 1$, so the "1" in the denominator is negligible. Substituting: $$n \approx \int_{E_c}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_c} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \, dE$$ ### Step B4: Factor out constants and simplify the integral Let $u = E - E_c$ (a substitution that shifts the origin to the conduction band edge, where $u \geq 0$). Then $E = E_c + u$ and $dE = du$: $$n = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{u} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{u}{k_B T}\right) \, du$$ > **Note on factoring:** The exponential $\exp(-(E - E_F)/k_BT) = \exp(-(E_c + u - E_F)/k_BT) = \exp(-(E_c - E_F)/k_BT) \cdot \exp(-u/k_BT)$. The first factor is independent of the integration variable $u$ and is pulled out of the integral. ### Step B5: Evaluate the standard integral The integral $\int_0^{\infty} \sqrt{u} \, e^{-u/(k_BT)} \, du$ is of the form $\int_0^{\infty} u^{1/2} e^{-au} du = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} a^{-3/2}$, where $a = 1/(k_B T)$: $$\int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{u} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{u}{k_B T}\right) du = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} (k_B T)^{3/2}$$ ### Step B6: Assemble the result $$n = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} (k_B T)^{3/2} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$ $$n = \frac{1}{4\pi^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{(2m_e^*)^{3/2}}{\hbar^3} \cdot (k_B T)^{3/2} \sqrt{\pi} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$ $$n = \frac{1}{4\pi^{3/2}} \cdot \frac{(2m_e^*)^{3/2}}{\hbar^3} \cdot (\pi)^{1/2} \cdot (k_B T)^{3/2} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$ $$n = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{(2\pi m_e^* k_B T)^{3/2}}{h^3} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$ ### Step B7: Define effective density of states $N_c$ We define the **effective density of states** in the conduction band as: $$N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$ Using $\hbar = h / 2\pi$, this can also be written as $N_c = 2\left(\dfrac{m_e^* k_B T}{2\pi \hbar^2}\right)^{3/2}$. ### Step B8: Final expression for electron density $$\boxed{n = N_c \, \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right)}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation Part C: Density of Holes in the Valence Band ### Step C1: Holes as empty states in the valence band A **hole** is defined as the absence of an electron in an otherwise filled valence band. The probability that a state at energy $E$ in the valence band is **empty** is: $$1 - f(E) = \frac{\exp\left(\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)}{1 + \exp\left(\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)} = \frac{1}{1 + \exp\left(-\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)}$$ ### Step C2: Setting up the hole density integral The number of holes per unit volume in the valence band is: $$p = \int_{-\infty}^{E_v} g_v(E) \, [1 - f(E)] \, dE$$ > **Physical interpretation:** $g_v(E) \, dE$ is the number of available states; $1 - f(E)$ is the probability that a state is empty (i.e., contains a hole). ### Step C3: Apply Boltzmann approximation for the hole case For the valence band, the relevant condition is $E_F - E \gg k_B T$ (Fermi level lies well above the valence band states of interest): $$1 - f(E) \approx \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E}{k_B T}\right) = \exp\left(\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$ ### Step C4: Density of states in the valence band The DOS expression for the valence band, with energy measured from $E_v$ (but inverted because states are below $E_v$): $$g_v(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_h^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E_v - E}$$ ### Step C5: Substituting and simplifying $$p = \int_{-\infty}^{E_v} \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_h^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E_v - E} \cdot \exp\left(\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \, dE$$ Let $u = E_v - E$ (so $E = E_v - u$, $dE = -du$, and the limits become $u = \infty$ to $u = 0$): $$p = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_h^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \exp\left(\frac{E_v - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{u} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{u}{k_B T}\right) \, du$$ > **Note:** The limits are reversed by $-du$, which flips the sign, restoring the correct integral from $0$ to $\infty$. ### Step C6: Evaluate the integral (same form as before) $$\int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{u} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{u}{k_B T}\right) du = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} (k_B T)^{3/2}$$ ### Step C7: Final expression for hole density $$p = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2} \exp\left(\frac{E_v - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$ Define the **effective density of states** in the valence band: $$N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$$ ### Step C8: Final boxed expression $$\boxed{p = N_v \, \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_v}{k_B T}\right)}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation Part D: The Mass Action Law (np Product) Multiplying the two carrier density expressions: $$n \cdot p = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_v}{k_B T}\right) = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$ For an intrinsic semiconductor, $n = p = n_i$, so: $$n_i^2 = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$ Therefore, the **law of mass action** states: $$\boxed{n \cdot p = n_i^2}$$ > This relation is **temperature-dependent** through $N_c(T), N_v(T)$ and $E_g(T)$ but is **independent of doping** — a fundamental invariant for any non-degenerate semiconductor in thermal equilibrium. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Numerical Estimation at 300 K For **Silicon (Si)** at $T = 300$ K with $m_e^* = 1.08 m_0$ and $m_h^* = 0.81 m_0$: | **Parameter** | **Value** | **Computation Check** | |---|---|---| | $N_c$ for Si | $\approx 2.8 \times 10^{25}$ **m$^{-3}$** | $2 \times (2\pi \times 0.26 \times 9.11\times10^{-31} \times 1.38\times10^{-23} \times 300 / (6.626\times10^{-34})^2)^{3/2}$ | | $N_v$ for Si | $\approx 1.04 \times 10^{25}$ **m$^{-3}$** | Similar with $m_h^* = 0.81 m_0$ | | $E_g$ (Si, 300 K) | $1.12$ **eV** | Standard textbook value | | $n_i$ (Si, 300 K) | $\approx 1.0 \times 10^{16}$ **m$^{-3}$** | From $n_i = \sqrt{N_c N_v} \exp(-E_g / 2k_B T)$ | | $k_B T$ at 300 K | $\approx 0.0259$ **eV** | $T/11600$ rule of thumb | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Energy Band Diagram with Electron & Hole Distribution The following Mermaid block renders a schematic flow showing how the density of states combines with the Fermi-Dirac distribution to produce carrier densities in the conduction and valence bands of an n-type semiconductor. ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeStart([Energy E axis: vertical]) --> nodeCB nodeCB[CONDUCTION BAND E c and above
DOS gcE is parabolic in E minus Ec
fd distribution tail fE is small] --> nodeEF nodeEF[FERMI LEVEL EF lies just below Ec for n-type
Acts as reference energy] --> nodeVB nodeVB[VALENCE BAND E v and below
DOS gvE is parabolic in Ev minus E
1 minus fE tail is small] nodeCB --> nIntegral[Electron density n equals integral of gcE times fE dE from Ec to infinity] nodeVB --> pIntegral[Hole density p equals integral of gvE times 1 minus fE dE from minus infinity to Ev] nIntegral --> nResult[Result: n equals Nc times exp minus Ec minus EF over kB T] pIntegral --> pResult[Result: p equals Nv times exp minus EF minus Ev over kB T] nResult --> massAction[Mass action law: n times p equals ni squared] pResult --> massAction massAction --> nodeEnd([Closed-form carrier statistics ready for device analysis]) ``` --- ## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram: Derivation Pipeline This block diagram maps the **logical pipeline** of the derivation from fundamental quantum inputs to final engineering outputs. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[INPUT: Quantum boundary conditions on box volume V] --> B[Generate allowed k vectors on cubic lattice] B --> C[Apply parabolic dispersion E equals hbar squared k squared over 2m star] C --> D[Count states in spherical shell of thickness dk in k space] D --> E[Convert integration variable from k to E] E --> F[OUTPUT 1: Density of states gE proportional to square root of E] F --> G[INPUT: Fermi-Dirac distribution fE] G --> H{Multiply gE times fE and apply Boltzmann approximation} H --> I[Integrate over conduction band Ec to infinity] H --> J[Integrate 1 minus fE over valence band minus infinity to Ev] I --> K[OUTPUT 2: Electron density n equals Nc exp minus Ec minus EF over kB T] J --> L[OUTPUT 3: Hole density p equals Nv exp minus EF minus Ev over kB T] K --> M[Multiply n times p to obtain mass action law] L --> M M --> N[FINAL OUTPUT: np equals ni squared temperature dependent invariant] ``` --- ## 4.3 Schematic Comparison: Intrinsic vs Doped Semiconductors ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph intrinsic["INTRINSIC SEMICONDUCTOR: EF = Ei near midgap"] i1[Conduction band Ec] --> i2[Thermal excitation creates electron-hole pairs] i2 --> i3[EF lies exactly at midgap: Ei] i3 --> i4[Equal densities: n equals p equals ni] end subgraph ntype["N-TYPE: Donor doping shifts EF upward"] n1[Donor level Ed near Ec] --> n2[Electrons donated to conduction band] n2 --> n3[EF shifts toward Ec] n3 --> n4[n is large, p is small: n greater than p] end subgraph ptype["P-TYPE: Acceptor doping shifts EF downward"] p1[Acceptor level Ea near Ev] --> p2[Holes created in valence band] p2 --> p3[EF shifts toward Ev] p3 --> p4[p is large, n is small: p greater than n] end intrinsic --> deviceApp[Engineering Application: pn-junction diode, BJT, MOSFET, solar cell] ntype --> deviceApp ptype --> deviceApp ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** ### Question 1: Define the term "Density of States" and state its mathematical form for a 3D free electron gas. **Model Answer:** The **Density of States (DOS)**, denoted $g(E)$, is defined as the number of available quantum states per unit volume per unit energy interval at a given energy $E$. For a 3D free electron gas (or conduction band electrons near the band edge), it is derived from the parabolic dispersion relation and the periodic boundary conditions on the wave vector: $$g(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E}$$ where $m^*$ is the effective mass of the electron, $\hbar$ is the reduced Planck's constant, and the energy $E$ is measured from the conduction band edge. The factor of 2 in the prefactor accounts for the two possible electron spin orientations ($\uparrow$ and $\downarrow$). The $\sqrt{E}$ dependence reflects the increasing number of higher-energy states as the spherical shell in k-space grows in volume with $k^2$. --- ### Question 2: What is the Boltzmann approximation? Under what conditions is it valid in semiconductor statistics? **Model Answer:** The **Boltzmann approximation** is a mathematical simplification of the Fermi-Dirac distribution function that is valid when the energy of interest lies several $k_BT$ above the Fermi level, i.e., when $E - E_F \gg k_BT$. Under this condition, the exponential term in the denominator dominates over the "1", and the function reduces to: $$f(E) \approx \exp\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)$$ **Validity conditions in semiconductors:** - The semiconductor must be **non-degenerate** (lightly or moderately doped). - The Fermi level must lie **deep inside the band gap**, typically more than $3k_BT$ away from both band edges. - This is satisfied for **intrinsic semiconductors** and for **lightly doped extrinsic semiconductors** at room temperature (300 K). For heavily doped (degenerate) semiconductors, where $E_F$ lies inside the conduction or valence band, the Boltzmann approximation fails and the full Fermi-Dirac integral must be used. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]** ### Question A: Derive the expression for the density of electrons in the conduction band of an intrinsic semiconductor. Show all intermediate steps clearly. Also derive the expression for the effective density of states $N_c$. **Model Solution Outline (14 marks: a=7, b=7):** #### Part (a) — Derivation of Density of States $g(E)$ (7 marks) **Step 1:** Consider an electron of effective mass $m_e^*$ confined in a 3D crystal volume $V$. The time-independent Schrödinger equation is: $$-\frac{\hbar^2}{2m_e^*}\nabla^2 \psi = E \psi$$ **Step 2:** Apply periodic boundary conditions (Born–von Karman): $k_i = 2\pi n_i / L$ for $i = x, y, z$, where $n_i$ are integers. **Step 3:** Each allowed state occupies a k-space volume of $(2\pi/L)^3 = 8\pi^3 / V$. The number of states per unit real-space volume in k-space is $1/(8\pi^3)$. **Step 4:** Count states in a spherical shell of radius $k$ and thickness $dk$ in k-space: $$N(k) \, dk = 2 \times \frac{4\pi k^2 \, dk}{8\pi^3} = \frac{k^2 \, dk}{\pi^2}$$ where the factor 2 accounts for electron spin. **Step 5:** Convert to energy using $E = \hbar^2 k^2 / (2m_e^*)$, so $k = \sqrt{2m_e^* E / \hbar^2}$ and $dk/dE = (1/2)\sqrt{2m_e^* / (\hbar^2 E)}$: $$g(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E}$$ **Step 6:** Units check: $g(E)$ has dimensions of $\text{m}^{-3}\,\text{J}^{-1}$ — verified. #### Part (b) — Derivation of Electron Density $n$ (7 marks) **Step 1:** The electron density is the integral of $g(E) f(E)$ over the conduction band: $$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} g_c(E) \, f(E) \, dE$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $g_c(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_c}$ and $f(E)$: $$n = \int_{E_c}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_c} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \exp\left(\dfrac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right)} \, dE$$ **Step 3:** Apply Boltzmann approximation (valid since $E - E_F \gg k_BT$): $$n \approx \int_{E_c}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E - E_c} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \, dE$$ **Step 4:** Substitute $u = E - E_c$ and factor out the constant exponential: $$n = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_e^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{u} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{u}{k_B T}\right) du$$ **Step 5:** Evaluate the standard integral: $\int_0^{\infty} u^{1/2} e^{-u/k_BT} du = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}(k_BT)^{3/2}$. **Step 6:** After algebra, define $N_c$ and write: $$\boxed{n = N_c \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right), \quad N_c = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_e^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}}$$ --- ### Question B (Internal Choice): Derive the expression for the density of holes in the valence band of an intrinsic semiconductor, and using the result, establish the law of mass action $np = n_i^2$. **Model Solution Outline (14 marks: a=7, b=7):** #### Part (a) — Derivation of Hole Density $p$ (7 marks) **Step 1:** A hole is an empty electron state. The probability that a state at energy $E$ in the valence band is empty is $1 - f(E)$. **Step 2:** The hole density integral is: $$p = \int_{-\infty}^{E_v} g_v(E) [1 - f(E)] \, dE$$ **Step 3:** For the valence band states, $E_F - E \gg k_BT$, so $1 - f(E) \approx \exp(-(E_F - E)/k_BT)$. **Step 4:** The valence band DOS (measured from $E_v$) is: $$g_v(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_h^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E_v - E}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute and use $u = E_v - E$: $$p = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2m_h^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \exp\left(\frac{E_v - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \int_0^{\infty} \sqrt{u} \exp\left(-\frac{u}{k_B T}\right) du$$ **Step 6:** Evaluate the integral, factor out constants, and write the final result: $$\boxed{p = N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_v}{k_B T}\right), \quad N_v = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_h^* k_B T}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}}$$ #### Part (b) — Establishment of the Law of Mass Action (7 marks) **Step 1:** Multiply the expressions for $n$ and $p$: $$n \cdot p = N_c \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F}{k_B T}\right) \cdot N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_F - E_v}{k_B T}\right)$$ **Step 2:** Combine the exponentials (the $E_F$ terms cancel): $$n \cdot p = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_F + E_F - E_v}{k_B T}\right) = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_c - E_v}{k_B T}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Recognize that $E_c - E_v = E_g$ (the band gap energy): $$n \cdot p = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$ **Step 4:** For an intrinsic semiconductor, $n = p = n_i$, so: $$n_i^2 = N_c N_v \exp\left(-\frac{E_g}{k_B T}\right)$$ **Step 5:** Therefore, in **any** non-degenerate semiconductor in thermal equilibrium: $$\boxed{n \cdot p = n_i^2}$$ > This is a **temperature-dependent invariant** — independent of doping, but strongly temperature-sensitive through $E_g(T)$, $N_c(T)$, and $N_v(T)$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting the spin degeneracy factor of 2** in the density of states derivation — this costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Using the full Fermi-Dirac function** instead of the Boltzmann approximation — the integral becomes intractable, and you will not get the closed-form result. > 3. **Not justifying the Boltzmann approximation** — always state "$E - E_F \gg k_BT$" explicitly to earn the reasoning mark. > 4. **Sign errors in the hole density exponent** — the answer must be $\exp(-(E_F - E_v)/k_BT)$, not $\exp((E_F - E_v)/k_BT)$. > 5. **Confusing $g_c(E)$ vs $g_v(E)$** — energy is measured from the band edge in each case; the integrand form is $\sqrt{E - E_c}$ for conduction band and $\sqrt{E_v - E}$ for valence band. > 6. **Forgetting to define $N_c$ and $N_v$ explicitly** before writing the final answer — this is a 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation. > 7. **Not showing the substitution $u = E - E_c$** (or $u = E_v - E$) — KTU examiners specifically look for the change of variable as a critical algebraic step. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### 🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist - ✅ **Density of States** $g(E) = \dfrac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\dfrac{2m^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{3/2} \sqrt{E}$ — parabolic in energy, includes spin degeneracy factor of 2. - ✅ **Fermi-Dirac distribution** $f(E) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \exp((E - E_F)/k_BT)}$ — exact form valid for all energies. - ✅ **Boltzmann approximation** $f(E) \approx \exp(-(E - E_F)/k_BT)$ — applies when $E - E_F \gg k_BT$ (non-degenerate case). - ✅ **Electron density** $n = N_c \exp(-(E_c - E_F)/k_BT)$ where $N_c = 2(2\pi m_e^* k_B T / h^2)^{3/2}$. - ✅ **Hole density** $p = N_v \exp(-(E_F - E_v)/k_BT)$ where $N_v = 2(2\pi m_h^* k_B T / h^2)^{3/2}$. - ✅ **Mass action law** $np = n_i^2 = N_c N_v \exp(-E_g / k_BT)$ — fundamental invariant. - ✅ **Effective density of states** scales as $T^{3/2}$ — this is why carrier density is so temperature-sensitive. - ✅ **$n$ depends on $E_c - E_F$**, while $p$ depends on $E_F - E_v$ — this **asymmetry** is what allows doping to shift $E_F$ and create excess electrons or holes. - ✅ **Boltzmann approximation is valid** for: intrinsic Si at 300 K ($E_F \approx E_i$ deep in gap), lightly doped extrinsic Si at 300 K. - ✅ **Numerical values to memorize (Si at 300 K):** $N_c \approx 2.8 \times 10^{25}$ m$^{-3}$, $N_v \approx 1.04 \times 10^{25}$ m$^{-3}$, $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{16}$ m$^{-3}$, $E_g \approx 1.12$ eV. ### 🎯 Common Numerical Problem Setups for KTU Exam 1. **Given $E_c - E_F = 0.2$ eV at 300 K, find $n$.** - Use $n = N_c \exp(-0.2/0.0259) = 2.8 \times 10^{25} \times e^{-7.72} \approx 1.16 \times 10^{22}$ m$^{-3}$. 2. **Find the temperature at which $n_i$ doubles for Si.** - Use $n_i(T) \propto T^{3/2} \exp(-E_g / 2k_BT)$; solve iteratively or use the known rule of thumb: $n_i$ doubles every ~11 K near 300 K. 3. **Show that for an intrinsic semiconductor, $n = p = n_i$.** - Charge neutrality: $n = p$. Combined with $np = n_i^2$, this gives $n = p = n_i$. 4. **Calculate $E_F - E_i$ for an n-type Si with donor concentration $N_D = 10^{21}$ m$^{-3}$.** - $n \approx N_D = N_c \exp(-(E_c - E_F)/k_BT)$ → solve for $E_c - E_F$, then $E_i \approx (E_c + E_v)/2$, so $E_F - E_i = (E_c - E_F) - E_g/2$. > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Exam Tip:** Whenever you see a problem involving carrier concentration in a semiconductor, immediately write down three formulas on the answer sheet before starting: (1) the expression for $n$, (2) the expression for $p$, and (3) the mass action law. This anchors your derivation and signals to the examiner that you know the framework.

topics.title: Intrinsic Carrier Concentration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Intrinsic Carrier Concentration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **intrinsic semiconductor** is a perfectly pure, undoped single-crystal semiconductor (e.g., pure Si, pure Ge, pure GaAs) in which the electrical conduction properties are governed entirely by the **thermally generated electron–hole pairs (EHPs)** created when valence-band electrons acquire enough thermal energy to jump across the forbidden energy gap ($E_g$) into the conduction band.

The **Intrinsic Carrier Concentration**, denoted $n_i$, is the number of free electrons per unit volume in the conduction band (or equivalently, free holes per unit volume in the valence band) of an intrinsic semiconductor at a given absolute temperature $T$.

$$\boxed{\;n_i = p_i = \text{concentration of free electrons = concentration of free holes in a pure crystal}\;}$$

Mathematically, the KTU-board expression is:

$$n_i = \sqrt{N_C \, N_V} \,\, \exp\!\left(\!-\frac{E_g}{2kT}\right)$$

where every symbol has its standard meaning (defined in the formula sheet of SECTION 2).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU-Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** A student must be able to *derive* $n_i$, locate the **intrinsic Fermi level** $E_{Fi}$, and explain the **temperature dependence** of $n_i$ — these three outcomes are high-weight topics in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Two-Staircase Library"

Imagine a **two-storey library building** connected by a **single staircase**:

| Element in Analogy | Real Semiconductor Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Upper floor (open reading hall) | **Conduction Band** ($E_C$) |
| Lower floor (stacked books) | **Valence Band** ($E_V$) |
| Vertical distance between floors | **Forbidden Energy Gap** ($E_g$) |
| Readers climbing up | **Electrons** leaving the valence band |
| Empty seats left behind on the lower floor | **Holes** left in the valence band |
| Energy needed to climb | **Thermal energy** $kT$ |
| Density of available seats on each floor | **Effective Density of States** $N_C$ / $N_V$ |

At any temperature, a few readers (electrons) are energetic enough to climb the stairs. The number of climbers **always equals** the number of empty seats left below — this is exactly why $n = p = n_i$ in a pure crystal. The wider the staircase ($E_g$ large), the fewer climbers you get at room temperature.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants Used in this Module

- Boltzmann constant: **$k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K = $8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K**
- Reduced Planck constant: **$\hbar = 1.0546 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s**
- Free electron mass: **$m_0 = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}$ kg**
- Thermal voltage at 300 K: **$V_T = kT/q \approx 0.0259$ V**

> [!NOTE]
> For **Silicon (Si)** at 300 K (the KTU reference material): $E_g \approx \mathbf{1.12\ eV}$, $m_n^* \approx 1.08\,m_0$, $m_p^* \approx 0.56\,m_0$, and $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$.
> For **Germanium (Ge)** at 300 K: $E_g \approx 0.66\ \text{eV}$, $n_i \approx 2.4 \times 10^{13}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$.
> For **GaAs** at 300 K: $E_g \approx 1.42\ \text{eV}$, $n_i \approx 1.8 \times 10^{6}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Building Blocks of Carrier Concentration

The KTU syllabus breaks the derivation of $n_i$ into **three conceptual pillars**. Every step that follows in SECTION 3 is built on these.

**Pillar 1 — Density of Available States (Quantum Mechanical)**  
The number of allowed energy levels per unit volume per unit energy, $g_C(E)$ in the conduction band and $g_V(E)$ in the valence band, depends on the effective mass of the carrier.

- Near the bottom of the conduction band ($E \ge E_C$):
$$g_C(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2 m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{\!\!3/2}\sqrt{E - E_C}$$

- Near the top of the valence band ($E \le E_V$):
$$g_V(E) = \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\left(\frac{2 m_p^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{\!\!3/2}\sqrt{E_V - E}$$

**Pillar 2 — Probability of Occupation (Fermi–Dirac Statistics)**  
The probability that a state at energy $E$ is occupied by an electron is governed by the Fermi–Dirac distribution function. The KTU board allows the **Boltzmann approximation** for non-degenerate semiconductors ($E - E_F \gg kT$):
$$f(E) \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{kT}\right) \quad ; \quad 1 - f(E) \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_F - E}{kT}\right)$$

**Pillar 3 — Carrier Density as an Integral of Product**  
The actual carrier density = (density of states) × (probability of occupation), integrated over the band:
$$n = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} g_C(E) \, f(E) \, dE \quad ; \quad p = \int_{-\infty}^{E_V} g_V(E) \, [1 - f(E)] \, dE$$

### 2.2 The KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

| # | Quantity | Symbolic Expression | Definition / Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Conduction-band state density | $N_C = 2\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{\!3/2}$ | Effective number of states per unit volume at $E_C$ (cm$^{-3}$ or m$^{-3}$); $T^{3/2}$ dependence. |
| 2 | Valence-band state density | $N_V = 2\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_p^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{\!3/2}$ | Effective number of states per unit volume at $E_V$; $T^{3/2}$ dependence. |
| 3 | Free electron density | $n = N_C \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_C - E_F}{kT}\right)$ | Boltzmann-approximated electron concentration. |
| 4 | Free hole density | $p = N_V \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_F - E_V}{kT}\right)$ | Boltzmann-approximated hole concentration. |
| 5 | Mass-action law (general) | $n p = n_i^{\,2} = N_C N_V \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{kT}\right)$ | Product $np$ is **doping-independent** at a fixed $T$. |
| 6 | Intrinsic carrier density | $n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V}\,\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_g}{2kT}\right)$ | The defining KTU result for $n_i$. |
| 7 | Intrinsic Fermi level | $E_{Fi} = \dfrac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \dfrac{3kT}{4}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{m_p^*}{m_n^*}\right)$ | Lies close to the **mid-gap**; shifted slightly by mass asymmetry. |
| 8 | Intrinsic conductivity | $\sigma_i = n_i q (\mu_n + \mu_p)$ | $q$ is the electronic charge; $\mu$ is mobility (m$^2$/V·s). |
| 9 | Temperature scaling of $N_C N_V$ | $N_C N_V \propto T^{3}$ | Comes from the $T^{3/2}$ dependence in $N_C$ and $N_V$. |
| 10 | Mid-gap energy (reference) | $E_{\text{mid}} = \dfrac{E_C + E_V}{2}$ | Geometric centre of the bandgap; equal to $E_V + E_g/2$. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** In the mass-action law, $n_i^2$ is **not** the square of "one $n_i$" in a particular doped sample — it is the intrinsic carrier concentration **of the host material at the same temperature**. The product $np$ for *any* (non-degenerate) semiconductor equals this constant.

### 2.3 Why This Matters in Engineering

- **Device design:** Every PN-junction diode, BJT, MOSFET, and solar cell starts from $n_i$. Reverse-saturation current $I_S \propto n_i^2$, so leakage in Ge devices is huge at room $T$ while Si stays usable.
- **Temperature sensors & thermistors:** The strong $T$-dependence of $n_i$ is exploited to build semiconductor temperature sensors.
- **High-temperature electronics:** Above the "intrinsic temperature," a doped Si device reverts to intrinsic behaviour — designers must operate below this.
- **Photovoltaics:** Optical generation rates are compared to $n_i$ to determine whether photo-effects are observable.

The '**why**' behind every formula on the cheat sheet can be summarized as: **the available states set a ceiling, the Fermi function sets the occupancy, and the integral of their product gives the carrier population.** The next section shows this rigorously.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Expression for $n_i$ (The Headline Result)

**Step 1 — Set up the integral for electron density in the conduction band:**

$$n = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} g_C(E)\, f(E)\, dE$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the parabolic density of states and the Boltzmann approximation of the Fermi–Dirac function.** Justification: in a non-degenerate semiconductor, the Fermi level lies several $kT$ away from $E_C$, so $E - E_F \gg kT$ for all energies near $E_C$ and the exponential tail is valid:

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \int_{E_C}^{\infty} \left[\frac{1}{2\pi^2}\!\left(\frac{2 m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{\!\!3/2}\sqrt{E - E_C}\right] \exp\!\left(-\frac{E - E_F}{kT}\right) dE
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Pull the constant terms out and define a substitution.** Let $u = (E - E_C)/kT$, so that $E - E_F = (E - E_C) + (E_C - E_F) = kT\,u + (E_C - E_F)$, and $dE = kT\,du$. Also factor out the exponential containing $E_C - E_F$:

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\!\left(\frac{2 m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{\!\!3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{kT}\right) \int_{0}^{\infty} \sqrt{E - E_C}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E - E_C}{kT}\right)dE
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the standard integral.** The identity $\int_0^{\infty} \sqrt{x}\,e^{-x/kT} dx = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}(kT)^{3/2}$ is a tabulated Gamma-function result. Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{1}{2\pi^2}\!\left(\frac{2 m_n^*}{\hbar^2}\right)^{\!\!3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{kT}\right) \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}(kT)^{3/2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by collecting constants and inserting $\hbar = h/2\pi$.** Using $h^2 = (2\pi\hbar)^2$, the constants combine into the canonical $N_C$ expression. The result is the KTU-board formula:

$$\boxed{\;n = N_C \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_F}{kT}\right)\;}$$

where

$$N_C = 2\left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{\!3/2}$$

**Step 6 — Repeat the same procedure symmetrically for holes in the valence band.** The derivation mirrors the electron case but with $E_V$ as the upper limit, $m_p^*$ as the effective mass, and $1 - f(E)$ replaced by its Boltzmann form. The result is:

$$\boxed{\;p = N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_F - E_V}{kT}\right)\;}$$

with $N_V = 2\left(\dfrac{2\pi m_p^* kT}{h^2}\right)^{3/2}$.

**Step 7 — Apply the intrinsic condition $n = p = n_i$.** Multiplying the two boxed results:

$$\begin{aligned}
n \cdot p &= N_C N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{(E_C - E_F) + (E_F - E_V)}{kT}\right) \\
          &= N_C N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_V}{kT}\right) \\
          &= N_C N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{kT}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 8 — Equate to $n_i^{\,2}$ (since $n = p = n_i$):**

$$\boxed{\;n_i^{\,2} = N_C N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{kT}\right)\;}$$

**Step 9 — Take the square root to obtain the final intrinsic concentration:**

$$\boxed{\;n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V} \,\,\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2kT}\right)\;}$$

> [!TIP]
> The factor **$1/2$** in the exponent comes from the **square root** operation in step 9 — *not* from the bandgap being halved at any physical stage. This is a classic KTU examination confusion point.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Position of the Intrinsic Fermi Level $E_{Fi}$

**Step 1 — Start from the equality $n = p$ for an intrinsic crystal, using the expressions derived above:**

$$N_C \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_C - E_{Fi}}{kT}\right) = N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_{Fi} - E_V}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\ln N_C - \frac{E_C - E_{Fi}}{kT} = \ln N_V - \frac{E_{Fi} - E_V}{kT}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $E_{Fi}$.** Bring the exponential terms to one side and the logarithmic terms to the other:

$$\frac{E_{Fi} - E_V}{kT} - \frac{E_C - E_{Fi}}{kT} = \ln N_V - \ln N_C$$

$$\frac{2E_{Fi} - E_V - E_C}{kT} = \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $E_{Fi}$:**

$$E_{Fi} = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{kT}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the explicit $T^{3/2}$ forms of $N_C$ and $N_V$.** All $T^{3/2}$ and $2$-factors cancel, leaving only the mass ratio to the power $3/2$:

$$\frac{N_V}{N_C} = \left(\frac{m_p^*}{m_n^*}\right)^{\!3/2}$$

**Step 6 — Use the identity $(3/2)\ln x = (3/4)\cdot 2\ln x$ to obtain the final KTU formula:**

$$\boxed{\;E_{Fi} = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{3kT}{4}\ln\!\left(\frac{m_p^*}{m_n^*}\right)\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> - If $m_p^* = m_n^*$, then $E_{Fi}$ lies **exactly at mid-gap** ($E_{Fi} = E_{\text{mid}}$).
> - If $m_p^* > m_n^*$, then $E_{Fi}$ is **shifted upward** toward $E_C$ (the band with the smaller effective mass has a *tighter* density of states, so the Fermi level must rise to equalize carrier populations).
> - The shift is small at 300 K — at most a few $kT$ — but the *direction* matters for understanding junction built-in potentials.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Temperature Dependence of $n_i$

**Step 1 — Start from the $n_i$ expression and make the $T$-dependence explicit:**

$$n_i^{\,2} = N_C N_V \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{kT}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the explicit $T^{3/2}$ forms of $N_C$ and $N_V$.** Since each is $\propto T^{3/2}$, their product is $\propto T^{3}$:

$$n_i^{\,2} = \underbrace{\left[2\!\left(\frac{2\pi m_n^* k}{h^2}\right)^{\!3/2}\right]\!\left[2\!\left(\frac{2\pi m_p^* k}{h^2}\right)^{\!3/2}\right]}_{\propto T^3\,\text{via the (kT) terms}} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{kT}\right)$$

A more explicit form keeps the constants together:

$$n_i^{\,2} = A\, T^{3}\, \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{kT}\right)$$

where $A = 4\left(\dfrac{2\pi k}{h^2}\right)^{\!3}(m_n^* m_p^*)^{3/2}$ is a material constant.

**Step 3 — Take the natural log of both sides to expose a linear-in-$1/T$ behaviour:**

$$\ln n_i^{\,2} = \ln A + 3\ln T - \frac{E_g}{kT}$$

$$\ln n_i = \frac{1}{2}\ln A + \frac{3}{2}\ln T - \frac{E_g}{2kT}$$

**Step 4 — Graphical interpretation.** A plot of $\ln n_i$ vs. $1/T$ is approximately a straight line with slope $-E_g/2k$ (the $3\ln T$ term is a slow correction). This is the basis of the **experimental method** used to determine $E_g$ from electrical measurements.

**Step 5 — Physical meaning of the $T^{3/2}$ pre-factor vs. the exponential:**

- The $T^{3/2}$ term comes from the **increasing density of available states** as the band broadens with thermal agitation.
- The exponential term dominates: as $T$ rises, the Boltzmann tail across $E_g$ grows explosively.
- At high $T$, $n_i$ approaches the density of atoms (~$10^{22}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$); the crystal ceases to behave as a semiconductor and becomes "intrinsic-degenerate."

> [!WARNING]
> **Do not write** "$n_i$ doubles for every 10 °C rise." That rule-of-thumb belongs to **reverse saturation current** $I_S$ (which scales as $n_i^2$ and therefore has $2\times$ per ~10 K behaviour *for specific materials in a narrow range*). The $n_i$ itself rises **exponentially** with $T$ — there is no universal "doubling per 10 K" rule.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Energy Band Architecture of an Intrinsic Semiconductor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CB["CONDUCTION BAND (Ec)"]
        ECB["Bottom edge of CB"]
        LCB["Empty allowed states (parabolic density)"]
    end
    subgraph BG["FORBIDDEN ENERGY GAP (Eg)"]
        EFi["INTRINSIC FERMI LEVEL (Efi) — sits at mid-gap, slightly shifted by mass asymmetry"]
    end
    subgraph VB["VALENCE BAND (Ev)"]
        HVB["Filled states with vacancies (holes) at the top"]
        EVB["Top edge of VB"]
    end
    ECB --- EFi
    EFi --- EVB
    THERMAL["Thermal Energy kT"] -.-> ECB
    THERMAL -.-> EVB
    GEN["Electron-Hole Pair Generation"] --> ECB
    GEN --> HVB
    REC["Recombination (steady-state balance)"] --> GEN
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the diagram, `EFi` is the *intrinsic Fermi level*. The arrows represent the **dynamic equilibrium** between thermal generation and recombination, which is what enforces the equality $n = p$ in a pure crystal.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Derivation Flow of $n_i$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Effective Masses (mn*, mp*)"] --> B["Density of States (gc, gv)"]
    C["Temperature T and Energy Levels (Ec, Ev, Ef)"] --> D["Fermi-Dirac Statistics"]
    B --> E["Carrier Density Integral"]
    D --> E
    E --> F["nc = Nc exp(-(Ec-Ef)/kT)"]
    E --> G["nv = Nv exp(-(Ef-Ev)/kT)"]
    F --> H["Intrinsic Condition: n = p = ni"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["ni^2 = Nc Nv exp(-Eg/kT)"]
    I --> J["ni = sqrt(Nc Nv) exp(-Eg/2kT)"]
    K["T Dependence of Nc, Nv ~ T^3/2"] --> I
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Temperature Regimes of a Semiconductor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LOW["Low T: Frozen-out regime - very few carriers, ni ~ exp(-Eg/2kT)"]
    MID["Room T: Extrinsic regime - doping controls np, ni fixed by host"]
    HIGH["High T: Intrinsic regime - thermally generated EHPs dominate, ni approaches 10^22 /m^3"]
    LOW -->|T rises| MID
    MID -->|T rises further| HIGH
```

> [!TIP]
> The **transition temperature** between extrinsic and intrinsic regimes is what limits the maximum operating temperature of devices. For Si with $10^{15}\ \text{cm}^{-3}$ doping, this is roughly 200 °C — a key KTU design number.

### 4.4 Schematic Description — Why $E_{Fi}$ Sits Near Mid-Gap

If the conduction band has a *larger* effective density of states (heavier $m_n^*$ or wider band), then even a small electron population creates a large effective "push" downward. To keep $n = p$, the Fermi level must adjust until both bands contribute equally. Mathematically this is encoded in the $\ln(N_V/N_C)$ term of the $E_{Fi}$ formula (SECTION 3.2). The shift is **toward the band with the smaller effective mass.**
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, June 2024]  
**Define an intrinsic semiconductor. Why does the intrinsic carrier concentration depend on temperature?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- *(1 mark)* An **intrinsic semiconductor** is a chemically pure, single-crystal semiconductor in which the electrical properties are determined solely by the thermal generation of electron–hole pairs across the forbidden gap. Examples: pure Si, pure Ge, pure GaAs.
- *(1 mark)* The thermal energy $kT$ excites electrons from $E_V$ to $E_C$. The number of such electrons per unit volume is given by $n_i = \sqrt{N_C N_V}\exp(-E_g/2kT)$. As $T$ rises, the Boltzmann tail across $E_g$ grows exponentially, so $n_i$ increases sharply.
- *(1 mark)* Mathematically, $n_i$ is exponentially sensitive to $1/T$ through the $\exp(-E_g/2kT)$ factor; this is the origin of all temperature effects in semiconductor devices.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Dec 2023]  
**State the law of mass action and write its mathematical form for an intrinsic semiconductor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
