The number of electrons per unit volume in the conduction band is obtained by integrating the product of density of states $g(E)$ and the Fermi-Dirac distribution $f(E)$ from $E_C$ to $\infty$:

$$n = \int_{E_C}^{\infty} g(E) \, f(E) \, dE$$

Applying the **Boltzmann approximation** ($E - E_F \gg k_BT$) and assuming parabolic density of states near band edges, this integral evaluates to:

$$n = N_C \, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_BT}$$

Similarly, the hole concentration is:

$$p = N_V \, e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_BT}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the intrinsic condition $n = p$.**

For an intrinsic semiconductor, $n = p = n_i$, so:

$$N_C \, e^{-(E_C - E_F^{(i)})/k_BT} = N_V \, e^{-(E_F^{(i)} - E_V)/k_BT}$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

\begin{aligned}
\ln(N_C) - \frac{E_C - E_F^{(i)}}{k_BT} &= \ln(N_V) - \frac{E_F^{(i)} - E_V}{k_BT}
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate $E_F^{(i)}$.**

\begin{aligned}
-\frac{E_C - E_F^{(i)}}{k_BT} + \frac{E_F^{(i)} - E_V}{k_BT} &= \ln(N_V) - \ln(N_C)
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\frac{-E_C + E_F^{(i)} + E_F^{(i)} - E_V}{k_BT} &= \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\frac{2E_F^{(i)} - (E_C + E_V)}{k_BT} &= \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)
\end{aligned}

**Step 5 — Solve explicitly for $E_F^{(i)}$.**

\begin{aligned}
2E_F^{(i)} - (E_C + E_V) &= k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(i)} &= \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{k_BT}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)
\end{aligned}

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation.**

If $N_C = N_V$ (which would require equal effective masses for electrons and holes), the second term vanishes and $E_F^{(i)} = (E_C + E_V)/2$, exactly at mid-gap. In real materials like Si, $m_h^* > m_e^*$, so $N_V > N_C$, which makes $\ln(N_V/N_C) > 0$ and pushes $E_F^{(i)}$ slightly **above** the mid-gap.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Fermi Level in N-Type Semiconductor

**Given:** Donor concentration $N_D$ is added; at room temperature, full ionization occurs so $n \approx N_D$.

**Step 1 — Use the electron concentration formula with $n = N_D$.**

$$N_D = N_C \, e^{-(E_C - E_F^{(n)})/k_BT}$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm.**

\begin{aligned}
\ln(N_D) &= \ln(N_C) - \frac{E_C - E_F^{(n)}}{k_BT}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Rearrange.**

\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_C - E_F^{(n)}}{k_BT} &= \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(n)} = E_C - k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right)
\end{aligned}

**Step 4 — Alternative form using $n_i$ via mass-action law.**

Since $N_C = n_i \, e^{(E_C - E_F^{(i)})/k_BT}$ from the intrinsic relation, substitute:

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(n)} &= E_C - k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{n_i \, e^{(E_C - E_F^{(i)})/k_BT}}{N_D}\right)
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(n)} &= E_C - k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{n_i}{N_D}\right) - (E_C - E_F^{(i)})
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(n)} &= E_F^{(i)} + k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right)
\end{aligned}

This shows that $E_F^{(n)}$ lies **above** the intrinsic Fermi level by an amount $k_BT \ln(N_D/n_i)$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Fermi Level in P-Type Semiconductor

**Given:** Acceptor concentration $N_A$ is added; at room temperature, $p \approx N_A$.

**Step 1 — Use the hole concentration formula with $p = N_A$.**

$$N_A = N_V \, e^{-(E_F^{(p)} - E_V)/k_BT}$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm and solve.**

\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_F^{(p)} - E_V}{k_BT} &= \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right)
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(p)} = E_V + k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_A}\right)
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Express in terms of intrinsic Fermi level.**

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(p)} &= E_F^{(i)} - k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)
\end{aligned}

This shows that $E_F^{(p)}$ lies **below** the intrinsic Fermi level by $k_BT \ln(N_A/n_i)$.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: The Law of Mass Action

**Step 1 — Multiply the expressions for $n$ and $p$.**

\begin{aligned}
n \cdot p &= \left[N_C \, e^{-(E_C - E_F)/k_BT}\right] \cdot \left[N_V \, e^{-(E_F - E_V)/k_BT}\right]
\end{aligned}

**Step 2 — Simplify the exponent.**

\begin{aligned}
n \cdot p &= N_C N_V \, e^{-[(E_C - E_F) + (E_F - E_V)]/k_BT}
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
n \cdot p &= N_C N_V \, e^{-(E_C - E_V)/k_BT}
\end{aligned}

**Step 3 — Recognize $E_C - E_V = E_g$ (band gap energy).**

\begin{aligned}
n \cdot p = N_C N_V \, e^{-E_g/k_BT} = n_i^2
\end{aligned}

This is the **Law of Mass Action**, a cornerstone relation stating that the product $np$ is **constant at a given temperature**, independent of doping.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — KTU Problem-Solving Strategy

| Given in Question | Required to Find | Key Equations to Use |
|-------------------|------------------|----------------------|
| $N_D$, temperature | $E_F^{(n)}$ position | $E_F^{(n)} = E_C - k_BT \ln(N_C/N_D)$ |
| $N_A$, temperature | $E_F^{(p)}$ position | $E_F^{(p)} = E_V + k_BT \ln(N_V/N_A)$ |
| $E_F$ shift from mid-gap | Verify doping type | Sign of shift → n-type or p-type |
| $E_g$, temperature | Intrinsic $n_i$ | $n_i^2 = N_C N_V e^{-E_g/k_BT}$ |
| $n$ and $n_i$ | Minority hole $p$ | $p = n_i^2/n$ |
| Heavy doping (degenerate) | Validity check | Check $E_C - E_F > 3k_BT$ |

### 3.6 Numerical Illustration (KTU Style)

**Problem:** For Silicon at 300 K, $N_C = 2.8 \times 10^{19}$ /cm³, $N_V = 1.04 \times 10^{19}$ /cm³, $E_g = 1.12$ eV. Calculate the intrinsic Fermi level position relative to mid-gap.

**Solution:**

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(i)} - E_{mid} &= \frac{k_BT}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right) = \frac{0.0259}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{1.04 \times 10^{19}}{2.8 \times 10^{19}}\right)
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
&= 0.01295 \times \ln(0.3714) = 0.01295 \times (-0.9905)
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
&= -0.01283 \text{ eV}
\end{aligned}

The negative sign indicates $E_F^{(i)}$ lies slightly **below** mid-gap in Si. (The textbook convention may reverse signs depending on the $N_C, N_V$ values; the physical principle remains: $E_F$ shifts toward the band with the larger effective density of states.)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Band Diagram — Fermi Level Positions

The following Mermaid block represents a block-level functional flow mapping the relative positions of the Fermi level in different semiconductor configurations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Band Diagram of Semiconductor] --> B[Conduction Band Edge EC]
    A --> C[Valence Band Edge EV]
    A --> D[Band Gap Energy Eg = EC - EV]
    B --> E[Intrinsic Semiconductor]
    B --> F[N-Type Semiconductor]
    C --> G[P-Type Semiconductor]
    E --> E1[Fermi Level EFI at mid gap]
    F --> F1[Fermi Level EFN shifted upward toward EC]
    G --> G1[Fermi Level EFP shifted downward toward EV]
    F1 --> F2[Donor Level ED just below EC]
    G1 --> G2[Acceptor Level EA just above EV]
    F2 --> F3[Majority carriers Electrons]
    F2 --> F4[Minority carriers Holes]
    G2 --> G3[Majority carriers Holes]
    G2 --> G4[Minority carriers Electrons]
    F3 --> F5[Doping with Pentavalent atoms P As Sb]
    G3 --> G5[Doping with Trivalent atoms B Ga In]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Factors Affecting Fermi Level Position

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Doping Type] --> Q1[Intrinsic]
    P1 --> Q2[Donor Doping ND]
    P1 --> Q3[Acceptor Doping NA]
    P2[Temperature T] --> R1[Boltzmann Approximation validity]
    P3[Effective Masses me mh] --> S1[Effective Density of States NC NV]
    Q1 --> T1[EFI at Mid Gap]
    Q2 --> T2[EFN moves up toward EC]
    Q3 --> T3[EFP moves down toward EV]
    S1 --> T1
    S1 --> T2
    S1 --> T3
    R1 --> U1[If E_C minus E_F much greater than kT then Boltzmann valid]
    T2 --> V1[Majority Electron Density n approx ND]
    T3 --> V2[Majority Hole Density p approx NA]
    V1 --> W1[Mass Action np equals ni squared]
    V2 --> W1
```

### 4.3 Comparison Flow — Intrinsic vs Extrinsic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Semiconductor Classification] --> X1[Pure Material]
    X --> X2[Doped Material]
    X1 --> X1a[Intrinsic Type i]
    X1a --> X1b[Equal electrons and holes n equals p equals ni]
    X1a --> X1c[Fermi level at mid gap]
    X2 --> X2a[N Type with Donor impurities]
    X2 --> X2b[P Type with Acceptor impurities]
    X2a --> X2c[Majority electrons n much greater than p]
    X2a --> X2d[Fermi level shifted toward EC]
    X2b --> X2e[Majority holes p much greater than n]
    X2b --> X2f[Fermi level shifted toward EV]
    X1c --> Y1[Energy band gap Eg is fixed by material]
    X2d --> Y1
    X2f --> Y1
    Y1 --> Z1[Device behavior governed by EF position]
```

### 4.4 Temperature Dependence of Fermi Level

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[Very Low Temperature T near 0 K] --> T0a[Fermi level near donor level for n type]
    T1[Room Temperature 300 K] --> T1a[Full ionization EF near mid gap shifted by doping]
    T2[High Temperature T greater than 600 K] --> T2a[Intrinsic regime EF returns to mid gap]
    T0a --> T3[Fermi Level Migration]
    T1a --> T3
    T2a --> T3
    T3 --> T4[Carrier type can change with temperature]
```

### 4.5 Schematic Block — Carrier Concentration vs Fermi Level

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Conduction Band EC] --> A2[Empty States]
    A2 --> A3[Filled by electrons as EF moves up]
    B1[Valence Band EV] --> B2[Filled States]
    B2 --> B3[States vacated by holes as EF moves down]
    A3 --> C1[n increases exponentially with EF moving up]
    B3 --> C2[p increases exponentially with EF moving down]
    C1 --> D1[Result EF position is a carrier density indicator]
    C2 --> D1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Define Fermi level in a semiconductor. Why does it lie very close to the middle of the forbidden energy gap in an intrinsic semiconductor?**

**Model Answer:**
The Fermi level $E_F$ is the energy level at which the probability of electron occupation is 50% according to the Fermi-Dirac distribution. In an intrinsic semiconductor, the number of electrons in the conduction band ($n$) equals the number of holes in the valence band ($p = n_i$). Setting $n = p$ and solving the Boltzmann-approximated expressions yields:

$$E_F^{(i)} = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{k_BT}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)$$

Since $N_C$ and $N_V$ are nearly equal (effective masses are of the same order), the logarithmic term is small, so $E_F^{(i)}$ lies very close to the mid-gap $E_{mid} = (E_C + E_V)/2$. (3 marks)

---

> **Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Distinguish between intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors with respect to Fermi level position.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Intrinsic | Extrinsic (N-type) | Extrinsic (P-type) |
|---------|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Doping | None | Donor (e.g., P in Si) | Acceptor (e.g., B in Si) |
| Carrier equality | $n = p = n_i$ | $n \gg p$ | $p \gg n$ |
| $E_F$ position | Near mid-gap | Above mid-gap, near $E_C$ | Below mid-gap, near $E_V$ |
| Fermi shift | $\Delta E_F = 0$ | $\Delta E_F = +k_BT \ln(N_D/n_i)$ | $\Delta E_F = -k_BT \ln(N_A/n_i)$ |

The Fermi level position acts as a **p-type indicator** when closer to $E_V$ and **n-type indicator** when closer to $E_C$. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the position of the Fermi level in an intrinsic semiconductor. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** For an n-type silicon sample at 300 K, the donor concentration is $N_D = 10^{16}$ /cm³. Given $N_C = 2.8 \times 10^{19}$ /cm³ and $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ /cm³, calculate: (i) the position of the Fermi level relative to the conduction band edge $E_C$, and (ii) the position of the Fermi level relative to the intrinsic Fermi level $E_F^{(i)}$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

Starting from charge neutrality $n = p = n_i$ in an intrinsic semiconductor, write:

$$n_i = N_C \, e^{-(E_C - E_F^{(i)})/k_BT} = N_V \, e^{-(E_F^{(i)} - E_V)/k_BT}$$

Equating and taking the natural logarithm:

\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_V}\right) &= \frac{E_C - E_F^{(i)}}{k_BT} - \frac{E_F^{(i)} - E_V}{k_BT}
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
\ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_V}\right) &= \frac{E_C + E_V - 2E_F^{(i)}}{k_BT}
\end{aligned}

Solving:

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(i)} = \frac{E_C + E_V}{2} + \frac{k_BT}{2}\ln\!\left(\frac{N_V}{N_C}\right)
\end{aligned}

When $N_C \approx N_V$, the second term $\approx 0$ and $E_F^{(i)} \approx (E_C + E_V)/2 = E_{mid}$, confirming that the Fermi level lies at the middle of the band gap. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Position of $E_F$ relative to $E_C$:**

Using $E_F^{(n)} = E_C - k_BT \ln(N_C/N_D)$:

\begin{aligned}
E_C - E_F^{(n)} &= k_BT \ln\!\left(\frac{N_C}{N_D}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{2.8 \times 10^{19}}{10^{16}}\right)
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
&= 0.0259 \times \ln(2800) = 0.0259 \times 7.937
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
E_C - E_F^{(n)} &\approx 0.2056 \text{ eV}
\end{aligned}

**(ii) Position of $E_F$ relative to $E_F^{(i)}$:**

Using $\Delta E_F = k_BT \ln(N_D/n_i)$:

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(n)} - E_F^{(i)} &= 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{16}}{1.5 \times 10^{10}}\right)
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
&= 0.0259 \times \ln(6.667 \times 10^5) = 0.0259 \times 13.41
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(n)} - E_F^{(i)} &\approx 0.3473 \text{ eV}
\end{aligned}

So the Fermi level in this n-type sample lies **0.2056 eV below $E_C$** and **0.3473 eV above the intrinsic Fermi level**. (7 marks)

---

> **Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the position of the Fermi level in n-type and p-type semiconductors. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** For a p-type germanium sample at 300 K, the acceptor concentration is $N_A = 5 \times 10^{15}$ /cm³. Given $N_V = 6.0 \times 10^{18}$ /cm³, $n_i = 2.4 \times 10^{13}$ /cm³, and $E_g = 0.67$ eV, calculate: (i) the position of the Fermi level relative to $E_V$, and (ii) the minority carrier (electron) concentration. (7 marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

In an **n-type semiconductor**, donor atoms introduce energy levels $E_D$ located a few meV below the conduction band edge $E_C$. At room temperature, electrons from donor levels are thermally excited into the conduction band, leaving behind positively charged donor ions. The Fermi level $E_F^{(n)}$ is located **above the intrinsic Fermi level** $E_F^{(i)}$ and lies between $E_D$ and $E_C$, shifting closer to $E_C$ as $N_D$ increases.

In a **p-type semiconductor**, acceptor atoms introduce energy levels $E_A$ located a few meV above the valence band edge $E_V$. At room temperature, electrons from the valence band jump to acceptor levels, creating holes in the valence band. The Fermi level $E_F^{(p)}$ is located **below the intrinsic Fermi level** $E_F^{(i)}$ and lies between $E_A$ and $E_V$, shifting closer to $E_V$ as $N_A$ increases.

The position is given mathematically by:

- $E_F^{(n)} = E_F^{(i)} + k_BT \ln(N_D/n_i)$
- $E_F^{(p)} = E_F^{(i)} - k_BT \ln(N_A/n_i)$

(7 marks for the diagram, explanation, and equations)

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Position of $E_F$ relative to $E_V$:**

Using $E_F^{(p)} = E_V + k_BT \ln(N_V/N_A)$:

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(p)} - E_V &= 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{6.0 \times 10^{18}}{5 \times 10^{15}}\right)
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
&= 0.0259 \times \ln(1200) = 0.0259 \times 7.090
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
E_F^{(p)} - E_V &\approx 0.1836 \text{ eV}
\end{aligned}

So the Fermi level lies **0.1836 eV above $E_V$**.

**(ii) Minority carrier (electron) concentration:**

Using the mass-action law $n \cdot p = n_i^2$ and $p \approx N_A$:

\begin{aligned}
n_0 = \frac{n_i^2}{p_0} = \frac{(2.4 \times 10^{13})^2}{5 \times 10^{15}} = \frac{5.76 \times 10^{26}}{5 \times 10^{15}}
\end{aligned}

\begin{aligned}
n_0 = 1.152 \times 10^{11} \text{ /cm}^3
\end{aligned}

(7 marks)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Sign Confusion in Fermi Shift:** Many students write $E_F^{(n)} = E_C + k_BT \ln(N_C/N_D)$ instead of the **correct minus sign**. The formula is $E_F^{(n)} = E_C - k_BT \ln(N_C/N_D)$. Always check that as $N_D$ increases, $E_F$ moves **closer to $E_C$** (i.e., $E_C - E_F$ decreases).
>
> 2. **Forgetting $k_BT$ in eV:** In numerical problems, you must use $k_BT = 0.0259$ eV at 300 K, **not** $1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. Mixing units is the #1 reason students get zero marks despite writing the right formula.
>
> 3. **Confusing $E_F^{(i)}$ and $E_{mid}$:** The intrinsic Fermi level is **not exactly** at mid-gap; it is offset by $(k_BT/2)\ln(N_V/N_C)$. Examiners award full marks only when this offset term is included.
>
> 4. **Using Wrong Carrier Majority:** In n-type, the **majority** carrier is the electron ($n = N_D$); in p-type, the **majority** carrier is the hole ($p = N_A$). Reversing these leads to incorrect Fermi level positions.
>
> 5. **Missing Units in Final Answer:** Always report $E_F - E_C$ or $E_F - E_V$ in **eV**, not J/cm³. KTU strict evaluation requires proper SI/eV units.
>
> 6. **Boltzmann Approximation Abuse:** For degenerate (heavily doped) semiconductors where $E_C - E_F < 3k_BT$, the exponential form fails. Examiners may deduct marks if you blindly apply the formula without checking the validity condition.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- **Fermi level $E_F$** is a *statistical* energy reference, not a discrete atomic level; the probability of occupation at $E = E_F$ is exactly 0.5 only at 0 K.
- **Fermi-Dirac function:** $f(E) = 1/[1 + e^{(E - E_F)/k_BT}]$ — the bedrock of all carrier statistics.
- **Intrinsic Fermi level** lies near mid-gap; exact position: $E_F^{(i)} = (E_C + E_V)/2 + (k_BT/2)\ln(N_V/N_C)$.
- **N-type:** $E_F$ shifts **upward** toward $E_C$ by $k_BT \ln(N_D/n_i)$ — majority carriers are electrons.
- **P-type:** $E_F$ shifts **downward** toward $E_V$ by $k_BT \ln(N_A/n_i)$ — majority carriers are holes.
- **Mass-action law:** $n \cdot p = n_i^2$ — holds in thermal equilibrium regardless of doping.
- **Boltzmann approximation** is valid only when $|E_C - E_F| \gg k_BT$ and $|E_F - E_V| \gg k_BT$.
- **Effective density of states** $N_C$ and $N_V$ depend on effective masses and temperature; $N_C = 2(2\pi m_e^* k_BT/h^2)^{3/2}$.
- **Temperature effect:** At very high T, all doped semiconductors revert to intrinsic behavior and $E_F \to E_{mid}$.
- **Degenerate semiconductors:** When doping is so high that $E_F$ enters the band, Boltzmann statistics fail and Fermi-Dirac must be used.
- **Engineering importance:** Fermi level control is the basis for p-n junctions, MOSFETs, solar cells, LEDs, and essentially every semiconductor device in modern electronics.
- **Units to remember at 300 K:** $k_BT = 0.0259$ eV, $V_T = 25.9$ mV.
- **For Silicon (300 K):** $E_g = 1.12$ eV, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ /cm³, $N_C = 2.8 \times 10^{19}$ /cm³, $N_V = 1.04 \times 10^{19}$ /cm³.
- **For Germanium (300 K):** $E_g = 0.67$ eV, $n_i = 2.4 \times 10^{13}$ /cm³, $N_C = 1.04 \times 10^{19}$ /cm³, $N_V = 6.0 \times 10^{18}$ /cm³.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Band Diagram of P-N Junction, Qualitative Description of Charge Flow Across a P-N Junction, Forward and Reverse Biased P-N Junctions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Band Diagram of P-N Junction, Qualitative Description of Charge Flow Across a P-N Junction, Forward and Reverse Biased P-N Junctions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **P-N Junction**: A P-N junction is the fundamental building block of semiconductor devices, formed when a **P-type semiconductor** (excess holes, acceptor-doped) and an **N-type semiconductor** (excess electrons, donor-doped) are brought into intimate atomic contact within a single crystal lattice. At the metallurgical interface, a **depletion region** (also called the *space-charge region* or *transition region*) is spontaneously established due to carrier diffusion and subsequent recombination, giving rise to a **built-in electric field** $E_{bi}$ and a **contact potential** $V_{bi}$.

The **Energy Band Diagram** of a P-N junction is a graphical representation of electron energy (in eV) versus spatial position $x$ across the device. It illustrates:
- The **Conduction Band edge** ($E_C$)
- The **Valence Band edge** ($E_V$)
- The **Fermi Level** ($E_F$)
- The **vacuum level** ($E_0$)
- The **band bending** in the depletion region
- The **built-in potential barrier** $qV_{bi}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exam**: The energy band diagram of an *isolated* P-type and isolated N-type material shows **flat bands** with Fermi levels at different positions ($E_{Fp}$ near $E_V$ and $E_{Fn}$ near $E_C$). The energy band diagram of a **junction** shows **bent bands** with a **single, constant Fermi level** under equilibrium.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Water Dam" Analogy**: Imagine a deep reservoir (the N-side with electrons at high energy) connected to a lower lake (the P-side with holes). When you remove the separating wall, water naturally flows from high to low level. The P-N junction behaves similarly: when brought together, electrons from the N-side "roll downhill" across the junction into the P-side (diffusion), creating a "dam" of ionized donor atoms (+) on the N-side and ionized acceptor atoms (−) on the P-side. This dam (the depletion region) creates an opposing electric field that eventually **stops** further net flow — this is **equilibrium**.

**Simple Plain-English Explanation**:
1. P-type material has many **holes** (missing electrons) — like a parking lot with many empty spots.
2. N-type material has many **free electrons** — like cars cruising around.
3. When joined, electrons from N-side **diffuse** into P-side and **fill holes** near the junction.
4. This leaves behind **positive donor ions** on the N-side (cars left without their drivers) and creates **negative acceptor ions** on the P-side.
5. These fixed charges set up a **built-in electric field** pointing from N → P, which opposes further diffusion.
6. The result: a **depleted zone** (no mobile carriers) with a **potential barrier** that must be overcome for current to flow.

---

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants Used in P-N Junction Analysis** (memorize for KTU exams):
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K $= 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K
> - Electronic charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
> - Thermal voltage at 300 K: $V_T = k_B T / q \approx 0.0259$ V $\approx$ **25.9 mV** (often taken as **26 mV** for KTU problems)
> - Intrinsic carrier concentration of Si at 300 K: $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ /cm³
> - Band gap of Si: $E_g = 1.12$ eV
> - Relative permittivity of Si: $\varepsilon_r = 11.7$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Formation of a P-N Junction

When a P-type and N-type semiconductor are brought into contact:

**Step 1 — Initial State (Before Junction)**:
- P-side: Fermi level $E_{Fp}$ is located **just above the valence band edge** $E_V$ (since $E_F - E_V = k_B T \ln(N_v / N_A)$, and $N_A$ is large).
- N-side: Fermi level $E_{Fn}$ is located **just below the conduction band edge** $E_C$ (since $E_C - E_F = k_B T \ln(N_c / N_D)$, and $N_D$ is large).
- The bands are **flat** on both sides.

**Step 2 — Carrier Diffusion at the Interface**:
- Holes from P-side diffuse into N-side (driven by concentration gradient).
- Electrons from N-side diffuse into P-side.
- These mobile carriers **recombine** near the interface, annihilating each other.

**Step 3 — Formation of Space-Charge Region**:
- After recombination, **uncompensated ionized acceptors** (negative charge, $N_A^-$) are left on the P-side.
- **Uncompensated ionized donors** (positive charge, $N_D^+$) are left on the N-side.
- A **depletion width** $W$ is established with width $W_p$ on the P-side and $W_n$ on the N-side.

**Step 4 — Built-in Electric Field**:
- The exposed ionic charges create an electric field $E_{bi}$ pointing from N → P.
- This field generates a **contact potential difference** $V_{bi}$ (also called built-in potential, barrier potential, or diffusion voltage).

**Step 5 — Equilibrium (No Net Current)**:
- The diffusion current (carriers moving down the concentration gradient) is **exactly cancelled** by the drift current (carriers moving under the influence of the built-in field).
- The Fermi level becomes **flat (constant)** across the entire device — a fundamental thermodynamic requirement for thermal equilibrium.

---

### 2.2 Why Do the Bands Bend? — The Deep "Why"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Reason for Band Bending**: In equilibrium, the Fermi level $E_F$ **must be constant** throughout the structure (otherwise electrons would flow from regions of high $E_F$ to low $E_F$, contradicting equilibrium). Since the band edges $E_C$ and $E_V$ are tied to the local electrostatic potential $\phi(x)$ via $E_C(x) = -q\phi(x) + \text{const}$, the bands **must bend** in the region where the electrostatic potential varies — that is, in the depletion region.

The total band bending equals $qV_{bi}$, the built-in potential barrier.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Formulas for KTU 2024 Scheme Exam** (memorize the boxed equations!):

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Meaning / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Built-in potential | $V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$ | Potential barrier in Volts; $V_T = k_B T / q$ |
| Total depletion width | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right)}$ | Width in meters; $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ |
| Depletion width (one-sided, $N_A \ll N_D$) | $W \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_A}}$ | Used for $p^+n$ or $n^+p$ junctions |
| Depletion widths (charge neutrality) | $N_A W_p = N_D W_n$ | Smaller doping → wider depletion side |
| Maximum electric field | $E_{\max} = \dfrac{2 V_{bi}}{W}$ | At the metallurgical junction $x = 0$ |
| Capacitance per unit area | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_s}{W}$ | Parallel-plate capacitor model |
| Current under forward bias (Shockley) | $I = I_0 \!\left(e^{V/V_T} - 1\right)$ | $I_0$ = reverse saturation current |
| Reverse saturation current | $I_0 = q A \!\left(\dfrac{D_p n_i^{\,2}}{L_p N_D} + \dfrac{D_n n_i^{\,2}}{L_n N_A}\right)$ | $A$ = cross-section; $L_{p,n}$ = diffusion lengths |
| Barrier height under bias | $V_{\text{eff}} = V_{bi} - V$ | Forward bias: $V > 0$ reduces barrier |
| Dynamic resistance | $r_d = \dfrac{V_T}{I}$ | Slope of $I$–$V$ curve at operating point |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical**: For doping asymmetry, the depletion region extends **mostly into the lightly doped side**. For example, in a $p^+n$ junction (where $N_A \gg N_D$), $W_n \gg W_p$, and the junction behaves as a one-sided abrupt junction.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why This Topic Matters in Engineering**:
> - **Diodes** (rectifiers, signal demodulators, Zener diodes) — built on P-N junctions.
> - **Solar cells / Photovoltaics** — P-N junction separates photogenerated electron-hole pairs.
> - **LEDs (Light Emitting Diodes)** — radiative recombination at the junction emits photons.
> - **Bipolar Junction Transistors (BJTs)** — two back-to-back P-N junctions (NPN or PNP).
> - **Varactor diodes** — exploit the voltage-dependent depletion capacitance.
> - **Integrated Circuits (ICs)** — every transistor and resistor on a chip is built from P-N junctions.
> - **Thyristors, TRIACs, Power devices** — high-voltage P-N junctions control kilowatts of power.

The P-N junction is often called the **"workhorse of modern electronics"** — every digital device relies on it.

## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation: Built-in Potential $V_{bi}$

We want to find the contact potential difference between the N-side and P-side.

**Starting point**: The position of the Fermi level in a non-degenerate semiconductor is given by the carrier statistics equations.

For the **N-side** (with donor concentration $N_D$):
$$n_n \approx N_D = n_i \exp\!\left(\frac{E_{Fn} - E_i}{k_B T}\right)$$

Solving for $E_{Fn}$:
$$E_{Fn} = E_i + k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right)$$

For the **P-side** (with acceptor concentration $N_A$):
$$p_p \approx N_A = n_i \exp\!\left(\frac{E_i - E_{Fp}}{k_B T}\right)$$

Solving for $E_{Fp}$:
$$E_{Fp} = E_i - k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)$$

In **equilibrium**, the Fermi levels must align:
$$E_{Fn} = E_{Fp} = E_F$$

The total band bending (built-in potential times $q$) equals the original difference in Fermi levels:
$$q V_{bi} = E_{Fn}^{\text{(isolated)}} - E_{Fp}^{\text{(isolated)}}$$

Substituting:
$$q V_{bi} = \left[E_i + k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right)\right] - \left[E_i - k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right)\right]$$

Simplifying:
$$q V_{bi} = k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_D}{n_i}\right) + k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A}{n_i}\right) = k_B T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$$

Dividing both sides by $q$ and using $V_T = k_B T / q$:

$$\boxed{\,V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)\,}$$

**Physical interpretation**:
- Higher doping → larger $V_{bi}$.
- Smaller intrinsic carrier concentration (larger $E_g$ material) → larger $V_{bi}$.
- For Si with $N_A = 10^{17}$ /cm³ and $N_D = 10^{15}$ /cm³ at 300 K: $V_{bi} \approx 0.0259 \times \ln(10^{32} / 2.25 \times 10^{20}) \approx 0.69$ V (typical for silicon).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Depletion Width $W$ (Abrupt Junction Model)

**Assumption**: All ionized dopants within the depletion region are fully depleted (mobile carriers are negligible). The transition is **abrupt** at $x = 0$.

**Step 1 — Charge Density**:
On the N-side ($0 < x < W_n$): $\rho(x) = +q N_D$
On the P-side ($-W_p < x < 0$): $\rho(x) = -q N_A$
Outside the depletion region: $\rho(x) = 0$ (neutral bulk).

**Step 2 — Poisson's Equation**:
$$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate in N-region** ($0 \le x \le W_n$):
$$\frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

Integrating once:
$$\frac{d \phi}{dx} = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} x + C_1$$

Boundary condition: Electric field is **zero** at the edge of the depletion region, $x = W_n$:
$$0 = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} W_n + C_1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad C_1 = \frac{q N_D W_n}{\varepsilon_s}$$

So:
$$\frac{d \phi}{dx} = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}(W_n - x)$$

Integrating again:
$$\phi(x) = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}\!\left(W_n x - \frac{x^2}{2}\right) + C_2$$

Boundary condition: Reference potential $\phi(0) = 0$ at the metallurgical junction:
$$C_2 = 0$$

Therefore:
$$\phi(x) = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}\!\left(W_n x - \frac{x^2}{2}\right)$$

At $x = W_n$ (edge of N-depletion):
$$\phi(W_n) = \frac{q N_D W_n^{\,2}}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 4 — Integrate in P-region** ($-W_p \le x \le 0$):
By charge neutrality, $N_A W_p = N_D W_n$. By symmetry of analysis:
$$\phi(-W_p) = -\frac{q N_A W_p^{\,2}}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 5 — Total built-in potential**:
$$V_{bi} = \phi(W_n) - \phi(-W_p) = \frac{q N_D W_n^{\,2}}{2 \varepsilon_s} + \frac{q N_A W_p^{\,2}}{2 \varepsilon_s}$$

Using $N_A W_p = N_D W_n$, we have $W_p = N_D W_n / N_A$. Substituting:

$$V_{bi} = \frac{q N_D W_n^{\,2}}{2 \varepsilon_s} + \frac{q N_A}{2 \varepsilon_s}\!\left(\frac{N_D W_n}{N_A}\right)^{\!2} = \frac{q N_D W_n^{\,2}}{2 \varepsilon_s} + \frac{q N_D^{\,2} W_n^{\,2}}{2 \varepsilon_s N_A}$$

Factoring:
$$V_{bi} = \frac{q N_D W_n^{\,2}}{2 \varepsilon_s}\!\left(1 + \frac{N_D}{N_A}\right)$$

Solving for $W_n$:
$$W_n = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}\!\left(\frac{N_A}{N_A + N_D}\right)} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}\!\left(\frac{1}{1 + N_D/N_A}\right)}$$

Similarly:
$$W_p = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_A}\!\left(\frac{1}{1 + N_A/N_D}\right)}$$

**Total depletion width** $W = W_n + W_p$. Using the relation $N_A W_p = N_D W_n$, we can simplify:

$$\boxed{\,W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\!\left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}\,}$$

For a **one-sided junction** ($N_A \gg N_D$, a $p^+n$ junction), this reduces to:

$$\boxed{\,W \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical insight**: The depletion width scales as $\sqrt{V_{bi}}$. Under reverse bias, the effective barrier becomes $V_{bi} + V_R$, so $W$ **increases** with reverse voltage. This is the basis of the **varactor (varicap) diode**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Electric Field

From the integrated Poisson equation, the field is maximum at the metallurgical junction $x = 0$. Using $\phi(W_n) = q N_D W_n^2 / (2\varepsilon_s)$ and $E = -d\phi/dx$:

At $x = 0$:
$$E_{\max} = -\left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = -\frac{q N_D W_n}{\varepsilon_s}\cdot(-1) \cdot \text{(direction)} $$

The magnitude is:
$$\vert E_{\max} \vert = \frac{q N_D W_n}{\varepsilon_s}$$

Using $W_n \approx W$ (one-sided limit):
$$\vert E_{\max} \vert = \frac{q N_D W}{\varepsilon_s} = \frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 q N_D V_{bi}}{\varepsilon_s}}$$

For a general abrupt junction, using $V_{bi} = \frac{1}{2} E_{\max} W$ (triangular field profile), we get:

$$\boxed{\,E_{\max} = \frac{2 V_{bi}}{W}\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Forward vs Reverse Bias

| **Property** | **Forward Bias ($V > 0$)** | **Reverse Bias ($V < 0$)** |
|---|---|---|
| Applied voltage polarity | P-side positive, N-side negative | P-side negative, N-side positive |
| External field direction | Opposes built-in field | Same direction as built-in field |
| Net internal field | Reduced: $E_{\text{eff}} = E_{bi} - E_{\text{applied}}$ | Enhanced: $E_{\text{eff}} = E_{bi} + \vert E_{\text{applied}} \vert$ |
| Barrier height | Reduced: $V_{bi} - V$ | Increased: $V_{bi} + \vert V_R \vert$ |
| Depletion width $W$ | Decreases | Increases |
| Majority carrier flow | Large current flows (exponential) | Almost no current (only $I_0$) |
| Minority carrier flow | Reduced drift | Enhanced drift (saturated) |
| Current magnitude | $I = I_0(e^{V/V_T} - 1) \approx I_0 e^{V/V_T}$ | $I \approx -I_0$ (saturates) |
| Charge flow mechanism | Diffusion-dominated | Drift-dominated |
| Dominant carriers | Majority (electrons from N, holes from P) | Minority (thermally generated) |

## 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Block Diagram: P-N Junction Formation & Energy Band Evolution

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Isolated P-type Semiconductor] --> C[Bring P-type and N-type into Atomic Contact]
    B[Isolated N-type Semiconductor] --> C
    C --> D{Diffusion of Majority Carriers}
    D --> D1[Electrons from N diffuse into P]
    D --> D2[Holes from P diffuse into N]
    D1 --> E[Recombination near Interface]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F[Uncompensated Ionized Donors on N-side]
    E --> G[Uncompensated Ionized Acceptors on P-side]
    F --> H[Built-in Electric Field E_bi]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Balance Achieved?}
    I -->|No| J[Continued diffusion opposes by drift]
    I -->|Yes| K[Thermal Equilibrium]
    J --> I
    K --> L[Constant Fermi Level Across Junction]
    K --> M[Bent Energy Bands in Depletion Region]
    L --> N[Energy Band Diagram of P-N Junction]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Apply External Bias]
    O --> P[Forward Bias: Barrier Reduced]
    O --> Q[Reverse Bias: Barrier Increased]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Charge Flow Across a P-N Junction (Qualitative)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph "P-Side"
        PA[Fixed Acceptor Ions: Negative]
        PM[Mobile Holes: Positive]
    end

    subgraph "Depletion Region"
        DR[No Mobile Carriers]
        EBI[Built-in Electric Field E_bi points from N to P]
    end

    subgraph "N-Side"
        NM[Mobile Electrons: Negative]
        NA[Fixed Donor Ions: Positive]
    end

    PM -->|Diffusion of Holes| DR
    NM -->|Diffusion of Electrons| DR
    DR -->|Stops diffusion at equilibrium| EQ[Net Current equals Zero]

    EQ --> FB{Apply Forward Bias V_F}
    EQ --> RB{Apply Reverse Bias V_R}

    FB --> FB1[Barrier Reduced to V_bi minus V]
    FB1 --> FB2[Majority Carriers Cross Junction]
    FB2 --> FB3[Large Forward Current I_F]

    RB --> RB1[Barrier Increased to V_bi plus V_R]
    RB1 --> RB2[Majority Carriers Blocked]
    RB2 --> RB3[Only Minority Carriers Drift]
    RB3 --> RB4[Small Reverse Saturation Current I_0]
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Forward vs Reverse Bias Operating Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Apply External DC Voltage to P-N Junction] --> Polar{Polarity?}

    Polar -->|Positive on P, Negative on N| FWD[Forward Bias Mode]
    Polar -->|Negative on P, Positive on N| REV[Reverse Bias Mode]

    FWD --> F1[External field cancels built-in field]
    F1 --> F2[Barrier reduced from V_bi to V_bi minus V]
    F2 --> F3[Depletion width shrinks]
    F3 --> F4[Majority carriers injected across junction]
    F4 --> F5[Diffusion current dominates]
    F5 --> F6[Current grows exponentially with V]
    F6 --> F7[ON State: Conducts like closed switch]

    REV --> R1[External field adds to built-in field]
    R1 --> R2[Barrier increased to V_bi plus V_R]
    R2 --> R3[Depletion width widens]
    R3 --> R4[Majority carriers pushed back into bulk]
    R4 --> R5[Minority carriers drift across junction]
    R5 --> R6[Small constant current equals minus I_0]
    R6 --> R7[OFF State: Blocks like open switch]
    R6 -.->|If V_R exceeds breakdown| BR[Zener or Avalanche Breakdown]
    BR --> BR1[Sharp current increase in reverse direction]
```

---

### 4.4 Architecture Flow: Energy Band Diagram Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph "Energy Axis Y"
        EY1[Vacuum Level E_0]
        EY2[Conduction Band Edge E_C]
        EY3[Fermi Level E_F - constant in equilibrium]
        EY4[Valence Band Edge E_V]
    end

    subgraph "Position Axis X"
        PX1[P-region left]
        PX2[Depletion region middle]
        PX3[N-region right]
    end

    PX1 --> PX2 --> PX3
    EY1 --> EY2 --> EY3 --> EY4

    PX1 --> B1[Bands flat far from junction]
    PX2 --> B2[Bands bend down on P-side edge and up on N-side edge]
    PX3 --> B3[Bands flat far from junction]

    B2 --> B4[Total band bending equals qV_bi]
    B2 --> B5[Built-in potential opposes majority carrier flow]
    B2 --> B6[Depletion width W equals W_p plus W_n]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading a Band Diagram for KTU Exams**:
> 1. The **slope** of $E_C$ or $E_V$ in the depletion region indicates the **electric field** direction.
> 2. Bands slope **downward** from N → P in a normal P-N junction (since $E_C$ is higher on the N-side).
> 3. The **total vertical drop** in $E_C$ (or $E_V$) across the depletion region equals $qV_{bi}$.
> 4. Under **forward bias**, bands unbend partially; under **reverse bias**, they bend even more.

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define built-in potential in a P-N junction. What factors does it depend on?**

**Model Answer**:
The **built-in potential** $V_{bi}$ is the internal potential difference that develops across a P-N junction at thermal equilibrium due to the diffusion of majority carriers and the resulting space-charge region.

$$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$$

**Dependencies**:
1. **Doping concentrations** $N_A$ and $N_D$ — higher doping increases $V_{bi}$.
2. **Intrinsic carrier concentration** $n_i$ — material-dependent (Si, Ge, GaAs have different $V_{bi}$).
3. **Temperature** $T$ — appears inside the logarithm and in $V_T$; $V_{bi}$ decreases with temperature because $n_i$ increases rapidly with $T$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q2. Distinguish between forward bias and reverse bias in a P-N junction with the help of barrier height changes.**

**Model Answer**:

| **Aspect** | **Forward Bias** | **Reverse Bias** |
|---|---|---|
| Polarity | P connected to +ve terminal | P connected to −ve terminal |
| Barrier height | $V_{bi} - V$ (reduced) | $V_{bi} + V_R$ (increased) |
| Depletion width | Decreases | Increases |
| Current | Large, exponential in $V$ | Small, saturated at $-I_0$ |
| Carrier flow | Majority carriers (diffusion) | Minority carriers (drift) |

Under **forward bias**, the applied voltage opposes the built-in field, lowering the barrier, and majority carriers are injected across the junction → large current flows.

Under **reverse bias**, the applied voltage adds to the built-in field, raising the barrier, and majority carrier flow is suppressed → only a tiny drift current of minority carriers flows.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]**

**Q (a)** With a neat energy band diagram, explain the formation of a depletion region in a P-N junction. Define built-in potential and derive its expression. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Step 1 — Diagram** (4 marks typically for labeled band diagram):
Draw the energy band diagram showing:
- $E_C$ and $E_V$ on the P-side (flat bands far from junction)
- $E_C$ and $E_V$ on the N-side (flat bands far from junction)
- Bent bands in the depletion region
- A **single, flat Fermi level** $E_F$ across the entire device
- Label $V_{bi}$, $W_p$, $W_n$, and the built-in field direction

**Step 2 — Explanation** (1 mark):
When P-type and N-type materials are joined, electrons from N-side diffuse into P-side and holes from P-side diffuse into N-side. They recombine near the interface, leaving behind **ionized donors** (positive) on the N-side and **ionized acceptors** (negative) on the P-side. This forms the **depletion region** of width $W = W_p + W_n$.

**Step 3 — Definition** (0.5 marks):
**Built-in potential** $V_{bi}$ is the potential difference developed across the depletion region at thermal equilibrium due to the space-charge region. It opposes further diffusion of majority carriers.

**Step 4 — Derivation** (1.5 marks):
Using the equilibrium condition $E_{Fn} = E_{Fp} = E_F$ and Fermi-Dirac statistics:
$$E_{Fn} = E_i + k_B T \ln(N_D / n_i)$$
$$E_{Fp} = E_i - k_B T \ln(N_A / n_i)$$

Setting equal and solving for $qV_{bi} = E_{Fn} - E_{Fp}$:
$$\boxed{\,V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)\,}$$

---

**Q (b)** A silicon P-N junction at 300 K has $N_A = 10^{17}$ /cm³ and $N_D = 10^{15}$ /cm³. Calculate (i) the built-in potential, and (ii) the depletion width. Given: $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ /cm³, $\varepsilon_r = 11.7$, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-14}$ F/cm. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Given**:
- $T = 300$ K, $V_T = 0.0259$ V
- $N_A = 10^{17}$ /cm³, $N_D = 10^{15}$ /cm³
- $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ /cm³
- $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} = 1.036 \times 10^{-12}$ F/cm

**Part (i) — Built-in potential**:
$$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right)$$

$$V_{bi} = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{17} \times 10^{15}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right)$$

$$V_{bi} = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{32}}{2.25 \times 10^{20}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(4.444 \times 10^{11})$$

$$\ln(4.444 \times 10^{11}) = \ln(4.444) + 11 \ln(10) = 1.491 + 25.328 = 26.819$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{bi} \approx 0.0259 \times 26.819 \approx 0.6946 \text{ V} \approx 0.69 \text{ V}\,}$$

**Part (ii) — Depletion width**:
$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q}\!\left(\frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D}\right)}$$

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.6946}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \times \left(\frac{1}{10^{17}} + \frac{1}{10^{15}}\right)}$$

Compute the term $\left(\frac{1}{10^{17}} + \frac{1}{10^{15}}\right) = \frac{1}{10^{17}} (1 + 100) = \frac{101}{10^{17}} = 1.01 \times 10^{-15}$ cm$^{-1}$.

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{1.439 \times 10^{-12}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \times 1.01 \times 10^{-15}}$$

$$W = \sqrt{8.984 \times 10^{6} \times 1.01 \times 10^{-15}} = \sqrt{9.074 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\boxed{\,W \approx 9.526 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} \approx 0.95 \text{ μm}\,}$$

**Verification using one-sided approximation** (since $N_D \ll N_A$):
$$W \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_{bi}}{q N_D}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.6946}{1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{15}}}$$

$$W \approx \sqrt{8.98 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 9.48 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm}$$

Both methods give nearly the same result, confirming the depletion region extends predominantly into the lightly doped N-side.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Similar Pattern]**

**Q (a)** Explain with energy band diagrams the operation of a P-N junction under (i) forward bias and (ii) reverse bias. Discuss the qualitative charge flow in each case. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Forward Bias (3.5 marks)**:

When the **P-side is connected to the positive terminal** and the **N-side to the negative terminal** of a battery, the junction is forward biased.

- The applied voltage **opposes** the built-in field.
- The **barrier height is reduced** from $V_{bi}$ to $V_{bi} - V$.
- The **depletion width decreases**.
- The **bands unbend partially** — $E_C$ on the P-side rises, $E_C$ on the N-side falls.
- **Majority carriers are injected** across the junction:
  - Electrons from N-side are pushed into the P-side (diffusion).
  - Holes from P-side are pushed into the N-side (diffusion).
- This results in a **large forward current** $I \approx I_0 e^{V/V_T}$ that grows exponentially with applied voltage.
- The P-N junction behaves like a **closed switch** (low resistance).

**Reverse Bias (3.5 marks)**:

When the **P-side is connected to the negative terminal** and the **N-side to the positive terminal**, the junction is reverse biased.

- The applied voltage **adds to** the built-in field.
- The **barrier height is increased** from $V_{bi}$ to $V_{bi} + V_R$.
- The **depletion width increases**.
- The **bands bend more steeply**.
- **Majority carriers are pulled away** from the junction into the bulk (no injection).
- Only **minority carriers** that happen to diffuse to the depletion edge are swept across by the strong field (drift current).
- This results in a **tiny reverse saturation current** $I \approx -I_0$ (typically nA to μA).
- The P-N junction behaves like an **open switch** (high resistance), provided $V_R$ does not exceed the breakdown voltage.

---

**Q (b)** A silicon P-N junction has $N_A = 5 \times 10^{16}$ /cm³, $N_D = 10^{16}$ /cm³ at 300 K. Find the maximum electric field at equilibrium. Given: $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ /cm³, $\varepsilon_r = 11.7$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer**:

**Step 1 — Calculate $V_{bi}$**:
$$V_{bi} = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^{\,2}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{16} \times 10^{16}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right)$$

$$V_{bi} = 0.0259 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{32}}{2.25 \times 10^{20}}\right) = 0.0259 \times \ln(2.222 \times 10^{12})$$

$$\ln(2.222 \times 10^{12}) = \ln(2.222) + 12 \ln(10) = 0.798 + 27.631 = 28.429$$

$$V_{bi} \approx 0.0259 \times 28.429 \approx 0.7363 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate depletion width $W$**:
$$\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-14} = 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/cm}$$

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-12} \times 0.7363}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \times \left(\frac{1}{5 \times 10^{16}} + \frac{1}{10^{16}}\right)}$$

$$\frac{1}{5 \times 10^{16}} + \frac{1}{10^{16}} = \frac{1 + 5}{5 \times 10^{16}} = \frac{6}{5 \times 10^{16}} = 1.2 \times 10^{-16} \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{1.5256 \times 10^{-12}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-16}}$$

$$W = \sqrt{9.523 \times 10^{6} \times 1.2 \times 10^{-16}} = \sqrt{1.143 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$W \approx 3.38 \times 10^{-5} \text{ cm} = 0.338 \text{ μm}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate maximum electric field**:
$$E_{\max} = \frac{2 V_{bi}}{W} = \frac{2 \times 0.7363}{3.38 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$\boxed{\,E_{\max} \approx 4.357 \times 10^{4} \text{ V/cm} \approx 4.36 \times 10^{6} \text{ V/m}\,}$$

**Interpretation**: This is the peak field at the metallurgical junction. The triangular field profile drops linearly to zero at both edges of the depletion region.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams**:
>
> 1. **Confusing the direction of band bending**: Some students draw bands sloping *upward* from N to P. Correct: bands slope **downward** from N to P (since the N-side has higher electron energy).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the thermal voltage $V_T \approx 0.0259$ V** at 300 K — if the problem doesn't specify, **always use 26 mV** for silicon at room temperature.
>
> 3. **Mixing up $V_{bi}$ with applied voltage $V$**: $V_{bi}$ is the *internal* built-in barrier. Under forward bias, the *effective* barrier becomes $V_{bi} - V$, not just $V$.
>
> 4. **Wrong units in depletion width formula**: When using $\varepsilon_s$ in F/cm, $N$ in /cm³, and $V_{bi}$ in V, $W$ comes out in **cm**, not meters. Be careful with conversions.
>
> 5. **Drawing the Fermi level as non-flat in equilibrium**: This is an automatic **deduction of 1–2 marks**. In equilibrium (no bias), $E_F$ **must be perfectly flat** across the entire device.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to show the vacuum level** $E_0$ and electron affinity $\chi$ in the band diagram when the question asks for a "neat labeled diagram" — examiners specifically check for these.
>
> 7. **Writing the Shockley equation incorrectly**: The correct form is $I = I_0 (e^{V/V_T} - 1)$, not $I = I_0 e^{V/V_T}$ (the "$-1$" is important at small $V$).
>
> 8. **Not specifying units in numerical answers**: Always state the final answer with units (V, cm, m, A/m², etc.).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam — Module 1, P-N Junction**:

**1. Core Concepts**:
- A P-N junction is formed by joining P-type and N-type semiconductors in a single crystal.
- Diffusion of majority carriers creates a **depletion region** with ionized dopants.
- A **built-in electric field** $E_{bi}$ develops, pointing from N to P.
- The **built-in potential** $V_{bi}$ acts as a barrier to further diffusion.
- In equilibrium, **diffusion current = drift current** (net current is zero).
- The **Fermi level is flat** across the junction at equilibrium.

**2. Energy Band Diagram Essentials**:
- Isolated P-type: $E_F$ near $E_V$ (above), bands flat.
- Isolated N-type: $E_F$ near $E_C$ (below), bands flat.
- After junction: bands **bend** so that $E_F$ becomes constant.
- Total band bending = $qV_{bi}$.
- The slope of $E_C$ or $E_V$ in the depletion region indicates the **direction of $E$**.

**3. Key Formulas (Must Memorize)**:
- $V_{bi} = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$, where $V_T = k_BT/q \approx 26$ mV at 300 K.
- $W = \sqrt{(2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}/q)(1/N_A + 1/N_D)}$.
- $E_{\max} = 2V_{bi}/W$ at the metallurgical junction.
- $N_A W_p = N_D W_n$ (charge neutrality).
- Shockley diode equation: $I = I_0(e^{V/V_T} - 1)$.
- Effective barrier under bias: $V_{\text{eff}} = V_{bi} - V$ (forward), $V_{bi} + V_R$ (reverse).

**4. Biasing Behavior**:
- **Forward bias** ($V > 0$): Barrier reduced, depletion shrinks, large exponential current, majority carrier injection, **ON state**.
- **Reverse bias** ($V < 0$): Barrier increased, depletion widens, tiny saturated current $-I_0$, minority carrier drift, **OFF state**.

**5. Charge Flow Mechanisms**:
- **Forward**: Diffusion of majority carriers across the lowered barrier.
- **Reverse**: Drift of minority carriers across the widened depletion region.
- **Equilibrium**: Diffusion and drift currents exactly cancel — net zero current.

**6. Real-World Devices Built on P-N Junctions**:
- Rectifier diodes, Zener diodes, varactor diodes, photodiodes, solar cells, LEDs, laser diodes, BJTs, thyristors, power devices, integrated circuits.

**7. Common Numerical Values for Silicon at 300 K**:
- $V_T = 0.0259$ V
- $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10}$ /cm³
- $E_g = 1.12$ eV
- $\varepsilon_r = 11.7$
- Typical $V_{bi} \approx 0.6$ to $0.9$ V depending on doping.

**8. One-Sided Junction Trick**:
- When $N_A \gg N_D$ (or vice versa), the depletion region extends **almost entirely into the lightly doped side**.
- Use $W \approx \sqrt{2\varepsilon_s V_{bi}/(q N_{\text{light}})}$ for quick estimation.

**9. Energy Band Diagram Drawing Checklist** (for full marks):
- ✅ Show $E_C$ and $E_V$ on both sides.
- ✅ Show flat $E_F$ in equilibrium.
- ✅ Label the band bending as $qV_{bi}$.
- ✅ Indicate the direction of $E_{bi}$ (N → P).
- ✅ Mark the depletion width $W = W_p + W_n$.
- ✅ Show direction of majority carrier diffusion (P to P, N to N — no, electrons from N to P and holes from P to N).

**10. Examiner's Pet Topics** (most frequently asked):
- Derivation of $V_{bi}$ — asked almost every year.
- Numerical on $V_{bi}$, $W$, or $E_{\max}$ — almost guaranteed.
- Energy band diagram with labels — high-scoring if drawn correctly.
- Qualitative comparison of forward vs reverse bias — guaranteed 3-mark question.
- Shockley diode equation and its use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Word for KTU 2024 Aspirants**: Master the energy band diagram — it is the **universal language** of semiconductor devices. Once you can draw and interpret it, the rest of the course (diodes, transistors, optoelectronics) becomes intuitive. Practice at least 5 numerical problems on $V_{bi}$ and $W$ before the exam!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Diode Equation (Derivation)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Diode Equation (Derivation)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Diode Equation**, also known as the **Shockley Diode Equation**, is a fundamental mathematical relationship that describes the **current-voltage (I-V) characteristic** of a p-n junction diode under both forward and reverse bias conditions. It expresses the diode current $I$ as an exponential function of the applied voltage $V$ across the junction, taking into account carrier diffusion, recombination, and the thermal energy of charge carriers at the junction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (GBPHT121 - Module 1):**
> The Diode Equation is a **mandatory derivation topic**. Students must be able to derive it from first principles using the continuity equation, minority carrier diffusion profiles, and Boltzmann statistics. The equation is the cornerstone of all semiconductor device analysis in subsequent modules.

The general form of the Diode Equation is given by:

$$I = I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{eV}{\eta k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

where each symbol carries a precise physical meaning rooted in semiconductor physics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam and Water Flow" Model

Imagine a **water reservoir** (the depletion region) separating two lakes (the p-side and n-side regions). The dam wall has a height controlled by an applied external force — this is your **applied voltage $V$**.

- When the applied force **pushes water over the dam** (forward bias), water (current) flows exponentially — even a small push creates a disproportionately large flow because of how the water level cascades.
- When you **pull back** (reverse bias), only a tiny trickle of water leaks through the dam's cracks — this is the **reverse saturation current $I_0$**, which is small and nearly constant.
- The **temperature $T$** of the water affects how easily molecules can jump over — hotter water (higher $T$) means more flow at the same push.

This is exactly what the diode equation models: an exponential surge of current under forward bias and a tiny, temperature-dependent leakage under reverse bias.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants Used Throughout the Derivation:**
> - $e$ = Electronic charge = **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C**
> - $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant = **$1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K**
> - $T$ = Absolute temperature in **Kelvin (K)**
> - $V_T = k_B T / e$ = Thermal voltage ≈ **$25.85$ mV** at room temperature (300 K)
> - $\eta$ = Ideality factor (typically **1 to 2**, dimensionless)
> - $I_0$ = Reverse saturation current (typically **$10^{-12}$ to $10^{-6}$ A**)

These values must be memorized — they appear in **every numerical problem** in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational Building Blocks of the Derivation

The derivation rests on **three pillars** of semiconductor physics. A student who understands these will find the derivation intuitive rather than mechanical.

### Pillar 1: Minority Carrier Injection at the Junction Edge

When a forward bias voltage $V$ is applied across the p-n junction, the equilibrium carrier concentrations at the depletion region edges are disturbed. According to **Boltzmann statistics** and the **law of the junction**:

$$p_n(-x_p) = p_{n0} \exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right)$$

$$n_p(+x_n) = n_{p0} \exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right)$$

Here $p_{n0}$ and $n_{p0}$ are the **thermal equilibrium minority carrier concentrations** in the n-side and p-side respectively. The exponential term represents the **enhancement** of minority carriers due to the applied bias.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** At equilibrium (V = 0), the exponential becomes 1, recovering the equilibrium values. As $V$ becomes large and positive, minority carrier concentrations at the junction edge grow exponentially — this is the seed of exponential diode behavior.

### Pillar 2: Minority Carrier Diffusion Profiles

The injected minority carriers **diffuse away** from the junction into the bulk regions, recombining as they travel. The **continuity equation** under steady-state and low-level injection yields exponential decay profiles:

$$\delta p_n(x) = p_{n0} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1 \right] \exp\left(\frac{-x}{L_p}\right), \quad x > 0$$

$$\delta n_p(x) = n_{p0} \left[ \exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1 \right] \exp\left(\frac{x}{L_n}\right), \quad x < 0$$

Here $L_p$ and $L_n$ are the **diffusion lengths** of holes in the n-region and electrons in the p-region respectively, defined as $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ and $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$.

### Pillar 3: Diffusion Current Density via Fick's First Law

Charge transport in the quasi-neutral regions (away from the depletion layer) is governed by **diffusion** rather than drift, because the electric field in these regions is negligible. Using **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**:

$$J_p(x) = -e D_p \frac{dp_n(x)}{dx}$$

$$J_n(x) = e D_n \frac{dn_p(x)}{dx}$$

The total current density is the **sum** of the hole and electron diffusion components (since the total current must be continuous across the junction):

$$J = J_p(x) + J_n(x)$$

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** | **Units** | **Boundary / Typical Value** |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| $V_T = k_B T / e$ | Thermal voltage | Volts (V) | ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K |
| $I = I_0 \left[\exp(eV/\eta k_B T) - 1\right]$ | Shockley Diode Equation | Amperes (A) | Forward: exponential; Reverse: $-I_0$ |
| $I_0 = e A \left[\dfrac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \dfrac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right]$ | Reverse saturation current | Amperes (A) | $10^{-12}$ to $10^{-6}$ A |
| $p_{n0} = n_i^2 / N_D$ | Equilibrium minority hole density in n-side | $m^{-3}$ | — |
| $n_{p0} = n_i^2 / N_A$ | Equilibrium minority electron density in p-side | $m^{-3}$ | — |
| $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ | Hole diffusion length in n-region | m | — |
| $L_n = \sqrt{D_n \tau_n}$ | Electron diffusion length in p-region | m | — |
| $\delta p_n(x) = p_{n0}[\exp(eV/k_BT) - 1]\exp(-x/L_p)$ | Excess hole profile in n-side | $m^{-3}$ | Boundary at $x = 0$ |
| $\delta n_p(x) = n_{p0}[\exp(eV/k_BT) - 1]\exp(x/L_n)$ | Excess electron profile in p-side | $m^{-3}$ | Boundary at $x = 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Note:** The factor $[\exp(eV/k_BT) - 1]$ is universally present across all three components of the derivation. Students who drop the "$-1$" term lose 2 marks instantly in KTU valuation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Shockley Diode Equation is the **backbone of all semiconductor device engineering**:
- **Rectifier design:** Power supplies in every electronic appliance.
- **Photovoltaic cells:** Solar panels are essentially p-n junctions operating in the fourth quadrant of the I-V curve.
- **Signal processing:** Zener diodes, varactor diodes, and tunnel diodes all derive characteristics from variations of this equation.
- **Integrated circuits:** Every transistor in a microchip contains p-n junctions whose behavior is governed by this relationship.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Impact:** Without the Diode Equation, the entire field of modern electronics — from smartphones to spacecraft — would be uncalculable. It is the single most derived equation in device physics.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Setup: The Forward-Biased p-n Junction Geometry

Consider an abrupt p-n junction with:
- **p-side** (at $x < 0$): uniformly doped with acceptor concentration $N_A$
- **n-side** (at $x > 0$): uniformly doped with donor concentration $N_D$
- **Depletion region** width $W$ straddling the metallurgical junction at $x = 0$
- **Quasi-neutral regions** extend from $x = -x_p$ to $-\infty$ (p-side) and from $x = +x_n$ to $+\infty$ (n-side)
- **Cross-sectional area** of the device: $A$
- **Applied forward bias**: $V$ volts (p-side positive with respect to n-side)

The applied voltage $V$ reduces the built-in potential barrier from $V_0$ to $(V_0 - V)$.

## 3.2 Step 1 — Equilibrium Minority Carrier Concentrations

At thermal equilibrium (no applied bias), the **mass action law** dictates the minority carrier densities. The product of majority and minority carriers equals the square of the intrinsic carrier concentration $n_i$:

$$p_{n0} \cdot n_{n0} = n_i^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad p_{n0} = \frac{n_i^2}{N_D}$$

$$n_{p0} \cdot p_{p0} = n_i^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_{p0} = \frac{n_i^2}{N_A}$$

**Reasoning:** On the n-side, electrons are the majority carriers with density $\approx N_D$, and holes are the minority carriers with density $p_{n0}$. Their product must equal $n_i^2$ by the mass action law.

## 3.3 Step 2 — Boundary Conditions at the Depletion Edges (Law of the Junction)

When forward bias $V$ is applied, the barrier potential drops. Using **Boltzmann statistics** for the carrier densities at the depletion region edges, the **Law of the Junction** states:

At $x = -x_p$ (edge of depletion region on p-side, where minority electrons reside):

$$n_p(-x_p) = n_{p0} \exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right)$$

At $x = +x_n$ (edge of depletion region on n-side, where minority holes reside):

$$p_n(+x_n) = p_{n0} \exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right)$$

**Reasoning:** The applied forward voltage $V$ effectively reduces the potential barrier, allowing exponential injection of minority carriers. The Boltzmann factor $\exp(eV/k_BT)$ captures this injection physics exactly.

## 3.4 Step 3 — Excess Minority Carrier Concentrations

The **excess** minority carrier concentration is the injected density above the equilibrium value. By definition:

$$\delta p_n(x) = p_n(x) - p_{n0} \quad ; \quad \delta n_p(x) = n_p(x) - n_{p0}$$

At the boundary $x = +x_n$:

$$\delta p_n(+x_n) = p_{n0} \exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - p_{n0} = p_{n0} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right]$$

At the boundary $x = -x_p$:

$$\delta n_p(-x_p) = n_{p0} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right]$$

Far from the junction, the excess carriers vanish (they recombine before reaching the ohmic contacts):

$$\delta p_n(x \to +\infty) = 0 \quad ; \quad \delta n_p(x \to -\infty) = 0$$

## 3.5 Step 4 — Solving the Continuity Equation for Diffusion Profiles

The **continuity equation** for minority carriers in steady state (no generation, no external excitation, low-level injection) reduces to:

$$D_p \frac{d^2 \delta p_n}{dx^2} - \frac{\delta p_n}{\tau_p} = 0$$

This is a standard second-order linear homogeneous ODE. The general solution is:

$$\delta p_n(x) = C_1 \exp\left(\frac{x}{L_p}\right) + C_2 \exp\left(\frac{-x}{L_p}\right)$$

where $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ is the **hole diffusion length**.

**Applying boundary conditions:**
- As $x \to +\infty$: $\delta p_n \to 0$ → requires $C_1 = 0$ (to prevent exponential blow-up).
- At $x = +x_n \approx 0$ (taking the depletion edge as origin): $\delta p_n(0) = p_{n0}[\exp(eV/k_BT) - 1]$ → $C_2 = p_{n0}[\exp(eV/k_BT) - 1]$.

Therefore, the **excess hole concentration in the n-region** is:

$$\delta p_n(x) = p_{n0} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right] \exp\left(\frac{-x}{L_p}\right), \quad x \geq 0$$

**By analogous reasoning**, the **excess electron concentration in the p-region** is:

$$\delta n_p(x) = n_{p0} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right] \exp\left(\frac{x}{L_n}\right), \quad x \leq 0$$

## 3.6 Step 5 — Hole Diffusion Current Density in the n-Region

In the quasi-neutral n-region, the current is carried by **diffusion of holes** (drift is negligible because the electric field in the neutral region is essentially zero). By **Fick's First Law**:

$$J_p(x) = -e D_p \frac{d(\delta p_n(x))}{dx}$$

Differentiating $\delta p_n(x)$:

$$\frac{d(\delta p_n)}{dx} = p_{n0} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right] \cdot \left(\frac{-1}{L_p}\right) \exp\left(\frac{-x}{L_p}\right)$$

Substituting into Fick's Law:

$$J_p(x) = -e D_p \cdot p_{n0} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right] \cdot \left(\frac{-1}{L_p}\right) \exp\left(\frac{-x}{L_p}\right)$$

Simplifying the two negative signs:

$$J_p(x) = \frac{e D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right] \exp\left(\frac{-x}{L_p}\right)$$

**At the boundary** $x = 0$ (edge of depletion region on n-side):

$$J_p(0) = \frac{e D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right]$$

## 3.7 Step 6 — Electron Diffusion Current Density in the p-Region

By symmetry and analogous derivation, the **electron diffusion current density** in the p-region is:

$$J_n(x) = e D_n \frac{d(\delta n_p(x))}{dx}$$

Differentiating $\delta n_p(x)$:

$$\frac{d(\delta n_p)}{dx} = n_{p0} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right] \cdot \left(\frac{1}{L_n}\right) \exp\left(\frac{x}{L_n}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$J_n(x) = e D_n \cdot n_{p0} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right] \cdot \left(\frac{1}{L_n}\right) \exp\left(\frac{x}{L_n}\right)$$

Simplifying:

$$J_n(x) = \frac{e D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right] \exp\left(\frac{x}{L_n}\right)$$

**At the boundary** $x = 0$ (edge of depletion region on p-side):

$$J_n(0) = \frac{e D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right]$$

## 3.8 Step 7 — Total Current Density (Continuity Principle)

The **principle of current continuity** in a p-n junction dictates that the total current density must be the **same** at every cross-section of the device, because charge is conserved (no charge accumulates indefinitely in the steady state). Therefore, we evaluate both components at the junction boundary $x = 0$ and sum:

$$J_{total} = J_p(0) + J_n(0)$$

Substituting the expressions derived in Steps 5 and 6:

$$J = \frac{e D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right] + \frac{e D_n n_{p0}}{L_n} \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right]$$

Factoring the common exponential term:

$$J = \left[\frac{e D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{e D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right] \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right]$$

## 3.9 Step 8 — Defining the Reverse Saturation Current Density

Defining the bracketed term as the **reverse saturation current density** $J_0$:

$$J_0 = e \left[\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right]$$

**Reasoning for the name:** Under reverse bias, $V$ is large and negative, so $\exp(eV/k_BT) \approx 0$, and the current becomes $J \approx -J_0$. This negative constant is the small, temperature-dependent leakage current observed in reverse bias.

## 3.10 Step 9 — The Final Diode Equation

The **complete Shockley Diode Equation** in current density form:

$$J = J_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right]$$

Multiplying by the cross-sectional area $A$ to obtain the **total diode current**:

$$\boxed{I = I_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{eV}{k_B T}\right) - 1\right]}$$

where $I_0 = A J_0$ is the **reverse saturation current**.

## 3.11 Step 10 — Engineering Modification: The Ideality Factor

In **real diodes**, the exponential is slightly less steep than the ideal case because of:
- **Generation-recombination currents** in the depletion region (Shockley-Read-Hall recombination)
- **Surface leakage currents**
- **Tunneling effects** in heavily doped junctions

To account for this, an **ideality factor** $\eta$ (typically between 1 and 2) is introduced:

$$\boxed{I = I_0 \left[\exp\left(\frac{V}{\eta V_T}\right) - 1\right] \quad \text{where} \quad V_T = \frac{k_B T}{e}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** For Germanium diodes, $\eta \approx 1$; for Silicon diodes, $\eta \approx 2$ in the low-forward-bias region. The examiner may provide $\eta$ explicitly in numerical problems.

## 3.12 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Summary

| **Step** | **Action** | **Resulting Equation** | **Marks Allocated (KTU Style)** |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| 1 | Equilibrium minority carriers | $p_{n0} = n_i^2/N_D$; $n_{p0} = n_i^2/N_A$ | 1 |
| 2 | Boundary conditions (Law of Junction) | $p_n(x_n) = p_{n0}\exp(eV/k_BT)$ | 2 |
| 3 | Excess carrier boundary values | $\delta p_n(0) = p_{n0}[\exp(eV/k_BT) - 1]$ | 1 |
| 4 | Continuity equation & diffusion profile | $\delta p_n(x) = p_{n0}[\exp(eV/k_BT)-1]\exp(-x/L_p)$ | 3 |
| 5 | Hole diffusion current | $J_p(0) = eD_p p_{n0}/L_p \cdot [\exp(eV/k_BT)-1]$ | 2 |
| 6 | Electron diffusion current | $J_n(0) = eD_n n_{p0}/L_n \cdot [\exp(eV/k_BT)-1]$ | 2 |
| 7 | Total current by continuity | $J = [eD_p p_{n0}/L_p + eD_n n_{p0}/L_n][\exp(eV/k_BT)-1]$ | 2 |
| 8 | Final Shockley Diode Equation | $I = I_0[\exp(V/\eta V_T) - 1]$ | 1 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Derivation Logic Chain

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **logical dependency chain** of the diode equation derivation, showing how each physical input feeds into the next computational stage:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STEP 1 APPLIED FORWARD BIAS VOLTAGE V"] --> B["STEP 2 BUILT IN POTENTIAL REDUCES FROM V0 TO V0 MINUS V"]
    B --> C["STEP 3 BOLTZMANN INJECTION AT JUNCTION EDGE"]
    C --> D["STEP 4 EXCESS MINORITY CARRIER CONCENTRATION AT BOUNDARY"]
    D --> E["STEP 5 CONTINUITY EQUATION SECOND ORDER ODE"]
    E --> F["STEP 6 DIFFUSION LENGTH LP AND LN APPEAR"]
    F --> G["STEP 7 EXPONENTIAL DECAY PROFILE DELTA P N AND DELTA N P"]
    G --> H["STEP 8 FICK FIRST LAW DIFFUSION CURRENT DENSITY"]
    H --> I["STEP 9 HOLE DIFFUSION JP AT X EQUALS ZERO"]
    G --> J["STEP 10 ELECTRON DIFFUSION JN AT X EQUALS ZERO"]
    I --> K["STEP 11 TOTAL CURRENT DENSITY JP PLUS JN BY CONTINUITY"]
    J --> K
    K --> L["STEP 12 DEFINE REVERSE SATURATION CURRENT DENSITY J0"]
    L --> M["STEP 13 FINAL SHOCKLEY DIODE EQUATION I EQUALS I0 EXP V ETA VT MINUS 1"]
    M --> N["STEP 14 IDEALITY FACTOR ETA ADDS REAL DIODE CORRECTION"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Energy Band Diagram Under Forward Bias

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> C["DEPLETION REGION EC AND EV BEND DOWN"]

    D --> E["HOLES INJECT FROM P TO N"]
    E --> F["EXPONENTIAL MINORITY CARRIER INJECTION DELTA N P AND DELTA P N"]
    F --> G["DIFFUSION CURRENTS JP AND JN FLOW"]
    G --> H["TOTAL DIODE CURRENT I EQUALS I0 EXP V ETA VT MINUS 1"]
```

```mermaid
    A --> C["P REGION X LESS THAN ZERO"]

```
