```mermaid
    ZD --> GND1[Ground]
    RL --> GND2[Ground]
    GND1 --- GND2
    NODE -. Vout constant equal to Vz .-> OUT[Regulated DC Output Vout]

    classDef input fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff
    classDef passive fill:#a98467,stroke:#6c584c,color:#ffffff
    classDef diode fill:#bb3e03,stroke:#9a3412,color:#ffffff
    classDef output fill:#386641,stroke:#1b4332,color:#ffffff
    class IN,OUT input
    class RS,RL passive
    class ZD diode
    class NODE,GNDo,GNDp output
```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**

- The **DC input source** $V_{in}$ feeds a **series resistor $R_S$**, which is the *current-limiting* element (it absorbs any excess voltage and prevents the Zener from drawing destructive current).
- The **regulator node** is where the **Zener diode (reverse biased)** and the **load $R_L$** are connected in parallel to ground.
- The output $V_{out}$ is taken across the Zener (= across $R_L$).
- The Zener *clamping* action keeps the node voltage pinned at $V_Z$, irrespective of variations in $V_{in}$ or $R_L$.

### 4.2 V-I Characteristic Curve (Block-Equivalent Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FORWARD["FORWARD REGION V greater than 0"]
        FW["Diode conducts heavily above 0.7 V for Si, current rises exponentially, identical to ordinary p n junction diode"]
    end
    subgraph REVERSE_PRE["REVERSE BLOCKING V less than Vz"]
        RB["Tiny reverse saturation current Is, of order nA to uA, essentially flat segment on the curve, drawn below the V axis"]
    end
    subgraph BREAKDOWN["BREAKDOWN REGION V less than or equal to Vz"]
        BK["Sharp knee at Vz, voltage remains almost constant, current Iz can swing from IzK to IzM, this is the USEFUL operating region for regulation"]
    end
    ORIGIN((Origin 0,0)) --> FORWARD
    ORIGIN --> REVERSE_PRE
    REVERSE_PRE --> BREAKDOWN

    classDef region fill:#e9f5db,stroke:#386641,color:#1b4332
    classDef knee fill:#ffd166,stroke:#cc8b00,color:#3a2e00
    class FORWARD,REVERSE_PRE region
    class BREAKDOWN knee
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Moving **right** along the horizontal axis = forward bias. After the cut-in voltage (~0.7 V for Si), current rises steeply.
- Moving **left** along the horizontal axis = reverse bias. A tiny saturation current flows until $V = -V_Z$ is reached.
- At $V = -V_Z$, the curve bends sharply into a nearly **vertical** segment — the *breakdown knee*. The voltage stays close to $V_Z$ while the current varies over a wide range.
- The usable *regulation region* is the vertical portion of the curve (between $I_{ZK}$ and $I_{ZM}$).

### 4.3 Energy-Band Picture — Tunnelling vs. Impact Ionisation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ZB["ZENER BREAKDOWN TUNNELLING"]
        direction LR
        PZ["p side valence band FULL of electrons"] -->|thin barrier quantum tunnel| NZ["n side conduction band EMPTY states"]
    end
    subgraph AB["AVALANCHE BREAKDOWN IMPACT IONISATION"]
        direction LR
        CB1["conduction band electron accelerated by E field"] -->|gains energy greater than Eg plus Eg| IMPACT[impact collision creates new electron hole pair]
        IMPACT --> CB2["two carriers now accelerated"]
        CB2 --> IMPACT2[second impact creates two more]
    end

    classDef zener fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#0d47a1
    classDef aval fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c,color:#bf360c
    class PZ,NZ zener
    class CB1,CB2,IMPACT,IMPACT2 aval
```

**Interpretation:**

- **Zener (tunnelling) channel:** an electron in the *filled* valence band on the p-side tunnels through a thin, tilted forbidden gap into an *empty* conduction-band state on the n-side. No energy exchange with the lattice is required; it is a *purely quantum* event.
- **Avalanche (impact) channel:** an electron in the conduction band is *accelerated by the field*, gains kinetic energy $\ge E_i$, and *knocks a bound electron* out of a covalent bond. One becomes two; two become four; the chain reaction is the "avalanche".

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Differentiate between Zener breakdown and Avalanche breakdown mechanisms in a p-n junction diode. Mention the approximate crossover voltage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Zener Breakdown | Avalanche Breakdown |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Quantum-mechanical **tunnelling** of valence-band electrons through a thin barrier | **Impact ionisation** — energetic carriers create new electron-hole pairs |
| Doping | Very heavy | Moderate to heavy |
| Depletion width | Very narrow | Wider |
| Voltage range | $V_Z < 5.6$ V | $V_Z > 5.6$ V |
| Temperature coefficient | Negative | Positive |

**Crossover voltage:** ≈ **5.6 V** for silicon.

*(Examiner's allocation: 1 mark mechanism, 1 mark doping/voltage, 1 mark crossover value.)*

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Draw and explain the V-I characteristics of a Zener diode. Mark the Zener voltage, knee current, and breakdown region.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Forward region** (right of origin): Identical to a normal Si diode, cut-in at ≈ 0.7 V, current rises exponentially.
- **Reverse blocking region** ($0$ to $-V_Z$): A small saturation current $I_S$ flows, nearly flat segment.
- **Breakdown region** (at and beyond $-V_Z$): Sharp *knee* at $V_Z$, followed by a near-vertical segment. Current $I_Z$ can increase from $I_{ZK}$ (knee current) to $I_{ZM}$ (max) with $V_Z$ essentially **constant**.
- The **breakdown region is the useful operating region** for voltage regulation.

*(Examiner's allocation: 1 mark axes/forward, 1 mark reverse blocking, 1 mark breakdown knee + labelled $V_Z$ and $I_{ZK}$.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Many students draw the *forward* characteristic (right side) correctly but forget to **explicitly mark $V_Z$ and $I_{ZK}$ on the reverse axis**. This loses at least **½ to 1 mark**. Always label the **knee point** as $(-V_Z, -I_{ZK})$ and indicate the operating range as $I_{ZK} \le I_Z \le I_{ZM}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *Zener Diode as Voltage Regulator*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**
**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a Zener diode as a **voltage regulator** in the reverse-bias breakdown region. Derive the expressions for the Zener current and the load current. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Zener diode with $V_Z = 12$ V and $P_{ZM} = 0.5$ W is used to regulate a DC input that varies from 15 V to 20 V. The load resistance is $R_L = 600\ \Omega$. Design a suitable series resistor $R_S$ and find the minimum and maximum Zener currents. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

- Draw the regulator circuit: $V_{in} \to R_S \to$ node → (Zener || $R_L$) → ground. **Neat diagram with arrow on Zener showing reverse bias direction.** (1 mark)
- **KVL:** $V_{in} = I_S R_S + V_Z \;\Rightarrow\; I_S = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_S}$. (1 mark)
- **KCL at output node:** $I_S = I_Z + I_L$. (1 mark)
- Hence $I_Z = I_S - I_L = \dfrac{V_{in} - V_Z}{R_S} - \dfrac{V_Z}{R_L}$. (1 mark)
- Working explanation: as $V_{in}$ rises, $I_S$ rises; the *extra* current flows through the Zener (not the load), so $V_{out} = V_Z$ stays constant. Similarly, if $R_L$ decreases, $I_L$ rises, $I_Z$ falls, $V_{out}$ unchanged. (2 marks)
- **Condition for regulation:** $I_{ZK} \le I_Z \le I_{ZM}$, where $I_{ZM} = P_{ZM}/V_Z$. (1 mark)

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Find the maximum Zener current from the power rating:**

$$I_{ZM} = \frac{P_{ZM}}{V_Z} = \frac{0.5\ \text{W}}{12\ \text{V}} = 41.67\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 2 — Find the load current (constant, since $V_L = V_Z$):**

$$I_L = \frac{V_Z}{R_L} = \frac{12\ \text{V}}{600\ \Omega} = 20\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 3 — Worst case for $R_S$ sizing** is the *smallest* $V_{in}$ combined with the *largest* allowable $I_Z$. The Zener must still stay in breakdown, so the *minimum* $I_S$ (at $V_{in,\min} = 15$ V) must satisfy $I_S \ge I_L$ (i.e., $I_Z \ge 0$ ideally, but for safe regulation, $I_Z \ge I_{ZK}$).

Assume $I_{ZK} \approx 0$ for simplicity (problem doesn't specify):

$$R_{S,\max} = \frac{V_{in,\min} - V_Z}{I_L + I_{ZK}} = \frac{15 - 12}{20\ \text{mA} + 0} = \frac{3}{0.020} = 150\ \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Worst case for $I_Z$** is the *largest* $V_{in}$ with the chosen $R_S$:

$$I_S^{\max} = \frac{V_{in,\max} - V_Z}{R_S} = \frac{20 - 12}{150} = \frac{8}{150} = 53.33\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 5 — Compute Zener current limits:**

- Maximum: $I_{Z,\max} = I_S^{\max} - I_L = 53.33 - 20 = 33.33$ mA.
- Minimum: $I_{Z,\min} = I_S^{\min} - I_L = 20 - 20 = 0$ mA (Zener just on the edge of breakdown).

**Step 6 — Verification** of thermal limit: $I_{Z,\max} = 33.33$ mA < $I_{ZM} = 41.67$ mA ✓ Safe.

**Answer:** $R_S = 150\ \Omega$ (½ W rating recommended), $I_{Z,\max} \approx 33.3$ mA, $I_{Z,\min} = 0$ mA.

*(Mark allocation: 1 mark $I_{ZM}$, 1 mark $I_L$, 2 marks $R_S$ calculation, 2 marks $I_Z$ limits, 1 mark verification.)*

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Breakdown Mechanisms in Detail*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**
**(a)** Discuss the **Zener (tunnelling) breakdown** mechanism in a heavily doped p-n junction. Explain why the depletion width must be small. Derive the condition for the critical electric field. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **avalanche (impact ionisation) breakdown** mechanism. Show that the breakdown voltage depends on doping and temperature. Compare the temperature coefficients of the two mechanisms. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a — 7 marks):**

- **Heavy doping** ⇒ depletion width $W$ is very small (of order nm). (1 mark)
- The **energy-band diagram** under reverse bias shows a thin, triangular potential barrier separating the filled valence band on the p-side from the empty conduction band on the n-side. (1 mark)
- According to **quantum mechanics**, a particle has a finite probability of *tunnelling* through a thin barrier. The Zener/Kane tunnelling probability is:

$$T \approx \exp\!\left[-\frac{\pi \sqrt{m^*}\,E_g^{3/2}}{2\sqrt{2}\,e\hbar E}\right]$$

- As the reverse bias increases, the field $E$ increases, $W$ decreases, and $T$ rises sharply. When $T$ reaches a critical value, a measurable current flows — this defines the breakdown. (1 mark)
- **Setting the argument = 1** (or using the empirical critical field for Si):

$$E_{crit}^{Zener} \approx 3 \times 10^7\ \text{V/m} \quad \text{for Si}$$

- **Why narrow $W$ is essential:** the tunnelling probability decreases *exponentially* with barrier width. For $W > 50$ nm, $T$ becomes negligibly small. Hence Zener breakdown requires heavy doping + thin depletion region. (1 mark)
- **Depletion-width derivation** (recall from §3.1):

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon_s(V_{bi}+V_R)}{eN_D}}$$

- Substituting into $E_{max} = eN_DW/\varepsilon_s$ and equating to $E_{crit}$:

$$V_Z = \frac{\varepsilon_s E_{crit}^2}{2 e N_D} - V_{bi}$$

- **Observation:** as $N_D$ *increases*, $V_Z$ *decreases* — this is why Zener diodes with low breakdown voltages require heavier doping. (1 mark)

**Model Solution (Part b — 7 marks):**

- **Avalanche breakdown** occurs when a carrier accelerated across the depletion region gains enough energy ($\ge E_i$, the ionisation energy) to create a new electron-hole pair by impact. (1 mark)
- The newly created carriers are also accelerated, creating further ionisations → **chain reaction (avalanche)**. (1 mark)
- The ionisation coefficient $\alpha_i$ (ionisations per unit length) depends strongly on the electric field:

$$\alpha_i(E) = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{B}{E}\right)$$

- The breakdown condition (multiplication factor $M \to \infty$):

$$\int_0^W \alpha_i(E)\,dx = 1$$

- **Dependence on doping:** Higher $N_D$ → narrower $W$ → need higher $V_R$ to reach the critical $E_{max}$ for ionisation. Hence:

$$V_{BR} \propto N_D^{\,a} \quad \text{with } a \approx 0.5 \text{ to } 0.75 \text{ (empirical)}$$

(1 mark)

- **Dependence on temperature:** As $T$ rises, the mean free path $\lambda$ decreases (more phonon scattering), so the carrier must traverse a longer distance to gain ionisation energy. This requires a **higher breakdown voltage**. Therefore $\alpha_{VZ} > 0$ for avalanche. (1 mark)
- **Comparison of temperature coefficients** (1 mark):

| Mechanism | $V_Z$ range | $\alpha_{VZ}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Zener (tunnelling) | $< 5.6$ V | **Negative** — at higher $T$, band gap $E_g$ shrinks, barrier becomes effectively thinner, tunnelling easier, $V_Z$ decreases. |
| Avalanche (impact) | $> 5.6$ V | **Positive** — phonon scattering reduces $\lambda$, needs higher $E$ ⇒ higher $V_Z$. |

- **Engineering implication:** at $V_Z \approx 5.6$ V, the two coefficients cancel → near-zero net temperature dependence. This is exploited in **precision reference diodes** (e.g., 1N829) that intentionally cascade a Zener and a forward-biased diode for temperature compensation. (1 mark)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Zener-diode questions:**
> 1. **Confusing forward and reverse operation** — students often describe forward bias when asked about breakdown. Always state: *"The Zener diode is reverse-biased for breakdown operation."*
> 2. **Forgetting to verify thermal limit** — in regulator design problems, after finding $I_{Z,\max}$, you MUST check $I_{Z,\max} \le I_{ZM} = P_{ZM}/V_Z$. Skipping this loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Stating "Zener breakdown happens at high voltage"** — this is *wrong*. Zener (tunnelling) breakdown dominates at **low** voltages (< 5.6 V). Avalanche dominates at high voltages.
> 4. **Omitting the crossover voltage ≈ 5.6 V** — a frequently tested value; not stating it loses 1 mark in a 3-mark question.
> 5. **Forgetting KCL / KVL derivations in regulator problems** — direct substitution of numbers without the underlying equations loses up to 2 marks in a 7-mark sub-part.
> 6. **Negative temperature coefficient sign** — many students write "temperature coefficient is positive" for Zener breakdown. The correct sign is **negative**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- A **Zener diode** is a **heavily doped** p-n junction diode designed to operate in **reverse-bias breakdown** as a voltage reference.
- The breakdown voltage is sharply defined and called the **Zener voltage $V_Z$**.
- Two physical mechanisms of breakdown exist:
  - **Zener (tunnelling) breakdown** — quantum-mechanical, dominates at **$V_Z < 5.6$ V**, **negative** temperature coefficient, very heavy doping, very narrow depletion width.
  - **Avalanche (impact-ionisation) breakdown** — classical, dominates at **$V_Z > 5.6$ V**, **positive** temperature coefficient, moderate-to-heavy doping.
- The **crossover voltage** for Si is **≈ 5.6 V**.
- The **depletion width** shrinks as doping increases:

$$W \propto \sqrt{\frac{V_R}{N_D}}$$

- The **peak electric field** at the junction is:

$$E_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 e N_D V_R}{\varepsilon_s}} \quad \text{(one-sided junction)}$$

- **Dynamic (Zener) impedance** $Z_Z = \Delta V_Z / \Delta I_Z$ is a figure of merit — lower is better for regulation.
- **Regulator design** steps:
  1. Compute $I_{ZM} = P_{ZM}/V_Z$.
  2. Compute load current $I_L = V_Z/R_L$.
  3. Size $R_S$ for worst-case $V_{in,\min}$: $R_S = (V_{in,\min} - V_Z)/(I_L + I_{ZK})$.
  4. Verify $I_{Z,\max} \le I_{ZM}$.
  5. Check power rating of $R_S$: $P_{R_S} = I_S^2 R_S$.
- **Applications**: voltage regulators, clippers, clampers, reference sources, over-voltage protection.
- **KTU keyword triggers** to expect in questions: *"breakdown mechanism"*, *"crossover voltage"*, *"V-I characteristic"*, *"voltage regulator"*, *"temperature coefficient"*, *"dynamic impedance"*.
- **Typical numerical values to remember**: $E_g^{Si} = 1.12$ eV, $E_{crit}^{Zener} \approx 3 \times 10^7$ V/m, $V_{cross} \approx 5.6$ V.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tunnel Diode - V-I Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Tunnel Diode - V-I Characteristics** # SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Tunnel Diode** (also known as the **Esaki Diode**, named after Japanese physicist **Leo Esaki** who discovered it in 1957 and was awarded the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1973) is a heavily doped **p-n junction diode** that exhibits **quantum mechanical tunneling** of charge carriers through a narrow depletion barrier, resulting in a unique **negative differential resistance (NDR)** region in its forward V-I characteristics. > [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight** > The doping concentration in a tunnel diode is extraordinarily high — typically **1 part in 10³** atoms (compared to **1 part in 10⁸** in ordinary diodes). This produces an extremely thin depletion region (≈ **10 nm** or less), enabling electrons to "tunnel" through the potential barrier rather than climbing over it thermally. > [!NOTE] **Key Distinguishing Feature** > The Tunnel Diode is the only **two-terminal** passive device that intrinsically exhibits **negative resistance** over a controlled voltage range. This single property powers its use in oscillators, amplifiers, and ultra-high-speed switching circuits operating into the **microwave region (GHz range)**. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Tunnel Under the Wall" Imagine a thick, tall brick wall separating two courtyards — one filled with eager football players (electrons in the n-side) and the other empty (p-side). Normally, the players can only reach the other side by throwing the ball **over** the wall (which requires enormous energy — this is classical drift over the barrier). Now, suppose the wall becomes **extremely thin** (almost paper-thin, ~10 nm). Quantum mechanics says that the football players can now **"tunnel through"** the wall from one side to the other, without needing to climb over it. This is **classically forbidden** but **quantum-mechanically allowed** because the electron's wavefunction does not abruptly stop at the barrier — it has a non-zero probability of being found on the other side. > [!TIP] **Plain-English Summary** > **Heavy doping → Thin barrier → Quantum tunneling → Negative resistance → Ultra-fast switching.** That is the entire physics of the tunnel diode in one line. ### Why Negative Resistance Occurs - As forward voltage increases, the filled states on the n-side first align with empty states on the p-side → **tunnel current rises** (peak region). - Further increase misaligns the bands → **fewer filled states face empty states** → current **decreases** (valley region — *negative differential resistance*). - Beyond the valley, ordinary **diffusion current** (normal diode behavior) takes over and current rises again. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | |---|---|---| | Doping concentration | Nᴅ, Nᴬ | **10¹⁹ to 10²⁰ cm⁻³** | | Depletion width | W | **≈ 5–10 nm** | | Peak voltage | Vₚ | **≈ 50–100 mV** | | Valley voltage | Vᵥ | **≈ 400–600 mV** | | Peak current | Iₚ | **≈ 1–10 mA** | | Valley current | Iᵥ | **≈ 0.1–1 mA** | | Peak-to-Valley Current Ratio (PVCR) | Iₚ/Iᵥ | **≈ 8–15** | | Switching speed | — | **< 1 ns** | | Operating temperature range | T | **–40 °C to +200 °C** | # SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Operating Principle: Quantum Tunneling Explained The tunnel diode operation is best understood by tracking the **relative position of the Fermi levels** in the n-side and p-side as external bias changes. Because the doping is so heavy, the **Fermi level lies inside the conduction band** on the n-side and **inside the valence band** on the p-side (degenerate semiconductor). ### The Four Critical Bias Regimes **Stage 0 — Zero Bias (Thermal Equilibrium)** - The Fermi levels of n-side and p-side are aligned. - The filled states on the n-side conduction band face **forbidden gap** states on the p-side. - **Net tunneling current = 0** (electron flow n→p equals p→n). **Stage 1 — Small Forward Bias (Rising to Peak, 0 < V < Vₚ)** - The n-side Fermi level rises relative to the p-side. - Filled states in the n-side conduction band begin to align with **empty states in the p-side valence band** at the same energy. - **Tunneling current increases rapidly** → reaches **peak current Iₚ** at **peak voltage Vₚ**. **Stage 2 — Intermediate Forward Bias (Vₚ < V < Vᵥ) — NEGATIVE RESISTANCE REGION** - The bands shift further; the alignment of filled vs empty states worsens. - The number of available tunneling states **decreases** with increasing voltage. - **Tunnel current drops** from Iₚ to **valley current Iᵥ** at **valley voltage Vᵥ**. - Slope **dI/dV < 0** → this is the famous **negative differential resistance (NDR)** region. **Stage 3 — Large Forward Bias (V > Vᵥ)** - The bands are now offset such that the conduction band of n-side lines up with the conduction band of p-side. - Tunneling essentially stops; **ordinary diffusion (Shockley) current** dominates. - The V-I curve reverts to that of a **conventional p-n junction diode**. ## 2.2 Why Tunneling Happens: The Physics in Brief The **tunneling probability (transmission coefficient T)** through a potential barrier of height V₀ and width W, for an electron of energy E < V₀, is given by the **WKB approximation**: $$ T \approx \exp\!\left(-2 \int_{0}^{W} \vert k(x) \vert \, dx\right) $$ For a **rectangular barrier** of height V₀ and width W, this simplifies to: $$ T \approx \exp\!\left(- \frac{2}{\hbar}\,\sqrt{2 m^{*}\,(V_{0} - E)}\,\,W\right) $$ where: - $m^{*}$ = effective mass of the electron - $\hbar$ = reduced Planck's constant - $V_0 - E$ = barrier height above electron energy - $W$ = barrier width (≈ depletion width) > [!IMPORTANT] **Key Insight** > The tunneling current is **exponentially sensitive** to the depletion width W. This is why **heavy doping (W ≈ 10 nm)** is the *sine qua non* of tunnel diode operation. If W were just 30 nm instead of 10 nm, the tunneling current would drop by orders of magnitude and the device would behave like an ordinary diode. --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Expression | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $W = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\varepsilon V_{0}}{q}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{N_{A}}+\dfrac{1}{N_{D}}\right)}$ | Depletion width of p-n junction | m | | 2 | $T \approx \exp\!\left(- \frac{2}{\hbar}\sqrt{2m^{*}(V_0 - E)}\,W\right)$ | Tunneling transmission coefficient (WKB approx.) | dimensionless | | 3 | $J_{tunnel} \propto T \cdot N_{tunnel}$ | Tunnel current density depends on T and density of available states | A/m² | | 4 | $R_{d} = \dfrac{dV}{dI}$ | Differential resistance of the diode | Ω | | 5 | $R_{n} = \dfrac{V_{v} - V_{p}}{I_{p} - I_{v}}$ | **Negative resistance** magnitude in the NDR region | Ω | | 6 | $PVCR = \dfrac{I_{p}}{I_{v}}$ | Peak-to-Valley Current Ratio (figure of merit) | dimensionless | | 7 | $I_{p} \approx \dfrac{q\,A\,N_{D}\,v_{th}}{2}$ (order-of-magnitude) | Peak current estimate | A | | 8 | $E_{g} \approx 0.7$ eV (Ge), $1.1$ eV (Si), $1.4$ eV (GaAs) | Common semiconductor bandgaps | eV | | 9 | $v_{th} = \sqrt{\dfrac{3k_{B}T}{m^{*}}}$ | Thermal velocity of carriers | m/s | | 10 | $f_{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{n} C_{j}}$ | Maximum oscillation frequency | Hz | > [!NOTE] > **PVCR (Peak-to-Valley Current Ratio)** is the single most quoted figure of merit for tunnel diodes. Higher PVCR means more pronounced negative resistance → better oscillator/amplifier performance. Germanium tunnel diodes historically had the highest PVCR; modern **III-V** heterostructure tunnel diodes (e.g., InGaAs/InP Esaki diodes) push PVCR > 20. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Microwave Oscillators**: Tunnel diodes generate stable oscillations up to **100 GHz**, used in radar, satellite communication, and local oscillators. - **High-Speed Switching**: Switching times below **1 ns** make tunnel diodes suitable for fast logic circuits, pulse generators, and trigger circuits. - **Amplifiers**: Low-noise parametric amplifiers in microwave receivers. - **Resonant Tunneling Diodes (RTDs)**: Modern nanoscale descendants used in **terahertz (THz) imaging** and **quantum cascade lasers**. - **Memory Elements**: Used in early **memristor** research and bistable memory circuits exploiting the NDR region. # SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Depletion Width of a Heavily Doped p-n Junction We begin with Poisson's equation in 1-D for the depletion region, with charge density $\rho = q(N_D - N_A)$ approximated as a step function (abrupt junction). **Step 1 — Poisson's equation in the n-side depletion region ($-x_n \le x \le 0$):** $$ \frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{q N_{D}}{\varepsilon} $$ **Step 2 — Integrate once with boundary condition $dV/dx = 0$ at $x = -x_n$:** $$ \frac{dV}{dx} = -\frac{q N_{D}}{\varepsilon}(x + x_{n}) $$ **Step 3 — Apply charge neutrality:** $N_{D}\,x_{n} = N_{A}\,x_{p}$, so the total depletion width is $W = x_{n} + x_{p}$. After applying the boundary condition that $V = 0$ at the edge of the depletion region and $V = V_0$ (built-in potential) at the metallurgical junction, integration yields: $$ V_{0} = \frac{q}{2\varepsilon}\!\left(N_{D}\,x_{n}^{2} + N_{A}\,x_{p}^{2}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Substitute $x_{n} = W \cdot N_{A}/(N_{A}+N_{D})$ and $x_{p} = W \cdot N_{D}/(N_{A}+N_{D})$:** $$ V_{0} = \frac{q\,W^{2}}{2\varepsilon}\!\left(\frac{N_{A}\,N_{D}}{N_{A}+N_{D}}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Solve for W (the standard textbook result):** $$ \boxed{\,W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon V_{0}}{q}\!\left(\frac{1}{N_{A}}+\frac{1}{N_{D}}\right)}\,} $$ **Numerical Illustration (typical Tunnel Diode parameters):** Take $\varepsilon = 11.7\,\varepsilon_{0} = 1.04\times 10^{-10}$ F/m (Silicon), $V_0 = 0.9$ V, $N_A = N_D = 5 \times 10^{19}$ cm⁻³ = $5 \times 10^{25}$ m⁻³, and $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C. $$ W = \sqrt{\frac{2(1.04\times 10^{-10})(0.9)}{1.6\times 10^{-19}}\times\frac{2}{5\times 10^{25}}} $$ $$ W = \sqrt{(1.17\times 10^{9})\times(4\times 10^{-26})} $$ $$ W = \sqrt{4.68\times 10^{-17}} \approx 6.84\times 10^{-9}\text{ m} = 6.84\text{ nm} $$ > [!TIP] **Interpretation** > This width (~**7 nm**) is comparable to the de Broglie wavelength of thermal electrons at room temperature (~ **10 nm**). This is precisely why tunneling dominates over thermionic emission in this device. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Tunneling Transmission Coefficient (Square Barrier, WKB Approximation) **Step 1 — Time-independent Schrödinger equation inside a constant potential region V₀:** $$ -\frac{\hbar^{2}}{2m^{*}}\frac{d^{2}\psi}{dx^{2}} + V_{0}\psi = E\psi $$ Rearranging: $$ \frac{d^{2}\psi}{dx^{2}} = \frac{2m^{*}(V_{0}-E)}{\hbar^{2}}\,\psi = \kappa^{2}\psi $$ where $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m^{*}(V_{0}-E)}}{\hbar}$ (real, since $E < V_0$ inside the barrier). **Step 2 — General solution inside the barrier (0 ≤ x ≤ W):** $$ \psi(x) = A\,e^{\kappa x} + B\,e^{-\kappa x} $$ **Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions: at $x = 0$, $\psi(0) = 1$ (incoming wave normalized), and at $x = W$, only the transmitted wave survives.** With $A = 1, B = e^{-2\kappa W}$ matching the wavefunction and its derivative: $$ T \approx e^{-2\kappa W} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{2W}{\hbar}\sqrt{2m^{*}(V_{0}-E)}\right) $$ $$ \boxed{\,T \approx \exp\!\left(-\frac{2W}{\hbar}\sqrt{2m^{*}(V_{0}-E)}\right)\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > This result is approximate (WKB); exact solutions for rectangular barriers use hyperbolic sine functions, but the exponential dependence on $W$ and $\sqrt{V_0 - E}$ is the same. The crucial point is the **exponential sensitivity** to barrier width — halving W increases T by many orders of magnitude. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Negative Differential Resistance in the NDR Region The differential resistance at any operating point is defined as: $$ R_{d} = \frac{dV}{dI} $$ In the NDR region (between $V_p$ and $V_v$), the V-I curve has a **negative slope** ($dI/dV < 0$), which means: $$ R_{d} = \frac{dV}{dI} < 0 $$ The **magnitude** of the negative resistance, useful for circuit design, is given by the **secant line** between the peak and valley points: $$ R_{n} = -\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} = \frac{V_{v} - V_{p}}{I_{p} - I_{v}} $$ **Derivation from the data points:** Let $V_p = 60$ mV, $V_v = 500$ mV, $I_p = 5$ mA, $I_v = 0.5$ mA. $$ R_{n} = \frac{0.500 - 0.060}{0.005 - 0.0005} = \frac{0.440}{0.0045} $$ $$ R_{n} \approx 97.8 \;\Omega $$ This negative resistance **exactly cancels** the positive parasitic resistance of the surrounding circuit, enabling sustained oscillations in an LC tank: $$ f_{osc} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} $$ provided $\vert R_n \vert \ge R_{parasitic}$. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix (V-I Curve Region by Region) | Region | Voltage Range | Current Behavior | Dominant Physical Mechanism | Sign of dI/dV | |---|---|---|---|---| | **0 → Peak** | $0 \to V_p$ | Rises sharply to $I_p$ | Tunneling — increasing band alignment | **Positive** | | **Peak → Valley (NDR)** | $V_p \to V_v$ | Falls from $I_p$ to $I_v$ | Tunneling — decreasing band alignment | **Negative** | | **Valley → Forward knee** | $V_v \to V_{\gamma}$ | Rises slowly | Tunneling ends; diffusion begins | **Positive (small)** | | **Beyond knee** | $V > V_{\gamma}$ | Rises exponentially | Ordinary Shockley diffusion current | **Positive (large)** | | **Reverse bias** | $V < 0$ | Rises sharply | Reverse tunneling (Zener-like) | **Positive (large)** | # SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 4.1 Energy Band Diagram Evolution with Forward Bias The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the four critical biasing states, the energy-band alignment, and the resulting tunneling current behavior. This is the KTU board examiner's "go-to" diagram for this topic. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["STAGE 0: Zero Bias
Fermi Levels Aligned
Net Current = 0"] A1["STAGE 1: Small Forward Bias
V less than Vp
Filled n-states align with empty p-states
Tunnel current RISES"] A2["STAGE 2: Intermediate Forward Bias
Vp less than V less than Vv
NDR REGION
Filled states no longer face empty states
Tunnel current FALLS"] A3["STAGE 3: Large Forward Bias
V greater than Vv
Diffusion current dominates
Behaves like ordinary diode"] R0["I = 0
Equilibrium"] R1["Current rises to Ip
at V = Vp"] R2["Current falls to Iv
NEGATIVE RESISTANCE"] R3["Current rises exponentially
Standard diode"] A0 --> R0 A1 --> R1 A2 --> R2 A3 --> R3 A0 ==> A1 A1 ==> A2 A2 ==> A3 style A0 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000 style A1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,color:#000 style A2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#000 style A3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style R0 fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,color:#000 style R1 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 style R2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 style R3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#BF360C,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Tunnel Diode in an Oscillator Circuit The following diagram shows the **functional architecture** of a tunnel-diode-based negative-resistance oscillator — a common KTU question stem. ```mermaid flowchart LR DC["DC Bias Source
Vbias between Vp and Vv"] --> TUNNEL[("Tunnel Diode
Negative Resistance Rn")] TUNNEL --> LC["LC Tank Circuit
L in parallel with C"] LC --> OUTPUT["AC Output
Sinusoidal Oscillation"] OUTPUT --> FBACK["Feedback Path
Maintains Oscillation"] TUNNEL -. "Cancels parasitic R" .-> LC FBACK -. "Sustained oscillation
freq = 1 by 2pi sqrt LC" .-> TUNNEL style DC fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000 style TUNNEL fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000 style LC fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 style OUTPUT fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style FBACK fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 V-I Characteristics — Schematic Plot (ASCII Rendering) The figure below depicts the **complete V-I curve** of a tunnel diode. Forward bias is to the right, reverse bias to the left. ``` I (mA) ^ | 5.0 | . . . . . . . . . . . . | . . (Ordinary 4.0 | . Ip = 5 mA . diffusion | / \\ . region) 3.0 | / \\ . | / \\ . 2.0 | / \\ . | / \\ . 1.0 | / \\ . | / \\ . Iv = 0.5 mA 0 |--/---------------\\--/----------------- V (mV) | 0 50 100 250 500 (Forward) -2.0 |- - | \\ / -4.0 | \\ / | \\ / -6.0 | \\____________________________/ | Reverse tunnel current (Zener-like) +-----------------------------------------> V (V) ``` **Key feature callouts:** | Label | Coordinates | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Origin (0, 0)** | $V = 0, I = 0$ | Zero bias, equilibrium | | **Peak Point P** | $V_p \approx 60$ mV, $I_p \approx 5$ mA | Maximum tunneling current | | **Valley Point V** | $V_v \approx 500$ mV, $I_v \approx 0.5$ mA | Minimum current after NDR | | **Forward Knee** | $V \approx V_v$ onwards | Transition to normal diode | | **NDR Region** | Between P and V | **Slope is negative** | | **Reverse Region** | $V < 0$ | Sharp current rise due to reverse tunneling | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] Define a tunnel diode. Why is the depletion width in a tunnel diode extremely small compared to an ordinary p-n junction diode? **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **tunnel diode** is a heavily doped p-n junction diode in which the depletion region is so thin (~**10 nm**) that charge carriers can **quantum-mechanically tunnel** through the potential barrier, producing a region of **negative differential resistance** in the forward V-I characteristics. The depletion width is given by: $$ W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon V_{0}}{q}\!\left(\frac{1}{N_{A}}+\frac{1}{N_{D}}\right)} $$ Since $W \propto 1/\sqrt{N}$, the doping concentration in tunnel diodes ($10^{19}$ to $10^{20}$ cm⁻³) is **about a million times** higher than in ordinary diodes ($10^{14}$ to $10^{16}$ cm⁻³). This reduces the depletion width from ~**1 μm** in ordinary diodes to ~**10 nm** in tunnel diodes — small enough for significant quantum tunneling. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] What is meant by **negative differential resistance (NDR)** in a tunnel diode? Write the expression for the negative resistance. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Negative differential resistance (NDR)** refers to the operating region in the V-I characteristics of a tunnel diode where the current **decreases** with **increasing** voltage, giving a negative slope ($dI/dV < 0$). This occurs between the **peak point (Vₚ, Iₚ)** and the **valley point (Vᵥ, Iᵥ)**. The magnitude of the negative resistance is given by the secant slope: $$ R_{n} = \frac{V_{v} - V_{p}}{I_{p} - I_{v}} $$ This NDR property allows the tunnel diode to be used as an **amplifier, oscillator, and high-speed switching element**. **(3 marks)** --- ## PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and is typically divided into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each** (e.g., part (a) and part (b)). You are given an **internal choice**: attempt **either Option A OR Option B** completely. --- ### ⭐ **OPTION A (14 Marks)** #### **Q3(a).** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] With the help of energy band diagrams, explain the working principle of a tunnel diode in **four distinct forward-bias regions** and identify the region of negative differential resistance. **Model Answer:** The tunnel diode operation is explained using the relative positions of the **conduction band (E_C)**, **valence band (E_V)**, and **Fermi levels (E_Fn on n-side, E_Fp on p-side)** of the heavily doped degenerate semiconductor. **Region 1: Zero Bias (Equilibrium)** - $E_{Fn} = E_{Fp}$ - The conduction band of the n-side is filled up to $E_{Fn}$ (which lies *inside* the conduction band due to heavy doping). - The valence band of the p-side is filled up to $E_{Fp}$ (which lies *inside* the valence band). - Filled n-side conduction band states face the **forbidden gap** of the p-side. - **Net tunneling current = 0.** **Region 2: Small Forward Bias (0 < V < Vₚ)** - The n-side bands shift down relative to the p-side. - Filled states in the n-side conduction band now begin to align with **empty states in the p-side valence band** at the same energy level. - Electrons tunnel from n to p → **tunnel current rises sharply** with voltage. - At $V = V_p$, maximum alignment occurs → **peak current $I_p$** flows. **Region 3: Intermediate Forward Bias (Vₚ < V < Vᵥ) — NEGATIVE RESISTANCE REGION** - Further forward bias shifts the n-side bands even lower. - The previously aligned filled states now face the **forbidden gap** of the p-side again. - **Number of available tunneling channels decreases** as voltage increases. - Current **falls** from $I_p$ to the **valley current $I_v$** at $V = V_v$. - This is the **NDR region**: $\dfrac{dI}{dV} < 0$. **(4 marks for band diagrams + 3 marks for explanation)** **Region 4: Large Forward Bias (V > Vᵥ)** - The n-side conduction band aligns with the p-side conduction band. - Tunneling effectively ceases; the **ordinary diffusion current** (Shockley equation) takes over. - The diode behaves like a **conventional p-n junction**. **Conclusion:** The NDR region between Vₚ and Vᵥ is the unique feature of the tunnel diode and is the basis of its applications in oscillators and high-speed switches. **(7 marks total)** --- #### **Q3(b).** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] A germanium tunnel diode has the following parameters: $V_p = 60$ mV, $I_p = 5$ mA, $V_v = 500$ mV, $I_v = 0.5$ mA. Calculate (i) the peak-to-valley current ratio (PVCR), (ii) the magnitude of the negative resistance, and (iii) the maximum oscillation frequency if the junction capacitance is $C_j = 10$ pF. **Model Answer:** **(i) Peak-to-Valley Current Ratio (PVCR):** $$ PVCR = \frac{I_{p}}{I_{v}} = \frac{5 \text{ mA}}{0.5 \text{ mA}} = 10 $$ **(ii) Magnitude of Negative Resistance $R_n$:** $$ R_{n} = \frac{V_{v} - V_{p}}{I_{p} - I_{v}} = \frac{(500 - 60)\text{ mV}}{(5 - 0.5)\text{ mA}} = \frac{440 \text{ mV}}{4.5 \text{ mA}} $$ $$ R_{n} \approx 97.78 \;\Omega $$ **(iii) Maximum Oscillation Frequency $f_{max}$:** $$ f_{max} = \frac{1}{2\pi R_{n} C_{j}} = \frac{1}{2\pi (97.78)(10\times 10^{-12})} $$ $$ f_{max} = \frac{1}{6.144\times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.628 \times 10^{8} \text{ Hz} \approx \textbf{162.8 MHz} $$ **Results:** PVCR = **10**, $R_n \approx$ **97.78 Ω**, $f_{max} \approx$ **162.8 MHz**. **(7 marks: 2 + 3 + 2)** --- ### ⭐ **OPTION B (14 Marks)** — *Internal Alternative* #### **Q4(a).** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] Derive the expression for the depletion width of an abrupt p-n junction and show that heavy doping reduces the depletion width to a few nanometers in a tunnel diode. **Model Answer:** Starting from Poisson's equation in the depletion region: $$ \frac{d^{2}V}{dx^{2}} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon} $$ For the n-side depletion region ($-x_n \le x \le 0$), the charge density is $\rho = q N_D$ (ionized donors). Integrating twice with the boundary conditions $dV/dx = 0$ at $x = -x_n$ and continuity of potential at the junction, we obtain: $$ V_{0} - V_{app} = \frac{q}{2\varepsilon}\!\left(N_{D}\,x_{n}^{2} + N_{A}\,x_{p}^{2}\right) $$ Using charge neutrality $N_D x_n = N_A x_p$ and the identity $W = x_n + x_p$, we get: $$ V_{0} - V_{app} = \frac{q W^{2}}{2\varepsilon} \cdot \frac{N_{A} N_{D}}{N_{A} + N_{D}} $$ Solving for W: $$ \boxed{\,W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon (V_{0} - V_{app})}{q}\!\left(\frac{1}{N_{A}}+\frac{1}{N_{D}}\right)}\,} $$ **Numerical verification (silicon tunnel diode):** With $N_A = N_D = 10^{19}$ cm⁻³, $\varepsilon = 11.7\,\varepsilon_0$, $V_0 - V_{app} \approx 0.9$ V: $$ W \approx \sqrt{\frac{2(11.7 \times 8.85\times10^{-12})(0.9)}{1.6\times 10^{-19}}\times \frac{2}{10^{25}}} $$ $$ W \approx 1.55 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m} = 15.5 \text{ nm} $$ (For higher doping $10^{20}$ cm⁻³, this drops to ≈ **5 nm**.) This is small enough to permit significant quantum tunneling. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Q4(b).** [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] Sketch and explain the V-I characteristics of a tunnel diode. Mark the peak point, valley point, and the negative resistance region. List **four applications** of tunnel diodes. **Model Answer:** **V-I Characteristics (Sketch Description):** The V-I curve of a tunnel diode has the following features: 1. In **reverse bias**, the current rises sharply (Zener-like reverse tunneling) and the diode acts as an excellent low-voltage rectifier. 2. In **small forward bias** (0 to Vₚ), the current rises rapidly to a **peak current Iₚ** at a low **peak voltage Vₚ** (≈ 50–100 mV). 3. Between Vₚ and Vᵥ, the current **decreases** as voltage increases — this is the **NDR region**. 4. At Vᵥ, the current reaches a minimum **valley current Iᵥ**. 5. Beyond Vᵥ, the current rises again like a **normal p-n junction diode**, following the diffusion equation $I = I_0(e^{qV/kT} - 1)$. **(4 marks for sketch + explanation)** **Four Applications:** 1. **Microwave Oscillators:** Using the NDR region in a resonant LC circuit to generate high-frequency oscillations (up to 100 GHz). 2. **High-Speed Switching Circuits:** Sub-nanosecond switching speeds make tunnel diodes ideal for fast logic and pulse circuits. 3. **Low-Noise Microwave Amplifiers:** The NDR property enables amplification of weak microwave signals. 4. **Frequency Converters and Mixers:** Used in radio and radar front-ends for signal mixing. 5. *(Bonus — any 4 of the above)* **(3 marks for applications)** --- ## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing "negative resistance" with "negative current."** The current itself is **positive** in the NDR region; only the **slope $dI/dV$** is negative. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this error. > > 2. **Forgetting to mention "quantum mechanical tunneling"** in any definition. The phrase is **mandatory** — without it, the answer is incomplete. > > 3. **Wrong formula for PVCR.** PVCR = $I_p/I_v$ (current ratio, **not** voltage ratio). Mixing these up costs 2 marks. > > 4. **Drawing the V-I curve without marking the NDR region** explicitly with arrows or shading. Always mark $V_p$, $I_p$, $V_v$, $I_v$ and the NDR region on the sketch. > > 5. **Mixing up tunnel diode and Zener diode.** Both involve tunneling, but a Zener diode is a **voltage regulator** in reverse breakdown, while a tunnel diode exploits **NDR in forward bias** for switching/oscillation. > > 6. **Skipping the energy band diagrams** in derivation questions. Band diagrams are worth **4–5 marks** in any 14-mark question on this topic. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER - **Tunnel Diode = Heavily doped p-n junction (Esaki diode)**, Nobel Prize 1973 (Leo Esaki, 1957 discovery). - **Depletion width** in tunnel diode: **≈ 5–15 nm** (vs. ~1 μm in ordinary diodes). - **Mechanism:** Quantum mechanical tunneling of electrons through a thin barrier. - **Doping required:** $10^{19}$ to $10^{20}$ cm⁻³ — degenerate semiconductor. - **Four operating regions** of forward V-I curve: 1. Rising tunnel current (0 → $V_p$) 2. **NDR region** ($V_p$ → $V_v$): $\frac{dI}{dV} < 0$ 3. Valley (minimum current $I_v$) 4. Normal diffusion current (beyond $V_v$) - **Peak Voltage $V_p$:** ≈ **50–100 mV** (very low!). - **Valley Voltage $V_v$:** ≈ **400–600 mV**. - **PVCR** $= I_p / I_v$ — typical value **5–15**, sometimes up to 20+ in III-V devices. - **Negative Resistance Magnitude:** $R_n = (V_v - V_p)/(I_p - I_v)$. - **Key formula:** $W \propto 1/\sqrt{N}$ — **doubling doping → W drops by factor $\sqrt{2}$**. - **Tunneling transmission coefficient:** $T \approx \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2W}{\hbar}\sqrt{2m^{*}(V_0 - E)}\right)$ — exponentially sensitive to W. - **Switching speed:** sub-nanosecond; **oscillation frequency:** up to **100 GHz**. - **Applications:** Microwave oscillators, high-speed switches, low-noise amplifiers, frequency converters, resonant tunneling devices (THz). - **Why heavy doping?** Heavy doping → thin depletion width → high tunneling probability → NDR behavior. Without heavy doping, no tunneling, no NDR, no tunnel diode. - **Energy band diagram is the heart of any 14-mark answer** on this topic — always draw and label all four biasing stages. - **The NDR region is the "soul" of the device** — without it, the tunnel diode would just be an ordinary diode with a low turn-on voltage.

topics.title: Applications of Zener and Tunnel Diodes, Photonic Devices (Qualitative), Photodetectors (Junction and PIN Photodiodes)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications of Zener and Tunnel Diodes, Photonic Devices (Qualitative), Photodetectors (Junction and PIN Photodiodes)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Zener Diode

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Term):**
A **Zener Diode** is a heavily doped, reverse-biased p–n junction diode specifically engineered to operate reliably in the **breakdown region** of its V–I characteristic. It exhibits a controlled, sharp, and nearly vertical reverse-bias breakdown at a precisely defined voltage called the **Zener Voltage (V_Z)**, above which the voltage across the device remains essentially constant despite large changes in reverse current.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:** "A Zener diode is a silicon p–n junction device optimized for stable voltage reference operation in the reverse-breakdown regime, exploiting either Zener breakdown (tunneling, V_Z < 5.8 V) or Avalanche breakdown (impact ionization, V_Z > 5.8 V)."

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Pressure Relief Valve":**
Imagine a water tap connected to a high-pressure pipeline. Below a set pressure, the valve is shut and holds the line steady. Once the pressure crosses the threshold, the valve opens and *bleeds off any extra fluid*, keeping the downstream pressure locked exactly at the threshold. A Zener diode behaves identically for **electrical pressure (voltage)**: in reverse bias, when the voltage tries to exceed V_Z, the diode conducts heavily and *clamps* the voltage across it to V_Z, regardless of how much current flows.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Zener diodes are the *workhorse* of analog voltage regulation circuits, waveform clippers, and over-voltage protection modules in KTU lab experiments and power supplies.

---

### 1.2 The Tunnel Diode

**Formal Definition:**
A **Tunnel Diode** (Esaki Diode) is an *extremely heavily doped* p–n junction diode (doping ≈ 10²⁵ m⁻³) where the depletion width is so thin (≈ 10 nm) that **quantum mechanical tunneling** of charge carriers through the potential barrier becomes the dominant conduction mechanism. It exhibits a characteristic **negative differential resistance (NDR)** region in its forward V–I curve.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Quantum Ghost Doorway":**
Imagine two adjacent rooms separated by a very thin wall. Classically, you cannot walk through the wall. But if the wall is thin *enough* (≈ 10 nm), quantum mechanics says a particle can "teleport" (tunnel) through the wall with finite probability. In the tunnel diode, electrons in the conduction band of the n-side can *tunnel directly* into empty states in the valence band of the p-side, producing a current that first **rises**, then **falls** (negative resistance), and finally rises again as normal diffusion takes over.

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR):** A region where dI/dV < 0, meaning *increasing* voltage produces *decreasing* current. This is the key property exploited in oscillators and high-speed switching.

---

### 1.3 Photonic Devices (Qualitative)

**Formal Definition:**
**Photonic devices** are semiconductor components that **generate, detect, modulate, or transmit light (photons)** through controlled electron–photon interactions. Photonics deals with the *quantum unit of light* — the photon — analogous to how electronics deals with electrons.

**Three Primary Physical Processes:**

1. **Absorption** — A photon is absorbed; its energy excites an electron from the valence band to the conduction band.
2. **Spontaneous Emission** — An excited electron drops to a lower energy state, *randomly* emitting a photon.
3. **Stimulated Emission** — An incoming photon *triggers* an excited electron to drop, releasing a *second identical* photon (the foundation of LASERs and optical amplifiers).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a photon of energy hν to be absorbed, the photon energy must satisfy: **hν ≥ E_g** (bandgap). This is the fundamental *wavelength selection rule* for any photonic semiconductor device.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The 3-Way Light Switch":**
- **Absorption** is like someone walking into a dark room and *switching on the light* (energy absorbed, electron excited).
- **Spontaneous emission** is like the light *flickering off by itself* (electron falls, photon released randomly).
- **Stimulated emission** is like a friend clapping in rhythm, making the light *flash in sync* (one photon causes another identical one).

---

### 1.4 Photodetectors — Junction and PIN Photodiodes

**Formal Definition (Junction Photodiode):**
A **Junction Photodiode** is a reverse-biased p–n junction device that converts incident optical (light) energy into electrical current through the **photovoltaic / photoconductive effect**. Photons with energy hν ≥ E_g generate electron–hole pairs in or near the depletion region; the built-in and applied fields sweep the carriers to produce a measurable photocurrent.

**Formal Definition (PIN Photodiode):**
A **PIN Photodiode** inserts a wide, *intrinsic* (undoped, nearly pure semiconductor) layer between the p and n regions, denoted **P — Intrinsic — N**. This intrinsic layer:
- **Widens the depletion region** across the whole intrinsic zone, dramatically increasing the absorption volume.
- **Reduces junction capacitance**, enabling ultra-high-speed response (GHz range).
- **Improves quantum efficiency** at long wavelengths.

> [!NOTE]
> **Junction Photodiode vs. PIN Photodiode — Quick Distinction:**
> - **Junction Photodiode:** Simple p–n structure; depletion width ≈ μm; moderate speed; used in low-cost light sensing.
> - **PIN Photodiode:** p–i–n structure with intrinsic drift region; depletion width ≈ 10–100 μm; high speed (ns–ps); used in optical fiber communication.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Sunken Net":**
- A **junction photodiode** is like a small fishing net cast in shallow water — it catches some fish (photons) but misses many that swim past.
- A **PIN photodiode** is like a *massive deep-sea net* dropped in the ocean path — the wide intrinsic region acts as a large "catching zone" that traps almost every photon that enters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The intrinsic layer in a PIN diode is *not* a true insulator; it has a very high but finite resistivity. The applied reverse bias sweeps out carriers from this region, creating a *uniform high-field drift zone*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Zener Diode — Operating Regimes and Applications

#### 2.1.1 Two Breakdown Mechanisms

| Mechanism | Dominant When | Physical Cause | Voltage Range |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Zener Breakdown** | Very heavy doping, narrow depletion | **Quantum tunneling** of electrons from valence band (p-side) to conduction band (n-side) through the thin barrier | V_Z < 5.8 V |
| **Avalanche Breakdown** | Light-to-moderate doping | **Impact ionization**: carriers gain KE from field, collide with lattice, generate new e–h pairs (chain reaction) | V_Z > 5.8 V |

> [!NOTE]
> At V_Z ≈ 5.8 V, both mechanisms contribute equally. This crossover is a **KTU favorite conceptual question**.

#### 2.1.2 Zener Diode as a Voltage Regulator — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Input Stage:** An unregulated DC source V_in (with ripple) feeds a series resistor R_S.
2. **Shunt Stage:** The Zener diode is connected in *parallel* with the load R_L, reverse-biased.
3. **Condition Setup:** V_in is chosen such that V_in > V_Z (ensures Zener is in breakdown).
4. **Voltage Clamping:** The voltage across the Zener (and hence across R_L) is fixed at V_Z.
5. **Current Steering:** Any *excess* input current is shunted through the Zener; only I_L flows through the load.
6. **Regulation:** When V_in increases, I_Z increases proportionally (R_S absorbs the surplus as V_R = I·R_S), but V_L = V_Z stays constant.

**Design Constraint (KTU standard):**
$$I_{Z,\min} \le I_Z \le I_{Z,\max}$$

where I_Z,min is the minimum current required to keep the diode in breakdown, and I_Z,max is the maximum allowable power dissipation limit: **I_Z,max = P_Z(max) / V_Z**.

#### 2.1.3 Other Zener Diode Applications

- **Waveform Clipper:** Two Zeners back-to-back clip AC signals symmetrically at ±V_Z.
- **Voltage Reference:** Provides a stable reference voltage for ADCs, comparators, op-amp circuits.
- **Over-Voltage Protection (Crowbar/Clamping):** Protects sensitive ICs from voltage transients.
- **Switching Element:** Acts as a fast ON/OFF switch at V_Z threshold.
- **Meter Protection:** Limits voltage across sensitive analog meters.

---

### 2.2 Tunnel Diode — Operating Regions and Applications

#### 2.2.1 V–I Curve and Critical Points

The forward V–I characteristic of a tunnel diode has **three critical currents/voltages**:

| Point | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|-------|--------|------------------|
| Peak Point | (I_p, V_p) | Maximum tunneling current; tunneling begins to drop as bands misalign |
| Valley Point | (I_v, V_v) | Minimum current; tunneling ceases, diffusion takes over |
| Forward Rise | (I_f, V_f) | Current rises again like a normal diode |

**Key Performance Ratio (KTU Important):**
$$\text{Peak-to-Valley Current Ratio (PVR)} = \frac{I_p}{I_v}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Higher PVR → Better diode quality.** For a *good* tunnel diode, PVR should be **≥ 8** (typical GaAs: PVR ≈ 15–20; Ge: PVR ≈ 8–12).

#### 2.2.2 Three Operating Regions

1. **Region I (0 to V_p):** *Tunneling regime.* Current rises as overlap between filled n-side states and empty p-side states increases.
2. **Region II (V_p to V_v):** *Negative Differential Resistance (NDR).* Current *decreases* with increasing voltage — bands misalign, fewer filled states can tunnel.
3. **Region III (V > V_v):** *Diffusion regime.* Normal forward-bias diode behavior resumes.

#### 2.2.3 Tunnel Diode Applications

- **High-Frequency Oscillators (Microwave):** Exploits NDR to convert DC → RF oscillations; can reach **GHz to THz** ranges.
- **High-Speed Switching:** Switching time ≈ *nanoseconds* due to tunneling (not minority carrier storage).
- **Microwave Amplifiers:** NDR cancels circuit losses → amplification.
- **Frequency Converters and Mixers:** Used in radar and satellite communication front-ends.
- **Logic Memory Cells:** Bistable circuits using the two stable states in the NDR region.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The *negative resistance* of the tunnel diode cancels the *positive resistance* of the circuit, satisfying the **Barkhausen criterion** for sustained oscillation.

---

### 2.3 Photonic Devices — Qualitative Working Principles

#### 2.3.1 Energy Relations in Semiconductors

For photon absorption/emission, the **bandgap energy E_g** is the central quantity:

$$E_g = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

where **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** (Planck's constant) and **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** (speed of light).

**Cutoff Wavelength:**
$$\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}~\mu\text{m}$$

| Material | E_g (eV) | λ_c (μm) | Spectral Region |
|----------|----------|----------|------------------|
| Silicon (Si) | 1.12 | 1.107 | Near IR / Visible |
| Germanium (Ge) | 0.67 | 1.851 | Near IR |
| GaAs | 1.42 | 0.873 | Red / Near IR |
| InGaAs | 0.75 | 1.653 | Telecom (1.55 μm window) |

#### 2.3.2 Application Domains of Photonic Devices

- **Optical Fiber Communication** (LEDs, Lasers, Photodetectors)
- **Solar Cells** (photovoltaic conversion)
- **Optoelectronic Displays** (LEDs, OLEDs)
- **Sensing & Imaging** (CCD, CMOS image sensors)
- **Medical Diagnostics & Laser Surgery**

---

### 2.4 Photodetectors — Junction and PIN Photodiodes

#### 2.4.1 Junction Photodiode — Operating Principle

**Step-by-Step Photocurrent Generation:**

1. **Photon Incidence:** Light of energy hν ≥ E_g strikes the depletion region.
2. **Electron–Hole Pair Generation:** Each absorbed photon creates *one* e–h pair.
3. **Carrier Separation:** The strong reverse-bias electric field sweeps electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side.
4. **Photocurrent Flow:** The swept carriers constitute the photocurrent I_ph in the external circuit.
5. **Dark Current:** Even without light, a small reverse saturation current I_0 flows (thermally generated e–h pairs).

**Total Current Equation (Shockley + Photocurrent):**

$$I = I_0 \left[ \exp\left(\frac{eV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right] - I_{ph}$$

where:
- **I_0** = reverse saturation current (dark current)
- **e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** (electronic charge)
- **k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** (Boltzmann constant)
- **T** = absolute temperature (K)
- **I_ph** = photocurrent = R · P_opt (R = responsivity, P_opt = incident optical power)

#### 2.4.2 PIN Photodiode — Engineering the Intrinsic Layer

**Structural Advantages of the Intrinsic (i) Layer:**

1. **Wide Depletion Region:** Almost the entire i-region is depleted → large photon-absorption volume.
2. **Low Capacitance:** C_junction ∝ 1/W; with W ≈ 10–100 μm, C is small → high bandwidth.
3. **High Quantum Efficiency:** More photons absorbed → higher η.
4. **Fast Response:** Carriers traverse the i-region at *saturation drift velocity* (~10⁵ m/s) → transit time ≈ ns.

**Transit Time Across Intrinsic Layer:**
$$t_{tr} = \frac{W_i}{v_{sat}}$$

#### 2.4.3 Key Performance Metrics

**Responsivity (R):** The output photocurrent per unit incident optical power.
$$R = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} \quad \text{(units: A/W)}$$

**Quantum Efficiency (η):** The fraction of incident photons that successfully generate collected electron–hole pairs.
$$\eta = \frac{\text{electrons collected per second}}{\text{photons incident per second}} = \frac{I_{ph}/e}{P_{opt}/h\nu} = \frac{R \cdot h\nu}{e}$$

**Maximum Theoretical Responsivity (η = 1):**
$$R_{max} = \frac{e}{h\nu} = \frac{e\lambda}{hc} = \frac{\lambda~(\mu\text{m})}{1.24}~\text{A/W}$$

**Detectivity (D*):** A figure of merit for detector sensitivity including noise.
$$D^* = \frac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{\text{NEP}} \quad \text{(units: cm·Hz}^{1/2}\text{/W)}$$

where NEP = Noise Equivalent Power.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $V_{out} = V_Z$ | Zener regulator output voltage | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $I_{Z,\max} = P_{Z,\max}/V_Z$ | Max Zener current from power limit | Amperes (A) |
| 3 | $V_R = I_S \cdot R_S$ | Voltage drop across series resistor | V |
| 4 | $I_S = I_Z + I_L$ | KCL at Zener node | A |
| 5 | $\text{PVR} = I_p/I_v$ | Peak-to-Valley ratio of tunnel diode | Dimensionless, ≥ 8 ideal |
| 6 | $E_g = h\nu = hc/\lambda$ | Photon energy condition | Joules (J) |
| 7 | $\lambda_c = 1.24/E_g(\text{eV})~\mu\text{m}$ | Cutoff wavelength | μm |
| 8 | $I = I_0[\exp(eV/kT) - 1] - I_{ph}$ | Photodiode total current | A |
| 9 | $R = I_{ph}/P_{opt}$ | Responsivity | A/W |
| 10 | $\eta = (R \cdot h\nu)/e$ | Quantum efficiency | Dimensionless (0 ≤ η ≤ 1) |
| 11 | $R_{max} = \lambda(\mu\text{m})/1.24$ | Max responsivity at λ | A/W (for η=1) |
| 12 | $t_{tr} = W_i/v_{sat}$ | Carrier transit time in i-layer | Seconds (s) |
| 13 | $f_{3dB} = 0.35/t_{tr}$ | Bandwidth limit | Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants to Memorize:**
> - Planck's constant **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
> - Speed of light **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - hc = 1.99 × 10⁻²⁵ J·m = 1240 eV·nm
> - Electronic charge **e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
> - Boltzmann constant **k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** = 8.617 × 10⁻⁵ eV/K
> - kT/e (thermal voltage) at T=300 K ≈ 25.85 mV

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Quantum Mechanical Justification of Zener Breakdown

The Zener effect occurs when the electric field in the depletion region is so intense that the **potential barrier becomes triangular and thin enough for electrons to quantum-tunnel through it**.

**Starting Point — Maximum Field in a Junction:**
The peak electric field at a step p–n junction is:
$$E_{max} = \frac{e N_D x_n}{\varepsilon} = \frac{e N_A x_p}{\varepsilon}$$

For a one-sided abrupt junction (e.g., N_D ≫ N_A), the depletion width is:
$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2\varepsilon (V_{bi} + V_R)}{e N_A}}$$

**Barrier Triangularization Under Reverse Bias:**
The barrier, originally of height eV_bi, becomes a triangle with base W and height eV_bi. The width of the barrier at any energy level E is:
$$\Delta x = \frac{E_{max} - E}{eE_{max}/\text{slope factor}}$$

**Tunneling Probability (WKB Approximation):**
For a triangular barrier, the tunneling probability is:
$$T \approx \exp\left[-\frac{4\sqrt{2 m^* (E_g/2)^3}}{3 e \hbar E_{max}}\right]$$

When E_max exceeds ≈ 10⁸ V/m, T becomes appreciable, producing measurable reverse current — this is **Zener breakdown**.

**Threshold Field:** Setting T ≈ 1 yields:
$$E_{Z,crit} \approx \frac{4\sqrt{2 m^* (E_g/2)^3}}{3 e \hbar}$$

For Ge (E_g = 0.67 eV, m* ≈ 0.12 m_0), E_Z,crit ≈ 2 × 10⁷ V/m.
For Si (E_g = 1.12 eV, m* ≈ 0.5 m_0), E_Z,crit ≈ 10⁸ V/m.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A *higher bandgap* material requires a *higher critical field* to undergo Zener breakdown. This is why Zener diodes are predominantly made of **silicon** (E_g = 1.12 eV), with controlled doping to set V_Z precisely.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tunnel Diode I–V Characteristic (Esaki Model)

The Esaki current arises from tunneling between the conduction band of the n-side and the valence band of the p-side.

**Step 1 — Define the Net Tunneling Current:**
$$I = A \int_{E_c}^{E_v} N_n(E) \, f_n(E) \, T(E) \, N_p(E) \, [1 - f_p(E)] \, dE - \text{(reverse term)}$$

where:
- **N_n(E)**, **N_p(E)** = density of states on n-side and p-side
- **f_n(E)**, **f_p(E)** = Fermi–Dirac occupation functions
- **T(E)** = tunneling transmission probability

**Step 2 — Apply the Tsu–Esaki Formula:**
Under the assumption of constant field in the junction and parabolic bands, the Esaki current density simplifies to:

$$
J = \frac{e m^*}{2\pi^2 \hbar^3} \int_{0}^{eV} \!\! T(E) \, dE
$$

For a triangular barrier approximation:

$$
T(E) = \exp\left[-\frac{4\sqrt{2 m^* (E_g - E)^3}}{3 e \hbar E_{max}}\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Combine and Identify the Peak:**
The integrand is maximized when the **overlap between filled n-states and empty p-states is maximum**. At this voltage V_p, the current density peaks at **J_p**.

**Step 4 — At the Valley Point:**
As V increases past V_p, the overlap region shifts and shrinks. The tunneling current decreases. At V_v, the conduction band of n-side aligns with the *top* of the valence band of p-side — tunneling essentially stops. After V_v, normal thermal diffusion current (Shockley diode current) takes over.

**Step 5 — Final I–V Characteristic (Conceptual Form):**

$$
I(V) = I_{tunnel}(V) + I_{diff}(V)
$$

where I_tunnel dominates for 0 < V < V_v (with a peak at V_p), and I_diff dominates for V > V_v.

The **negative differential resistance** is defined as:

$$
R_{NDR} = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta I} \quad \text{where} \quad \frac{\Delta I}{\Delta V} < 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A tunnel diode's NDR region is *unstable* when connected to a resistive load of value R_L > vert R_NDR vert . This is the **criterion for using the tunnel diode as an oscillator or amplifier**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Photodiode Current and Quantum Efficiency

**Step 1 — Photocurrent Generation Rate:**
If a photon flux Φ (photons/s) is incident and the fraction absorbed within the active region is η (quantum efficiency), then the carrier generation rate is:

$$
G = \eta \Phi = \eta \cdot \frac{P_{opt}}{h\nu}
$$

**Step 2 — Photocurrent:**
Each generated e–h pair contributes one electron of charge e to the external current:

$$
I_{ph} = e \cdot G = e \cdot \eta \cdot \frac{P_{opt}}{h\nu}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for Quantum Efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{I_{ph} \cdot h\nu}{e \cdot P_{opt}} = \frac{I_{ph}/e}{P_{opt}/h\nu}
$$

The numerator is *electrons collected per second* and the denominator is *photons incident per second*. This is the **operational definition of quantum efficiency**.

**Step 4 — Express in Terms of Responsivity:**
Since R = I_ph / P_opt, we substitute:

$$
\eta = R \cdot \frac{h\nu}{e} = R \cdot \frac{hc}{e\lambda}
$$

**Step 5 — Maximum Theoretical Responsivity (η = 1):**

$$
R_{max} = \frac{e}{h\nu} = \frac{e\lambda}{hc}
$$

Substituting numerical values (e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C, h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s):

$$
R_{max} = \frac{\lambda(\text{in μm})}{1.24} \quad \text{A/W}
$$

**Step 6 — Wavelength Dependence:**

$$
R(\lambda) = \frac{e\lambda}{hc} \eta(\lambda) = \frac{\lambda(\mu\text{m})}{1.24} \cdot \eta(\lambda) \quad \text{A/W}
$$

This shows **R increases linearly with λ** up to the cutoff λ_c, beyond which the semiconductor becomes transparent (no absorption).

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — PIN Photodiode Frequency Response

**Step 1 — Define the Transit-Time Limited Bandwidth:**
The dominant speed limit in a PIN diode is the time taken for carriers to drift across the intrinsic region:

$$
t_{tr} = \frac{W_i}{v_{sat}}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the First-Order RC Time Approximation:**
The 3-dB bandwidth for a transit-time-limited detector is:

$$
f_{3dB} = \frac{0.35}{t_{tr}} = \frac{0.35 \, v_{sat}}{W_i}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Trade-off:**
- **Wider W_i** → higher quantum efficiency (more absorption) → *lower* bandwidth.
- **Narrower W_i** → faster response → *lower* quantum efficiency.

**Design Optimization:** Choose W_i such that the optical absorption depth 1/α (where α is the absorption coefficient) ≈ W_i.

$$
W_i^{opt} = \frac{1}{\alpha(\lambda)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** The absorption coefficient α(λ) depends on material. For Si at λ = 850 nm, α ≈ 700 cm⁻¹, so 1/α ≈ 14 μm. For InGaAs at λ = 1550 nm, α ≈ 7000 cm⁻¹, so 1/α ≈ 1.4 μm.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Junction vs. PIN Photodiode)

| Parameter | Junction Photodiode | PIN Photodiode | KTU Significance |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| Structure | p–n | p–i–n | PIN has extra intrinsic layer |
| Depletion Width | ~1 μm | 10–100 μm | PIN has wider active region |
| Junction Capacitance | High | Low | PIN is faster |
| Quantum Efficiency (η) | 30–60% | 60–90% | PIN absorbs more photons |
| Response Time | ~μs | ~ns to ps | PIN is much faster |
| Bandwidth | Low–Medium (MHz) | High (GHz) | PIN used in telecom |
| Reverse Bias | Moderate (few V) | High (tens of V) | PIN needs higher bias to fully deplete i-region |
| Primary Application | Light meters, simple sensors | Optical fiber receivers, high-speed links | PIN dominates telecom |

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Zener Diode Voltage Regulator — Functional Architecture

The following block-level functional architecture describes the closed-loop current/voltage flow in a Zener shunt regulator.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["UNREGULATED DC INPUT Vin"] --> nodeB["SERIES RESISTOR Rs"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["JUNCTION NODE X"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["LOAD RESISTOR RL"]
    nodeC --> nodeE["ZENER DIODE REVERSE BIASED VZ"]
    nodeD --> nodeF["REGULATED OUTPUT Vout EQUALS VZ"]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG["GROUND REFERENCE"]
    nodeA --> nodeG
```

**Operational Description:**
- Input DC (possibly with AC ripple) is applied across the series combination of R_S, the Zener, and R_L.
- The Zener holds the output node at a constant V_Z.
- Excess current is shunted through the Zener to ground.
- The series resistor R_S absorbs the surplus voltage: V_Rs = (I_Z + I_L)·R_S.

---

### 4.2 Tunnel Diode Oscillator — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["DC BIAS SUPPLY VB"] --> nodeB["BIAS RESISTOR Rb"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["TUNNEL DIODE WITH NDR"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["PARALLEL LC TANK CIRCUIT"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["OUTPUT SINE WAVE RF"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["DC GROUND"]
    nodeA --> nodeF
```

**Operational Description:**
- The DC bias sets the operating point in the NDR region (between V_p and V_v).
- The NDR of the tunnel diode (−R_d) cancels the positive resistance of the LC tank + losses.
- Sustained oscillations occur when the **Barkhausen criterion** is satisfied: the magnitude of the loop gain ≥ 1 and total phase shift = 0 (or 2πn).
- The output frequency is set by the LC tank: f_osc = 1/(2π√(LC)).

---

### 4.3 PIN Photodiode Structure and Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["INCIDENT PHOTONS hv"] --> nodeB["ANTIREFLECTION COATING"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["P REGION THIN"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["INTRINSIC I REGION WIDE ABSORPTION ZONE"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["N REGION"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["REVERSE BIAS VOLTAGE VR"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["GENERATED ELECTRON HOLE PAIRS"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["DRIFT TOWARD CONTACTS"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["PHOTOCURRENT Iph AT OUTPUT"]
    nodeF --> nodeI
```

**Operational Description:**
- Photons enter through the antireflection coating and the thin p-region.
- Most photons are absorbed in the wide intrinsic (i) region, generating e–h pairs.
- The reverse bias creates a strong uniform electric field across the i-region.
- Electrons drift toward the n-contact; holes drift toward the p-contact.
- The resulting current is the photocurrent I_ph, which flows in the external circuit.

---

### 4.4 Optical Fiber Communication System — Top-Level Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["ELECTRICAL INPUT SIGNAL"] --> nodeB["DRIVER AND MODULATOR"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["LIGHT SOURCE LED OR LASER"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["OPTICAL FIBER TRANSMISSION MEDIUM"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["PIN PHOTODETECTOR"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["TRANSIMPEDANCE AMPLIFIER TIA"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["RECEIVER AND DECODER"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["ELECTRICAL OUTPUT SIGNAL"]
    nodeA --> nodeH
```

**Operational Description:**
- An electrical signal drives a modulator that controls a light source (LED or laser diode).
- The optical signal propagates through the fiber with low loss.
- At the receiver end, a PIN photodiode converts the light signal back to an electrical photocurrent.
- A transimpedance amplifier (TIA) converts the current to a usable voltage signal.
- The decoder reconstructs the original electrical information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Note:** The light source (LED/Laser) belongs to *emitter* devices, while the photodiode (PIN) is the *detector*. Together with the fiber as the *transmission medium*, they form the **electro-optical-electrical signal chain** that is the backbone of modern photonics and telecom engineering.

## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question 1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
*With the help of a neat V–I characteristic, explain the principle of operation of a Zener diode used as a voltage regulator. Mention the condition for proper regulation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A Zener diode is a heavily doped p–n junction diode operated in the reverse-breakdown region. In the breakdown region, the voltage across the diode remains nearly constant at the **Zener voltage V_Z**, even when the reverse current changes over a wide range. *(1 mark)*

In the regulator circuit, the Zener diode is connected in parallel with the load R_L, in reverse bias, with a series resistor R_S between the unregulated DC input and the load. The unregulated input V_in exceeds V_Z so that the diode is always in breakdown. Any increase in V_in increases the current I_Z through the Zener (which is shunted to ground), while the load voltage V_L = V_Z stays constant. *(1 mark)*

**Condition for proper regulation:** The Zener current must satisfy:
$$I_{Z,\min} \le I_Z \le I_{Z,\max}$$
where I_Z,min is the minimum current to keep the diode in breakdown, and I_Z,max = P_Z(max)/V_Z is the maximum allowed by the diode's power rating. If the load current drops such that I_Z exceeds I_Z,max, the diode burns out; if it falls below I_Z,min, regulation is lost. *(1 mark)*

---

**Question 2.** **[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024, Model Question]**
*What is meant by negative differential resistance in a tunnel diode? Mention any two applications exploiting this property.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** Negative Differential Resistance (NDR) is a property of a device in which, over a certain range of operating voltages, the current *decreases* as the applied voltage *increases*, i.e., dI/dV < 0. *(1 mark)*

In a tunnel diode, NDR occurs in the forward-bias region between the peak voltage V_p and the valley voltage V_v. As the bias increases from V_p to V_v, the overlap between filled electron states on the n-side conduction band and empty states on the p-side valence band decreases, reducing the tunneling current. The diode thus exhibits a *negative slope* in this region of the I–V curve. *(1 mark)*

**Two Applications Exploiting NDR:**
1. **High-frequency microwave oscillator:** The NDR cancels circuit resistance, satisfying the Barkhausen criterion and producing sustained RF oscillations (GHz–THz range).
2. **High-speed switching / logic memory circuit:** The NDR region has two stable operating points, enabling bistable flip-flop and high-speed digital switching applications. *(1 mark)*

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Zener Diode + Tunnel Diode

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the I–V characteristic of a tunnel diode and explain the physical origin of negative differential resistance. Discuss the role of doping concentration. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Design a Zener diode voltage regulator to deliver a regulated 9 V output to a load of 1 kΩ from an unregulated DC source that varies between 12 V and 15 V. Assume I_Z,min = 5 mA and I_Z,max = 50 mA. Calculate the value of the series resistor R_S. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Tunnel Diode I–V Derivation and NDR (7 marks):**

The tunnel diode has an extremely high doping concentration (≈ 10²⁵ m⁻³) on both sides. At zero bias, the Fermi level lies *inside* the conduction band on the n-side and *inside* the valence band on the p-side. *(0.5 mark)*

The depletion width is very small (≈ 10 nm), and electrons can **tunnel quantum-mechanically** from the n-side conduction band into empty states in the p-side valence band. The net current is given by the **Tsu–Esaki integral**: *(1 mark)*

$$
I(V) = A \int N_n(E) f_n(E) T(E) N_p(E) [1 - f_p(E)] \, dE
$$

As the forward bias V increases from 0:
- The bands shift so that the filled n-side states and empty p-side states come into alignment.
- At **V = V_p** (peak voltage), the *maximum overlap* occurs, and the current reaches its peak **I_p**.
- For V_p < V < V_v, the bands continue to shift; the overlap *decreases*, and so does the tunneling current — this is the **NDR region** (dI/dV < 0). *(2 marks)*

The negative differential resistance is defined as:
$$
R_{NDR} = \left. \frac{dV}{dI} \right|_{NDR} < 0
$$

At **V = V_v** (valley voltage), the overlap becomes negligible, and the tunneling current reaches its minimum **I_v**. Beyond V_v, normal thermal diffusion current takes over, and the I–V curve rises like a conventional diode. *(1 mark)*

**Role of doping:** The very high doping (≈ 10²⁵ m⁻³) is essential because it (i) shifts the Fermi level deep into the bands (degenerate semiconductor) and (ii) makes the depletion width extremely thin (≈ 10 nm), enabling significant tunneling probability. *(1.5 marks)*

> **KTU Examiner Tip:** *Always draw the band diagrams at three bias points — zero bias, V = V_p, and V = V_v — to clearly show the band alignment and overlap. This is a common 2-mark question on its own.*

---

**(b) Zener Regulator Design (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- V_out = V_Z = 9 V
- R_L = 1 kΩ
- V_in(min) = 12 V, V_in(max) = 15 V
- I_Z,min = 5 mA, I_Z,max = 50 mA

**Step 1 — Load Current (constant, since V_L and R_L are constant):**
$$
I_L = \frac{V_L}{R_L} = \frac{9}{1000} = 9~\text{mA}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Current through R_S:**
At the junction node KCL gives: I_S = I_Z + I_L

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Design (R_S calculated for both extremes, choose the value that works in both cases):**

**Case 1: V_in = 15 V (maximum input):**
We need I_Z ≤ I_Z,max = 50 mA to avoid burning out the diode:
$$
I_{S,\max} = I_{Z,\max} + I_L = 50 + 9 = 59~\text{mA}
$$
$$
R_{S,\min} = \frac{V_{in,\max} - V_Z}{I_{S,\max}} = \frac{15 - 9}{59 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{6}{0.059} = 101.7~\Omega
$$

**Case 2: V_in = 12 V (minimum input):**
We need I_Z ≥ I_Z,min = 5 mA to maintain regulation:
$$
I_{S,\min} = I_{Z,\min} + I_L = 5 + 9 = 14~\text{mA}
$$
$$
R_{S,\max} = \frac{V_{in,\min} - V_Z}{I_{S,\min}} = \frac{12 - 9}{14 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{3}{0.014} = 214.3~\Omega
$$

**Step 4 — Select R_S within the range:**
$$
101.7~\Omega \le R_S \le 214.3~\Omega
$$

A standard resistor value of **R_S = 150 Ω** (or 180 Ω) is suitable. *(1 mark for final selection)*

**Final Answer:** **R_S = 150 Ω (preferred standard value)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Photonic Devices and Photodetectors

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the working of a PIN photodiode. Why is the intrinsic layer included, and how does it improve the responsivity and bandwidth? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A silicon PIN photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 80% at 850 nm. Calculate (i) the responsivity, (ii) the cutoff wavelength, and (iii) the maximum photocurrent when 10 μW of optical power is incident. Given: E_g(Si) = 1.12 eV, h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PIN Photodiode — Working and Intrinsic Layer Analysis (7 marks):**

A PIN photodiode has three regions: a thin p-layer, a wide intrinsic (i) layer, and an n-layer. It is operated under **reverse bias**. *(0.5 mark)*

**Working (with reference to functional block diagram in SECTION 4.3):**
1. Photons of energy hν ≥ E_g enter through the antireflection coating and the thin p-region.
2. Most photons are absorbed in the **wide intrinsic layer**, generating electron–hole pairs.
3. The strong reverse-bias electric field across the i-region sweeps the electrons toward the n-contact and the holes toward the p-contact.
4. The flow of these carriers constitutes the photocurrent I_ph in the external circuit. *(2 marks)*

**Why the intrinsic layer is included (3 reasons):**
- **Wide depletion region:** Since the i-region is nearly insulating, the depletion region extends across the *entire* i-layer, which can be 10–100 μm thick. This provides a large photon-absorption volume. *(1 mark)*
- **Low junction capacitance:** C_j = εA/W; the large W gives a small C, which increases the speed of response and bandwidth. *(1 mark)*
