- **High quantum efficiency:** More photons are absorbed in the long depletion region, raising η from 30–60% (junction diode) to 60–90% (PIN). *(1 mark)*

**Improvement in Responsivity and Bandwidth:**
- *Responsivity:* R = η·e/hν increases because η increases.
- *Bandwidth:* f_3dB = 0.35·v_sat/W_i; for a typical W_i = 20 μm and v_sat = 10⁵ m/s, f_3dB ≈ 1.75 GHz — much higher than a simple p–n diode. *(1.5 marks)*

---

**(b) PIN Photodiode Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- λ = 850 nm = 850 × 10⁻⁹ m
- η = 80% = 0.80
- P_opt = 10 μW = 10 × 10⁻⁶ W
- E_g(Si) = 1.12 eV
- h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s

**Step 1 — Photon Energy hν:**
$$
h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{850 \times 10^{-9}}
$$
$$
h\nu = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{850 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.339 \times 10^{-19}~\text{J}
$$
Converting to eV: hν = 2.339 × 10⁻¹⁹ / 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ = 1.462 eV. *(1 mark)*

**Step 2 — (i) Responsivity:**
$$
R = \eta \cdot \frac{e\lambda}{hc} = \eta \cdot \frac{\lambda(\mu\text{m})}{1.24}
$$
$$
R = 0.80 \times \frac{0.850}{1.24} = 0.80 \times 0.6855 = 0.5484~\text{A/W} \approx 0.55~\text{A/W}
$$

*Alternative check using SI units:*
$$
R = \frac{\eta \cdot e}{h\nu} = \frac{0.80 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{2.339 \times 10^{-19}} = \frac{1.28 \times 10^{-19}}{2.339 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.547~\text{A/W} \quad \checkmark
$$

**R ≈ 0.55 A/W** *(2 marks)*

**Step 3 — (ii) Cutoff Wavelength:**
$$
\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})} = \frac{1.24}{1.12} = 1.107~\mu\text{m}
$$

**λ_c ≈ 1.107 μm** *(2 marks)*

> **KTU Insight:** Since λ_operating = 850 nm < λ_c = 1107 nm, the 850 nm photons have energy *greater* than the bandgap → they are *absorbed*. ✓

**Step 4 — (iii) Maximum Photocurrent:**
$$
I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt} = 0.5474 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 5.474 \times 10^{-6}~\text{A}
$$

**I_ph ≈ 5.47 μA** *(2 marks)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up Zener and Avalanche breakdown:** Examiners *specifically* test if you know the 5.8 V crossover. Writing "Zener breakdown occurs at high voltage" costs 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Forgetting the I_Z,min constraint:** In Zener regulator design, students often compute R_S from the V_in,max case *only*, forgetting to verify that I_Z ≥ I_Z,min at V_in,min. This loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Tunnel diode band diagram:** If asked to *draw* the I–V curve, you must label **V_p, I_p, V_v, I_v** and the NDR region. Skipping the NDR label = −2 marks.
> 4. **PIN photodiode intrinsic layer:** Do not call it an "insulator." Call it a *high-resistivity, nearly intrinsic semiconductor*. Saying "insulator" is a factual error (−1 mark).
> 5. **Units in Quantum Efficiency:** η is *dimensionless*. Writing η in A/W or % in the formula table is a common error.
> 6. **Sign of photocurrent:** In the photodiode equation, the photocurrent term is *subtracted* (it flows in the reverse direction). Many students get the sign wrong (−1 mark).
> 7. **Cutoff wavelength formula:** Use **λ_c = 1.24/E_g(eV)** in μm. Substituting E_g in joules and forgetting to convert is a frequent mistake (−1 mark).

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Zener Diode:**
  - Operates in *reverse-breakdown* region; voltage is clamped to V_Z.
  - *Zener effect* (tunneling) dominates for V_Z < 5.8 V; *avalanche effect* (impact ionization) for V_Z > 5.8 V.
  - Used as **voltage regulator**, **clipper**, **reference voltage**, **over-voltage protector**.
  - Design constraint: **I_Z,min ≤ I_Z ≤ I_Z,max**.

- **Tunnel Diode (Esaki Diode):**
  - Extremely heavy doping (≈ 10²⁵ m⁻³); depletion width ≈ 10 nm.
  - Conduction is by **quantum mechanical tunneling**, not thermal diffusion.
  - Exhibits **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)** between V_p and V_v.
  - **Peak-to-Valley Ratio (PVR) = I_p/I_v** is the key quality metric (≥ 8 for a good diode).
  - Used in **microwave oscillators, high-speed switches, frequency converters, bistable logic**.

- **Photonic Devices (Qualitative):**
  - Three fundamental processes: **absorption, spontaneous emission, stimulated emission**.
  - Photon energy condition: **hν ≥ E_g** for absorption.
  - Cutoff wavelength: **λ_c = 1.24/E_g(eV)** μm.

- **Junction Photodiode:**
  - Reverse-biased p–n junction; photons absorbed in/near depletion region.
  - Total current: **I = I_0[exp(eV/kT) − 1] − I_ph**.
  - Photocurrent: **I_ph = R · P_opt**.

- **PIN Photodiode:**
  - p–i–n structure with a wide intrinsic layer.
  - **Intrinsic layer** widens depletion, lowers capacitance, increases η and bandwidth.
  - Used in **optical fiber receivers**, **high-speed photodetection**.

- **Key Performance Metrics:**
  - Responsivity: **R = I_ph / P_opt** (A/W).
  - Quantum Efficiency: **η = (R·hν)/e** (dimensionless, 0 to 1).
  - Maximum R: **R_max = λ(μm)/1.24** A/W (for η = 1).
  - Bandwidth: **f_3dB = 0.35·v_sat / W_i**.

- **Constants to Memorize:**
  - h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s
  - c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s
  - e = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
  - k = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K
  - hc = 1240 eV·nm

- **Cross-Connection Tip:**
  - The Zener diode is a *voltage-clamping* device; the tunnel diode is a *current-controlled* device with NDR.
  - The junction photodiode is a *photoconductive* detector; the PIN photodiode is a *high-speed, high-efficiency* version used in telecom.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What does "Applications" mean in Module 2 of GBPHT121?

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Physics for Electrical Science* (GBPHT121), **Module 2** is dedicated to the foundations of **Quantum Physics and Oscillatory/Wave Phenomena**. The "Applications" topic bridges these abstract theoretical concepts with real-world electrical and electronic engineering devices. Mastering applications is critical because the KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) frequently ask for device-based numerical or descriptive problems worth **7 to 14 marks**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Applications):**
> Applications refer to the engineering use-cases of quantum mechanical phenomena (tunneling, wave-particle duality, photon emission) and wave/oscillation concepts (resonance, damped oscillations, coupled oscillations) in modern electrical devices such as the **Tunnel Diode, Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM), LASER, Photovoltaic Cells, Ultrasonic Transducers, and Oscillator Circuits.**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Wall That Isn't There"

Imagine you are rolling a tennis ball toward a small hill. Classically (Newtonian physics), if the ball does not have enough energy to climb over the hill, it will roll back — it can **never** appear on the other side. 

However, in the **quantum world**, particles behave like waves. A wave striking a thin barrier does not simply stop; part of it *leaks* through to the other side, even if the particle's energy is less than the barrier's height. This is called **quantum tunneling**.

- A small hill = a thin potential barrier (like the p-n junction of a Tunnel Diode)
- Tennis ball = an electron
- The leak-through = tunneling current

This single quantum effect powers the **Tunnel Diode** and the **Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM)**, both of which are milestone inventions in modern electronics.

### 1.3 List of Core Applications You MUST Know for KTU ESE

| # | Application | Underlying Physics Principle | Engineering Use |
|---|------------|------------------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Tunnel Diode** (Esaki Diode) | Quantum Tunneling | High-speed switching, microwave circuits |
| 2 | **Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM)** | Quantum Tunneling | Atomic-resolution imaging |
| 3 | **LASER** | Stimulated Emission of Radiation | Optical communication, surgery, barcode readers |
| 4 | **Photovoltaic / Solar Cell** | Photoelectric Effect | Renewable energy generation |
| 5 | **Photodiode / Phototransistor** | Photon-induced electron-hole pairs | Optical sensors, fiber-optic receivers |
| 6 | **Ultrasonic Transducer** | High-frequency mechanical resonance | SONAR, medical imaging, NDT |
| 7 | **LC Oscillator Circuit** | Resonance in electrical circuits | Signal generation, tuning in radios |
| 8 | **Optical Fiber** | Total Internal Reflection of EM waves | Long-distance communication |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constants to Memorize for KTU Numerical Problems:**
> - Planck's Constant: **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
> - Reduced Planck's Constant: **ℏ = 1.0546 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
> - Electron Mass: **mₑ = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg**
> - Electron Charge: **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
> - Speed of Light: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - 1 eV = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Application #1 — The Tunnel Diode (Esaki Diode)

The **Tunnel Diode** was invented by Japanese physicist **Leo Esaki in 1957** (Nobel Prize, 1973). It is a heavily doped p-n junction diode where the depletion region is extremely thin (~10 nm), allowing electrons to **tunnel** through the barrier.

**Operating Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. In a normal diode, electrons must climb over the potential barrier — they need energy ≥ barrier height.
2. In a Tunnel Diode, doping is so heavy (10¹⁹ to 10²⁰ atoms/cm³) that the depletion width becomes extremely small.
3. According to quantum mechanics, the electron wavefunction does **not** terminate abruptly at the barrier — it decays exponentially but is **non-zero** on the other side.
4. This non-zero probability allows electrons to "tunnel" through, producing a **negative differential resistance (NDR)** region in the I-V curve.

### 2.2 Application #2 — The Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM)

Invented by **Gerd Binnig and Heinrich Rohrer in 1981** (Nobel Prize, 1986), the STM uses a sharp metallic tip (typically Tungsten) brought within ~1 nm of a conducting sample surface.

**Operating Logic:**

1. A small bias voltage (mV to a few V) is applied between tip and sample.
2. Electrons tunnel through the vacuum gap (which acts as the potential barrier).
3. The **tunneling current** is exquisitely sensitive to the gap distance — it varies exponentially with distance.
4. By scanning the tip across the surface and using a feedback loop to maintain a constant current, the topography of the surface can be mapped with **sub-Ångstrom (atomic) resolution**.

### 2.3 Application #3 — The LASER

**LASER = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.** Invented by **Theodore Maiman in 1960** using a Ruby crystal.

**Three Essential Conditions for Lasing:**

1. **Stimulated Emission:** An incoming photon of energy E = hν triggers an excited electron to drop to a lower energy level, emitting a *second* photon identical in phase, frequency, and direction.
2. **Population Inversion:** More atoms must exist in the excited state than the ground state (achieved by "pumping").
3. **Optical Resonator:** Two mirrors (one fully reflective, one partially reflective) form a resonant cavity to amplify light through multiple passes.

### 2.4 Application #4 — Quantum Mechanical Tunneling: The Core Formula

When a particle of energy **E** encounters a rectangular potential barrier of height **V₀ > E** and width **a**, the transmission probability (tunneling probability) is:

$$ T = \left[ 1 + \dfrac{V_0^2 \sinh^2(\kappa a)}{4E(V_0 - E)} \right]^{-1} $$

where the **decay constant κ** is:

$$ \kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Simplified Formula (used in most KTU numericals):**
> When κa ≫ 1, the transmission coefficient is approximately:
> 
> $$ T \approx \dfrac{16E(V_0 - E)}{V_0^2} \, e^{-2\kappa a} $$

### 2.5 Application #5 — Damped Harmonic Oscillator in RLC Circuits

An RLC series circuit is the **electrical analogue** of a damped harmonic oscillator.

The charge q(t) on the capacitor satisfies:

$$ L\dfrac{d^2q}{dt^2} + R\dfrac{dq}{dt} + \dfrac{q}{C} = 0 $$

**Three Regimes of Damping:**

| Regime | Condition | Behavior | KTU Relevance |
|--------|-----------|----------|---------------|
| **Underdamped** | R² < 4L/C | Oscillatory decay | Oscillator circuits, AC filters |
| **Critically Damped** | R² = 4L/C | Fastest non-oscillatory return | Galvanometers, door closers |
| **Overdamped** | R² > 4L/C | Slow non-oscillatory return | Circuit breakers |

### 2.6 Application #6 — Resonance in AC Circuits

At **resonance**, the inductive reactance cancels the capacitive reactance:

$$ X_L = X_C \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_0 L = \dfrac{1}{\omega_0 C} $$

$$ \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{(Resonant frequency, rad/s)} $$

$$ f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} \quad \text{(Resonant frequency, Hz)} $$

The **Quality Factor (Q)** of a resonant circuit is:

$$ Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} $$

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | de Broglie Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{p} = \dfrac{h}{mv}$ | h = Planck's constant, p = momentum |
| 2 | Tunneling Decay Constant | $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar}$ | m = particle mass, V₀ = barrier height, E = particle energy |
| 3 | Tunneling Transmission | $T \approx \dfrac{16E(V_0 - E)}{V_0^2} e^{-2\kappa a}$ | a = barrier width |
| 4 | Schrödinger Equation (1D) | $-\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\dfrac{d^2\psi}{dx^2} + V\psi = E\psi$ | ψ = wavefunction, V = potential |
| 5 | Energy of Photon | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | ν = frequency, λ = wavelength |
| 6 | Work Function | $\phi = h\nu_0$ | ν₀ = threshold frequency |
| 7 | Stopping Potential | $eV_s = h\nu - \phi$ | Vₛ = stopping potential |
| 8 | Resonant Frequency (LC) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | L = inductance, C = capacitance |
| 9 | Quality Factor | $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | R = resistance |
| 10 | Damping Coefficient | $\gamma = \dfrac{R}{2L}$ | — |
| 11 | Beat Frequency | $f_{beat} = \vert f_1 - f_2 \vert$ | Use `\vert` for absolute value |
| 12 | Standing Wave Frequency | $f_n = \dfrac{n}{2L}\sqrt{\dfrac{T}{\mu}}$ | T = tension, μ = linear density |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these applications matter for an Electrical Engineer:**
> - Tunnel Diodes → high-frequency microwave oscillators (used in radar, satellite communication)
> - STMs → nanotechnology and semiconductor fabrication
> - LASERs → fiber-optic communication backbone of the internet
> - Solar Cells → renewable energy systems
> - LC resonance → tuning circuits in every radio and TV receiver
> - Ultrasonics → medical diagnostics, industrial cleaning
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Transmission Coefficient of a Rectangular Potential Barrier

**Setup:** A particle of mass *m* and total energy *E* < V₀ moves in the +x direction and encounters a rectangular barrier of height *V₀* and width *a*, located between *x = 0* and *x = a*.

The time-independent Schrödinger equation in three regions is:

**Region I (x < 0):**

$$ -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\dfrac{d^2 \psi_I}{dx^2} = E\psi_I $$

$$ \dfrac{d^2 \psi_I}{dx^2} = -k^2 \psi_I, \quad \text{where} \quad k^2 = \dfrac{2mE}{\hbar^2} $$

General solution: $\psi_I(x) = A e^{ikx} + B e^{-ikx}$ (incident + reflected wave)

**Region II (0 ≤ x ≤ a):**

$$ -\dfrac{\hbar^2}{2m}\dfrac{d^2 \psi_{II}}{dx^2} + V_0 \psi_{II} = E\psi_{II} $$

$$ \dfrac{d^2 \psi_{II}}{dx^2} = \kappa^2 \psi_{II}, \quad \text{where} \quad \kappa^2 = \dfrac{2m(V_0 - E)}{\hbar^2} $$

General solution: $\psi_{II}(x) = C e^{\kappa x} + D e^{-\kappa x}$ (growing + decaying exponentials)

**Region III (x > a):**

$$ \psi_{III}(x) = F e^{ikx} \quad \text{(only transmitted wave assumed)} $$

**Step 1 — Apply Boundary Conditions at x = 0:**

Continuity of wavefunction:
$$ \psi_I(0) = \psi_{II}(0) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A + B = C + D $$

Continuity of derivative:
$$ \left.\dfrac{d\psi_I}{dx}\right|_0 = \left.\dfrac{d\psi_{II}}{dx}\right|_0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; ik(A - B) = \kappa(C - D) $$

**Step 2 — Apply Boundary Conditions at x = a:**

$$ \psi_{II}(a) = \psi_{III}(a) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; C e^{\kappa a} + D e^{-\kappa a} = F e^{ika} $$

$$ \kappa C e^{\kappa a} - \kappa D e^{-\kappa a} = ik F e^{ika} $$

**Step 3 — Solve for F/A (transmission amplitude):**

After lengthy but standard algebraic manipulation, the ratio of the transmitted to incident probability current densities gives the **transmission coefficient**:

$$ T = \dfrac{\vert F \vert^2}{\vert A \vert^2} = \left[ 1 + \dfrac{V_0^2 \sinh^2(\kappa a)}{4E(V_0 - E)} \right]^{-1} $$

**Step 4 — Simplification for thick barrier (κa ≫ 1):**

Since $\sinh(\kappa a) \approx \dfrac{1}{2}e^{\kappa a}$ for large $\kappa a$:

$$ T \approx \dfrac{16E(V_0 - E)}{V_0^2} \, e^{-2\kappa a} $$

This shows that the tunneling probability **decays exponentially** with barrier width — this is the key to understanding why tunnel diodes need ultra-thin barriers.

### 3.2 Analytical Worked Example — Tunneling Probability Calculation

**KTU-Style Numerical:**
> An electron with energy E = 2 eV encounters a potential barrier of height V₀ = 5 eV and width a = 1 nm. Calculate the tunneling probability. Given: m = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg, ℏ = 1.0546 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J.

**Step 1 — Convert energies to Joules:**

$$ E = 2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 3.204 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} $$

$$ V_0 = 5 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 8.010 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} $$

$$ V_0 - E = 5.006 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} $$

**Step 2 — Compute κ:**

$$ \kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar} $$

$$ 2m(V_0 - E) = 2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 5.006 \times 10^{-19} $$

$$ 2m(V_0 - E) = 9.121 \times 10^{-49} \text{ kg·J} $$

$$ \sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)} = \sqrt{9.121 \times 10^{-49}} = 9.551 \times 10^{-25} \text{ kg}^{1/2}\text{·J}^{1/2} $$

$$ \kappa = \dfrac{9.551 \times 10^{-25}}{1.0546 \times 10^{-34}} = 9.056 \times 10^{9} \text{ m}^{-1} $$

**Step 3 — Compute κa:**

$$ \kappa a = 9.056 \times 10^{9} \times 1 \times 10^{-9} = 9.056 $$

**Step 4 — Compute the prefactor:**

$$ \dfrac{16E(V_0 - E)}{V_0^2} = \dfrac{16 \times 2 \times (5 - 2)}{5^2} = \dfrac{16 \times 2 \times 3}{25} = \dfrac{96}{25} = 3.84 $$

**Step 5 — Compute the exponential factor:**

$$ e^{-2\kappa a} = e^{-2 \times 9.056} = e^{-18.112} $$

$$ e^{-18.112} \approx 1.34 \times 10^{-8} $$

**Step 6 — Final Tunneling Probability:**

$$ T \approx 3.84 \times 1.34 \times 10^{-8} = 5.15 \times 10^{-8} $$

$$ T \approx 5.15 \times 10^{-8} \quad \text{(or about } 0.00000515\%) $$

**Engineering Insight:** Although this seems tiny, when **billions of electrons** attempt tunneling per second (as in a Tunnel Diode), the resulting current is significant. Also, halving the barrier width to 0.5 nm would increase T by a factor of $e^{9.056} \approx 8.5 \times 10^3$, dramatically increasing the current.

### 3.3 Derivation: Resonant Frequency of an LC Circuit

**Step 1 — Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) for a series LC circuit:**

$$ V_L + V_C = 0 $$

$$ L\dfrac{dI}{dt} + \dfrac{q}{C} = 0 $$

**Step 2 — Using I = dq/dt:**

$$ L\dfrac{d^2 q}{dt^2} + \dfrac{q}{C} = 0 $$

$$ \dfrac{d^2 q}{dt^2} + \dfrac{1}{LC} q = 0 $$

**Step 3 — Compare with the standard SHM equation $\ddot{x} + \omega_0^2 x = 0$:**

$$ \omega_0^2 = \dfrac{1}{LC} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} $$

**Step 4 — Convert to frequency:**

$$ f_0 = \dfrac{\omega_0}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}} $$

This is the **resonant frequency** of an LC tank circuit, which is the heart of every radio tuner, oscillator, and filter.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Damping Regimes Derivation

Starting from the general RLC equation:

$$ L\dfrac{d^2 q}{dt^2} + R\dfrac{dq}{dt} + \dfrac{q}{C} = 0 $$

Assume $q(t) = e^{\alpha t}$:

$$ L\alpha^2 + R\alpha + \dfrac{1}{C} = 0 $$

$$ \alpha = \dfrac{-R \pm \sqrt{R^2 - 4L/C}}{2L} $$

| Regime | Discriminant | Roots | Solution Form |
|--------|--------------|-------|---------------|
| Underdamped | $R^2 - 4L/C < 0$ | Complex conjugates | $q(t) = q_0 e^{-\gamma t}\cos(\omega_d t + \phi)$ where $\omega_d = \sqrt{\omega_0^2 - \gamma^2}$ |
| Critically damped | $R^2 - 4L/C = 0$ | Equal real roots | $q(t) = (A + Bt)e^{-\gamma t}$ |
| Overdamped | $R^2 - 4L/C > 0$ | Two real roots | $q(t) = A e^{\alpha_1 t} + B e^{\alpha_2 t}$ |

where the **damping coefficient** is $\gamma = \dfrac{R}{2L}$ and $\omega_0 = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Quantum Tunneling — From Physics to Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Quantum Mechanical Principle] --> B[Particle Wavefunction psi of x]
    B --> C[Encounters Potential Barrier V0]
    C --> D{Is Energy E less than V0?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Classically Forbidden Region]
    D -- No --> F[Particle Crosses Over]
    E --> G[Wavefunction Decays Exponentially]
    G --> H[Non-zero Amplitude on Other Side]
    H --> I[Tunneling Probability T]
    I --> J[Tunnel Diode Esaki 1957]
    I --> K[Scanning Tunneling Microscope 1981]
    I --> L[Flash Memory Floating Gate Transistor]
    I --> M[Josephson Junction Superconductivity]
    J --> N[High-speed Microwave Oscillator]
    K --> O[Atomic Scale Imaging]
    L --> P[Non-volatile Data Storage]
    M --> Q[SQUID Magnetometer and Qubits]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: LASER Working Principle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrical or Optical Pump] --> B[Energy Input to Gain Medium]
    B --> C[Atoms Excited to Higher Level E2]
    C --> D{Population Inversion Achieved?}
    D -- No --> E[Continue Pumping]
    D -- Yes --> F[Spontaneous Emission Triggers Cascade]
    F --> G[Stimulated Emission Photons]
    G --> H[Optical Resonator Two Mirrors]
    H --> I[Photons Bounce Multiple Times]
    I --> J[Amplified Coherent Beam]
    J --> K[Output via Partially Reflective Mirror]
    K --> L[Applications Optical Communication Surgery Barcode Reader]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: RLC Series Circuit and Damping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RLC Series Circuit] --> B[Source V t]
    A --> C[Resistor R]
    A --> D[Inductor L]
    A --> E[Capacitor C]
    C --> F{Damping Condition}
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[R squared less than 4L over C]
    F --> H[R squared equals 4L over C]
    F --> I[R squared greater than 4L over C]
    G --> J[Underdamped Oscillatory Decay]
    H --> K[Critically Damped Fastest Return]
    I --> L[Overdamped Slow Return]
    J --> M[Application Tuning Circuit Radio Receiver]
    K --> N[Application Galvanometer Door Closer]
    L --> O[Application Circuit Breaker]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Comparison of Tunnel Diode vs Conventional Diode

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Conventional p-n Diode] --> A1[Doping 10 to the 15 per cm cubed]
    A1 --> A2[Thick Depletion Region 1 micrometer]
    A2 --> A3[Electrons Climb Over Barrier]
    A3 --> A4[No Tunneling Current]
    A4 --> A5[No Negative Resistance]
    B[Tunnel Diode Esaki] --> B1[Doping 10 to the 19 per cm cubed]
    B1 --> B2[Ultra-thin Depletion Region 10 nm]
    B2 --> B3[Electrons Tunnel Through Barrier]
    B3 --> B4[Tunneling Current Significant]
    B4 --> B5[Negative Differential Resistance NDR]
    A5 --> C[Application Rectifier Switch]
    B5 --> D[Application High-frequency Oscillator Amplifier]
```

### 4.5 Energy Level Diagram: Three-Level LASER System (ASCII Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P1[Ground State E1] -->|Pumping| P2[Excited State E3]
    P2 -->|Fast Non-radiative| P3[Metastable State E2]
    P3 -->|Stimulated Emission| P1
    P3 -.->|Spontaneous Emission Photon h nu| P1
```

**Explanation:** The atom is pumped from the ground state E1 to the excited state E3. It rapidly decays (non-radiatively) to the metastable state E2, where it stays for a relatively long time. When a photon of energy hν = E2 − E1 passes by, it triggers stimulated emission, and the atom drops to E1, releasing a second identical photon.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*What is meant by quantum tunneling? Mention any two practical applications of this phenomenon.*

**Model Answer:**
Quantum tunneling is the quantum mechanical phenomenon in which a particle with energy E less than the potential barrier height V₀ has a finite non-zero probability of crossing (penetrating) the barrier due to the wave-like nature of matter. The transmission probability decays exponentially with the barrier width.

**Two practical applications:**
1. **Tunnel Diode (Esaki Diode):** A heavily doped p-n junction where electrons tunnel through an extremely thin depletion region, producing a negative differential resistance used in high-frequency oscillators.
2. **Scanning Tunneling Microscope (STM):** Used to image surfaces at atomic resolution by measuring the tunneling current between a sharp tip and the sample surface.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**
*Define the Quality Factor of a resonant circuit. Write its expression in terms of L, C, and R.*

**Model Answer:**
The Quality Factor (Q-factor) of a resonant circuit is a dimensionless parameter that measures the sharpness of resonance. It is defined as 2π times the ratio of the total energy stored in the circuit to the energy dissipated per cycle.

**Expression:**

$$ Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R} = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}} $$

A higher Q-factor indicates a narrower, sharper resonance peak, which is desirable in radio tuning circuits and filters.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model Paper]**

**a)** Derive the expression for the transmission probability of a particle of energy E tunneling through a rectangular potential barrier of height V₀ (V₀ > E) and width a. State the approximations used. **(7 marks)**

**b)** An electron with energy 1.5 eV approaches a potential barrier of height 4 eV and width 0.8 nm. Calculate the tunneling probability. Take m = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg, ℏ = 1.0546 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, 1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Setup the Schrödinger equation in three regions:**

**Region I (x < 0):** $k^2 = \dfrac{2mE}{\hbar^2}$, solution $\psi_I = Ae^{ikx} + Be^{-ikx}$

**Region II (0 ≤ x ≤ a):** $\kappa^2 = \dfrac{2m(V_0 - E)}{\hbar^2}$, solution $\psi_{II} = Ce^{\kappa x} + De^{-\kappa x}$

**Region III (x > a):** $\psi_{III} = Fe^{ikx}$

**Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions:**

At x = 0: $\psi_I(0) = \psi_{II}(0)$ and $\psi_I'(0) = \psi_{II}'(0)$

At x = a: $\psi_{II}(a) = \psi_{III}(a)$ and $\psi_{II}'(a) = \psi_{III}'(a)$

**Step 3 — Solve the system for F/A:**

After algebra (see Section 3.1 above for the detailed derivation), the transmission coefficient is:

$$ T = \left[ 1 + \dfrac{V_0^2 \sinh^2(\kappa a)}{4E(V_0 - E)} \right]^{-1} $$

**Step 4 — Simplification for thick barrier (κa ≫ 1):**

Using $\sinh(\kappa a) \approx \dfrac{1}{2}e^{\kappa a}$:

$$ T \approx \dfrac{16E(V_0 - E)}{V_0^2} e^{-2\kappa a} $$

**Approximations used:**
- The barrier is wide enough that $\kappa a \gg 1$
- We consider only the dominant exponential term

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert energies:**

$$ E = 1.5 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 2.403 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} $$

$$ V_0 = 4 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 6.408 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} $$

$$ V_0 - E = 4.005 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} $$

**Step 2 — Compute κ:**

$$ \kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.11 \times 10^{-31} \times 4.005 \times 10^{-19}}}{1.0546 \times 10^{-34}} $$

$$ \kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{7.297 \times 10^{-49}}}{1.0546 \times 10^{-34}} = \dfrac{8.543 \times 10^{-25}}{1.0546 \times 10^{-34}} $$

$$ \kappa = 8.10 \times 10^{9} \text{ m}^{-1} $$

**Step 3 — Compute κa:**

$$ \kappa a = 8.10 \times 10^{9} \times 0.8 \times 10^{-9} = 6.48 $$

Since 6.48 > 1, the approximation is valid.

**Step 4 — Compute prefactor:**

$$ \dfrac{16E(V_0 - E)}{V_0^2} = \dfrac{16 \times 1.5 \times (4 - 1.5)}{4^2} = \dfrac{16 \times 1.5 \times 2.5}{16} = 3.75 $$

**Step 5 — Compute exponential:**

$$ e^{-2 \times 6.48} = e^{-12.96} \approx 2.34 \times 10^{-6} $$

**Step 6 — Final answer:**

$$ T \approx 3.75 \times 2.34 \times 10^{-6} = 8.78 \times 10^{-6} $$

$$ \boxed{T \approx 8.78 \times 10^{-6} \;\; (\text{or } 0.000878\%)} $$

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — December 2022 Model Paper]**

**a)** What is LASER? Explain the principle of stimulated emission and population inversion. **(7 marks)**

**b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Tunnel Diode. Why does a tunnel diode exhibit negative differential resistance? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Definition:** LASER stands for **Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation**. It is a device that produces a highly coherent, monochromatic, and collimated beam of light by the process of stimulated emission in a suitably pumped gain medium.

**Principle of Stimulated Emission:**

When an atom is in an excited energy state E₂, an external photon of energy hν = E₂ − E₁ can induce the atom to drop to the lower state E₁. During this transition, the atom emits a photon that is **identical** to the incident photon in:
- **Frequency** (monochromaticity)
- **Phase** (coherence)
- **Direction** (collimation)
- **Polarization**

This emitted photon can then trigger another excited atom to emit, leading to a chain reaction called an **avalanche of stimulated emissions**, producing an amplified light beam.

**Population Inversion:**

Under normal (thermal) conditions, more atoms occupy the lower energy level E₁ than the higher one E₂. In this state, absorption dominates over emission. To achieve net amplification, we must create a non-equilibrium state where **N₂ > N₁**. This unnatural condition is called **population inversion**, and it is achieved by an external energy source called the **pump** (electrical, optical, or chemical).

**Three-Level vs Four-Level Lasers:** A simple three-level system requires pumping more than half the atoms to E₂ for inversion, which is inefficient. A four-level system uses an intermediate level for faster depopulation, making the inversion easier.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Working of a Tunnel Diode — Block Diagram:**

A tunnel diode is a heavily doped p-n junction diode (doping ~10¹⁹ to 10²⁰ atoms/cm³). At such high doping, the depletion width becomes extremely narrow (~10 nm), which is the order of the electron de Broglie wavelength.

**Step-by-step operation:**

1. **Forward Bias — Low Voltage:** Filled states in the conduction band of the n-side align with empty states in the valence band of the p-side. Electrons tunnel through the thin barrier, producing a large **tunneling current**.

2. **Forward Bias — Increased Voltage:** The bands shift, and fewer filled states align with empty states. The tunneling current **decreases** even though the applied voltage is increasing — this is the **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)** region.

3. **Forward Bias — High Voltage:** The bands shift so much that the conduction band of the n-side aligns with the conduction band of the p-side. Tunneling is no longer possible at the junction, and the diode behaves like a normal forward-biased p-n junction.

**Why NDR occurs:**

The I-V curve has a region where $\dfrac{dI}{dV} < 0$ (current decreases with increasing voltage). This happens because the number of available states for tunneling depends on the **band alignment**, which changes with bias. In a small voltage range, increasing the bias actually *reduces* the overlap between filled and empty states, thus reducing the tunneling current.

**Applications:** High-speed switching circuits, microwave oscillators, frequency converters, and high-frequency amplifiers.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> **1. Unit Inconsistency Trap:** KTU examiners often mark zero if you compute κ in SI units (m⁻¹) but use energies in eV. Always convert eV → Joules **before** computing κ. (1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J.)
> 
> **2. Wrong Use of h vs ℏ:** Remember that ℏ = h / (2π) ≈ 1.0546 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, while h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s. The Schrödinger equation uses **ℏ**, not h.
> 
> **3. NDR Confusion:** Many students incorrectly state that "current becomes negative" in the NDR region. This is wrong — the current is still positive and flowing; only its *slope* dI/dV is negative.
> 
> **4. Forgetting Population Inversion:** When asked "Why is population inversion necessary?", students often give vague answers. State clearly: "Without population inversion, absorption (E₁ → E₂) dominates over stimulated emission (E₂ → E₁), and no net light amplification occurs."
> 
> **5. Missing the Phrase "Coherent" in LASER:** Always emphasize that LASER light is **coherent, monochromatic, and collimated** — this is the key difference from ordinary light.
> 
> **6. RLC Sign Error:** In the RLC equation, the damping term is $+R\dfrac{dq}{dt}$ (positive), but many students write it as negative. Remember, the resistor *dissipates* energy, so it appears as a positive damping coefficient in the differential equation.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Applications (Module 2, GBPHT121)**
> 
> - **Quantum Tunneling** is a purely wave-mechanical effect; classical particles cannot tunnel.
> - **Transmission coefficient** $T \approx \dfrac{16E(V_0 - E)}{V_0^2}e^{-2\kappa a}$ where $\kappa = \dfrac{\sqrt{2m(V_0 - E)}}{\hbar}$.
> - **Tunnel Diode** = Esaki Diode; key feature = **Negative Differential Resistance (NDR)**.
> - **STM** works because tunneling current is **exponentially sensitive** to tip-sample distance.
> - **LASER** requires three conditions: **stimulated emission, population inversion, optical resonator**.
> - **LASER properties:** Coherent, Monochromatic, Collimated, High Intensity.
> - **Resonant frequency of LC circuit:** $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$.
> - **Q-factor of resonant circuit:** $Q = \dfrac{1}{R}\sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$.
> - **Damping regimes:** Underdamped $(R^2 < 4L/C)$, Critically damped $(R^2 = 4L/C)$, Overdamped $(R^2 > 4L/C)$.
> - **Photoelectric equation:** $eV_s = h\nu - \phi$ (K.E. of photoelectron depends linearly on frequency, not intensity).
> - **Constants to memorize:** h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, ℏ = 1.0546 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, mₑ = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg, e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C.
> - **KTU 2024 Bloom's Level:** Expect **Apply-level** (L3) numericals on tunneling, resonance, and damping, and **Analyze-level** (L4) descriptive questions on LASER and Tunnel Diode working.
> - **Mark weightage tip:** Applications questions typically appear in **Module 2 Part B (14 marks)** with internal choice. Practice at least **5 full-length past year questions** before the ESE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Cells - V-I Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Solar Cells - V-I Characteristics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **Solar Cell** (also called a *photovoltaic cell*) is a specialized semiconductor p-n junction device that converts the energy of incident photons from sunlight directly into electrical energy through the **photovoltaic effect**. The **V-I (Voltage-Current) Characteristic** of a solar cell is the graphical and mathematical relationship between the terminal current $I$ delivered by the cell and the terminal voltage $V$ developed across it, under a specified level of illumination (irradiance) and temperature. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > A solar cell is essentially a *current source* in parallel with a diode. The V-I curve is non-linear and lies in the **fourth quadrant** of the conventional I-V plane (current flowing out of the positive terminal when illuminated). ## 1.2 The Photovoltaic Effect — The Underlying Physics The photovoltaic effect is the generation of a voltage and electric current in a material upon exposure to light. It is a quantum phenomenon comprising three sequential sub-processes: 1. **Photon Absorption:** A photon with energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ (band gap energy) is absorbed by a valence electron. 2. **Electron-Hole Pair (EHP) Generation:** The absorbed energy excites the electron from the valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), leaving behind a hole in the VB. 3. **Charge Separation & Collection:** The built-in electric field at the depletion region of the p-n junction sweeps the electron to the n-side and the hole to the p-side, producing a photo-generated current $I_L$. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Wheel Under a Waterfall" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy: Solar Cell = Intelligent Water Wheel** > > Imagine a water wheel placed under a controllable waterfall. > - The **waterfall** represents *sunlight (photons)*. > - The **rotating wheel** represents the *electron-hole pairs* generated in the semiconductor. > - The **flowing water onto buckets** corresponds to *photogenerated current $I_L$*. > - The **pipe at the bottom of the wheel** (with a valve) represents the *external load*. > - When the valve (load) is **fully closed**, water collects — this is the **Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{OC}$)** state. > - When the valve is **fully open**, water flows freely but the wheel has no back-pressure — this is the **Short-Circuit Current ($I_{SC}$)** state. > - Somewhere in between, the wheel extracts the **maximum mechanical power** — this is the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**. > > Just as the engineer tunes the valve to extract maximum power, the solar cell designer tunes the load to operate the cell at its **fill-factor-optimized** point. ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Symbol | Constant / Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | $q$ | Electronic charge | $\mathbf{1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ C}$ | | $k_B$ | Boltzmann constant | $\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ J/K}$ | | $h$ | Planck's constant | $\mathbf{6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ J \cdot s}$ | | $T$ | Cell operating temperature | **300 K** (standard test condition) | | $G$ | Standard irradiance | $\mathbf{1000\ W/m^2}$ (AM 1.5G spectrum) | | $E_g$ (Si) | Band gap of silicon | **1.12 eV** | | $A$ | Diode ideality factor | **1 to 2** (typically $\approx 1.3$) | | $I_0$ | Reverse saturation current | $\mathbf{10^{-12}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-8}\ A/cm^2}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** All solar cell V-I curves are universally measured at $G = 1000\ W/m^2$, $T = 25\ ^\circ C$, and Air Mass 1.5G spectrum. KTU problems assume STC unless otherwise stated. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Construction of a Typical Crystalline Silicon Solar Cell A standard silicon solar cell is a thin wafer (typically $\mathbf{200 - 300\ \mu m}$ thick) of single-crystal or multi-crystal silicon, fabricated as a p-n junction: - **p-type base:** Boron-doped silicon (majority carriers: holes). - **n-type emitter (top):** Phosphorus-doped silicon, very thin ($\approx 0.3\ \mu m$) (majority carriers: electrons). - **Front Contact:** A fine metallic grid (usually silver) on the n-side to allow light in while collecting electrons. - **Anti-Reflection Coating (ARC):** A thin layer of silicon nitride ($Si_3N_4$) or titanium dioxide ($TiO_2$) to reduce reflection losses. - **Back Contact:** A continuous metal layer (usually aluminum) on the p-side. ## 2.2 Equivalent Circuit of an Illuminated Solar Cell The illuminated solar cell can be modeled using the **Two-Diode Model** (more accurate) or the widely-used **Single-Diode Model** (preferred for KTU problems). ### 2.2.1 Single-Diode Equivalent Circuit The cell is represented as: - A constant **photo-generated current source $I_L$** in parallel with - A **p-n junction diode** (dark current $I_D$), in parallel with - A **shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** (representing leakage across the junction), all in series with - A **series resistance $R_s$** (representing contact and bulk resistance). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** $R_s$ degrades the **fill factor** and reduces the slope of the I-V curve near $V_{OC}$, while $R_{sh}$ degrades the slope near $I_{SC}$. An *ideal* solar cell has $R_s = 0$ and $R_{sh} = \infty$. ## 2.3 The I-V Characteristic Equation Applying **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** at the top node of the equivalent circuit, the terminal current $I$ delivered to the external load is: The current through the diode follows the **Shockley diode equation**: $$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + I R_s)}{A k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]$$ The current through the shunt resistance is: $$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$ The photogenerated current $I_L$ is supplied by the current source. Therefore, the complete **KTU Solar Cell I-V Equation** is: $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + I R_s)}{A k_B T}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$ For an *ideal* solar cell ($R_s = 0$, $R_{sh} = \infty$), this simplifies to the **canonical solar cell equation** (most tested in KTU): $$\boxed{\,I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{A k_B T}\right) - 1 \right]\,}$$ ## 2.4 Four Critical Operating Points on the V-I Curve The V-I characteristic has **four landmark points**, each carrying a specific physical meaning: | # | Point | Notation | Definition | Circuit Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Short-Circuit Point** | $I_{SC}$ | Current when $V = 0$ | Output terminals shorted | | 2 | **Open-Circuit Point** | $V_{OC}$ | Voltage when $I = 0$ | Output terminals open | | 3 | **Maximum Power Point** | $I_m,\ V_m$ | Operating point where $P = IV$ is maximum | Optimum load | | 4 | **Dark Current Point** | $I_0$ | Diode reverse saturation current | No illumination | ### 2.4.1 Short-Circuit Current ($I_{SC}$) When the terminals are shorted ($V = 0$), no current flows through the diode (since the diode sees zero bias) and no current flows through $R_{sh}$. Therefore the **entire photogenerated current flows out**: $$I_{SC} = I_L \quad (\text{ideal cell})$$ For a real cell: $I_{SC} = I_L - I_0(\exp(qI_{SC}R_s/Ak_BT) - 1) - I_{SC}R_s/R_{sh} \approx I_L$. ### 2.4.2 Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{OC}$) When the terminals are open ($I = 0$), the photogenerated current circulates entirely through the diode in the forward direction. Setting $I = 0$ in the canonical equation: $$0 = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{OC}}{Ak_BT}\right) - 1\right]$$ Solving for $V_{OC}$: $$\boxed{\,V_{OC} = \dfrac{A k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) \approx \dfrac{A k_B T}{q} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)\,}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** $V_{OC}$ depends on the **logarithm** of the light-generated current, while $I_{SC}$ depends **linearly** on irradiance. This is why $V_{OC}$ is *relatively* insensitive to light intensity, whereas $I_{SC}$ scales almost perfectly with irradiance. ### 2.4.3 Maximum Power Point and Fill Factor The output power is $P = I \cdot V$. The **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** is located at $(V_m, I_m)$ where $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$. The **Fill Factor (FF)** is the ratio of the maximum power to the product $V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}$: $$\boxed{\,FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}\,}$$ Typical silicon solar cell fill factors lie in the range **0.7 to 0.85**. ### 2.4.4 Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$) The **overall energy conversion efficiency** of the solar cell is defined as the ratio of maximum electrical power output to the incident optical power: $$\boxed{\,\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A_{cell}}\,}$$ where $G$ is the incident solar irradiance ($\mathbf{W/m^2}$) and $A_{cell}$ is the cell area ($\mathbf{m^2}$). ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU student must memorize for the V-I characteristics of solar cells. Vertical bars are written using `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact. | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Typical Unit / Range | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Canonical Solar Cell I-V Equation | $I = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{Ak_BT}\right) - 1\right]$ | Current in **A**, Voltage in **V** | | 2 | Real Solar Cell I-V Equation | $I = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V+IR_s)}{Ak_BT}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | Includes parasitic losses | | 3 | Short-Circuit Current | $I_{SC} = I_L$ | Typically **3 – 8 A** per cell | | 4 | Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{OC} = \dfrac{Ak_BT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Typically **0.5 – 0.7 V** for Si | | 5 | Thermal Voltage | $V_T = \dfrac{k_BT}{q}$ | $\mathbf{\approx 25.85\ mV}$ at 300 K | | 6 | Maximum Power | $P_{max} = V_m \cdot I_m$ | **Watts (W)** | | 7 | Fill Factor | $FF = \dfrac{V_m I_m}{V_{OC} I_{SC}}$ | Dimensionless, **0.7 – 0.85** | | 8 | Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{OC} I_{SC} FF}{G \cdot A_{cell}}$ | Dimensionless, **15 – 22 %** for c-Si | | 9 | Temperature Coefficient of $V_{OC}$ | $\dfrac{dV_{OC}}{dT} \approx -2\ mV/^\circ C$ (per cell) | Negative for Si | | 10 | Photocurrent vs Irradiance | $I_{SC} \propto G$ | Linear dependence | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Photovoltaic (PV) Power Plants:** Arrays of solar cells convert sunlight into grid-scale electricity. - **Satellite Power Systems:** Solar cells power nearly all Earth-orbiting satellites; III-V multi-junction cells ($\eta > 30\%$) are used. - **IoT & Wearable Electronics:** Flexible thin-film cells (CdTe, CIGS, perovskites) power sensors and watches. - **Electric Vehicles:** Solar roofs on EVs provide auxiliary charging. - **Solar-Pumped Lasers and Water Purification:** Remote-area power systems. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Canonical I-V Equation We derive the relationship between terminal current $I$ and terminal voltage $V$ for an *ideal* illuminated p-n junction. ### Step 1: Start with the Shockley Diode Equation in the Dark For a p-n junction diode in the dark, the current-voltage relationship is given by Shockley's equation: $$I_{dark} = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{Ak_BT}\right) - 1 \right]$$ This expresses that under forward bias, diffusion current dominates; under reverse bias, the current saturates to $-I_0$. ### Step 2: Introduce the Photogenerated Current When the junction is illuminated, photons of energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ generate electron-hole pairs. The built-in electric field at the depletion region separates these carriers, producing a unidirectional **photocurrent $I_L$** that flows from the n-side to the p-side internally (opposite to the conventional forward diode current). By the **principle of superposition** (valid in the low-injection regime), the total current is the sum of the dark diode current and the photogenerated current: $$I_{total} = I_{dark} - I_L$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Sign Convention for the External Load The current $I$ measured flowing **out** of the positive terminal to the external load is the negative of the internal junction current. The KTU convention defines $I$ as the output (load) current, which gives: $$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{Ak_BT}\right) - 1 \right]$$ This is the **canonical solar cell equation**. The negative sign of the diode current indicates it opposes the photocurrent. ### Step 4: Identify $V_{OC}$ — Set $I = 0$ When the external circuit is open, no current can flow out. The photogenerated current is balanced entirely by the forward-biased diode current: $$0 = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{OC}}{Ak_BT}\right) - 1\right]$$ Rearranging: $$I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{OC}}{Ak_BT}\right) - 1\right] = I_L$$ $$\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{OC}}{Ak_BT}\right) = \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1$$ Taking the natural logarithm on both sides: $$\dfrac{qV_{OC}}{Ak_BT} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$ Solving for $V_{OC}$: $$V_{OC} = \dfrac{Ak_BT}{q} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$ Since $I_L \gg I_0$ under typical illumination ($\sim 10^{10}$ times), the "+1" can be neglected to excellent approximation: $$V_{OC} \approx \dfrac{Ak_BT}{q} \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$ ### Step 5: Identify $I_{SC}$ — Set $V = 0$ When the terminals are shorted, the diode experiences zero bias. The exponential term becomes $\exp(0) = 1$, and therefore the diode current is zero: $$I = I_L - I_0[1 - 1] = I_L$$ Thus $I_{SC} = I_L$, confirming the photogenerated current is the short-circuit current. ### Step 6: Maximum Power Point Condition The power delivered to the load is $P(V) = V \cdot I(V)$. At the maximum power point, $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$: $$\dfrac{dP}{dV} = I + V \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0$$ Differentiating the canonical equation with respect to $V$: $$\dfrac{dI}{dV} = -\dfrac{q I_0}{Ak_BT} \exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_m}{Ak_BT}\right) = -\dfrac{q}{Ak_BT}(I_L - I_m + I_0)$$ Substituting back into $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$: $$I_m + V_m \cdot \left[-\dfrac{q}{Ak_BT}(I_L - I_m + I_0)\right] = 0$$ Let $V_T = \dfrac{k_BT}{q}$ be the thermal voltage ($\approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K). Then: $$I_m = \dfrac{(I_L + I_0)V_m}{A V_T + V_m} \approx \dfrac{I_L V_m}{A V_T + V_m}$$ This transcendental equation must be solved numerically (Newton-Raphson) for the exact $(V_m, I_m)$ pair. The result is then used to compute $FF$. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** A silicon solar cell has $I_L = 2.5\ A$, $I_0 = 10^{-10}\ A$, and ideality factor $A = 1.3$ at $T = 300\ K$. Compute (a) the open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$, (b) the short-circuit current $I_{SC}$, and (c) the thermal voltage $V_T$. ### Solution: **(a) Thermal Voltage:** $$V_T = \dfrac{k_BT}{q} = \dfrac{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}$$ $$V_T = \dfrac{4.143 \times 10^{-21}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585\ V = 25.85\ mV$$ **(b) Short-Circuit Current:** $$I_{SC} = I_L = 2.5\ A$$ **(c) Open-Circuit Voltage:** $$V_{OC} = A V_T \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$ $$\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} = \dfrac{2.5}{10^{-10}} = 2.5 \times 10^{10}$$ $$\ln(2.5 \times 10^{10}) = \ln(2.5) + 10 \ln(10) = 0.9163 + 23.0259 = 23.9422$$ $$V_{OC} = (1.3)(0.02585)(23.9422) = (0.03361)(23.9422)$$ $$V_{OC} \approx 0.8046\ V$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** This is the voltage a single silicon solar cell produces under open-circuit conditions with the given illumination. Real c-Si cells typically give $V_{OC} \approx 0.6 - 0.7\ V$ due to additional recombination losses (modeled by $A > 1$ and $I_0$ being larger than the ideal $10^{-15}\ A$). ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Effect of Real-World Parameters | Parameter Variation | Effect on $I_{SC}$ | Effect on $V_{OC}$ | Effect on $FF$ | Effect on $\eta$ | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Increase Irradiance $G$** | $\uparrow$ (linear) | $\uparrow$ (logarithmic) | $\uparrow$ slightly | $\uparrow$ | | **Increase Temperature $T$** | $\uparrow$ slightly | $\downarrow$ ($\sim -2$ mV/°C/cell) | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | | **Increase $R_s$** | Negligible | $\downarrow$ slightly | $\downarrow$ sharply | $\downarrow$ sharply | | **Decrease $R_{sh}$** | $\downarrow$ slightly | $\downarrow$ slightly | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | | **Larger $A$ (worse diode)** | Negligible | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | | **Larger $I_0$ (more recombination)** | Negligible | $\downarrow$ sharply | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Solar Cell Cross-Section (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram renders a **functional block-level architecture** of a crystalline silicon solar cell, mapping all layers and their physical roles. ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["SUNLIGHT
PHOTON FLUX
h nu greater than Eg"] node2["ANTI REFLECTION COATING
Si3N4 LAYER
Thickness lambda over 4"] node3["N TYPE EMITTER
Phosphorus Doped Si
Thickness 0.3 micrometre"] node4["PN JUNCTION DEPLETION REGION
Built in Electric Field
Charge Separation Zone"] node5["P TYPE BASE
Boron Doped Si
Thickness 200 to 300 micrometre"] node6["BACK CONTACT
Aluminum Metal Layer
Hole Collector"] node7["FRONT CONTACT GRID
Silver Fingers
Electron Collector"] node8["EXTERNAL LOAD
Circuit Output Terminals
Delivers IV Power"] node9["PHOTOGENERATED CURRENT IL
Flows from N to P internally
Driven by built in field"] node10["OUTPUT VOLTAGE V AND CURRENT I
Fourth Quadrant Operation
Solar Cell Supplies Power"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node3 --> node7 node7 --> node8 node6 --> node8 node4 --> node9 node9 --> node10 node8 --> node10 ``` ## 4.2 Equivalent Circuit Model (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["IL
Photocurrent Source
Depends on G"] nodeB["DIODE
Dark Current ID
Shockley Equation"] nodeC["Rsh
Shunt Resistance
Leakage Path"] nodeD["Rs
Series Resistance
Bulk and Contact"] nodeE["TERMINAL OUTPUT
V and I to Load"] nodeF["LOAD
External Circuit"] nodeG["NODE A TOP
KCL Applied Here"] nodeA --- nodeG nodeB --- nodeG nodeC --- nodeG nodeG --- nodeD nodeD --- nodeE nodeE --- nodeF ``` ## 4.3 I-V Characteristic Curve (Mermaid Block Schematic) The V-I curve of a solar cell lies in the **fourth quadrant** (positive $V$, negative internal current direction, with the current convention showing $I$ on the y-axis decreasing as $V$ increases). The diagram below maps the four key operating points. ```mermaid flowchart TB p1["ORIGIN
V equals 0 and I equals 0"] p2["POINT B ISC
Short Circuit Current
V equals 0 and I equals ISC
Maximum Current Point"] p3["POINT C VOC
Open Circuit Voltage
I equals 0 and V equals VOC
Maximum Voltage Point"] p4["POINT D MPP
Maximum Power Point
Vm and Im
Highest IV Product"] p5["QUADRANT IV REGION
Power Generating Region
Both V and I Positive to Load"] p6["IF V greater than VOC
Cell Acts as Forward Diode
Absorbs Power"] p7["IF V less than 0
Cell is Forward Biased Externally
Acts as LED"] p8["RECTANGLE VOC times ISC
Theoretical Maximum Power"] p9["RECTANGLE Vm times Im
Actual Maximum Power"] p10["FILL FACTOR FF
Ratio of Actual to Theoretical
Typical 0.7 to 0.85"] p1 --- p2 p2 --- p5 p3 --- p5 p5 --- p4 p4 --- p2 p4 --- p3 p6 --- p5 p7 --- p5 p8 --- p10 p9 --- p10 ``` ## 4.4 Typical V-I Curve Coordinates (Tabular Form) | Operating Point | Voltage $V$ (V) | Current $I$ (A) | Power $P = IV$ (W) | |---|---|---|---| | Short-Circuit (B) | 0 | $I_{SC} = 2.5$ | 0 | | Maximum Power (D) | $V_m = 0.45$ | $I_m = 2.2$ | **0.99** | | Open-Circuit (C) | $V_{OC} = 0.6$ | 0 | 0 | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set A]** **Define the open-circuit voltage ($V_{OC}$) of a solar cell. Obtain an expression for $V_{OC}$ in terms of the photocurrent $I_L$ and reverse saturation current $I_0$.** **Model Answer:** The **open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$** is defined as the terminal voltage developed across a solar cell when the external circuit is open, i.e., when the load current $I = 0$. It is the *maximum voltage* a solar cell can deliver. From the canonical solar cell equation with $I = 0$: $$0 = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV_{OC}}{Ak_BT}\right) - 1\right]$$ Rearranging and solving for $V_{OC}$: $$V_{OC} = \dfrac{Ak_BT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right) \approx \dfrac{Ak_BT}{q}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}\right)$$ This shows that $V_{OC}$ depends *logarithmically* on the light-generated current and the diode quality (through $A$ and $I_0$). --- ### **Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Set B]** **What is the fill factor (FF) of a solar cell? Why is it always less than unity?** **Model Answer:** The **fill factor (FF)** is the ratio of the maximum power $P_{max}$ obtainable from a solar cell to the product of its open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current: $$FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}$$ It is always less than unity ($FF < 1$) because of the inherent **non-linear** nature of the I-V curve, which prevents the rectangular area $V_{OC} \times I_{SC}$ from being fully utilized. Specifically, the diode's exponential current-voltage characteristic and parasitic resistances ($R_s$, $R_{sh}$) cause the curve to "bow inward," reducing the inscribed rectangle $V_m \times I_m$ below the ideal rectangle $V_{OC} \times I_{SC}$. Typical values for silicon cells are $0.7 \le FF \le 0.85$. ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign convention errors:** Students frequently confuse the direction of $I_L$ with the diode current. Remember: $I_L$ flows in the *forward* direction of the diode (it is generated by light, not by external bias). > 2. **Forgetting the "-1" in Shockley equation:** In $V_{OC}$ derivation, some students write $V_{OC} = (Ak_BT/q)\ln(I_L/I_0)$ directly without showing the intermediate step with the "-1" — this loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Unit mismatch:** Mixing $T$ in °C and $K$. KTU expects $T$ in **Kelvin** for the thermal voltage calculation. > 4. **FF definition confusion:** Some write $FF = P_{max}/P_{in}$ — that is *efficiency* $\eta$, not fill factor. FF is purely an *electrical* dimensionless parameter. > 5. **Series/parallel confusion:** $R_s$ is in *series* with the load; $R_{sh}$ is in *parallel* (shunt) with the diode. Drawing the equivalent circuit incorrectly costs 2 marks. --- ### **Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Dec Session]** **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the equivalent circuit of a solar cell. Derive the expression for the I-V characteristics of an ideal solar cell under illumination. **(b) [7 Marks]** A silicon solar cell has the following parameters: $I_L = 3.0\ A$, $I_0 = 1.5 \times 10^{-9}\ A$, ideality factor $A = 1.2$, and temperature $T = 300\ K$. Calculate (i) the open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$, (ii) the short-circuit current $I_{SC}$, and (iii) the thermal voltage $V_T$. Take $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ J/K$ and $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ C$. #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Equivalent Circuit and I-V Derivation:** **Step 1 — Draw the equivalent circuit:** The illuminated solar cell is modeled as a current source $I_L$ (representing photogenerated carriers) in parallel with a diode (representing the dark p-n junction current) and a shunt resistance $R_{sh}$, all in series with a series resistance $R_s$ and the external load terminals. **Step 2 — Apply KCL at the top node of the parallel combination:** The current $I$ flowing out of the positive terminal is: $$I = I_L - I_D - I_{sh}$$ where $I_D$ is the diode current and $I_{sh}$ is the current through the shunt resistance. **Step 3 — Express the diode current (Shockley equation):** The voltage across the diode is $V_D = V + I R_s$ (terminal voltage plus drop across series resistance). The diode current is: $$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{Ak_BT}\right) - 1 \right]$$ **Step 4 — Express the shunt current:** By Ohm's law: $$I_{sh} = \dfrac{V + I R_s}{R_{sh}}$$ **Step 5 — Combine all three contributions:** $$I = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{q(V+IR_s)}{Ak_BT}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$ For an *ideal* solar cell ($R_s = 0,\ R_{sh} = \infty$), the last two terms simplify: $$I = I_L - I_0\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{qV}{Ak_BT}\right) - 1\right]$$ This is the canonical I-V equation of an ideal solar cell. **(b) Numerical Computation:** **(i) Thermal Voltage:** $$V_T = \dfrac{k_BT}{q} = \dfrac{(1.381 \times 10^{-23})(300)}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 0.02585\ V = 25.85\ mV$$ **(ii) Short-Circuit Current:** At $V = 0$: $I_{SC} = I_L = 3.0\ A$. **(iii) Open-Circuit Voltage:** $$V_{OC} = A V_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$ $$\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} = \dfrac{3.0}{1.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.0 \times 10^{9}$$ $$\ln(2.0 \times 10^{9}) = \ln(2.0) + 9 \ln(10) = 0.6931 + 20.7233 = 21.4164$$ $$V_{OC} = (1.2)(0.02585)(21.4164) = 0.6644\ V \approx \mathbf{0.66\ V}$$ --- ### **Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Alternative Choice]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Define (i) fill factor and (ii) conversion efficiency of a solar cell. Explain with a labeled V-I characteristic curve the significance of $V_{OC}$, $I_{SC}$, and the maximum power point (MPP). **(b) [7 Marks]** A solar cell delivers $I_{SC} = 2.8\ A$ and $V_{OC} = 0.62\ V$ at $T = 300\ K$. The maximum power point is at $V_m = 0.50\ V$ and $I_m = 2.5\ A$. The cell area is $100\ cm^2$ and the incident solar power is $1000\ W/m^2$. Calculate (i) the fill factor, (ii) the maximum power, and (iii) the conversion efficiency. #### **Model Solution:** **(a) Definitions and V-I Curve:** **(i) Fill Factor (FF):** The ratio of maximum power to the ideal rectangle $V_{OC} \times I_{SC}$. It measures the "squareness" of the I-V curve. $$FF = \dfrac{P_{max}}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}} = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}}$$ **(ii) Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$):** The ratio of maximum electrical power output to the incident solar power: $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{OC} I_{SC} FF}{G \cdot A_{cell}}$$ **V-I Curve Significance:** - The I-V curve lies in the **fourth quadrant** — positive voltage with current flowing out of the positive terminal (power *generation* mode). - **$I_{SC}$ (y-intercept):** Maximum current deliverable; occurs when terminals are shorted. - **$V_{OC}$ (x-intercept):** Maximum voltage; occurs at open circuit. - **MPP (D):** The "knee" of the curve where the product $IV$ is maximized. Operating the cell at MPP via a Maximum Power Point Tracker (MPPT) extracts maximum energy. **(b) Numerical Computation:** **(i) Fill Factor:** $$FF = \dfrac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC}} = \dfrac{(0.50)(2.5)}{(0.62)(2.8)} = \dfrac{1.25}{1.736} = \mathbf{0.720}$$ **(ii) Maximum Power:** $$P_{max} = V_m \cdot I_m = (0.50)(2.5) = \mathbf{1.25\ W}$$ **(iii) Conversion Efficiency:** Cell area: $A_{cell} = 100\ cm^2 = 100 \times 10^{-4}\ m^2 = 0.01\ m^2$. Incident power: $P_{in} = G \cdot A_{cell} = 1000 \times 0.01 = 10\ W$. $$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{1.25}{10} = 0.125 = \mathbf{12.5\ \%}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Result Interpretation:** A 12.5% efficiency is in the typical range for commercial polycrystalline silicon cells (12 – 18%). High-efficiency monocrystalline cells achieve 18 – 22%, and laboratory multi-junction cells exceed 30%. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:** - **Solar cell = p-n junction + light.** The photovoltaic effect is the operational principle: photon absorption → EHP generation → charge separation by built-in field. - **Canonical I-V equation (MUST memorize):** $I = I_L - I_0[\exp(qV/Ak_BT) - 1]$. This is the **single most important equation** in Module 2. - **$I_{SC} = I_L$** (in the ideal case); occurs at $V = 0$. - **$V_{OC} = (Ak_BT/q)\ln(I_L/I_0 + 1)$**; occurs at $I = 0$. Depends *logarithmically* on light intensity. - **Fill Factor (FF)** measures the "squareness" of the I-V curve: $FF = V_m I_m / (V_{OC} I_{SC})$. Typical range: **0.7 to 0.85**. - **Efficiency** $\eta = V_{OC} I_{SC} FF / (G \cdot A_{cell})$; this is the *output* metric quoted on datasheets. - **Thermal voltage** $V_T = k_BT/q \approx 25.85$ mV at 300 K. **Always use $T$ in Kelvin.** - **$R_s$ is in series, $R_{sh}$ is in parallel (shunt).** $R_s \uparrow \Rightarrow FF \downarrow$. $R_{sh} \downarrow \Rightarrow$ leakage $\uparrow$, all metrics suffer. - **Temperature effect:** $V_{OC}$ has a *negative* temperature coefficient ($-2$ mV/°C per cell); $I_{SC}$ slightly *increases* with $T$. Net effect: efficiency *decreases* with temperature. - **Irradiance effect:** $I_{SC} \propto G$ (linear), $V_{OC} \propto \ln(G)$ (logarithmic). - **The I-V curve lies in the fourth quadrant** — solar cells *generate* power; they do not consume it (in the operating region). - **Equivalent circuit:** photocurrent source $I_L$ $\parallel$ diode $\parallel R_{sh}$, in series with $R_s$. - **Standard Test Conditions (STC):** $G = 1000\ W/m^2$, AM 1.5G, $T = 25\ ^\circ C$ (298 K). Use these unless told otherwise. - **For numerical problems:** Always show your KCL step, simplify the exponential, take $\ln$ on both sides, and substitute values with units. - **Common confusion to avoid:** "$V_{OC}$ is the voltage at which current is maximum" — *false*. $V_{OC}$ is the voltage when $I = 0$. $I_{SC}$ is the current when $V = 0$.

topics.title: Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Physics for Electrical Science**, **Efficiency ($\eta$)** is defined as the dimensionless ratio of the useful energy or power output delivered by an electrical system or device to the total energy or power input supplied to it. It is a fundamental performance metric that quantifies how effectively an electrical machine, transformer, or energy conversion device converts input electrical energy into the desired form of output energy.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Output Power}}{\text{Total Input Power}} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$$

Since $P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{loss}$, the efficiency can also be written as:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}} = \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{P_{loss}}{P_{out}}}$$

Efficiency is universally expressed as a **percentage** ($\%$) or as a **per-unit ratio (pu)** in power system analysis, with values satisfying the fundamental thermodynamic and physical constraint $0 < \eta \le 1$ (i.e., $0\% < \eta \le 100\%$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 2 of *Physics for Electrical Science*, efficiency is a central unifying concept that ties together energy losses (copper losses, iron/core losses, stray losses) in DC machines, transformers, and AC circuits. Mastery of this concept is essential for solving numerical problems on transformer regulation, motor performance, and power transmission lines.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Watering Can**
> Imagine you have a watering can with a small leak near the bottom. When you pour **10 litres** of water into the can, only **8 litres** reach the plants at the spout. The remaining **2 litres** are wasted through the leak. The efficiency of your watering can is therefore:
> $$\eta = \frac{8 \text{ L (useful)}}{10 \text{ L (input)}} = 0.8 = 80\%$$
> In an **electrical machine**, the "leak" represents energy dissipated as heat (due to resistance of windings), as sound, as vibration, and as hysteresis/eddy current losses in the magnetic core. The **useful output** is the mechanical work done (in a motor) or the electrical energy delivered (in a transformer/generator), and the **efficiency** tells us how much of our precious electrical "water" actually gets to where it needs to go. The lower the losses, the closer the efficiency gets to the **ideal value of 100%** — a target that is theoretically approachable but physically unattainable due to the laws of thermodynamics.

### 1.3 Categories of Efficiency in Electrical Systems

Electrical systems exhibit several distinct categories of efficiency, each capturing losses at different stages of energy conversion:

| Category | Definition | Typical Application |
|----------|------------|---------------------|
| **Electrical Efficiency** | Ratio of electrical output power to electrical input power | Transformers, Generators |
| **Mechanical Efficiency** | Ratio of mechanical power output to mechanical power input | Motors, Turbines |
| **Overall Efficiency** | Product of all individual stage efficiencies | Complete electromechanical systems |
| **Commercial Efficiency** | Ratio of useful output to total input including all losses | DC Machines, Induction Motors |

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following bolded constants and metrics are essential for KTU-level problem solving:

- **Joule's Constant of Heating (J):** $\mathbf{J = 4.186 \text{ J/cal}}$ — converts heat dissipated into equivalent electrical energy.
- **Resistivity of Copper at 20°C:** $\mathbf{\rho_{Cu} = 1.72 \times 10^{-8} \ \Omega \cdot m}$
- **Maximum Theoretical Efficiency of any heat engine:** $\mathbf{\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}}$ — the absolute upper bound.
- **Standard Reference Temperature for resistance correction:** $\mathbf{T_{ref} = 75°C}$ (for Class F/H insulation systems in KTU lab problems).
- **Unit of Efficiency:** Dimensionless (pu) or **percent (%)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Loss Mechanisms in Electrical Machines

Electrical devices lose energy through several distinct physical mechanisms. A rigorous understanding of these losses is the foundation for calculating efficiency in KTU board examinations.

**2.1.1 Copper Losses (I²R Losses / Ohmic Losses)**

When current $I$ flows through a conductor of resistance $R$, electrical energy is irreversibly converted to heat as described by **Joule's Law of Heating**:

$$P_{Cu} = I^2 R$$

These losses occur in the **armature winding**, **field winding**, and **brush contacts** of DC machines, and in the **primary and secondary windings** of transformers. Copper losses are **variable losses** — they scale with the square of the load current.

**2.1.2 Iron Losses / Core Losses ($P_{i}$)**

These occur in the ferromagnetic core of machines and transformers and consist of two sub-components:

- **Hysteresis Loss** ($P_h$): Energy dissipated due to the repeated reorientation of magnetic domains in the core material during each AC cycle. **Steinmetz's empirical relation** gives:

$$P_h = \eta_h \cdot B_{max}^{1.6} \cdot f \cdot V$$

where $\eta_h$ is the Steinmetz hysteresis coefficient, $B_{max}$ is the peak flux density, $f$ is the frequency, and $V$ is the core volume.

- **Eddy Current Loss** ($P_e$): Energy dissipated due to circulating currents induced in the core by the alternating flux. Given by:

$$P_e = \eta_e \cdot B_{max}^{2} \cdot f^2 \cdot t^2$$

where $\eta_e$ is the eddy current coefficient and $t$ is the lamination thickness. To minimize eddy currents, cores are constructed from **thin laminated sheets insulated from each other**.

Iron losses are **constant losses** — they are independent of the load and depend only on the supply voltage and frequency.

**2.1.3 Mechanical Losses ($P_{mec}$)**

These include **friction loss** (at bearings and brushes) and **windage loss** (air resistance on rotating parts). Mechanical losses are essentially **constant** at a given speed.

**2.1.4 Stray Load Losses ($P_{stray}$)**

These are additional losses arising from **leakage flux**, **harmonics**, and **non-uniform current distribution** in conductors. In KTU problems, they are often lumped with core losses or taken as a fixed percentage (typically **1% of output**).

### 2.2 Efficiency Formula for a DC Machine

For a **DC shunt motor** or **DC generator**, the efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{Cu} + P_{i} + P_{mec}}$$

where:
- $P_{out}$ = useful output power (mechanical for motor, electrical for generator)
- $P_{Cu}$ = total copper loss = $I_a^2 R_a$ (armature) + $I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$ (shunt field)
- $P_{i}$ = iron (core) loss
- $P_{mec}$ = mechanical (friction + windage) loss

### 2.3 Efficiency Formula for a Single-Phase Transformer

For a transformer, there are no mechanical losses, so the efficiency simplifies to:

$$\eta = \frac{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2}{V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2 + P_{Cu} + P_{i}}$$

where:
- $V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2$ = useful output power (real/active power delivered)
- $P_{Cu} = I_1^2 R_{01}$ (referred to primary) = full-load copper loss
- $P_{i}$ = constant core loss (equal to no-load power input)

### 2.4 Condition for Maximum Efficiency

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Theorem — Maximum Efficiency Condition**
> Efficiency is **maximum** when the **variable losses equal the constant losses**:
> $$P_{Cu} = P_{i} \quad \text{(for a transformer)}$$
> $$\text{Armature Cu Loss} = \text{Constant (Core + Friction + Windage) Loss} \quad \text{(for a DC machine)}$$

The **fraction of full load** at which maximum efficiency occurs is:

$$x = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{Cu, FL}}}$$

where $P_{Cu, FL}$ is the **full-load copper loss**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}}$ | General efficiency definition | Dimensionless or % |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{P_{loss}}{P_{out}}}$ | Alternate form emphasising losses | $P_{loss} = P_{in} - P_{out}$ |
| 3 | $P_{Cu} = I^2 R$ | Joule's law of heating (copper loss) | Watts (W) |
| 4 | $P_h = \eta_h B_{max}^{1.6} f V$ | Steinmetz hysteresis loss | W |
| 5 | $P_e = \eta_e B_{max}^{2} f^2 t^2$ | Eddy current loss | W |
| 6 | $P_i = P_h + P_e$ | Total iron/core loss | W |
| 7 | $R_{T_2} = R_{T_1}\left[1 + \alpha(T_2 - T_1)\right]$ | Resistance temperature correction | $\alpha_{Cu} = 0.00393 /°C$ |
| 8 | $x_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{P_i}{P_{Cu, FL}}}$ | Fraction of full load for $\eta_{max}$ | pu of full load |
| 9 | $\eta_{max} = \dfrac{x \cdot S \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cos\phi + 2 P_i}$ | Maximum efficiency of transformer | $S$ = rated apparent power |
| 10 | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | Carnot (thermodynamic upper limit) | $T$ in Kelvin |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of efficiency is the cornerstone of **power system design** and **energy economics**:

- **Power Transmission Lines:** High-voltage AC (HVAC) and HVDC systems are designed to operate at efficiencies above **95–98%** over hundreds of kilometres. A 1% efficiency loss in a 1000 MW line wastes 10 MW — enough to power 10,000 homes.
- **Electric Vehicles:** Modern EV traction motors (3-phase induction or PMSM) achieve efficiencies of **93–97%**, directly determining driving range.
- **Transformers:** Grid transformers are designed for efficiencies exceeding **99%** at full load; even 0.5% loss in a 500 MVA transformer wastes 2.5 MW continuously.
- **Industrial Motors:** A 10% improvement in motor efficiency in Indian industry can save over **10,000 GWh/year** of electrical energy — a key motivation behind KTU's emphasis on this topic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Efficiency of a Transformer

Let us rigorously derive the celebrated "variable loss = constant loss" condition for maximum efficiency in a single-phase transformer.

**Step 1 — Set up the efficiency expression.**

Consider a transformer delivering a load of apparent power $x \cdot S$ at power factor $\cos\phi$, where $x$ is the per-unit fraction of full load and $S$ is the rated apparent power.

The useful output (real) power is:
$$P_{out} = x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi$$

The copper loss at this load is proportional to the square of the load fraction:
$$P_{Cu,x} = x^2 \cdot P_{Cu,FL}$$

The iron loss $P_i$ remains constant (independent of load).

**Step 2 — Write the efficiency expression.**

$$\eta = \frac{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + x^2 \cdot P_{Cu,FL} + P_i}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the first-order optimality condition.**

For $\eta$ to be maximum, $\dfrac{d\eta}{dx} = 0$. It is mathematically simpler to work with the **reciprocal of efficiency**, or equivalently, to differentiate the loss-to-output ratio. Let us define:

$$f(x) = \frac{1}{\eta} - 1 = \frac{x^2 \cdot P_{Cu,FL} + P_i}{x \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{x \cdot P_{Cu,FL}}{S \cos\phi} + \frac{P_i}{x \cdot S \cos\phi}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate $f(x)$ with respect to $x$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{df}{dx} = \frac{P_{Cu,FL}}{S \cos\phi} - \frac{P_i}{x^2 \cdot S \cos\phi}$$

Setting $\dfrac{df}{dx} = 0$:

$$\frac{P_{Cu,FL}}{S \cos\phi} = \frac{P_i}{x^2 \cdot S \cos\phi}$$

The $S \cos\phi$ terms cancel:

$$P_{Cu,FL} = \frac{P_i}{x^2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x$ (the load fraction).**

$$x^2 = \frac{P_i}{P_{Cu,FL}}$$

$$\boxed{x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{Cu, FL}}}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify this is a maximum (second derivative test).**

$$\frac{d^2f}{dx^2} = \frac{2 P_i}{x^3 \cdot S \cos\phi} > 0 \quad \forall \ x > 0$$

Since $\dfrac{d^2 f}{dx^2} > 0$, the function $f(x)$ is **convex**, confirming that $\eta$ has a **maximum** at this critical point. The condition for maximum efficiency is therefore:

$$x^2 \cdot P_{Cu, FL} = P_i \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad P_{Cu, at\ max\ \eta} = P_{i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** At the point of maximum efficiency, every watt dissipated as copper loss is matched by a watt dissipated as iron loss. The total loss is then equally shared between the two loss categories, and any redistribution (by loading more or less) will always **increase the total loss** and hence **reduce the efficiency**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A 50 kVA, 2400/240 V single-phase transformer has a core loss of 180 W and full-load copper loss of 320 W. Calculate: (a) the efficiency at **half-load** at unity power factor, (b) the efficiency at **full load** at 0.8 power factor lagging, and (c) the load at which maximum efficiency occurs, and the value of this maximum efficiency at unity power factor.

**Step 1 — Extract given data.**

$S = 50 \text{ kVA} = 50{,}000 \text{ VA}$, $P_i = 180 \text{ W}$, $P_{Cu, FL} = 320 \text{ W}$.

**Step 2 (a) — Half-load efficiency at unity power factor.**

At $x = 0.5$, $\cos\phi = 1.0$:

$$P_{out} = 0.5 \times 50{,}000 \times 1.0 = 25{,}000 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{Cu} = (0.5)^2 \times 320 = 80 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{0.5} = \frac{25{,}000}{25{,}000 + 80 + 180} = \frac{25{,}000}{25{,}260}$$

$$\eta_{0.5} = 0.98971 = 98.97\%$$

**Step 3 (b) — Full-load efficiency at 0.8 power factor lagging.**

At $x = 1.0$, $\cos\phi = 0.8$:

$$P_{out} = 1.0 \times 50{,}000 \times 0.8 = 40{,}000 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{Cu} = (1.0)^2 \times 320 = 320 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{40{,}000}{40{,}000 + 320 + 180} = \frac{40{,}000}{40{,}500}$$

$$\eta_{FL} = 0.98765 = 98.77\%$$

**Step 4 (c) — Maximum efficiency load and value.**

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{Cu, FL}}} = \sqrt{\frac{180}{320}} = \sqrt{0.5625} = 0.75$$

So maximum efficiency occurs at **75% of full load**, i.e., at $0.75 \times 50 = 37.5$ kVA.

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{x_{max} \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi}{x_{max} \cdot S \cdot \cos\phi + 2 P_i}$$

At unity power factor ($\cos\phi = 1$):

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{0.75 \times 50{,}000 \times 1.0}{0.75 \times 50{,}000 \times 1.0 + 2 \times 180} = \frac{37{,}500}{37{,}500 + 360} = \frac{37{,}500}{37{,}860}$$

$$\eta_{max} = 0.99049 = 99.05\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification of the principle:** At $x = 0.75$, the copper loss is $P_{Cu} = (0.75)^2 \times 320 = 180 \text{ W}$, which exactly equals the iron loss $P_i = 180 \text{ W}$. The condition $P_{Cu} = P_i$ is satisfied — confirming our derivation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
