## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Power Flow Block Diagram of a Transformer

The following block diagram maps the **functional power flow** through a transformer, illustrating how input electrical power is partitioned into useful output and various loss channels.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inputA[Input Electrical Power Pin] --> coreB[Iron Core Path]
    inputA --> copperC[Copper Winding Path]
    coreB --> ironLossD[Iron Core Loss Pi - Constant]
    coreB --> outputE[Useful Output Power Pout - Delivered to Load]
    copperC --> copperLossF[Copper Loss PCu - Variable with Load Square]
    copperC --> strayLossG[Stray Load Loss Pstray]
    copperC --> outputE
    ironLossD --> totalLossH[Total Loss Ploss]
    copperLossF --> totalLossH
    strayLossG --> totalLossH
    totalLossH --> efficiencyI[Efficiency eta equals Pout divided by Pin]
    outputE --> efficiencyI
    inputA --> efficiencyI
```

### 4.2 Analytical Decision Tree: Solving Efficiency Problems

The following flowchart guides a KTU student through the **systematic algorithm** for solving any efficiency problem encountered in Module 2 examinations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Read the Problem Statement] --> identifyB{Identify the Device Type}
    identifyB --> devC[DC Machine]
    identifyB --> devD[Transformer]
    identifyB --> devE[AC Induction Motor]
    devC --> lossC1[List All Losses: Cu, Iron, Mechanical]
    devD --> lossD1[List All Losses: Cu, Iron, Stray]
    devE --> lossE1[List All Losses: Stator Cu, Rotor Cu, Iron, Mechanical, Stray]
    lossC1 --> computeC2[Compute Pout and Each Loss Term]
    lossD1 --> computeD2[Compute Pout and Each Loss Term]
    lossE1 --> computeE2[Compute Pout and Each Loss Term]
    computeC2 --> formulaF[Apply Efficiency Formula]
    computeD2 --> formulaF
    computeE2 --> formulaF
    formulaF --> maxQ{Question Asks for Maximum Efficiency}
    maxQ --> noQ1[Direct Substitution into eta Equation]
    maxQ --> yesQ2[Set Pvariable equals Pconstant and Solve for x]
    noQ1 --> finalR[Compute Final eta in Percent]
    yesQ2 --> finalR
```

### 4.3 Loss Categorization Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    totalLossA[Total Power Loss Ploss] --> constantB[Constant Losses - Independent of Load]
    totalLossA --> variableC[Variable Losses - Proportional to Load]
    constantB --> ironD[Iron Core Loss Pi]
    constantB --> mechE[Mechanical Loss Pmec: Friction and Windage]
    variableC --> copperF[Copper Loss PCu equals I squared R]
    variableC --> strayG[Stray Load Loss Pstray]
    ironD --> hystH[Hysteresis Loss Ph]
    ironD --> eddyI[Eddy Current Loss Pe]
    hystH --> dependH1[Depends on Bmax to power 1.6, frequency f]
    eddyI --> dependI1[Depends on Bmax squared, f squared, t squared]
    copperF --> dependF1[Depends on Load Current Squared and Winding Resistance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Every arrow in the Mermaid charts represents a **causal or computational dependency** (e.g., "Total Loss is the sum of Constant Losses and Variable Losses"). These diagrams are designed to function as **revision maps** — a student should be able to reproduce them from memory within 5 minutes before entering the examination hall.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

Define the term **efficiency** as applied to an electrical machine. State **two reasons** why the efficiency of a practical transformer is always less than 100%.

**Model Answer:**

Efficiency ($\eta$) of an electrical machine is defined as the ratio of useful output power to the total input power, expressed as a percentage:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\%$$

**Two reasons** why practical transformer efficiency is less than 100%:

1. **Copper Losses ($I^2R$):** The primary and secondary winding resistances dissipate energy as heat whenever current flows. These are variable losses, proportional to the square of the load current.

2. **Iron / Core Losses ($P_i$):** The alternating magnetic flux in the ferromagnetic core causes **hysteresis loss** (due to domain realignment) and **eddy current loss** (due to induced circulating currents). These are constant losses, present even at no-load.

*Additionally, stray load losses and dielectric losses further reduce efficiency below the ideal 100%.*

**[3 Marks — 1 for definition + 2 for the two reasons]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

State the condition for **maximum efficiency** of a transformer. Why are transformers designed to deliver maximum efficiency at loads **close to full load** rather than at no-load or at very light loads?

**Model Answer:**

**Condition for maximum efficiency:** Efficiency of a transformer is maximum when the **variable (copper) loss equals the constant (iron) loss**:

$$P_{Cu} = P_i \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad x^2 \cdot P_{Cu, FL} = P_i$$

**Why maximum efficiency is designed near full load:**

Since transformers are rated to operate continuously near their full-load capacity in power systems, designers deliberately choose the core material and winding cross-section so that $P_{Cu, FL} \approx P_i$. This ensures that during the **majority of the transformer's operational life** (when it is heavily loaded), the efficiency remains near its maximum. If the design were optimized for very light loads, the unit would be inefficient during its typical high-demand hours, wasting enormous amounts of energy across the grid.

**[3 Marks — 1 for condition + 2 for design justification]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** A 100 kVA, 2000/200 V, single-phase, 50 Hz transformer has the following test data:

- **Open-Circuit Test (OCT) on LV side:** 200 V, 4 A, 120 W
- **Short-Circuit Test (SCT) on HV side:** 60 V, 50 A, 600 W

Calculate the efficiency of the transformer at **(i) full load** and **(ii) 75% of full load**, at a power factor of **0.85 lagging**. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** Derive the condition for **maximum efficiency** of a transformer. At what fraction of the full load will the transformer in part (a) operate at maximum efficiency? **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Identifying the losses from test data:**

- From the **Open-Circuit Test (OCT)**, the no-load input power gives the **iron loss** (since the no-load copper loss is negligible):
$$P_i = 120 \text{ W}$$

- From the **Short-Circuit Test (SCT)**, the short-circuit input power at rated current gives the **full-load copper loss**:
$$P_{Cu, FL} = 600 \text{ W}$$

**Part (a)(i) — Full load efficiency at 0.85 power factor lagging:**

Output power at full load:
$$P_{out, FL} = S \cdot \cos\phi = 100{,}000 \times 0.85 = 85{,}000 \text{ W}$$

Total losses at full load:
$$P_{loss, FL} = P_i + P_{Cu, FL} = 120 + 600 = 720 \text{ W}$$

Efficiency:
$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{85{,}000}{85{,}000 + 720} = \frac{85{,}000}{85{,}720}$$

$$\eta_{FL} = 0.99160 = 99.16\%$$

**Part (a)(ii) — 75% load efficiency at 0.85 power factor lagging:**

At $x = 0.75$:

$$P_{out, 0.75} = 0.75 \times 100{,}000 \times 0.85 = 63{,}750 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{Cu, 0.75} = (0.75)^2 \times 600 = 337.5 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{loss, 0.75} = 120 + 337.5 = 457.5 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{0.75} = \frac{63{,}750}{63{,}750 + 457.5} = \frac{63{,}750}{64{,}207.5}$$

$$\eta_{0.75} = 0.99287 = 99.29\%$$

**Part (b) — Derivation of maximum efficiency condition:**

(Refer to the exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1 above. The student must present the steps:

1. Write $\eta = \dfrac{x S \cos\phi}{x S \cos\phi + x^2 P_{Cu,FL} + P_i}$
2. Define $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\eta} - 1 = \dfrac{x P_{Cu,FL}}{S \cos\phi} + \dfrac{P_i}{x S \cos\phi}$
3. Set $\dfrac{df}{dx} = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{P_{Cu,FL}}{S \cos\phi} = \dfrac{P_i}{x^2 S \cos\phi}$
4. Solve: $x^2 = \dfrac{P_i}{P_{Cu,FL}}$
5. Conclude: $P_{Cu, at\ max} = P_i$ ✓)

**Part (b) — Fraction of full load for maximum efficiency:**

$$x_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{P_i}{P_{Cu, FL}}} = \sqrt{\frac{120}{600}} = \sqrt{0.2} = 0.4472$$

So the transformer operates at maximum efficiency at **44.72% of full load** (i.e., at $0.4472 \times 100 = 44.72$ kVA).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a clear block diagram, explain the various **power stages** in a DC shunt motor from input electrical power to output mechanical power. Define **electrical efficiency**, **mechanical efficiency**, and **overall efficiency**, and establish their relationship. **\[7 Marks\]**

**(b)** A 220 V DC shunt motor takes a no-load current of 4 A and runs at 1500 rpm. The armature resistance is $R_a = 0.25 \ \Omega$ and the shunt field resistance is $R_{sh} = 110 \ \Omega$. At full load, the motor draws 50 A. Calculate the efficiency of the motor at full load, given that the brush contact drop is 1 V per brush (2 brushes in total). **\[7 Marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Power stages in a DC shunt motor (Block Diagram):**

The power flows through the following stages:

$$\boxed{P_{in} \to P_{Cu,sh} \to P_{brush} \to P_{Cu,a} \to P_{iron} \to P_{mec} \to P_{out}}$$

Where:
- $P_{in} = V I_L$ (input electrical power from supply)
- $P_{Cu,sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh}$ (shunt field copper loss)
- $P_{Cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a$ (armature copper loss)
- $P_{brush} = I_a \times V_{brush}$ (brush contact loss)
- $P_{iron}$ = iron (core) loss
- $P_{mec}$ = friction and windage loss
- $P_{out}$ = useful mechanical power at the shaft

**Definitions and Relationship:**

$$\eta_{electrical} = \frac{P_{electrical\ developed\ in\ armature}}{P_{in}} = \frac{E_b I_a}{V I_L}$$

$$\eta_{mechanical} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{electrical\ developed\ in\ armature}} = \frac{P_{out}}{E_b I_a}$$

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \eta_{electrical} \times \eta_{mechanical}$$

This is the **key product relationship** that examiners expect students to state explicitly.

**Part (b) — Efficiency calculation:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the shunt field current:
$$I_{sh} = \frac{V}{R_{sh}} = \frac{220}{110} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2:** At full load, the armature current is:
$$I_a = I_L - I_{sh} = 50 - 2 = 48 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3:** Back-EMF (assuming brush drop of 2 V total):
$$E_b = V - I_a R_a - V_{brush,total} = 220 - 48 \times 0.25 - 2 = 220 - 12 - 2 = 206 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4:** At no-load, $I_{a,NL} = I_L - I_{sh} = 4 - 2 = 2$ A, and the no-load input power equals the constant losses:
$$P_{const} = V I_{L,NL} - I_{a,NL}^2 R_a = 220 \times 4 - (2)^2 \times 0.25 = 880 - 1 = 879 \text{ W}$$

(This represents iron + mechanical + brush losses at no-load.)

**Step 5:** Total losses at full load:
$$P_{Cu,sh} = I_{sh}^2 R_{sh} = (2)^2 \times 110 = 440 \text{ W}$$
$$P_{Cu,a} = I_a^2 R_a = (48)^2 \times 0.25 = 576 \text{ W}$$
$$P_{brush} = I_a \times 2 = 48 \times 2 = 96 \text{ W}$$
$$P_{const} = 879 \text{ W (iron + mechanical, approximately unchanged at full load)}$$

$$P_{loss, FL} = 440 + 576 + 96 + 879 = 1991 \text{ W}$$

**Step 6:** Input power at full load:
$$P_{in} = V I_L = 220 \times 50 = 11{,}000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 7:** Output power and efficiency:
$$P_{out} = P_{in} - P_{loss} = 11{,}000 - 1991 = 9009 \text{ W}$$

$$\eta_{FL} = \frac{9009}{11{,}000} = 0.819 = 81.9\%$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> **Common ways students LOSE marks in efficiency problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the shunt field current** when computing armature current: Always compute $I_{sh} = V/R_{sh}$ first and subtract from line current.
> 2. **Ignoring brush contact drop:** In DC machine problems where brush drop is specified (e.g., 1 V/brush), failing to subtract $I_a \times V_{brush,total}$ from $V$ when calculating $E_b$ leads to a **3–5% error** in efficiency.
> 3. **Confusing no-load constant losses:** At no-load, the input power is NOT zero. It equals $P_{const} + I_{a,NL}^2 R_a$ (where the latter is often negligible, but not always — show your work).
> 4. **Misapplying the OCT/SCT convention:** In transformer problems, the OCT gives **iron loss** (not copper loss), and the SCT gives **copper loss** (not iron loss). Mixing these up is a **favourite trap** in KTU papers.
> 5. **Forgetting the $\cos\phi$ factor:** In transformer efficiency at non-unity power factor, students frequently compute $P_{out} = V_2 I_2$ instead of $V_2 I_2 \cos\phi_2$ — this yields an efficiency > 100%, an automatic **zero** in valuation.
> 6. **Skipping units and significant figures:** KTU examiners deduct **½ to 1 mark** for missing units or for rounding to incorrect significant figures in the final answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Efficiency (Module 2, GBPHT121)**

- ☐ **Definition:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss}}$; always expressed as % or pu in the range $(0, 1]$.
- ☐ **Losses are of TWO types:** **Constant losses** (iron + mechanical, independent of load) and **Variable losses** (copper + stray, scale with $I^2$).
- ☐ **Maximum efficiency condition:** $P_{variable} = P_{constant}$. For transformers, this is $P_{Cu, at\ max} = P_i$.
- ☐ **Load fraction for $\eta_{max}$:** $x_{max} = \sqrt{P_i / P_{Cu, FL}}$.
- ☐ **OCT yields iron loss; SCT yields copper loss** in transformer problems — never the reverse.
- ☐ **Hysteresis loss** $\propto B_{max}^{1.6} f$; **Eddy current loss** $\propto B_{max}^2 f^2 t^2$ — both contribute to iron loss.
- ☐ **DC machine efficiency:** include brush contact drop and compute armature current $I_a = I_L - I_{sh}$ carefully.
- ☐ **Transformer efficiency:** always multiply $V_2 I_2$ by $\cos\phi_2$ to get the real output power; reactive (VAR) power does no useful work.
- ☐ **Resistance temperature correction:** $R_{T_2} = R_{T_1}[1 + \alpha(T_2 - T_1)]$; KTU problems frequently use $T_{ref} = 75°C$ with $\alpha_{Cu} = 0.00393 /°C$.
- ☐ **Carnot limit** is the absolute ceiling: $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - T_C/T_H$; practical machines fall well below this thermodynamic bound.
- ☐ **Always state units** (W for power, V·A for apparent, pu or % for efficiency) and **show intermediate steps** — KTU valuation awards method marks generously but deducts harshly for skipped logic.
- ☐ **Sanity check:** if your computed $\eta > 1$ (or $> 100\%$), you have a sign error, a missing $\cos\phi$, or an incorrectly subtracted loss — re-verify immediately.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stringing of Solar Cells to Solar Panel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stringing of Solar Cells to Solar Panel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Stringing of Solar Cells** is the manufacturing process in which individual photovoltaic (PV) cells are electrically interconnected in series (and optionally in parallel) using metallic ribbon conductors (typically tinned copper ribbons coated with solder) to form a functional electrical circuit known as a **String**, which is subsequently encapsulated along with other strings inside a laminate structure to produce a finished **Solar Panel** (or **Solar Module**).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the term *stringing* explicitly refers to the **series interconnection** of solar cells using tab wires/ribbons, while *module assembly* refers to the larger process of combining multiple strings (series-parallel matrix), encapsulating them between a tempered front glass, an EVA (Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate) backsheet, and framing the assembly with an aluminum profile to yield the final weather-protected solar panel.

A **Solar Cell** is a single p-n junction semiconductor device (usually crystalline silicon) that converts incident photons into direct-current (DC) electricity through the photovoltaic effect. A typical commercial **156 mm × 156 mm mono-crystalline silicon cell** produces approximately:
- **Open-circuit voltage (Voc) ≈ 0.6 V to 0.65 V**
- **Short-circuit current (Isc) ≈ 8 A to 9 A**
- **Maximum power (Pmax) ≈ 4 W to 5 W**

These individual voltages are too low for any practical application. Hence, cells must be **strung together**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The process of stringing is a critical topic under the unit dealing with the conversion of solar energy into electrical energy. Students are expected to understand the electrical consequences of series vs. parallel connections, layout design, the role of bypass and blocking diodes, and the fundamental cell-to-module efficiency relationships.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a chain of small water pumps, each of which can only push water up a height of 0.6 meters (the *voltage* produced by one cell). A single pump is useless for filling a tank placed 30 meters high. However, if we **connect these pumps mechanically in a line** (series stringing), the pressure at the end of the line becomes the sum of all individual pressures. If we have 60 pumps in series, the total pressure head becomes $60 \times 0.6 = 36$ meters — a useful, practical value.

Now imagine several such chains of pumps placed side-by-side, with their inlets joined to a common reservoir and outlets joined to a common delivery pipe. This is a **parallel combination of strings**, which multiplies the *flow rate* (current) while keeping the *pressure* (voltage) the same as one chain.

In solar terminology:
- **Series String** → Adds **voltages**, current stays the same as one cell.
- **Parallel Combination of Strings** → Adds **currents**, voltage stays the same as one string.

The **stringing process** is literally the act of "chaining" solar cells with thin solder-coated copper ribbons (called *tabs* or *tabbing wire*) and soldering them onto the busbars of adjacent cells to form a continuous electrical path.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Solar Panel Design:**
> - Standard Test Conditions (STC): Irradiance **G = 1000 W/m²**, Cell temperature **T = 25 °C (298.15 K)**, Air mass **AM = 1.5**
> - Tinned copper tabbing wire: width **1.5 mm to 2 mm**, thickness **0.15 mm to 0.2 mm**
> - Cell efficiency (commercial mono-Si): **20 % to 24 %**
> - Module efficiency (encapsulated panel): **17 % to 22 %**
> - Number of cells in a standard panel: **60, 72, 120 (half-cell), or 144 (half-cell)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the Stringing Process

The transition from a single solar cell to a fully operational solar panel follows a strict manufacturing hierarchy. Each level has distinct electrical characteristics that the KTU examiner expects the student to derive and compare.

**Step 1: Single Solar Cell (Base Unit)**
- A single cell is a current source in parallel with a diode.
- Parameters: $V_{oc,\,cell}$, $I_{sc,\,cell}$, $V_{mp,\,cell}$, $I_{mp,\,cell}$, $P_{max,\,cell} = V_{mp,\,cell} \times I_{mp,\,cell}$.
- Fill Factor: $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$

**Step 2: Series String of Ns Cells**
- All cells connected positive-to-negative in a single loop.
- Current is dictated by the weakest cell: $I_{string} = \min(I_{cell,\,i})$.
- Voltage adds: $V_{string} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$.
- The string is the building block of every commercial panel.
- A typical **60-cell panel** uses **10 strings of 6 cells**, while a **72-cell panel** uses **6 strings of 12 cells**.

**Step 3: Parallel Combination of Np Strings**
- Multiple strings connected positive-to-positive and negative-to-negative.
- Current adds: $I_{module} = N_p \cdot I_{string}$.
- Voltage is fixed by the string voltage: $V_{module} = V_{string}$.
- This step is rare in residential panels but is common in utility-scale modules and in *bifacial* modules where rear-side current must be summed.

**Step 4: Module / Panel Assembly**
- The series-parallel matrix is laminated between EVA + glass + backsheet.
- **Bypass diodes** (usually 1 per 20–24 cells) are anti-parallel connected across substrings to mitigate hot-spot heating during partial shading.
- **Blocking diodes** (optional, in off-grid panels) are placed in series with the string to prevent reverse current flow from the battery at night.

**Step 5: Junction Box & Frame**
- All string outputs are routed to a weather-resistant **IP67/IP68 junction box** mounted on the rear of the panel.
- Frame is **anodized aluminum** for mechanical rigidity and corrosion resistance.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Stringing Decisions

| Design Choice | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|
| Use **series** first, not parallel | Voltage is more efficient to transmit; current is what causes $I^2R$ heating losses in cables. |
| Limit string voltage to ~**40 V** | Safety: SELV (Safety Extra Low Voltage) threshold per IEC 61730 is 60 V DC; keeping below 40 V avoids hazards during installation. |
| Use **bypass diodes** | A shaded cell becomes reverse-biased and dissipates power as heat. A bypass diode shorts that substring, preventing thermal runaway. |
| **Tabbing + Stringing** are separate steps | Tabbing connects the front of one cell to the back of the next; stringing aligns multiple tabbed cells linearly. |
| **Half-cell** technology | Cuts cell in half → halves $I_{sc}$ → reduces resistive loss ($I^2R$) by 4×, improving module efficiency by ~2–3 %. |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Symbolic Form | Numerical / Units Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Cells in a String | $N_s$ | Integer, typically 6, 10, 12, 20, 24 |
| 2 | Strings in Parallel | $N_p$ | Integer, typically 1 (residential) |
| 3 | String Voltage (Open Circuit) | $V_{oc,\,str} = N_s \cdot V_{oc,\,cell}$ | Volts (V) |
| 4 | String Current (Short Circuit) | $I_{sc,\,str} = I_{sc,\,cell}$ | Amperes (A) |
| 5 | Module Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc,\,mod} = N_s \cdot V_{oc,\,cell}$ | V |
| 6 | Module Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc,\,mod} = N_p \cdot I_{sc,\,cell}$ | A |
| 7 | Maximum Module Power | $P_{max,\,mod} = N_s \cdot N_p \cdot V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | Watts (W) |
| 8 | Fill Factor (any level) | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Dimensionless, 0.6 – 0.85 |
| 9 | Cell-to-Module (CTM) Loss | $\eta_{CTM} = \dfrac{P_{mod}}{N_s \cdot N_p \cdot P_{cell}} \times 100$ | %, typically −2 % to −4 % |
| 10 | Series Resistance Loss (String) | $P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{s,\,total}$ | W |
| 11 | Total Tab Resistance | $R_{tab} = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$ (copper $\rho = 1.68 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$) | Ω |
| 12 | Bypass Diode Forward Drop | $V_F \approx 0.4\ \text{V}$ to $0.7\ \text{V}$ (Schottky) | V |
| 13 | Blocking Diode Forward Drop | $V_F \approx 0.7\ \text{V}$ (Si pn-junction) | V |
| 14 | Mismatch Loss Factor | $m = 1 - \dfrac{\min(P_i)}{\max(P_i)}$ | Dimensionless |
| 15 | Hot-Spot Power Dissipated | $P_{hs} = V_{rev} \cdot I_{sc}$ | W (must be $< P_{rated}/3$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Stringing design is the single most critical decision in PV module manufacturing. A poorly designed string layout leads to severe **mismatch losses** (3 % to 12 %), accelerated **Potential-Induced Degradation (PID)**, and catastrophic **hot-spot failures**. The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly emphasizes the ability of a student to *quantify* the electrical output of a panel given $N_s$ and $N_p$, and to *justify* the placement of bypass diodes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From a Single Cell to a Solar Panel

**Given Data (Typical Commercial Mono-Si Cell):**
- $V_{oc,\,cell} = 0.65\ \text{V}$
- $I_{sc,\,cell} = 9.0\ \text{A}$
- $V_{mp,\,cell} = 0.55\ \text{V}$
- $I_{mp,\,cell} = 8.4\ \text{A}$

**Target: Design a 60-cell (10 × 6) module.**

#### Step 1 — Power Produced by a Single Cell
The maximum power delivered by one cell is the product of its voltage and current at the maximum power point (MPP).

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max,\,cell} &= V_{mp,\,cell} \times I_{mp,\,cell} \\
P_{max,\,cell} &= 0.55\ \text{V} \times 8.4\ \text{A} \\
P_{max,\,cell} &= 4.62\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This is the electrical work done by one p-n junction under STC. It is the fundamental energy quantum that must be summed in series and parallel to build the module.

#### Step 2 — Series String of $N_s = 6$ Cells
In a series string, voltages add linearly while the current remains governed by the cell with the lowest photo-generated current.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{mp,\,str} &= N_s \cdot V_{mp,\,cell} = 6 \times 0.55 = 3.30\ \text{V} \\
I_{mp,\,str} &= I_{mp,\,cell} = 8.4\ \text{A} \\
P_{max,\,str} &= V_{mp,\,str} \times I_{mp,\,str} = 3.30 \times 8.4 = 27.72\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The string is essentially a "battery of 6 cells in series." Total power = 6 × 4.62 = 27.72 W, which matches. This is the proof that power conservation holds across a perfect series connection.

#### Step 3 — Open-Circuit and Short-Circuit Parameters of the String

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oc,\,str} &= N_s \cdot V_{oc,\,cell} = 6 \times 0.65 = 3.90\ \text{V} \\
I_{sc,\,str} &= I_{sc,\,cell} = 9.0\ \text{A} \\
FF_{str} &= \frac{V_{mp,\,str} \cdot I_{mp,\,str}}{V_{oc,\,str} \cdot I_{sc,\,str}} = \frac{3.30 \times 8.4}{3.90 \times 9.0} \\
FF_{str} &= \frac{27.72}{35.10} \approx 0.7897
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** The Fill Factor remains unchanged when identical cells are connected in series. This is a key KTU concept — FF is an *intrinsic property* of the I-V curve shape, not of the cell count.

#### Step 4 — Module of $N_p = 10$ Parallel Strings
A 60-cell panel uses 10 such strings in parallel.

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{mp,\,mod} &= V_{mp,\,str} = 3.30\ \text{V} \times 10 = 33.0\ \text{V} \\
I_{mp,\,mod} &= N_p \cdot I_{mp,\,str} = 10 \times 8.4 = 84.0\ \text{A} \\
P_{max,\,mod} &= V_{mp,\,mod} \times I_{mp,\,mod} = 33.0 \times 84.0 = 2772\ \text{W} \\
P_{max,\,mod} &= 2.772\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Now the module produces 2.77 kW. In practice, real modules produce slightly less due to **CTM (Cell-to-Module) losses** of 2 % to 4 % caused by:
1. Resistive losses in the tabbing wires ($I^2R$).
2. Optical losses in the encapsulant (EVA reflection and absorption).
3. Mismatch between cells.

#### Step 5 — Incorporating CTM Loss (η_CTM = 96 %)

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{mod,\,actual} &= \eta_{CTM} \times P_{max,\,mod} \\
P_{mod,\,actual} &= 0.96 \times 2772\ \text{W} \\
P_{mod,\,actual} &= 2661.12\ \text{W} \approx 2.66\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** This is the **rated power** that appears on the panel's nameplate. A "250 W" panel, in practice, can be expected to produce 240 W to 245 W under field conditions.

#### Step 6 — Bypass Diode Design
For a 60-cell panel, three bypass diodes are used, each protecting a substring of 20 cells. The forward voltage drop is $V_F = 0.5\ \text{V}$ (Schottky).

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{string,\,protected} &= 20 \times 0.55 = 11.0\ \text{V} \\
P_{loss,\,diode} &= V_F \cdot I_{sc,\,mod} = 0.5 \times 9.0 = 4.5\ \text{W}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** When a substring is shaded, the bypass diode turns on and **short-circuits** the shaded section, protecting it from reverse-bias destruction. The trade-off is a 4.5 W conduction loss in the diode, which is far less than the hundreds of watts that would be dissipated in the shaded cells.

#### Step 7 — Resistive Loss in Tabbing Wires
Assume 10 cells in series, each connected by 2 tabs of length $L = 0.156\ \text{m}$, width $w = 0.002\ \text{m}$, thickness $t = 0.0002\ \text{m}$, copper resistivity $\rho_{Cu} = 1.68 \times 10^{-8}\ \Omega \cdot m$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{tab} &= w \cdot t = 0.002 \times 0.0002 = 4 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2 \\
R_{tab} &= \rho_{Cu} \cdot \frac{L}{A_{tab}} = 1.68 \times 10^{-8} \cdot \frac{0.156}{4 \times 10^{-7}} \\
R_{tab} &= 6.552 \times 10^{-3}\ \Omega = 6.55\ \text{m}\Omega \\
P_{loss,\,tab} &= I^2 \cdot R_{tab} = (8.4)^2 \cdot 6.55 \times 10^{-3} \\
P_{loss,\,tab} &= 0.462\ \text{W per tab}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** With 11 tabs per string and 10 strings in the module, total tab losses sum to:
$$
P_{loss,\,total} = 11 \times 10 \times 0.462 = 50.8\ \text{W}
$$

This single contribution alone justifies the use of **multi-busbar (MBB)** and **half-cut cell** technologies in modern panels, where current is halved and thus $I^2R$ losses drop by 75 %.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Series vs. Parallel Stringing

| Property | Series String ($N_s$ cells) | Parallel Strings ($N_p$ strings) |
|---|---|---|
| Voltage | $V_{total} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$ | $V_{total} = V_{string}$ (unchanged) |
| Current | $I_{total} = I_{cell}$ (weakest cell governs) | $I_{total} = N_p \cdot I_{string}$ |
| Power | $P = N_s \cdot V_{cell} \cdot I_{cell}$ | $P = V_{string} \cdot N_p \cdot I_{string}$ |
| Failure Mode | Open circuit of one cell = total string failure | Open circuit of one string = partial loss |
| Effect of Shading | Severe — entire string current limited | Mild — only shaded string affected |
| Cable Cross-Section | Smaller (low current) | Larger (high current) |
| Voltage Class | SELV-safe if $N_s \cdot V_{oc} < 60\ \text{V}$ | Generally > 60 V in series-parallel panels |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: From Cell to Solar Panel Manufacturing Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Solar Cell 156mm x 156mm"] --> B["Tabbing Process Apply copper ribbon to busbars"]
    B --> C["Single String Ns cells in series"]
    C --> D["Layup Stage Place strings on glass + EVA"]
    D --> E["Lamination EVA cross-links at 150 C"]
    E --> F["Framing Aluminum anodized frame"]
    F --> G["Junction Box IP67 with bypass diodes"]
    G --> H["Solar Panel Ready for STC Flash Test"]
    H --> I["Rated Power Pmax Vmp Imp Voc Isc"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Electrical Topology of a 60-Cell Panel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["String 1: 6 cells in series"]
        C1["Cell 1"] --- C2["Cell 2"] --- C3["Cell 3"] --- C4["Cell 4"] --- C5["Cell 5"] --- C6["Cell 6"]
    end
    subgraph S2["String 2: 6 cells in series"]
        D1["Cell 7"] --- D2["Cell 8"] --- D3["Cell 9"] --- D4["Cell 10"] --- D5["Cell 11"] --- D6["Cell 12"]
    end
    subgraph S3["String 3: 6 cells in series"]
        E1["Cell 13"] --- E2["Cell 14"] --- E3["Cell 15"] --- E4["Cell 16"] --- E5["Cell 17"] --- E6["Cell 18"]
    end
    BP1["Bypass Diode 1"] -.-> S1
    BP2["Bypass Diode 2"] -.-> S2
    BP3["Bypass Diode 3"] -.-> S3
    S1 --> J1["Junction Box Positive Terminal"]
    S2 --> J1
    S3 --> J1
    S1 --> J2["Junction Box Negative Terminal"]
    S2 --> J2
    S3 --> J2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow: Tabbing and Stringing Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Start: Receive cells from QA"] --> P2["Visually inspect cell for microcracks"]
    P2 --> P3["Solder tabbing wire onto front busbar"]
    P3 --> P4["Flip cell and solder tab to back busbar"]
    P4 --> P5["Repeat for 6 cells to form one string"]
    P5 --> P6{"Solder joint pull test > 1 N"}
    P6 -->|Fail| P7["Rework or scrap cell"]
    P6 -->|Pass| P8["Move string to layup station"]
    P8 --> P9["Align 10 strings on glass + EVA"]
    P9 --> P10["Add backsheet and second EVA layer"]
    P10 --> P11["Vacuum lamination at 150 C"]

```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above map the *physical process flow* (block diagram), the *electrical interconnection* (schematic), and the *manufacturing workflow* (process flow). Together they form a complete visual representation of stringing as required for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *What is meant by "stringing" in a solar PV module? Why is it necessary?*

**Model Answer:**
Stringing is the process of interconnecting individual solar cells in series using solder-coated copper ribbons (called tabbing wires) to form a higher-voltage electrical chain called a *string*. It is necessary because a single silicon solar cell produces only about **0.6 V** of open-circuit voltage, which is too low for any practical load or inverter. By connecting, for example, 60 cells in series, a panel can deliver an open-circuit voltage of approximately **39 V**, which is suitable for charging 24 V batteries or feeding a grid-tied inverter. Stringing also enables the assembly of modular building blocks that can be combined in parallel to scale up current and total power output.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Differentiate between the roles of a bypass diode and a blocking diode in a solar panel.*

**Model Answer:**
A **bypass diode** is connected in *anti-parallel* across a group of cells (typically 20–24 cells) within the panel. Its function is to provide a low-resistance path for current when the protected cells become reverse-biased due to partial shading, thereby preventing localized overheating and "hot-spot" damage. A **blocking diode** is connected in *series* with the panel output, usually in off-grid battery-based systems, to prevent reverse current flow from the battery back into the panel during the night, which would otherwise discharge the battery. In short, bypass diodes protect cells internally; blocking diodes protect external circuitry and batteries.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the manufacturing process of stringing solar cells and assembling them into a complete solar PV module. List the key electrical and mechanical tests performed on the finished panel. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Tabbing:** Copper ribbons coated with Sn60Pb40 or Sn96.5Ag3.0Cu0.5 (SAC) solder are pre-cut and pre-formed. Automated tabbing machines solder two ribbons onto the front busbars of each cell at ~340 °C for 1–2 seconds.

2. **Stringing:** The tabbed cells are then flipped, and the rear-side ribbon of one cell is soldered to the front-side ribbons of the next cell. Six to twelve cells are connected in this manner to form a string.

3. **Layup:** Strings are laid on a sheet of EVA (Ethylene-Vinyl Acetate) placed on tempered low-iron glass. The layout is a matrix of $N_p$ rows × $N_s$ columns. A second layer of EVA and a Tedlar/Polyester backsheet are added on top.

4. **Lamination:** The stack is placed in a vacuum laminator at **150 °C** for **15–20 minutes**, allowing EVA to cross-link, encapsulating the cells and providing optical coupling and moisture protection.

5. **Framing & Junction Box:** An anodized aluminum frame is attached with silicone sealant. A weather-resistant junction box is mounted on the rear, housing the bypass diodes and terminal connectors (MC4).

6. **Tests:**
   - **Electroluminescence (EL) Imaging** — detects microcracks.
   - **Hi-Pot (Dielectric Withstand)** — checks insulation at 3 kV DC.
   - **Flash Test (STC)** — measures $P_{max}$, $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, $FF$.
   - **Thermal Cycling** — verifies reliability between −40 °C and +85 °C.
   - **Mechanical Load Test** — 5400 Pa wind/snow load test.

**(b)** A 72-cell mono-crystalline solar panel uses 6 strings of 12 cells each. Each cell has $V_{oc} = 0.64\ \text{V}$, $I_{sc} = 9.2\ \text{A}$, $V_{mp} = 0.54\ \text{V}$, $I_{mp} = 8.6\ \text{A}$. Calculate: (i) the open-circuit voltage of the panel, (ii) the short-circuit current of the panel, (iii) the maximum power output at STC, and (iv) the Fill Factor. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Open-Circuit Voltage of the Panel**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oc,\,mod} &= N_s \cdot V_{oc,\,cell} \\
V_{oc,\,mod} &= 12 \times 0.64 \\
V_{oc,\,mod} &= 7.68\ \text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Wait — note that a 72-cell panel actually uses 6 substrings of 12 cells in parallel, not in series. We must re-read: "6 strings of 12 cells each" combined as parallel strings in a single panel.*

For a **72-cell panel with 6 parallel strings of 12 cells in series each**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{oc,\,str} &= 12 \times 0.64 = 7.68\ \text{V} \\
V_{oc,\,mod} &= V_{oc,\,str} = 7.68\ \text{V} \\
I_{sc,\,mod} &= N_p \cdot I_{sc,\,cell} = 6 \times 9.2 = 55.2\ \text{A} \\
V_{mp,\,mod} &= 12 \times 0.54 = 6.48\ \text{V} \\
I_{mp,\,mod} &= 6 \times 8.6 = 51.6\ \text{A} \\
P_{max,\,mod} &= 6.48 \times 51.6 = 334.37\ \text{W} \\
FF &= \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{334.37}{7.68 \times 55.2} = \frac{334.37}{423.94} \\
FF &\approx 0.7887
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) $V_{oc,\,mod} = 7.68\ \text{V}$
- (ii) $I_{sc,\,mod} = 55.2\ \text{A}$
- (iii) $P_{max,\,mod} = 334.37\ \text{W}$
- (iv) $FF \approx 0.789$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the concepts of "mismatch loss" and "hot-spot effect" in a series-stringed solar panel. How does the use of bypass diodes mitigate these issues? Include a typical diode placement diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mismatch Loss:**
In any series string, the current is limited by the cell with the lowest photocurrent. If cells in a string have different values of $I_{sc}$ due to manufacturing tolerances, partial shading, or soiling, the entire string operates at the *minimum* current. This causes the other cells to operate away from their individual MPPs, leading to a power loss called **mismatch loss**. Quantitatively, for two cells with $I_1 \neq I_2$:

$$
P_{mismatch} = P_{total} - N_s \cdot V_{mp} \cdot \min(I_1, I_2)
$$

**Hot-Spot Effect:**
When a cell in a series string is shaded, it stops generating current and instead becomes *reverse-biased* by the other illuminated cells in the string. The shaded cell then dissipates power as heat:

$$
P_{hotspot} = V_{reverse} \times I_{string}
$$

If $P_{hotspot}$ exceeds the cell's thermal dissipation limit, the cell can crack, delaminate, or even catch fire — a phenomenon called the **hot-spot effect**.

**Bypass Diode Mitigation:**
A bypass diode connected in *anti-parallel* across a substring of 20–24 cells becomes forward-biased when the substring voltage tries to reverse, effectively **short-circuiting** the shaded substring. The current then bypasses the shaded cells, and the remaining healthy substrings continue to deliver power. The diode forward drop ($V_F \approx 0.5\ \text{V}$ Schottky) introduces a small power loss but protects the cells from thermal destruction.

**(b)** A solar panel is rated at 250 W with $V_{oc} = 37.5\ \text{V}$, $I_{sc} = 8.85\ \text{A}$, $V_{mp} = 30.5\ \text{V}$, $I_{mp} = 8.20\ \text{A}$. Calculate: (i) the Fill Factor, (ii) the number of cells in series (assuming $V_{oc,\,cell} = 0.625\ \text{V}$), and (iii) the total mismatch loss percentage if one cell in the string produces only 95 % of the rated $I_{mp}$ while the rest are at 100 %. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Fill Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
FF &= \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} \\
FF &= \frac{30.5 \times 8.20}{37.5 \times 8.85} \\
FF &= \frac{250.10}{331.875} \\
FF &\approx 0.7536
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Number of Cells in Series:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_s &= \frac{V_{oc,\,mod}}{V_{oc,\,cell}} = \frac{37.5}{0.625} \\
N_s &= 60\ \text{cells}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Mismatch Loss Calculation:**

Assume a single string of 60 cells. One cell delivers $I_{mp,\,weak} = 0.95 \times 8.20 = 7.79\ \text{A}$, while the other 59 deliver 8.20 A.

The string current is limited by the weakest cell:
$$
I_{string,\,new} = 7.79\ \text{A}
$$

New maximum string power:
$$
P_{string,\,new} = 60 \times 0.5083 \times 7.79 \approx 237.6\ \text{W}
$$

Original power:
$$
P_{string,\,orig} = 250\ \text{W}
$$

Mismatch loss percentage:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Loss \%} &= \frac{P_{orig} - P_{new}}{P_{orig}} \times 100 \\
\text{Loss \%} &= \frac{250 - 237.6}{250} \times 100 \\
\text{Loss \%} &= 4.96\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Series vs. Parallel Confusion:** The single most common error is treating a "60-cell panel" as a series-only device. In practice, residential 60-cell panels use **10 parallel strings of 6 cells each**. Students who blindly multiply $V_{oc} \times N_s$ without considering the topology lose **3 to 4 marks** per question.
> 2. **Units of $V_{oc,\,cell}$:** Some textbooks give $V_{oc}$ per cell as **600 mV**. Students must convert to **0.6 V** before multiplying. Failure to convert is a silent 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting $N_p$:** When asked for $I_{sc,\,mod}$, students often write $I_{sc,\,cell}$ without multiplying by the number of parallel strings. This is a recurring 2-mark error.
> 4. **Bypass Diode Drop:** Always subtract the diode forward drop (0.4–0.7 V) when calculating the protected substring voltage during shading conditions.
> 5. **CTM Loss:** Don't claim $P_{mod} = N_s \cdot P_{cell}$ without acknowledging the 2–4 % loss from tabbing resistance, encapsulant absorption, and mismatch.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Stringing** = series interconnection of cells; **Module** = string + lamination + frame + junction box.
- A single Si cell ≈ **0.6 V**, **9 A**, **~4.5 W**; a panel combines many to reach tens of volts and hundreds of watts.
- Series adds **voltage**; Parallel adds **current**; Power is conserved at each level (minus CTM loss).
- **CTM loss** = 2 % to 4 %; caused by tab resistance, optical coupling, and mismatch.
- **Bypass diodes** are placed across every 20–24 cells to prevent hot-spot damage.
- **Blocking diodes** are placed in series at panel output for off-grid battery isolation.
- **Standard panel sizes:** 36-cell, 60-cell, 72-cell; modern panels use 120 or 144 half-cut cells.
- **Fill Factor (FF)** is intrinsic to the I-V curve shape and remains constant under ideal series connection.
- **Mismatch loss** is governed by the weakest cell in a string; bypass diodes partially recover the loss.
- **Module efficiency** = $\dfrac{P_{max,\,mod}}{A_{mod} \times 1000\ \text{W/m}^2}$, typically 17 % – 22 %.
- **Manufacturing sequence:** Tabbing → Stringing → Layup → Lamination (150 °C) → Framing → Junction Box → QC Tests.
- **Tests:** EL Imaging, Hi-Pot, Flash Test, Thermal Cycling, Mechanical Load Test.
- **Voltage safety:** A panel's $V_{oc}$ is kept below **60 V DC** for SELV (Safety Extra-Low-Voltage) compliance when possible.
- **Modern trends:** Multi-busbar (MBB), half-cut cells, shingled cells, and bifacial modules all aim to reduce $I^2R$ losses and improve CTM.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** Ability to (a) compute module parameters from cell data, (b) justify string topology, and (c) explain diode placement.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Light Emitting Diode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Light Emitting Diode**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** is a specially fabricated p–n junction semiconductor device that converts **electrical energy directly into light energy** through the process of **electroluminescence** when forward biased. Under forward bias, electrons from the n-region and holes from the p-region are injected into the depletion (active) region where they recombine, releasing the energy difference in the form of photons (light quanta) whose energy is approximately equal to the forbidden energy gap ($E_g$) of the semiconductor material.

$$E_{photon} \;\approx\; E_g \;\approx\; eV_f$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** The LED is a cornerstone optoelectronic device, classified under **Module 2 – Semiconductors and Optoelectronic Devices**. Students must grasp the bandgap–wavelength–forward voltage relationship, the recombination mechanism, and the material–color correspondence.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a staircase inside a building.**
> Electrons sit on the *upper staircase* (conduction band, n-side). Holes are empty spots on the *lower staircase* (valence band, p-side). When forward bias pushes them together, an electron "falls down" from the upper step into a hole on the lower step. The lost potential energy cannot simply vanish — it is released as a tiny packet of light (**a photon**). The *bigger the step (bandgap)*, the *more energetic (bluer)* the emitted light; a *smaller step* gives *redder* light.

Just as a firefly converts chemical energy into visible light (a *cold* light process), an LED converts electron energy into photons without the wasteful heat seen in incandescent bulbs. This is the heart of **electroluminescence** — light generated *electrically*, not thermally.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (in bold)

- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- **Electronic charge:** $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- **Useful product:** $hc = 1240$ **eV·nm** (the constant used most often in LED calculations)
- **Boltzmann constant:** $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- **Standard forward voltage (red/green GaAs/GaP LED):** **1.8 V – 3.2 V** depending on colour
- **Typical operating current:** **10 mA – 30 mA**
- **Typical luminous efficacy:** **20 – 150 lumens/Watt** (vs. ~15 lm/W for incandescent)

### 1.4 Common LED Materials and Their Emitted Colours

| Semiconductor Material | Bandgap $E_g$ (eV) | Emitted Wavelength $\lambda$ (nm) | Emitted Colour |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| GaAs (Gallium Arsenide) | 1.42 | 870 | Infrared |
| AlGaAs | 1.85 | 670 | Red |
| GaAs$_{0.6}$P$_{0.4}$ | 1.91 | 650 | Red |
| GaAs$_{0.35}$P$_{0.65}$ (N-doped) | 2.10 | 590 | Yellow |
| GaP (Gallium Phosphide) | 2.26 | 560 | Green |
| ZnSe (Zinc Selenide) | 2.70 | 460 | Blue |
| GaN (Gallium Nitride) | 3.40 | 365 | UV |
| InGaN (Indium Gallium Nitride) | 2.65 – 3.10 | 450 – 470 | Blue |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **colour of emitted light is NOT chosen by the casing** — it is determined **entirely by the bandgap** of the semiconductor crystal used in the active region. A red LED will *always* use a smaller-bandgap material than a blue LED.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Construction of an LED

An LED is structurally a heavily doped p–n junction (both sides are **degenerate semiconductors** — doping levels $\sim 10^{17}$ to $10^{19}$ cm$^{-3}$) housed in a transparent epoxy dome. The dome acts as both a mechanical protector and a lens to focus the emitted photons. The cathode is the larger of the two leads and a flat spot is provided on the dome rim to identify it.

Key constructional features:
- **n-type layer** (top, electron rich) – the **cathode** side.
- **p-type layer** (bottom, hole rich) – the **anode** side.
- **Active (depletion) region** – where radiative recombination occurs.
- **Recombination zone** is kept thin and located near the surface to minimise internal absorption.
- **Reflective cup** at the base of the anode lead to direct photons upward.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Forward bias application:** A DC voltage exceeding the *turn-on voltage* (≈ $E_g/e$) is applied across the junction. The depletion region narrows.
2. **Carrier injection:** Electrons from the conduction band of the n-side are injected into the active region; holes from the valence band of the p-side are similarly injected.
3. **Carrier recombination:** An injected electron falls from the conduction band edge ($E_C$) into an empty state (hole) in the valence band edge ($E_V$).
4. **Photon emission:** The energy released equals the bandgap, $E_g = E_C - E_V$. This energy emerges as a photon of frequency $f$ such that $E_g = hf$.
5. **Photon escape:** Photons travelling toward the surface either escape (contributing to output light) or are internally reflected and reabsorbed (loss mechanism).

### 2.3 The "Why" — Energy Band Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the LED band diagram different from an ordinary diode?**
> In a normal silicon diode, recombination is mostly *non-radiative* — energy becomes lattice vibrations (heat). In an LED material (direct bandgap semiconductor such as GaAs), recombination is *radiative* — energy emerges as a photon. This is because the conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at the **same value of crystal momentum (k)** in direct bandgap materials, allowing momentum-conserving photon emission.

### 2.4 I–V Characteristics of an LED

The current–voltage relationship is similar to a standard diode but with a higher cut-in voltage:

$$I = I_S \left(e^{\,eV/\eta k_B T} - 1\right)$$

where $\eta$ is the ideality factor (typically 1.5 – 2.5 for LEDs) and $I_S$ is the reverse saturation current (in the range of nA to µA). The LED conducts appreciably only when $V \geq V_f$, where $V_f \approx E_g/e$.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below consolidates **all the equations** the KTU 2024 examiner may ask you to recall, derive, or apply for the LED topic. Memorise the relationships in the left column — they are the **highest yield** facts.

| # | Formula / Relationship | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{photon} = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of an emitted photon |
| 2 | $E_g \;\vert_{\text{in eV}}\; = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda \;\vert_{\text{in nm}}\;}$ | Bandgap–Wavelength relation (master equation) |
| 3 | $V_f \;\vert_{\text{in V}}\; \approx \dfrac{E_g}{e} \;\vert_{\text{in eV}}\;$ | Forward voltage equals bandgap (in volts) |
| 4 | $\lambda \;\vert_{\text{in nm}}\; = \dfrac{1240}{E_g \;\vert_{\text{in eV}}\;}$ | Wavelength of emitted light |
| 5 | $P_{\text{optical}} = \dfrac{n_{ph} \cdot hf}{t}$ | Optical output power (Watts) |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{ext}} = \dfrac{\text{No. of photons emitted out}}{\text{No. of electrons injected}}$ | External quantum efficiency |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{int}} = \dfrac{\text{No. of photons generated internally}}{\text{No. of electrons injected}}$ | Internal quantum efficiency |
| 8 | $\eta_{\text{ext}} = \eta_{\text{int}} \times \eta_{\text{extraction}}$ | Where $\eta_{\text{extraction}} \approx \dfrac{1}{4n^2}$ for a planar LED |
| 9 | $\eta_L = \dfrac{\Phi_v}{P_{elec}}$ (lm/W) | Luminous efficacy — visible light per Watt |
| 10 | $R_{\text{rad}} = B(np - n_i^2)$ | Radiative recombination rate (B is bimolecular coefficient) |
| 11 | $\tau_{\text{rad}} = \dfrac{1}{B\,\Delta n}$ | Radiative carrier lifetime |
| 12 | $\Delta \lambda \approx \dfrac{\lambda^2 k_B T}{hc}$ | Spectral line width (FWHM due to thermal broadening) |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick KTU Trick:** If asked "what is the wavelength emitted by a GaAs LED?", simply do $\lambda = 1240 / E_g = 1240 / 1.42 = 873$ nm. That's infrared — used in TV remote controls.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

LEDs are the workhorse of the **optoelectronics industry** and are central to:
- **Solid-state lighting:** Domestic bulbs, streetlights, automotive headlamps (replacing incandescent/CFL due to higher efficacy and longer life).
- **Display technology:** LED TVs, mobile screens, scoreboards.
- **Optical communication:** LED sources for short-distance fibre-optic links (in conjunction with photodetectors).
- **Indicator electronics:** Power-on indicators, traffic lights, seven-segment displays.
- **Sensing:** Infrared LEDs in remote controls, optical encoders, proximity sensors.
- **Biomedical:** Photodynamic therapy, pulse oximetry, surgical illumination.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wavelength of Emitted Light in Terms of Bandgap

**Given:** A direct-bandgap semiconductor LED with bandgap energy $E_g$. The energy released upon electron–hole recombination is emitted as a single photon.

**To derive:** The expression $\lambda = 1240 / E_g$ (with $E_g$ in eV and $\lambda$ in nm).

**Step 1.** Write the Bohr–Einstein relation for the photon energy:

$$E_{photon} = h f$$

This is the standard quantum relation; the energy of a photon equals Planck's constant times its frequency.

**Step 2.** Express frequency $f$ in terms of wavelength $\lambda$ and the speed of light $c$:

$$f = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$

Substituting,

$$E_{photon} = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3.** Apply energy conservation. The photon energy must equal the energy lost by the electron when it falls across the bandgap. Therefore:

$$E_{photon} = E_g = h c / \lambda$$

Rearranging for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h c}{E_g}$$

**Step 4.** Insert numerical values to obtain a useful "engineering" constant.

$$h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J·s}, \quad c = 3 \times 10^8\,\text{m/s}, \quad 1\,\text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$$

Compute the numerator:

$$h c = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}\,\text{J·m}$$

Convert to eV·nm. First convert joules → eV:

$$\frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.2408 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{eV·m}$$

Convert metres → nanometres (multiply by $10^9$):

$$h c = 1240.8\,\text{eV·nm} \;\approx\; 1240\,\text{eV·nm}$$

**Step 5.** Therefore, the working KTU-ready equation is:

$$\boxed{\;\lambda\,(\text{nm}) \;=\; \dfrac{1240}{E_g\,(\text{eV})}\;}$$

**Sanity check:** For GaAs, $E_g = 1.42$ eV, so $\lambda = 1240 / 1.42 = 873$ nm, which matches the known near-infrared emission of GaAs. ✔

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Forward Voltage in Terms of Bandgap

**Given:** When a photon of energy $E_g$ is emitted, that energy has been supplied by the battery. The charge carrier involved (one electron) has charge $e$. The energy delivered per electron crossing the junction is $eV$.

**To derive:** $V_f = E_g / e$.

**Step 1.** Equate the energy supplied by the battery per electron to the photon energy:

$$e V_f = E_g$$

The left-hand side is the electrical work done by the battery in moving one electron of charge $e$ across a potential difference $V_f$. The right-hand side is the photon energy produced.

**Step 2.** Solve for $V_f$:

$$\boxed{\;V_f \;=\; \dfrac{E_g}{e}\;}$$

**Step 3.** When $E_g$ is expressed in **eV** and $e$ is taken as the unit electronic charge (1 e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C), the numerical value of $V_f$ (in volts) **numerically equals** the bandgap in eV.

For example, GaN has $E_g = 3.4$ eV, so $V_f \approx 3.4$ V — and indeed blue/white GaN LEDs have a forward voltage near 3.0 – 3.4 V. ✔

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — External Quantum Efficiency from Photon Counts

**Given:** An LED is driven by a forward current $I$ for time $t$. The number of photons actually emitted out of the device is $N_{out}$, and the number of electrons injected is $N_{inj} = It / e$.

**To derive:** $\eta_{\text{ext}} = N_{out} / N_{inj}$.

**Step 1.** Number of electrons injected in time $t$:

$$N_{inj} = \frac{I t}{e}$$

This is the total charge $It$ divided by the charge per carrier $e$.

**Step 2.** By definition, external quantum efficiency is the ratio of photons leaving the LED to electrons injected:

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{N_{out}}{N_{inj}} = \frac{N_{out} \cdot e}{I t}$$

**Step 3.** Express in terms of optical power. The energy of one photon is $E_g = hf = hc/\lambda$, so total optical energy emitted in time $t$ is:

$$P_{opt}\,t = N_{out} \cdot h f = N_{out} \cdot \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4.** Solve for $N_{out}$:

$$N_{out} = \frac{P_{opt}\, t\,\lambda}{h c}$$

**Step 5.** Substitute into the $\eta_{ext}$ equation:

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{P_{opt}\, t\,\lambda}{hc} \cdot \frac{e}{I t} = \frac{P_{opt}\, e\, \lambda}{h c\, I}$$

**Step 6.** Use the engineering constant $hc/e = 1240\,\text{nm·V}$ to simplify:

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{P_{opt}\,\lambda}{1240 \cdot I} \quad (\lambda \text{ in nm},\; P_{opt}\text{ in W},\; I \text{ in A})$$

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{ext} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{opt}\,\lambda}{1240\,I}\;}$$

**Sanity check:** A red LED ($\lambda = 660$ nm) driven at 20 mA giving 2 mW of optical output:

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 660}{1240 \times 20 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{1.32}{24.8} = 0.0532 = 5.32\%$$

This is realistic for an uncoupled red LED. ✔

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Light Extraction Limit for a Planar LED

**Given:** A photon reaching the semiconductor–air interface from inside the semiconductor (refractive index $n$) can escape only if its angle of incidence is less than the **critical angle** $\theta_c$, where $\sin\theta_c = 1/n$.

**To derive:** The maximum extraction efficiency $\eta_{ext,max} = 1 / (2 n^2)$.

**Step 1.** By Snell's law, the critical angle is:

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_{air}}{n_{semi}} = \frac{1}{n}$$

**Step 2.** The fraction of isotropically emitted photons within the escape cone (solid angle $\Omega_{esc}$) versus the full hemisphere ($2\pi$) is:

$$\frac{\Omega_{esc}}{2\pi} = \frac{2\pi(1 - \cos\theta_c)}{2\pi} = 1 - \cos\theta_c = 1 - \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_c} = 1 - \sqrt{1 - 1/n^2}$$

**Step 3.** For $n \gg 1$, the binomial expansion gives:

$$\sqrt{1 - \tfrac{1}{n^2}} \approx 1 - \tfrac{1}{2n^2}$$

So

$$1 - \sqrt{1 - 1/n^2} \approx \frac{1}{2n^2}$$

**Step 4.** Therefore, the maximum extraction efficiency for a planar LED is:

$$\boxed{\;\eta_{ext,max} \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{2 n^2}\;}$$

For GaAs ($n = 3.5$), $\eta_{ext,max} \approx 1/(2 \times 12.25) = 4.08\%$. This is why modern high-brightness LEDs use dome-shaped encapsulants and surface texturing to overcome this limit.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** A GaAsP LED has a bandgap of 1.9 eV. Calculate (a) the wavelength of emitted light, (b) the forward voltage, and (c) the photon energy in joules.

**Solution:**

**(a)** Using $\lambda = 1240 / E_g$:

$$\lambda = \frac{1240}{1.9} = 652.6\,\text{nm} \;\approx\; \text{Red region}$$

**(b)** Using $V_f = E_g / e$:

$$V_f = \frac{1.9\,\text{eV}}{1\,\text{(in eV units)}} = 1.9\,\text{V}$$

**(c)** Photon energy in joules:

$$E = h f = \frac{h c}{\lambda} = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{652.6 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.05 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$$

**Verification:** In eV, $E = 3.05 \times 10^{-19} / 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 1.9$ eV. ✔
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LED (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DC Power Supply] --> B[Current Limiting Resistor R]
    B --> C[Anode Lead of LED]
    C --> D[P-type Semiconductor Layer]
    D --> E[Active Recombination Region]
    E --> F[N-type Semiconductor Layer]
    F --> G[Cathode Lead of LED]
    G --> H[Return to Power Supply]
    E --> I[Radiative Recombination]
    I --> J[Photon Generation E equals hf equals Eg]
    J --> K[Photon Travels Through Epoxy Dome]
    K --> L[Output Light Beam]
    E --> M[Non-Radiative Recombination]
    M --> N[Heat Generation]

    classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    classDef junction fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000

    class A,B source
    class C,D,E,F,G junction
    class J,K,L output
    class M,N loss
```

### 4.2 Energy Band Diagram of an LED Under Forward Bias (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Pside[P-type Region] --> J1[Depletion Edge]
    J1 --> AR[Active Region Recombination Zone]
    AR --> J2[Depletion Edge]
    J2 --> Nside[N-type Region]
    EcP[Conduction Band Edge EC] --- EfP[Fermi Level EF]
    EvP[Valence Band Edge EV] --- EfP
    EcN[Conduction Band Edge EC] --- EfN[Quasi Fermi Level EFn]
    EvN[Valence Band Edge EV] --- EfN
    EfP --- EfN
    EcP --- EcN
    EvP --- EvN
    AR --> emit[Photon of Energy Eg emitted]

    classDef band fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
    classDef emitbox fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000
    class EcP,EvP,EcN,EvN,EfP,EfN band
    class emit emitbox
```

### 4.3 Process Flow: Electron-Hole Recombination (Mermaid Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1 Forward Bias Applied] --> S2[Step 2 Depletion Region Narrows]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Electrons Injected from N-side into Active Region]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Holes Injected from P-side into Active Region]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Electron and Hole Meet in Conduction-Valence Transition]
    S5 --> S6{Recombination Type}
    S6 -->|Direct Bandgap Material| S7[Radiative Photon Emission]
    S6 -->|Indirect Bandgap Material| S8[Non-Radiative Phonon Emission Heat]
    S7 --> S9[Step 6 Photon Escapes Through Transparent Dome]
    S9 --> S10[Step 7 Visible Light Output]
    S8 --> S11[Step 7 Heat Output Mostly Lost Energy]

    classDef process fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef success fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000
    classDef failure fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000

    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S9,S10 process
    class S6 decision
    class S7 success
    class S8,S11 failure
```

### 4.4 LED Construction Schematic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB

        Lead1[Anode Lead] --> Anvil[Anvil Post]
        Anvil --> Reflector[Reflective Cup]
        Reflector --> PJunction[P-type Semiconductor Layer]
        PJunction --> Active[Active Recombination Layer]
        Active --> NJunction[N-type Semiconductor Layer]
        NJunction --> Bond[Bond Wire]
        Bond --> Lead2[Cathode Lead]

    end

    classDef leads fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#c1121f,color:#000
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define electroluminescence. Explain how an LED produces light under forward bias.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Electroluminescence is the phenomenon of emission of light from a semiconductor material when an electric current (or strong electric field) is passed through it. The energy released during carrier recombination is radiated as photons.

**Mechanism (2 marks):** When a p–n junction LED is forward biased, electrons from the n-region and holes from the p-region are injected into the depletion (active) region. These minority carriers recombine, and the bandgap energy $E_g$ is released as a photon of frequency $f$ such that $E_g = hf$. The wavelength of emitted light is $\lambda = hc/E_g = 1240/E_g$ (nm, eV units). This process occurs continuously as long as forward current flows, producing steady light output.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is GaAs preferred over Si for fabricating LEDs? Justify with bandgap type.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Silicon is an **indirect bandgap** material, in which the conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at *different* crystal momentum (k) values. Radiative recombination in Si is therefore extremely inefficient — it requires a third particle (a phonon) to conserve momentum, and most recombination is non-radiative (heat).

**GaAs, in contrast, is a direct bandgap** semiconductor where the conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at the *same* k-vector. Electron–hole recombination is therefore highly probable to produce a photon without needing a phonon. Additionally, the GaAs bandgap of 1.42 eV corresponds to a useful near-infrared emission (≈ 870 nm), making it ideal for optoelectronic and fibre-optic communication applications. **(3 marks for: (1) direct vs indirect distinction, (2) momentum conservation argument, (3) numerical example.)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — OPTION A**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, End Semester]**

**(a) Derive the relationship between the wavelength of light emitted by an LED and the bandgap of the semiconductor used. Hence calculate the wavelength emitted by a GaAs LED ($E_g = 1.42$ eV).** **(7 marks)**

**(b) With a neat energy band diagram, explain the working of an LED. Compare the characteristics of LED with an ordinary silicon p–n junction diode.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation of $\lambda = 1240/E_g$ (7 marks)**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** When an electron in the conduction band recombines with a hole in the valence band across the bandgap, the energy released equals:

$$\Delta E = E_C - E_V = E_g$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** By Einstein's quantum relation, this energy is emitted as a photon of frequency $f$:

$$E_g = h f = \frac{h c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Rearranging for $\lambda$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h c}{E_g}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Inserting numerical values of $h$ and $c$ and converting to convenient engineering units (eV and nm):

$$h c = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25}\,\text{J·m}$$

Converting to eV·nm:

$$h c = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \times 10^{9} \approx 1240\,\text{eV·nm}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** The working equation is:

$$\boxed{\lambda\,(\text{nm}) = \frac{1240}{E_g\,(\text{eV})}}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Numerical calculation for GaAs:

$$\lambda = \frac{1240}{1.42} = 873\,\text{nm} \quad (\text{infrared region})$$

---

**Part (b) — Working & Comparison (7 marks)**

**Working (4 marks):**

Under forward bias, the applied voltage reduces the barrier potential at the junction. The applied voltage also splits the quasi-Fermi levels on the two sides by an amount $eV_f$. Electrons from the n-side conduction band and holes from the p-side valence band are injected into the thin active (depletion) region. There, they recombine radiatively: an electron falls from $E_C$ to $E_V$, releasing a photon of energy $E_g = h\nu = hc/\lambda$. The photons so generated travel through the transparent semiconductor and the epoxy dome to emerge as light.

> The energy band diagram shows the p-side with $E_F$ near $E_V$ and the n-side with $E_F$ near $E_C$. Under forward bias, the bands tilt such that injected electrons and holes occupy states at the band edges in the active region, and the photon energy is $E_g$.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Property | Ordinary Si p–n Diode | LED |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Rectification, switching | Light emission |
| Recombination | Non-radiative (heat) | Radiative (photon) |
| Material | Mostly Si (indirect) | Direct bandgap (GaAs, GaP, GaN) |
| Bandgap type | Indirect | Direct |
| Forward voltage | ~ 0.7 V | 1.5 – 3.5 V (matches $E_g/e$) |
| Output | Electrical | Optical (visible/IR) |
| Switching speed | Fast (ns) | Moderate (depends on carrier lifetime) |
| Reverse breakdown | May be high | Low (~ 5 V typical) |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — OPTION B (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester]**

**(a) Explain the construction and working of a light emitting diode with a suitable diagram. Discuss the role of direct and indirect bandgap semiconductors.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Define external quantum efficiency and luminous efficacy of an LED. A red LED ($\lambda = 660$ nm) is driven by 25 mA current and emits 3 mW of optical power. Calculate its external quantum efficiency.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Construction, Working & Bandgap Discussion (7 marks)**

**Construction (2 marks):** An LED is a heavily doped p–n junction (both sides degenerate, doping $\sim 10^{18}$ cm$^{-3}$) enclosed in a transparent epoxy dome. The anode lead has a reflective cup that holds the semiconductor die; the cathode is the larger lead with a flat marker on the dome. The active layer is kept thin and near the surface to reduce internal absorption.

**Working (3 marks):** Forward bias above the cut-in voltage causes electrons and holes to flood the active region. They recombine, releasing photons of energy $E_g = h\nu = hc/\lambda$. The emitted photons travel in random directions; those within the escape cone (angle $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(1/n)$) leave the device, the rest are internally reflected and lost. The colour of the emitted light is set by the bandgap of the active material.

**Direct vs Indirect bandgap (2 marks):** In **direct bandgap** materials (GaAs, InP, GaN), the conduction band minimum and valence band maximum occur at the same k-vector, so radiative recombination is efficient and light emission is strong. In **indirect bandgap** materials (Si, Ge), the band extrema occur at different k-values, so recombination must involve a phonon to conserve momentum, making radiative recombination very weak. Hence only direct bandgap semiconductors are used for LEDs.

---

**Part (b) — Efficiency Definitions and Numerical (7 marks)**

**Definitions (3 marks):**

- **External Quantum Efficiency (EQE, $\eta_{ext}$):** Ratio of the number of photons emitted out of the LED to the number of electrons injected into the device.

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{N_{ph,out}}{N_{e,in}}$$

- **Luminous Efficacy ($\eta_L$):** The ratio of the total visible light output (luminous flux $\Phi_v$ in lumens) to the electrical power input (in watts). It is measured in **lumens per Watt (lm/W)**.

$$\eta_L = \frac{\Phi_v}{P_{elec}} \quad [\text{lm/W}]$$

**Numerical calculation (4 marks):**

Given: $\lambda = 660$ nm, $I = 25$ mA = $25 \times 10^{-3}$ A, $P_{opt} = 3$ mW = $3 \times 10^{-3}$ W.

Number of electrons injected per second:

$$N_{e,in} = \frac{I}{e} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 1.56 \times 10^{17}\,\text{electrons/s}$$

Number of photons emitted per second (from $P_{opt} = N_{ph} \cdot h\nu$):

$$N_{ph,out} = \frac{P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{h c} = \frac{P_{opt} \cdot \lambda}{1240 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J·nm}}$$

$$N_{ph,out} = \frac{3 \times 10^{-3} \times 660 \times 10^{-9}}{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{1.98 \times 10^{-9}}{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}} = 9.96 \times 10^{15}\,\text{photons/s}$$

External quantum efficiency:

$$\eta_{ext} = \frac{N_{ph,out}}{N_{e,in}} = \frac{9.96 \times 10^{15}}{1.56 \times 10^{17}} = 0.0638 = 6.38\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{ext} \approx 6.4\%}$$

(Equivalently, using the shortcut formula derived in Section 3.3:
$\eta_{ext} = \dfrac{P_{opt}\lambda}{1240\,I} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^{-3} \times 660}{1240 \times 25 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{1.98}{31} = 0.0639$, matching.) ✔

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on LED Questions (Lose 2–4 marks each):**
> 1. **Unit Mix-up Trap:** Writing $\lambda = 1240/E_g$ without specifying the units of both quantities. The examiner will deduct **1 mark** if the units are not explicitly stated as nm and eV respectively.
> 2. **Forgetting the direct-vs-indirect bandgap argument:** Many students say "GaAs is used because its bandgap is suitable." This is incomplete. The crucial point is the **direct bandgap nature** that permits efficient photon emission. Without this, expect to lose **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Equating $V_f$ to $E_g$ dimensionlessly:** $V_f = E_g/e$ — if you drop the /e, you are mixing up energy (eV) with potential (V). The numerical coincidence hides the conceptual error, but the examiner **will** look for the explicit division.
> 4. **Plotting a wrong I–V curve:** The LED I–V curve is similar to a normal diode but with a higher cut-in voltage. Some students draw it with the *same* cut-in voltage (0.7 V) as Si — that is incorrect.
> 5. **Confusing EQE and IQE:** External quantum efficiency includes escape losses; internal does not. $\eta_{ext} = \eta_{int} \cdot \eta_{extraction}$. The examiner will check this distinction.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Definition:** LED = forward-biased direct-bandgap p–n junction that emits light by **electroluminescence**.
- ✅ **Key energy equation:** $E_g = h\nu = hc/\lambda$.
- ✅ **Master wavelength formula:** $\lambda\,(\text{nm}) = 1240 / E_g\,(\text{eV})$ — always **state the units**.
- ✅ **Forward voltage:** $V_f \approx E_g / e$ — numerically, $V_f$ in volts equals $E_g$ in eV.
- ✅ **Direct vs Indirect:** Only **direct bandgap** materials are efficient LEDs. Si and Ge are *not* used.
- ✅ **Colour is determined by bandgap**, not by the plastic casing. GaAs → IR; GaP → Green; GaN/InGaN → Blue/UV.
- ✅ **External Quantum Efficiency:** $\eta_{ext} = N_{ph,out}/N_{e,in} = P_{opt}\lambda/(1240\,I)$.
- ✅ **Luminous Efficacy:** $\Phi_v/P_{elec}$ in **lm/W** — quantifies "useful visible light per watt."
- ✅ **Light extraction limit** for planar LED: $\eta_{ext,max} \approx 1/(2n^2)$. For GaAs ($n=3.5$), only ~4 % of internally generated photons escape.
- ✅ **KTU numerical shortcuts:** Memorise $hc = 1240$ eV·nm; remember $1\text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J.
- ✅ **Required forward voltage range:** 1.5 – 3.5 V depending on colour (red ≈ 1.8 V, green ≈ 2.2 V, blue/white ≈ 3.0 – 3.4 V).
- ✅ **Materials family recall:** GaAs, AlGaAs, GaAsP, GaP, InGaN, GaN, ZnSe — bandgap increases from IR → visible → UV.
- ✅ **KTU examination pattern (2024 Scheme):** Expect a 3-mark definition in Part A and a 14-mark derivation + comparison/numerical in Part B. Always include a labelled diagram; without it, **2 marks are routinely deducted**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **End of Topic: Light Emitting Diode (Module 2, GBPHT121).** Students are advised to commit the table of formulae and the direct/indirect bandgap argument to memory — these are the most heavily tested items in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** is a semiconductor p–n junction device that emits **electroluminescent** light when forward-biased. When electrons from the conduction band of the n-region recombine with holes in the valence band of the p-region across the depletion region, the energy difference is released as photons whose wavelength is determined by the semiconductor's **band gap energy** $E_g$.

$$E_g = \frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{1.2407 \text{ eV·nm}}{\lambda(\text{nm})}$$

The emitted photon wavelength is therefore:

$$\lambda = \frac{1240}{E_g(\text{eV})} \text{ nm}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *direct band gap* semiconductor is mandatory for efficient LED operation. Materials like **GaAs**, **GaP**, **GaAsP**, **AlGaAs**, **InGaN**, and **SiC** are the standard active media. Indirect band gap materials like **Si** and **Ge** are *inefficient* light emitters because radiative recombination is phonon-assisted.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The One-Way Energy Waterfall"

Imagine a **waterfall** that only flows in one direction. A regular river (a resistor) lets water trickle down a gentle slope, releasing little energy as heat. An LED, however, is a *steep, engineered cliff* with a *trap door at the bottom*. Electrons "fall" down the band-gap cliff, and the trap door slams shut, releasing the energy not as heat (like in a resistor) but as a **visible photon** — a tiny packet of colored light. The height of the cliff (band gap $E_g$) determines the color: a short cliff (low $E_g$) emits red photons; a tall cliff (high $E_g$) emits blue/UV photons.

## 1.3 Why This Matters in Electrical Engineering

LEDs are not just "light bulbs" — they are **optical-electrical transducers** that also serve as detectors, sensors, communication sources, and even photovoltaic cells (in reverse). Every KTU 2024-scheme module on LEDs requires mastery of:

- **Spectral engineering** (band-gap tuning → color selection)
- **Power efficiency** (lumen-per-watt comparison with incandescent/CFL)
- **Modulation bandwidth** (for Li-Fi and optical communication)
- **Thermal management** (junction temperature $T_j$ vs lifetime)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - Forward voltage drop: $V_F \approx 1.8 - 3.5$ V (color dependent)
> - Typical drive current: $I_F = 10$ – $20$ mA (indicator LEDs)
> - High-power LEDs: $I_F = 350$ mA – $1$ A
> - Luminous efficacy: $100 - 200$ lm/W (modern white LEDs)
> - Lifespan: $25,000$ – $50,000$ hours
