The opposite holds for Type I: $\xi \gg \lambda_L$ — the material resists forming normal cores because doing so would cost too much condensation energy.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Flux Quantization in a Vortex

The Cooper-pair wavefunction must be **single-valued** when traversing a closed loop around the vortex core:

$$\oint \vec{p} \cdot d\vec{l} = nh \quad \text{(Bohr-Sommerfeld)}$$

For a Cooper pair of mass $2m$ and charge $2e$ moving with superfluid velocity $\vec{v}_s$:

$$\vec{p} = 2m\vec{v}_s + 2e\vec{A}$$

Therefore:

$$2m \oint \vec{v}_s \cdot d\vec{l} + 2e \oint \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{l} = nh$$

By the London equation, $\oint \vec{v}_s \cdot d\vec{l} = 0$ in the bulk. Using Stokes' theorem, $\oint \vec{A} \cdot d\vec{l} = \int \vec{B} \cdot d\vec{S} = \Phi$:

$$2e\Phi = nh \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Phi = \frac{nh}{2e}$$

The minimum non-zero flux ($n = 1$):

$$\boxed{\Phi_0 = \frac{h}{2e} \approx 2.0678 \times 10^{-15} \text{ Wb}}$$

This is the experimental "smoking gun" for the existence of **Cooper pairs** in the superconducting state. The factor of 2 in the denominator (charge $2e$, not $e$) was the key historical evidence for the **BCS theory** proposed by Bardeen, Cooper, and Schrieffer in 1957.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary — Master Comparison

| **Property** | **Type I** | **Type II** |
|:-------------|:-----------|:------------|
| GL parameter $\kappa$ | $< 1/\sqrt{2}$ | $> 1/\sqrt{2}$ |
| Critical field(s) | Single $H_c$ | Two: $H_{c1}, H_{c2}$ |
| Typical composition | Pure elemental metals | Alloys, compounds, ceramics |
| Coherence length vs. $\lambda_L$ | $\xi > \lambda_L$ | $\xi < \lambda_L$ |
| Magnetic response | Abrupt, first-order transition | Gradual, second-order transition |
| Mixed (vortex) state | **Absent** | **Present** for $H_{c1} < H < H_{c2}$ |
| Energy gap to vortex | Positive (unfavorable) | Negative (favorable) |
| $H_{c2}$ magnitude | Modest (typically < 0.1 T) | Very high (often > 10 T) |
| Practical use | Limited (lab-scale) | Dominant (MRI, accelerators, power) |
| Example | Pb, Hg, Sn | Nb-Ti, YBCO, Nb$_3$Sn |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid State Diagram — Magnetic Response of Type I vs Type II

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NormalMetal : T greater than Tc
    NormalMetal --> Superconducting : Cool below Tc, B equals zero

    state TypeIBehavior {
        direction LR
        Superconducting --> NormalMetal : H crosses Hc
        NormalMetal --> Superconducting : H drops below Hc
    }

    state TypeIIBehavior {
        direction LR
        Superconducting --> MixedVortex : H crosses Hc1
        MixedVortex --> Superconducting : H drops below Hc1
        MixedVortex --> NormalMetal : H crosses Hc2
        NormalMetal --> MixedVortex : H drops below Hc2
    }

    Superconducting --> TypeIBehavior : Type I material
    Superconducting --> TypeIIBehavior : Type II material
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Classification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start with a superconducting material] --> B[Measure London penetration depth lambdaL]
    B --> C[Measure coherence length xi]
    C --> D[Compute GL parameter kappa equals lambdaL over xi]
    D --> E{Kappa greater than 1 over root 2}
    E -->|No| F[Type I Superconductor]
    E -->|Yes| G[Type II Superconductor]
    F --> F1[Single critical field Hc]
    F --> F2[Sharp first order transition]
    F --> F3[No mixed state]
    G --> G1[Two critical fields Hc1 and Hc2]
    G --> G2[Gradual second order transition]
    G --> G3[Mixed vortex state between Hc1 and Hc2]
    G1 --> H[High Hc2 enables strong magnets]
    G3 --> H
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Architecture — Functional Mapping of a Type II Vortex

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph vortexCore["Vortex Core Region"]
        core1[Normal metallic region of radius xi]
        core2[Order parameter psi equals zero at center]
        core3[Recovers to bulk value over distance xi]
    end

    subgraph vortexField["Magnetic Field Region"]
        field1[Field B equals H applied at infinity]
        field2[Decays over length lambdaL outside core]
        field3[Total trapped flux equals one Phi zero]
    end

    subgraph vortexCurrent["Supercurrent Region"]
        curr1[Circular persistent current around core]
        curr2[Flows within penetration depth lambdaL]
        curr3[Carries the screening of the vortex]
    end

    subgraph vortexLattice["Vortex Lattice Formation"]
        lat1[Abrikosov lattice when H is between Hc1 and Hc2]
        lat2[Triangular close packed arrangement minimizes energy]
        lat3[Lattice constant depends on applied field]
    end

    core1 --> field1
    field2 --> curr1
    curr2 --> lat1
    field3 --> lat2
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Phase Diagram — H vs T for Both Types

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph typeIDiag["Type I Phase Boundary"]
        A1[Normal Phase above Tc] -->|Apply field at T below Tc| A2[Superconducting]
        A2 -->|H exceeds Hc at fixed T| A1
    end

    subgraph typeIIDiag["Type II Phase Boundary"]
        B1[Normal Phase above Tc] -->|Apply field| B2[Superconducting Meissner]
        B2 -->|H exceeds Hc1| B3[Mixed Vortex Phase]
        B3 -->|H exceeds Hc2| B1
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** *Distinguish between Type I and Type II superconductors with suitable examples.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Feature** | **Type I** | **Type II** |
|:------------|:-----------|:------------|
| Critical field | Single $H_c$ | Two: $H_{c1}$ and $H_{c2}$ |
| GL parameter | $\kappa < 1/\sqrt{2}$ | $\kappa > 1/\sqrt{2}$ |
| Transition | Abrupt, first-order | Gradual, second-order (mixed state) |
| Examples | Pb, Hg, Sn | Nb-Ti, YBCO, Nb$_3$Sn |
| $H_c$ magnitude | Small (mT range) | Large (tens of Tesla) |
| Practical utility | Limited (lab use) | High-field magnets, MRI |

*(1 mark for proper distinction, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for correct pairing of properties.)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** *Define London penetration depth. What is its order of magnitude for typical superconductors?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **London penetration depth $\lambda_L$** is the characteristic length over which an externally applied magnetic field decays exponentially inside a superconductor from its surface value to $1/e$ of that value.

$$\lambda_L = \sqrt{\frac{m}{\mu_0 n_s e^2}}$$

- For typical elemental superconductors, $\lambda_L \sim 50$ to $500$ nm.
- For high-$T_c$ cuprates (e.g., YBCO), $\lambda_L$ can reach $\sim 200$ to $1500$ nm along the $c$-axis.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for typical value.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks with a typical sub-division of **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. You are given an internal choice (Q-A or Q-B on the same module topic).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** *Derive the temperature dependence of the critical magnetic field of a Type I superconductor. Show that the empirical relation $H_c(T) = H_0[1-(T/T_c)^2]$ is consistent with the second-order phase transition at $T_c$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Empirical free-energy assumption:** 
For the two-fluid model, the free energy density difference between the normal ($n$) and superconducting ($s$) states near $T_c$ can be expanded as:

$$F_n - F_s = \alpha (T_c - T)^2 \quad \text{for } T \leq T_c$$

This vanishes continuously at $T = T_c$, consistent with a **second-order** transition.

**Step 2 — Define thermodynamic $H_c$:** 
The critical field is defined by equating the magnetic work done to the condensation energy:

$$\frac{\mu_0 H_c^2(T)}{2} = F_n(T) - F_s(T) = \alpha (T_c - T)^2$$

Taking the square root:

$$H_c(T) = \sqrt{\frac{2\alpha}{\mu_0}}\,(T_c - T)$$

**Step 3 — Convert to parabolic form:** 
Square both sides and compare at $T = 0$:

$$H_c^2(T) = H_c^2(0)\left(1 - \frac{T}{T_c}\right)^2 = H_c^2(0)\left[1 - \frac{2T}{T_c} + \frac{T^2}{T_c^2}\right]$$

By **symmetry** of the entropy argument and the Gorter–Casimir two-fluid model with $S_n - S_s \propto T$, the linear term cancels, leaving only the quadratic term:

$$\boxed{H_c(T) = H_c(0)\left[1 - \left(\frac{T}{T_c}\right)^2\right]}$$

**Step 4 — Verification of second-order character at $T_c$:** 
- $H_c(T_c) = 0$ ✓
- $\frac{dH_c}{dT}\Big\vert_{T_c} = -2H_0/T_c$ (finite) ✓
- $\frac{d^2H_c}{dT^2}\Big\vert_{T_c} = -2H_0/T_c^2$ (finite) ✓

The continuous derivative shows that the transition is **second-order** (Ehrenfest classification), matching experimental observations of zero latent heat at $T_c$.

**Mark Allocation:** (1) Free-energy expansion 1.5; (2) Equating magnetic work 1.5; (3) Parabolic derivation 2; (4) Second-order verification 2.

---

**(b)** *Explain the mixed (vortex) state in Type II superconductors. Discuss the role of the GL parameter and the significance of the lower and upper critical fields.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Existence of Two Critical Fields:**
Unlike Type I materials, Type II superconductors are characterized by the GL parameter:

$$\kappa = \frac{\lambda_L}{\xi} > \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$$

This condition implies that the surface energy between a normal region and a superconducting region is **negative**. Hence, it is energetically favorable to create interfaces, leading to the formation of normal "inclusions" inside the superconductor when a magnetic field is applied.

**2. The Three Regimes:**
- **$H < H_{c1}$:** Pure Meissner state. The superconductor behaves like a Type I material with perfect flux expulsion.
- **$H_{c1} < H < H_{c2}$:** The **mixed (Shubnikov) state**. Magnetic flux penetrates as an array of **Abrikosov vortices**.
- **$H > H_{c2}$:** Normal metallic state. Vortices overlap, superconductivity destroyed.

**3. Anatomy of an Abrikosov Vortex:**
- **Core:** A cylindrical normal region of radius $\sim \xi$ where the order parameter $\psi$ is suppressed to zero.
- **Magnetic field profile:** Maximum at the core, decays over $\sim \lambda_L$ outward.
- **Supercurrents:** Persistent circular currents surround the core, decaying over $\lambda_L$.
- **Flux trapped:** Exactly one flux quantum $\Phi_0 = h/(2e)$ per vortex.

**4. Significance of $H_{c1}$ and $H_{c2}$:**
- $H_{c1}$ marks the **entry field** at which the first vortex becomes thermodynamically favorable.
- $H_{c2}$ marks the **suppression field** at which the order parameter is killed everywhere.
- For high-field applications (MRI, accelerators), what matters is $H_{c2}$, which can exceed 30 T in materials like Nb$_3$Sn, BSCCO, and YBCO.

**5. Vortex Lattice:**
Above $H_{c1}$, vortices arrange into a 2D periodic **Abrikosov lattice** (triangular in clean materials) due to mutual magnetic repulsion. This is directly observable via neutron diffraction, Bitter decoration, and scanning Hall-probe microscopy.

**6. Engineering Importance:**
The mixed state is the *operating regime* of nearly all real-world high-field superconducting magnets, because:
- $H_{c2} \gg H_c$ of Type I materials.
- The vortex lattice can "pin" against material defects, preventing dissipative flux motion (the principle of **type-II superconductor engineering for high critical currents**).

**Mark Allocation:** (1) GL condition 1; (2) Three regimes 1.5; (3) Vortex anatomy 1.5; (4) $H_{c1}/H_{c2}$ significance 1.5; (5) Engineering relevance 1.5.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** *Starting from the London equation, derive the expression for the London penetration depth and show that the magnetic field inside a superconductor decays exponentially from its surface.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — The London Equation:** 
The second London equation, replacing Ohm's law for a superconductor, is:

$$\frac{\partial \vec{J}_s}{\partial t} = \frac{n_s e^2}{m}\vec{E} \quad \text{(first London equation)}$$

In the steady state with $\vec{E} = 0$ inside a superconductor, taking the curl of the first equation and using Faraday's law gives the **second London equation**:

$$\nabla \times \vec{J}_s = -\frac{n_s e^2}{m}\vec{B}$$

**Step 2 — Combine with Maxwell's Equation:** 
Ampère's law in magnetostatics states:

$$\nabla \times \vec{B} = \mu_0 \vec{J}_s$$

Take the curl of both sides:

$$\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{B}) = \mu_0 \nabla \times \vec{J}_s = -\frac{\mu_0 n_s e^2}{m}\vec{B}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Vector Identity:** 
Since $\nabla \cdot \vec{B} = 0$ always, the identity $\nabla \times (\nabla \times \vec{B}) = -\nabla^2 \vec{B}$ gives:

$$\nabla^2 \vec{B} = \frac{\mu_0 n_s e^2}{m} \vec{B} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nabla^2 \vec{B} = \frac{1}{\lambda_L^2}\vec{B}$$

where the **London penetration depth** is:

$$\boxed{\lambda_L = \sqrt{\frac{m}{\mu_0 n_s e^2}}}$$

**Step 4 — One-Dimensional Decay Solution:** 
For a planar surface at $x = 0$ with field along $\hat{z}$:

$$\frac{d^2 B_z}{dx^2} = \frac{B_z}{\lambda_L^2}$$

The general solution is $B_z = A e^{x/\lambda_L} + C e^{-x/\lambda_L}$. Boundedness as $x \to \infty$ rejects the growing exponential:

$$\boxed{B_z(x) = B_0 e^{-x/\lambda_L}}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation:** 
At $x = \lambda_L$, the field is $B_0/e \approx 0.368 B_0$; at $x = 3\lambda_L$, it is reduced to $B_0/20$, effectively zero. The penetration depth is typically 50–500 nm for most superconductors.

**Mark Allocation:** (1) London equation statement 1; (2) Combination with Maxwell 1.5; (3) Derivation of $\lambda_L$ 1.5; (4) Solving ODE 1.5; (5) Physical interpretation 1.5.

---

**(b)** *With a neat labeled diagram, explain the magnetic phase diagram of a Type II superconductor. Discuss how the Abrikosov vortex lattice responds to applied current and the phenomenon of flux pinning.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Magnetic Phase Diagram:**
For a Type II superconductor in the $H$–$T$ plane:
- Below $T_c$ and $H < H_{c1}(T)$: **Meissner phase** — perfect diamagnetism.
- $H_{c1}(T) < H < H_{c2}(T)$: **Mixed phase** — Abrikosov vortex lattice.
- Above $H_{c2}(T)$: **Normal phase**.

The curves $H_{c1}(T)$ and $H_{c2}(T)$ both follow approximately:

$$H_{c1,c2}(T) = H_{c1,c2}(0)\left[1 - \left(\frac{T}{T_c}\right)^2\right]$$

**2. Abrikosov Vortex Lattice Structure:**
When the field exceeds $H_{c1}$, vortices nucleate at the surface and arrange in a **2D triangular lattice** (Abrikosov lattice) under mutual repulsion. The lattice constant is:

$$a = \sqrt{\frac{2\Phi_0}{\sqrt{3}\,B}}$$

For $B = 1$ T, $a \approx 49$ nm, and vortex density $\sim 4.8 \times 10^{14}$ m$^{-2}$.

**3. Response to Transport Current — Lorentz Force:**
When a current density $J$ flows perpendicular to the vortices, each vortex experiences a **Lorentz force per unit length**:

$$\vec{F}_L = \vec{J} \times \Phi_0 \hat{n}$$

If vortices move freely under this force, the motion dissipates energy (via induced electric fields in the vortex cores) and the superconductor exhibits **flux-flow resistance** — destroying the zero-resistance property.

**4. Flux Pinning — Engineering the Critical Current:**
To prevent dissipation, defects are deliberately introduced (dislocations, grain boundaries, precipitates, irradiation-induced columnar defects). These defects **pin** the vortices via interaction with the local variation of $\xi$, $\lambda_L$, or condensation energy. The maximum current the superconductor can carry without vortex motion is the **critical current density $J_c$**.

The volumetric pinning force density is:

$$F_p = J_c \times B$$

In **Type II engineering**, the goal is to maximize $F_p$ to obtain commercially viable $J_c > 10^9$ A/m² at high fields (used in MRI magnets, accelerator dipoles).

**5. The Bean Model and Critical State:**
For a finite-size Type II sample in increasing field, the **Bean model** describes the critical state: the field penetrates up to a depth where the local current equals $\pm J_c$. This determines the AC loss and stability of superconducting coils.

**Mark Allocation:** (1) Phase diagram description 1.5; (2) Vortex lattice 1; (3) Lorentz force 1.5; (4) Flux pinning mechanism 2; (5) Engineering of $J_c$ 1.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "perfect conductor" with "superconductor."** A perfect conductor ($R = 0$) merely *traps* flux; only a superconductor *expels* flux via the Meissner effect. Marks are often docked for not making this distinction explicitly.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units in $\lambda_L$ formula.** Always show $\lambda_L = \sqrt{m/(\mu_0 n_s e^2)}$ with proper SI units. $\mu_0$ in H/m, $n_s$ in m$^{-3}$.
>
> 3. **Writing $H$ instead of $B$** (or vice-versa) in equations. Be consistent: $H$ is the **applied** field (A/m), $B$ is the magnetic induction (T) inside the material.
>
> 4. **In Type II $H$–$T$ diagrams, swapping $H_{c1}$ and $H_{c2}$.** Recall: $H_{c1} < H_c < H_{c2}$. The mixed state lies *between* $H_{c1}$ and $H_{c2}$, not below $H_{c1}$.
>
> 5. **Omitting the factor of 2 in $\Phi_0 = h/(2e)$.** The "2" represents the **Cooper pair charge $2e$**, the very heart of BCS theory. Writing $h/e$ costs at least 1 mark in derivations.
>
> 6. **In GL parameter questions**, students often write $\kappa = \xi/\lambda_L$ (inverted). The correct form is $\kappa = \lambda_L/\xi$ (Type II when this ratio *exceeds* $1/\sqrt{2}$).
>
> 7. **Not mentioning examples.** KTU examiners expect at least 1 example for each superconductor type (Pb/Hg for Type I, Nb-Ti/YBCO for Type II) to score full marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Definition of superconductor:** Two simultaneous properties — zero resistance **and** the Meissner effect.
- **Meissner effect:** Complete expulsion of $B$ from bulk; $\chi = -1$. Independent of zero-resistance property.
- **Type I superconductor:** Single critical field $H_c$; abrupt first-order transition; $\kappa < 1/\sqrt{2}$; $\xi > \lambda_L$; examples — Pb, Hg, Sn.
- **Type II superconductor:** Two critical fields $H_{c1}$, $H_{c2}$; gradual transition with mixed state for $H_{c1} < H < H_{c2}$; $\kappa > 1/\sqrt{2}$; $\lambda_L > \xi$; examples — Nb-Ti, YBCO, Nb$_3$Sn.
- **Ginzburg–Landau parameter:** $\kappa = \lambda_L / \xi$. Boundary at $\kappa = 1/\sqrt{2}$.
- **London penetration depth:** $\lambda_L = \sqrt{m/(\mu_0 n_s e^2)}$. Typical: 50–500 nm. Field decays as $B = B_0 e^{-x/\lambda_L}$.
- **Coherence length:** $\xi = \hbar v_F / (\pi \Delta(0))$. Sets the size of the vortex core in Type II.
- **Critical field law:** $H_c(T) = H_c(0)[1-(T/T_c)^2]$ — parabolic, zero at $T_c$.
- **Condensation energy:** $F_n - F_s = \frac{1}{2}\mu_0 H_c^2$ per unit volume.
- **Flux quantum:** $\Phi_0 = h/(2e) \approx 2.07 \times 10^{-15}$ Wb — proof of Cooper pairs.
- **Mixed (Shubnikov) state:** Abrikosov vortex lattice between $H_{c1}$ and $H_{c2}$ in Type II.
- **Flux pinning:** Defects immobilize vortices → enables high $J_c$ → real-world magnet engineering.
- **Engineering relevance:** MRI magnets (Nb-Ti at 4.2 K), LHC dipoles, maglev, fault-current limiters, superconducting qubits.
- **High-$T_c$ cuprates (YBCO):** $T_c \approx 92$ K → liquid-nitrogen cooling possible (77 K) → revolution in applied superconductivity.
- **BCS Theory (qualitative, expected in 2-mark questions):** Phonon-mediated Cooper pair formation below $T_c$ → bosonic condensation → energy gap $\Delta$ opens at the Fermi surface.
- **Key SI units:** $H_c$ in A/m, $B$ in T, $\lambda_L$ in m, $\xi$ in m, $J_c$ in A/m², $\Phi_0$ in Wb.

> **Final Exam Tip:** In any 7-mark derivation, allocate at least 1–2 lines for *physical interpretation* after the algebra. KTU 2024 Scheme rewards conceptual clarity, not just algebraic gymnastics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Superconductors, Dielectric Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Applications of Superconductors, Dielectric Constant ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Applications of Superconductors — Definition A **superconductor** is a material that, when cooled below a characteristic critical temperature $T_c$, exhibits two hallmark properties: 1. **Zero electrical resistivity** ($\rho = 0$), and 2. **Perfect diamagnetism** — the Meissner effect, characterised by magnetic susceptibility $\chi = -1$. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > A superconductor is a quantum-mechanical phase of matter in which the electrical resistance of a material drops abruptly to exactly zero below a characteristic critical temperature $T_c$, and the material expels all internal magnetic flux (Meissner effect). Engineering applications are derived from these twin phenomena. ### 1.2 Real-World Conceptual Analogy — Superconductors Think of a **superconductor as a frictionless highway for electrons**. In an ordinary copper wire, electrons "rub" against the atomic lattice, lose energy as heat, and the wire warms up — much like a rough road slows down a car. Inside a superconductor, electrons pair up into **Cooper pairs** (bound by lattice vibrations called phonons) and glide through the crystal lattice as a single quantum condensate, just as a convoy of synchronised vehicles on a glass-smooth track moves without losing momentum. This is the famous **BCS theory** (Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer, 1957). The two critical engineering parameters are: - **Critical Temperature** $T_c$ (in **K**) — the temperature below which superconductivity sets in. - **Critical Magnetic Field** $H_c$ (in **A/m** or **T**) — the maximum field a Type-I superconductor can expel. - **Critical Current Density** $J_c$ (in **A/m²**) — the maximum current density it can carry. ### 1.3 Dielectric Constant — Definition The **dielectric constant** (also called **relative permittivity**) of a material is the ratio of the permittivity of the material $\varepsilon$ to the permittivity of free space $\varepsilon_0$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition** > The dielectric constant (or relative permittivity) of a medium, denoted $\varepsilon_r$ (sometimes $K$), is defined as the ratio of the capacitance of a capacitor with the dielectric filling the space between its plates to the capacitance of the same capacitor with vacuum (or air) between the plates: > $$\varepsilon_r = \frac{C}{C_0} = \frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_0}$$ > It is a **dimensionless** quantity (a pure number), always $\varepsilon_r \geq 1$. ### 1.4 Real-World Conceptual Analogy — Dielectric Constant Imagine a parallel-plate capacitor as **two parallel walls with a "springiness field" between them**. When you fill the gap with a dielectric (an electrical insulator), the internal atoms act like **tiny compression springs** — they polarise (their positive and negative charge centres shift slightly) and create their own internal field that **partially cancels** the applied field. The result is that more charge can be stored on the plates for the same voltage — the capacitor's "storage capacity" goes up by a factor of $\varepsilon_r$. Hence a **water molecule** ($\varepsilon_r \approx 80$) is far better at this "spring buffering" than **vacuum** ($\varepsilon_r = 1$) or **dry air** ($\varepsilon_r \approx 1.0006$). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Engineering Applications of Superconductors Superconductors fall into two broad classes that determine their applications: - **Type-I Superconductors** — pure metals (Pb, Hg, Sn); complete Meissner effect up to $H_c$; usually soft, low $T_c$. - **Type-II Superconductors** — alloys and ceramics (Nb-Ti, Nb₃Sn, YBCO); allow magnetic flux penetration through quantised vortices between $H_{c1}$ and $H_{c2}$; carry far higher currents and fields. The key engineering applications, mapped to the underlying physical principle, are listed below. > [!NOTE] > **Why Superconductors Matter in Electrical Engineering** > Every application below is rooted in **one** of three physics facts: (1) zero resistance → lossless current transport, (2) Meissner effect → magnetic levitation/expulsion, (3) Josephson tunnelling → extreme magnetic-field sensitivity. #### A. Medical — MRI (Magnetic Resonance Imaging) - The strong, stable magnetic field (typically **1.5 T to 7 T**) needed to align hydrogen nuclei in the human body is generated by superconducting coils (commonly Nb-Ti) cooled by liquid helium at **4.2 K**. - Zero resistance means the enormous current circulates forever without power loss. #### B. Transportation — Maglev (Magnetic Levitation) Trains - The Meissner effect, combined with repulsive forces from induced currents in a superconductor, is used to levitate a train above its guideway, eliminating wheel–rail friction. - Commercial example: the **Shanghai Maglev** (Germany's Transrapid technology) reaches **431 km/h**. #### C. High-Energy Physics — Particle Accelerators - The Large Hadron Collider (LHC) at CERN uses **1232 dipole superconducting magnets** (Nb-Ti) cooled to **1.9 K** to bend proton beams of **7 TeV** each. - Superconducting RF cavities accelerate particles with minimal ohmic loss. #### D. Power Engineering — Superconducting Cables & Fault Current Limiters - Underground HTS (High-$T_c$ Superconductor, e.g., BSCCO) cables can carry **5–10×** the current of copper cables of the same cross-section, with **zero resistive loss**. - **Superconducting Fault Current Limiters (SFCL)** use the sudden loss of superconductivity (quench) above $J_c$ to suppress short-circuit currents in milliseconds. #### E. Quantum Electronics — SQUIDs - A **Superconducting Quantum Interference Device (SQUID)** is the most sensitive magnetometer ever built, capable of detecting fields as small as **$10^{-15}$ T** (about ten billion times smaller than Earth's field). - Working principle: DC Josephson effect + magnetic-flux quantisation $\Phi_0 = h / 2e \approx 2.067 \times 10^{-15}$ Wb. - Used in magnetoencephalography (MEG) to map brain activity. #### F. Energy Storage — SMES - A **Superconducting Magnetic Energy Storage** device stores energy in the magnetic field of a current loop. With zero resistance the current — and hence the stored energy — is essentially permanent. #### G. Electronics — Single-Photon Detectors (SNSPD) - Superconducting Nanowire Single-Photon Detectors are used in quantum communication and astronomy because of their near-unity quantum efficiency and low dark counts. --- ### 2.2 Dielectric Constant — Detailed Theory When a dielectric slab is inserted between the plates of a charged, isolated capacitor (charge $Q$ fixed): - The applied external field $\mathbf{E}_0$ polarises the molecules of the dielectric. - Each atom/molecule becomes a tiny **dipole** with dipole moment $\mathbf{p} = q \mathbf{d}$. - A bound surface charge density $\sigma_b$ appears on the dielectric faces, creating an **internal depolarising field** $\mathbf{E}_p$ opposing $\mathbf{E}_0$. The **net electric field** inside the dielectric is therefore reduced: $$\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{E}_0 + \mathbf{E}_p$$ The **electric displacement** $\mathbf{D}$ (which depends only on free charges, by Gauss's law) is related to $\mathbf{E}$ through the absolute permittivity $\varepsilon$: $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \mathbf{P}$$ where $\mathbf{P}$ is the **polarisation vector** (dipole moment per unit volume, in C/m²). For a linear, isotropic dielectric: $$\mathbf{P} = \varepsilon_0 \chi_e \mathbf{E}$$ where $\chi_e$ is the **electric susceptibility** (dimensionless). Substituting gives the fundamental identity: $$\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Takeaway** > Three coupled quantities — $\varepsilon_r$, $\chi_e$, and $P$ — describe the same physics. Memorise: $\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$. From this, $\varepsilon_r \geq 1$ always, with equality only for vacuum. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Table 2.1 — Dielectric Constant and Superconductor Formula Sheet** | Quantity | Symbol / Formula | Unit | Physical Meaning | |----------|------------------|------|------------------| | Dielectric constant (relative permittivity) | $\varepsilon_r = C/C_0$ | dimensionless | Capacitance enhancement factor | | Permittivity of free space | $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ | F/m | Fundamental constant | | Absolute permittivity | $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ | F/m | Material permittivity | | Susceptibility identity | $\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$ | dimensionless | Coupling between $P$ and $E$ | | Polarisation (linear) | $P = \varepsilon_0 \chi_e E$ | C/m² | Dipole moment per unit volume | | Capacitance with dielectric | $C = \varepsilon_r C_0 = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | F | Energy storage | | Energy stored in capacitor | $U = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E^2 \cdot (Ad)$ | J | Field energy density $u = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E^2$ | | Loss tangent | $\tan \delta = \varepsilon'' / \varepsilon'$ | dimensionless | Dielectric loss measure (AC) | | Critical temperature | $T_c$ | K | Onset of superconductivity | | Critical magnetic field (Type I) | $H_c(T) = H_{c0}\left[1 - (T/T_c)^2\right]$ | A/m | Empirical law | | London penetration depth | $\lambda_L = \sqrt{m/\mu_0 n_s q^2}$ | m | Depth of field decay inside SC | | Coherence length | $\xi$ | m | Size of a Cooper pair | | BCS energy gap | $2\Delta(0) \approx 3.528\, k_B T_c$ | J | Pair-binding energy | | Josephson flux quantum | $\Phi_0 = h/2e \approx 2.067 \times 10^{-15}$ | Wb | Fundamental flux unit | | Levitation force density | $f = B^2 / 2 \mu_0$ | N/m² | Meissner-repulsion estimate | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacitance Enhancement by a Dielectric (Parallel-Plate Case) **Statement to prove:** Inserting a dielectric slab of thickness $d$ (filling the gap) increases the capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor of area $A$ by a factor $\varepsilon_r$. **Step 1 — Capacitance of an empty (vacuum) parallel-plate capacitor.** A parallel-plate capacitor with vacuum between its plates of area $A$ and plate separation $d$ has capacitance: $$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$ This comes from solving Laplace's equation for the uniform field $E_0 = \sigma / \varepsilon_0$ between the plates and using the definition $C = Q/V$, with $V = E_0 d$ and $Q = \sigma A$. **Step 2 — Insert the dielectric and identify the polarisation charges.** The free surface charge density on the plates is $\sigma_f$. The dielectric becomes polarised, with bound surface charge density $\sigma_b$ appearing on the inner faces of the dielectric. The net (effective) surface charge density that contributes to the field inside the dielectric is: $$\sigma_{\text{eff}} = \sigma_f - \sigma_b$$ **Step 3 — Write the polarisation in terms of the field inside the dielectric.** For a linear, homogeneous, isotropic dielectric: $$P = \varepsilon_0 \chi_e E$$ Also, the bound surface charge density is the polarisation component normal to the surface: $$\sigma_b = P = \varepsilon_0 \chi_e E$$ **Step 4 — Apply Gauss's law using electric displacement $\mathbf{D}$.** The electric displacement is defined such that its flux through a closed surface equals only the *enclosed free charge*: $$\oint \mathbf{D} \cdot d\mathbf{A} = Q_{f,\text{enc}}$$ For one plate, this gives $D = \sigma_f$. Because $\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E}$, we have: $$\varepsilon E = \sigma_f \quad \Rightarrow \quad E = \frac{\sigma_f}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\sigma_f}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$ **Step 5 — Relate the voltage across the capacitor to the field.** For a uniform field between the plates, the potential difference is: $$V = E \cdot d = \frac{\sigma_f d}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}$$ **Step 6 — Compute the new capacitance.** $$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{\sigma_f A}{\sigma_f d / (\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r)} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$ **Step 7 — Form the ratio.** $$\frac{C}{C_0} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d}{\varepsilon_0 A / d} = \varepsilon_r$$ This proves the defining relationship $C = \varepsilon_r C_0$. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering interpretation:** A dielectric does **not** change the free charge $Q$ on an *isolated* (charge-fixed) capacitor, but it reduces the voltage by a factor $\varepsilon_r$ because of the opposing polarisation field. The same free charge at a lower voltage means a larger capacitance. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Connecting $P$, $E$, $\chi_e$, and $\varepsilon_r$ from First Principles **Step 1 — Start with the definition of $D$.** $$D = \varepsilon_0 E + P$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the linear-dielectric constitutive relation $P = \varepsilon_0 \chi_e E$.** $$D = \varepsilon_0 E + \varepsilon_0 \chi_e E = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) E$$ **Step 3 — Identify the absolute permittivity $\varepsilon$.** $$D = \varepsilon E \quad \text{with} \quad \varepsilon \equiv \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e)$$ **Step 4 — Divide both sides by $\varepsilon_0$ to extract $\varepsilon_r$.** $$\varepsilon_r \equiv \frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_0} = 1 + \chi_e$$ This identity is universal for linear, homogeneous, isotropic dielectrics. It is the single most-tested KTU relation in this topic. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Critical Magnetic Field vs. Temperature (Empirical Law) The critical magnetic field of a Type-I superconductor falls to zero at $T_c$. The empirical parabolic law is: $$H_c(T) = H_{c0}\left[1 - \left(\frac{T}{T_c}\right)^2\right]$$ where $H_{c0}$ is the critical field at absolute zero (a material constant). **Step 1 — At $T = 0$ K:** $H_c(0) = H_{c0}\left[1 - 0\right] = H_{c0}$ ✔ (definition). **Step 2 — At $T = T_c$:** $H_c(T_c) = H_{c0}\left[1 - 1\right] = 0$ ✔ (superconductivity vanishes). **Step 3 — Smooth parabolic interpolation in between.** > [!IMPORTANT] > This law is used in KTU problems to determine the maximum safe operating field at a given temperature — vital for designing MRI magnets, accelerator dipoles, and SMES coils. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Density Stored in a Dielectric For a parallel-plate capacitor, the electrostatic energy is $U = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^2$. Substituting $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ and $V = E d$: $$U = \frac{1}{2}\cdot \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} \cdot E^2 d^2 = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E^2 (Ad)$$ Dividing by the volume $V_{\text{vol}} = Ad$ gives the **energy density**: $$u = \frac{U}{V_{\text{vol}}} = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E^2 = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon E^2 = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{D} \cdot \mathbf{E}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters:** This shows the energy stored per unit volume is enhanced by a factor $\varepsilon_r$ over vacuum for the same field. It underpins capacitor selection in power-electronics filters, defibrillator paddles, and pulse-forming networks. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Superconductor Applications — Functional Block Architecture The following Mermaid diagram maps the **physics principle → enabling technology → engineering application** hierarchy. This is exactly the "mapping" examiners reward. ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["SUPERCONDUCTOR
CORE PHYSICS"] A1["Zero Resistivity
rho equals 0"] A2["Meissner Effect
chi equals minus 1"] A3["Flux Quantisation
Phi zero equals h over 2e"] A4["Josephson Effect
Tunnelling of Cooper pairs"] B0["ENABLING
TECHNOLOGY"] B1["Persistent High-Field
Solenoid Coils Nb-Ti Nb3Sn"] B2["Lossless Current
Carrying Cables HTS Tapes"] B3["Quench-triggered
Switching Elements"] B4["Ultra-sensitive
Magnetic Sensors SQUID"] B5["Quantum Bit Qubit
Platforms"] C0["ENGINEERING
APPLICATION"] C1["MRI Scanners
1.5 T to 7 T"] C2["LHC Dipole Magnets
CERN 8 T field"] C3["Maglev Trains
Shanghai 431 km per h"] C4["HTS Power Cables
5x copper current density"] C5["Fault Current Limiters
Sub-cycle protection"] C6["SMES Energy Storage
MW scale fast discharge"] C7["MEG Brain Mapping
fT sensitivity"] C8["Quantum Computing
IBM Google Rigetti"] C9["SNSPD Photon Detectors
Quantum communication"] A0 --> A1 A0 --> A2 A0 --> A3 A0 --> A4 A1 --> B1 A1 --> B2 A1 --> B3 A2 --> B1 A2 --> B2 A2 --> B3 A3 --> B4 A4 --> B4 A3 --> B5 A4 --> B5 B1 --> C1 B1 --> C2 B1 --> C6 B2 --> C4 B2 --> C3 B3 --> C5 B4 --> C7 B4 --> C9 B5 --> C8 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Each physics property spawns one or more technologies; each technology then enables specific applications. Examiners frequently ask "Name two applications based on the Meissner effect" — the left-hand column lets you trace the lineage quickly. --- ### 4.2 Dielectric Constant — Field-Suppression Functional Block The following diagram traces how the macroscopic field $E$ inside a dielectric is *built* from contributions of free charges and polarisation, the very process that yields $\varepsilon_r$. ```mermaid flowchart LR F1["Free Charges
plus sigma_f on plate"] F2["Gauss for D
D dot dA equals Q_free"] F3["D equals epsilon times E"] F4["Internal Field E
reduced vs vacuum"] F5["Polarisation P
bound surface charge minus sigma_b"] F6["Susceptibility Relation
P equals epsilon_0 chi_e E"] F7["Identity
epsilon_r equals 1 plus chi_e"] F8["Capacitance Boost
C equals epsilon_r C_0"] F9["Energy Density
u equals half epsilon_0 epsilon_r E squared"] F1 --> F2 --> F3 --> F4 F4 --> F5 F5 --> F6 F6 --> F7 F7 --> F8 F7 --> F9 ``` **Reading the diagram:** Start with the free charges on the capacitor plates (top-left), apply Gauss's law in terms of $\mathbf{D}$, and you arrive at a reduced internal field $E$. The polarisation $P$ is the *cause* of that reduction, and the identity $\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$ ties susceptibility to the observable capacitance boost and the energy density. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]** Define the term *dielectric constant* of a medium. The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is $10\,\text{pF}$ in air. Find its capacitance when a dielectric of relative permittivity $4$ is inserted between the plates (battery disconnected). **Model Answer (3 marks):** The dielectric constant (or relative permittivity) $\varepsilon_r$ of a medium is the ratio of the permittivity of the medium to that of free space: $$\varepsilon_r = \frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{C}{C_0}$$ When the dielectric is inserted and the battery is disconnected, the free charge $Q$ on the plates remains constant, while the voltage reduces by a factor $\varepsilon_r$, so the capacitance increases by the same factor. $$C = \varepsilon_r C_0 = 4 \times 10\,\text{pF} = 40\,\text{pF}$$ **Answer: $C = 40\,\text{pF}$.** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** Mention any **three** engineering applications of superconductors based on (a) the Meissner effect and (b) zero resistivity. **Model Answer (3 marks):** (a) **Applications based on the Meissner effect** (magnetic-flux expulsion): - Magnetic levitation in Maglev trains. - Magnetic shielding in sensitive experiments (e.g., SQUIDs, biomagnetism). - Trapping magnetic flux in plasma-confinement experiments. (b) **Applications based on zero resistivity** (lossless current): - Superconducting magnets in MRI scanners. - High-field dipole magnets in particle accelerators (e.g., LHC at CERN). - Lossless HTS power transmission cables. --- ### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > Following KTU 2024 ESE pattern, **either–or** style internal choice is provided. Each long question has two sub-parts, each carrying **7 marks**. --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — Dielectric Constant Focused **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** Derive the relation $\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$ starting from the definition of the electric displacement vector $\mathbf{D}$. State clearly the meaning of each symbol. **(b)** A parallel-plate capacitor has plates of area $200\,\text{cm}^2$ separated by a distance of $2\,\text{mm}$. The dielectric between the plates has a relative permittivity of $5$ and a dielectric strength of $50\,\text{kV/cm}$. (i) Calculate the capacitance of the capacitor. (ii) Calculate the maximum voltage that can be applied across the capacitor without breakdown. (iii) Calculate the energy stored at the maximum safe voltage. **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of $\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$ (7 marks):** The **electric displacement vector** $\mathbf{D}$ is defined via the relation: $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \mathbf{P}$$ where $\mathbf{E}$ is the electric field intensity and $\mathbf{P}$ is the polarisation vector (dipole moment per unit volume). For a linear, homogeneous, isotropic dielectric, the polarisation is proportional to the applied field: $$\mathbf{P} = \varepsilon_0 \chi_e \mathbf{E}$$ Substituting into the definition of $\mathbf{D}$: $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \varepsilon_0 \chi_e \mathbf{E} = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) \mathbf{E}$$ The absolute permittivity of the medium $\varepsilon$ is defined by $\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon \mathbf{E}$, so: $$\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e)$$ Dividing both sides by $\varepsilon_0$: $$\boxed{\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e}$$ **Meaning of symbols:** - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$ — permittivity of free space. - $\varepsilon_r$ — relative permittivity (dielectric constant), dimensionless. - $\chi_e$ — electric susceptibility, dimensionless. - $\mathbf{D}$ — electric displacement vector (C/m²). - $\mathbf{P}$ — polarisation vector (C/m²). **(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):** **Given:** $A = 200\,\text{cm}^2 = 200 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}^2$, $d = 2\,\text{mm} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, $\varepsilon_r = 5$, $E_{\text{max}} = 50\,\text{kV/cm} = 50 \times 10^5\,\text{V/m} = 5 \times 10^6\,\text{V/m}$. **(i) Capacitance (2 marks):** $$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(5)(2 \times 10^{-2})}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$C = \frac{8.854 \times 10^{-13}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.427 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{F} \approx 442.7\,\text{pF}$$ **(ii) Maximum safe voltage (2 marks):** $$V_{\text{max}} = E_{\text{max}} \times d = (5 \times 10^6\,\text{V/m})(2 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}) = 10^4\,\text{V} = 10\,\text{kV}$$ **(iii) Energy stored at $V_{\text{max}}$ (3 marks):** $$U = \frac{1}{2} C V_{\text{max}}^2 = \frac{1}{2} (4.427 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{F})(10^4\,\text{V})^2$$ $$U = \frac{1}{2} (4.427 \times 10^{-10})(10^8) = \frac{1}{2} (4.427 \times 10^{-2}) = 2.21 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{J}$$ $$\boxed{U \approx 22.1\,\text{mJ}}$$ --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Superconductors Focused (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** Explain **Meissner effect** in superconductors. Distinguish clearly between Type-I and Type-II superconductors on the basis of their magnetisation behaviour and critical fields. **(b)** A Type-I superconducting lead sample has $T_c = 7.2\,\text{K}$ and $H_{c0} = 6.5 \times 10^4\,\text{A/m}$. Calculate: (i) The critical magnetic field at $4.2\,\text{K}$ (liquid-helium temperature). (ii) The maximum current that can be carried by a lead wire of $1\,\text{mm}$ diameter at $4.2\,\text{K}$ without destroying superconductivity. **Model Solution:** **(a) Meissner effect & Type-I vs. Type-II (7 marks):** **Meissner effect:** When a superconducting material is cooled below its critical temperature $T_c$ in the presence of a magnetic field, it expels all magnetic flux from its interior, becoming a perfect diamagnet with magnetic susceptibility $\chi = -1$. This expulsion is *not* a consequence of zero resistance alone — it is a distinct thermodynamic property. The field inside an ideal superconductor is exactly zero, and surface supercurrents flow to cancel any applied field (within a thin surface layer of thickness $\lambda_L$, the **London penetration depth**). **Distinction (tabulated form recommended in answer scripts):** | Property | Type-I Superconductor | Type-II Superconductor | |----------|----------------------|------------------------| | Examples | Pb, Hg, Sn, Al | Nb-Ti, Nb₃Sn, YBCO | | Magnetisation curve | Sharp drop at $H_c$ | Two-step drop at $H_{c1}$ and $H_{c2}$ | | Number of critical fields | One ($H_c$) | Two ($H_{c1} < H_{c2}$) | | Magnetic state above $H_c$ | Normal (no flux penetration) | Mixed state (vortex lattice) | | Maximum field tolerated | Low (typically < 0.1 T) | High (can exceed 10 T) | | Applications | Lab demos, sensitive magnetometers | MRI, accelerators, HTS cables | **In Type-I:** Below $H_c$, complete Meissner expulsion. Above $H_c$, sudden transition to normal state. **In Type-II:** Below $H_{c1}$, complete Meissner expulsion. Between $H_{c1}$ and $H_{c2}$, magnetic flux penetrates as quantised flux tubes (vortices) each carrying one flux quantum $\Phi_0 = h/2e$, but the material remains superconducting. Above $H_{c2}$, the material becomes normal. **(b) Numerical problem on lead (7 marks):** **Given:** $T_c = 7.2\,\text{K}$, $H_{c0} = 6.5 \times 10^4\,\text{A/m}$, $T = 4.2\,\text{K}$, wire diameter $D = 1\,\text{mm} = 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$. **(i) Critical field at $4.2\,\text{K}$ (4 marks):** Using the empirical parabolic law: $$H_c(T) = H_{c0}\left[1 - \left(\frac{T}{T_c}\right)^2\right]$$ Substituting: $$\frac{T}{T_c} = \frac{4.2}{7.2} = 0.5833 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{T}{T_c}\right)^2 = 0.3403$$ $$H_c(4.2\,\text{K}) = (6.5 \times 10^4)\,(1 - 0.3403)\,\text{A/m} = (6.5 \times 10^4)(0.6597)$$ $$\boxed{H_c(4.2\,\text{K}) \approx 4.288 \times 10^4\,\text{A/m}}$$ **(ii) Maximum current at $4.2\,\text{K}$ (3 marks):** The critical current $I_c$ corresponds to the surface magnetic field reaching $H_c$. For a long straight wire of radius $r$, Ampère's law gives the surface field: $$H_{\text{surface}} = \frac{I_c}{2\pi r} = H_c(T) \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_c = 2\pi r H_c(T)$$ With $r = D/2 = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}$: $$I_c = 2\pi (5 \times 10^{-4})(4.288 \times 10^4) = 2\pi (21.44)$$ $$\boxed{I_c \approx 134.7\,\text{A}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting to square** the ratio $T/T_c$ in the parabolic law $H_c(T) = H_{c0}\left[1 - (T/T_c)^2\right]$. Many students write $H_{c0}(1 - T/T_c)$ and lose 2–3 marks. > 2. **Mixing up $\varepsilon_r$ and $\chi_e$.** They are *not* the same. $\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$. The dielectric constant is the *capacitance* enhancement factor, while susceptibility is the *polarisation* response. > 3. **Unit conversions in $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A/d$.** Use SI: $A$ in m², $d$ in m. Forgetting this gives answers off by factors of $10^4$ or $10^6$ — an immediate 1-mark penalty per sub-part. > 4. **Stating "Type-II is better than Type-I" without justification.** Examiners want the *reason*: higher $H_{c2}$, mixed state allows large currents and fields. > 5. **Conflating Meissner effect with perfect conductivity.** A perfect conductor would *trap* an applied field if cooled in it; a superconductor *expels* it. This is a 1-mark definition trap. > 6. **Forgetting to use $V = E \cdot d$ for breakdown voltage.** Students sometimes write $V_{\text{max}} = E_{\text{max}}$ directly. Always multiply by plate separation. --- ### 5.x Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU 2024 Module 3)** - [x] A **superconductor** is defined by *two* properties: zero resistivity **and** the Meissner effect ($\chi = -1$). - [x] Three critical parameters: $T_c$, $H_c$ (or $H_{c1}, H_{c2}$), and $J_c$. - [x] **BCS theory** (Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer): superconductivity arises from Cooper-pair condensation mediated by phonons. - [x] **Type-I** has a single $H_c$; **Type-II** has $H_{c1}$ and $H_{c2}$ with a "mixed state" (vortex lattice) in between. - [x] **MRI**, **Maglev**, **LHC magnets**, **HTS cables**, **SFCLs**, **SQUIDs**, **SMES**, and **SNSPDs** are the high-yield KTU applications. - [x] **Dielectric constant** $\varepsilon_r = C/C_0$ is dimensionless and $\varepsilon_r \geq 1$. - [x] Master the identity $\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$ and the polarisation relation $P = \varepsilon_0 \chi_e E$. - [x] Capacitance with dielectric: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$. - [x] Energy density in a dielectric: $u = \tfrac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E^2$. - [x] **Empirical critical-field law:** $H_c(T) = H_{c0}\left[1 - (T/T_c)^2\right]$ — must be memorised in full. - [x] **London penetration depth** $\lambda_L$ characterises how deep the magnetic field penetrates the surface. - [x] **Coherence length** $\xi$ is the spatial extent of a Cooper pair; $\xi > \lambda_L$ ⇒ Type-I, $\xi < \lambda_L$ ⇒ Type-II. - [x] **Flux quantum** $\Phi_0 = h/2e \approx 2.067 \times 10^{-15}\,\text{Wb}$ — used in SQUID analysis. - [x] Always convert to **SI units** ($\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{F/m}$, area in m², distance in m) before plugging into formulas. - [x] For a *charge-isolated* capacitor with dielectric, $Q$ is constant, $V$ drops by $\varepsilon_r$, $C$ rises by $\varepsilon_r$. - [x] For a *battery-connected* (constant-$V$) capacitor, $V$ is constant, $Q$ rises by $\varepsilon_r$, $C$ rises by $\varepsilon_r$.

topics.title: Polarization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Polarization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **POLARIZATION** is the phenomenon that describes the geometrical orientation of the oscillations of the electric field vector (**E**) of a transverse electromagnetic wave (such as light) at a fixed point in space as a function of time.

In an ordinary light source (e.g., a tungsten bulb, sunlight), the electric field vectors of individual photons vibrate in **all possible directions perpendicular to the direction of propagation** in a completely random manner. Such a wave is termed **unpolarized light**.

When, by some physical mechanism, these vibrations are restricted to a single plane (or follow a well-defined geometric path like a circle or ellipse), the resulting wave is called **polarized light**.

- The plane containing the electric field vector (**E**) and the direction of propagation is called the **plane of vibration**.
- The plane perpendicular to the plane of vibration (containing the magnetic field **B**) is called the **plane of polarization**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rope & Fence" Intuition

Imagine you are holding one end of a long rope, and the other end is tied to a wall. You can shake the rope:
- **Vertically only** → waves travel in a single vertical plane. This is **Plane-Polarized (Linear) light**.
- **In a circular motion** → the wave takes the shape of a rotating helix. This is **Circularly Polarized light**.
- **In an elliptical path** → this is **Elliptically Polarized light**.
- **Randomly in all directions (like a vibrating washing machine agitator)** → this is **Unpolarized (Natural) light**.

Now place a **vertical fence with horizontal slits** between you and the wall:
- Only the vertical component of the shake passes through.
- The transmitted wave is now vertically polarized.
- This fence is your **Polarizer**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Takeaway:** Polarization is exclusively a property of **transverse waves**. Longitudinal waves (like sound) **cannot** be polarized. This is a classic two-mark question in KTU exams.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The key constants and metrics that govern polarization phenomena are:

- **Speed of light in vacuum, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Refractive index of glass, n_g ≈ 1.5**
- **Refractive index of water, n_w ≈ 1.33**
- The **degree of polarization (P)** is a dimensionless quantity, ranging from 0 (unpolarized) to 1 (fully polarized).
- The intensity of light is measured in **W/m²** (watts per square meter) or in arbitrary units relative to the incident intensity I₀.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Polarized Light — Structural Breakdown

A light wave can exhibit any of the following polarization states, each a high-yield KTU topic:

### A. Linear (Plane) Polarization
- The electric field vector **E** oscillates along a single straight line in a plane perpendicular to propagation.
- The tip of the **E** vector traces a straight line as time progresses.
- Achieved by: reflection at Brewster's angle, transmission through a Polaroid, double refraction.

### B. Circular Polarization
- The tip of the **E** vector rotates uniformly in a circle at the angular frequency ω.
- The magnitude of **E** remains constant: $\vert E \vert = E_0$.
- Two sub-types:
  - **Right-Hand Circularly Polarized (RCP):** **E** rotates clockwise as seen by the observer toward whom the wave is moving.
  - **Left-Hand Circularly Polarized (LCP):** **E** rotates counter-clockwise.
- Achieved by: passing linearly polarized light through a **Quarter Wave Plate (QWP)** with the E-vector at **45°** to the optic axis.

### C. Elliptical Polarization
- The tip of the **E** vector traces an ellipse.
- This is the most general state of polarized light; circular and linear are special cases.
- Characterized by:
  - **Ellipticity** $\varepsilon$ (ratio of minor to major axis)
  - **Azimuth** $\psi$ (orientation of the major axis)

### D. Unpolarized (Natural) Light
- The **E** vector has no preferred orientation.
- The intensity is the average over all directions.

### E. Partially Polarized Light
- A mixture of polarized and unpolarized components.
- Characterized by the **Degree of Polarization (P)**:

$$P = \frac{I_{\text{max}} - I_{\text{min}}}{I_{\text{max}} + I_{\text{min}}}$$

where $I_{\text{max}}$ and $I_{\text{min}}$ are the maximum and minimum intensities transmitted through a rotating analyzer.

## 2.2 Malus's Law — Step-by-Step Logic

**Why is it needed?** To determine the intensity of plane-polarized light transmitted through a second polaroid (analyzer) when its transmission axis makes an angle θ with the first polaroid (polarizer).

**How it works:**
1. The electric field of plane-polarized light has amplitude $E_0$.
2. When this wave strikes the analyzer, only the component along the transmission axis passes through.
3. The transmitted amplitude is $E_0 \cos\theta$.
4. Since intensity is proportional to the square of the amplitude:

$$I = I_0 \cos^2 \theta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MALUS'S LAW (1810):** $I = I_0 \cos^2 \theta$, where $I_0$ is the maximum transmitted intensity (when $\theta = 0°$).
> - When $\theta = 0°$: $I = I_0$ (maximum transmission)
> - When $\theta = 90°$: $I = 0$ (crossed polarizers — complete extinction)
> - When $\theta = 45°$: $I = I_0 / 2$

## 2.3 Brewster's Law — The Polarization-by-Reflection Principle

**Why is it needed?** To find the angle of incidence at which reflected light becomes completely plane-polarized.

**The Phenomenon:**
- When unpolarized light strikes a dielectric surface, the reflected and refracted rays are generally **partially polarized**.
- At a specific angle of incidence (Brewster's angle, $i_p$), the reflected light becomes **completely plane-polarized**.
- The critical observation: at $i_p$, the reflected ray and the refracted ray are **mutually perpendicular**.

**Brewster's Law:**

$$\tan i_p = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = n_{21}$$

where:
- $n_1$ = refractive index of the incident medium
- $n_2$ = refractive index of the refracting medium
- $n_{21}$ = relative refractive index

## 2.4 Double Refraction (Birefringence)

When a light ray enters certain anisotropic crystals (e.g., **Calcite, Quartz, Tourmaline**), it splits into **two** refracted rays:
1. **Ordinary Ray (O-ray):** Obeys Snell's law; travels with the same velocity in all directions.
2. **Extraordinary Ray (E-ray):** Does **not** obey Snell's law; velocity depends on direction.

The crystal has two refractive indices:
- $n_o = c/v_o$ (ordinary refractive index)
- $n_e = c/v_e$ (extraordinary refractive index)

## 2.5 Wave Plates — Quarter and Half Wave Plates

A **wave plate** is a birefringent crystal cut parallel to the optic axis with a specific thickness to introduce a controlled phase difference between the O-ray and E-ray.

**Phase difference introduced:**

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} (n_e - n_o) \, t$$

where $t$ is the thickness of the plate and $\lambda$ is the wavelength.

| Wave Plate | Phase Difference $\Delta\phi$ | Thickness $t$ | Function |
|------------|-------------------------------|---------------|----------|
| **Quarter Wave Plate (QWP)** | $\pi/2$ (90°) | $\dfrac{\lambda}{4 \vert n_e - n_o \vert}$ | Converts linear ↔ circular/elliptical |
| **Half Wave Plate (HWP)** | $\pi$ (180°) | $\dfrac{\lambda}{2 \vert n_e - n_o \vert}$ | Rotates plane of polarization |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — Master Table

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Variables | Units |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | Malus's Law | $I = I_0 \cos^2\theta$ | $\theta$ = angle between polarizer axes | rad or ° |
| 2 | Degree of Polarization | $P = \dfrac{I_{\text{max}} - I_{\text{min}}}{I_{\text{max}} + I_{\text{min}}}$ | $I_{\text{max}}, I_{\text{min}}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Brewster's Law | $\tan i_p = n_2/n_1$ | $n_1, n_2$ = refractive indices | dimensionless |
| 4 | Brewster's Angle Geometry | $i_p + r_p = 90°$ | $r_p$ = angle of refraction | degrees |
| 5 | Phase Difference (Wave Plate) | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}(n_e - n_o)t$ | $t$ = plate thickness, $\lambda$ = wavelength | rad |
| 6 | QWP Thickness | $t = \dfrac{\lambda}{4 \vert n_e - n_o \vert}$ | — | m |
| 7 | HWP Thickness | $t = \dfrac{\lambda}{2 \vert n_e - n_o \vert}$ | — | m |
| 8 | Specific Rotation (Optically Active) | $\theta = S \cdot C \cdot l$ | $S$ = specific rotation, $C$ = concentration, $l$ = path length | deg·mL·g⁻¹·dm⁻¹ |
| 9 | Refractive Index (O-ray) | $n_o = c/v_o$ | $v_o$ = O-ray velocity | dimensionless |
| 10 | Refractive Index (E-ray) | $n_e = c/v_e$ | $v_e$ = E-ray velocity | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility — Why Should an Electrical Engineer Care?**
> - **LCD Displays:** Use twisted-nematic liquid crystals that act as wave plates. Voltage applied across the crystal changes its birefringence, controlling polarization and thus light transmission pixel-by-pixel.
> - **Optical Fiber Communication:** Polarization Mode Dispersion (PMD) limits bandwidth in high-speed fiber links.
> - **3D Cinema Glasses:** Use orthogonal polarizers to separate left/right eye images.
> - **Photodetectors & Sensors:** Many precision sensors (e.g., glucose sensors) use polarized light and optical activity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Malus's Law (Detailed)

**Statement to Prove:** When plane-polarized light of intensity $I_0$ is incident on an analyzer whose transmission axis makes an angle $\theta$ with the plane of polarization, the transmitted intensity is $I = I_0 \cos^2\theta$.

**Setup:** Consider a plane-polarized electromagnetic wave traveling along the X-axis. The electric field vector $\mathbf{E}$ lies in the Y-Z plane and oscillates along a single direction making angle $\theta$ with the Y-axis.

The incident electric field can be written as:

$$\mathbf{E} = E_0 \cos(\omega t - kx) \, \hat{n}$$

where $\hat{n}$ is a unit vector in the plane of vibration, and $E_0$ is the amplitude.

**Step 1:** Decompose the electric field into two orthogonal components — one along the transmission axis of the analyzer (Y-axis) and one perpendicular to it (Z-axis):

$$E_y = E_0 \cos\theta \cdot \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

$$E_z = E_0 \sin\theta \cdot \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

*Reason:* Any vector in the Y-Z plane can be resolved into its rectangular components using the standard trigonometric resolution rule.

**Step 2:** Apply the selection rule of the analyzer. The analyzer transmits only the component parallel to its transmission axis (the Y-component) and completely absorbs the perpendicular component (Z-component).

Therefore, the transmitted electric field is:

$$E_{\text{trans}} = E_0 \cos\theta \cdot \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 3:** Recall that the intensity of a wave is proportional to the **square of the amplitude**:

$$I \propto E_{\text{amplitude}}^2$$

The incident intensity is:

$$I_0 \propto E_0^2$$

The transmitted intensity is:

$$I \propto (E_0 \cos\theta)^2 = E_0^2 \cos^2\theta$$

**Step 4:** Form the ratio to eliminate the proportionality constant:

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = \frac{E_0^2 \cos^2\theta}{E_0^2} = \cos^2\theta$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{I = I_0 \cos^2\theta}$$

This is **Malus's Law**, verified by Malus experimentally in 1810.

**Special Cases Verification:**
- $\theta = 0°$: $I = I_0 \cos^2(0) = I_0$ → maximum transmission.
- $\theta = 90°$: $I = I_0 \cos^2(90°) = 0$ → complete extinction (crossed polarizers).
- $\theta = 45°$: $I = I_0 \cos^2(45°) = I_0 / 2$ → half intensity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Brewster's Law (Detailed)

**Statement to Prove:** $\tan i_p = n_2 / n_1$ where $i_p$ is Brewster's angle (angle of polarization).

**Physical Premise:** When unpolarized light is incident on a transparent dielectric surface, the reflected light is completely plane-polarized at a specific angle of incidence. At this angle, the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other.

**Step 1:** Apply the geometric condition at Brewster's angle:

$$i_p + r_p = 90°$$

This is the experimentally observed condition. Therefore:

$$r_p = 90° - i_p$$

**Step 2:** Apply Snell's law of refraction at the interface between medium 1 (refractive index $n_1$) and medium 2 (refractive index $n_2$):

$$n_1 \sin i_p = n_2 \sin r_p$$

*Reason:* Snell's law is universal and holds for all refraction phenomena, including Brewster's case.

**Step 3:** Substitute $r_p = 90° - i_p$ into Snell's law:

$$n_1 \sin i_p = n_2 \sin(90° - i_p)$$

**Step 4:** Use the trigonometric identity $\sin(90° - i_p) = \cos i_p$:

$$n_1 \sin i_p = n_2 \cos i_p$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange to isolate the ratio:

$$\frac{\sin i_p}{\cos i_p} = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 6:** Recognize the left-hand side as the tangent function:

$$\tan i_p = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{\tan i_p = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = n_{21}}$$

This is **Brewster's Law** (1815).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Quarter Wave Plate Thickness

**Statement to Prove:** The thickness of a QWP that introduces a phase difference of $\pi/2$ between the O-ray and E-ray is $t = \dfrac{\lambda}{4 \vert n_e - n_o \vert}$.

**Step 1:** Express the optical path difference between the E-ray and O-ray after traversing a birefringent crystal of thickness $t$:

$$\Delta x = (n_e - n_o) t$$

*Reason:* The O-ray travels with speed $v_o = c/n_o$ and the E-ray with $v_e = c/n_e$. After time $t/v$, the optical path is $n \cdot v \cdot t$ in the wave-optics convention.

**Step 2:** The corresponding phase difference is:

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \Delta x = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} (n_e - n_o) t$$

*Reason:* A path difference of one full wavelength $\lambda$ corresponds to a phase difference of $2\pi$ radians.

**Step 3:** Apply the QWP condition: $\Delta \phi = \pi/2$:

$$\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} (n_e - n_o) t = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Solve for $t$:

$$t = \frac{\pi/2 \cdot \lambda}{2\pi (n_e - n_o)} = \frac{\lambda}{4 (n_e - n_o)}$$

**Step 5:** Take the absolute value (since $n_e > n_o$ or $n_e < n_o$ depending on the crystal type — positive or negative crystal):

$$\boxed{t = \frac{\lambda}{4 \vert n_e - n_o \vert}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Resultant of Two Perpendicular Harmonic Waves (Elliptical Polarization)

A plane-polarized wave passing through a birefringent crystal splits into two perpendicular components:

$$E_y = E_{0y} \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

$$E_z = E_{0z} \cos(\omega t - kx - \delta)$$

where $\delta$ is the phase difference introduced by the crystal.

**Step 1:** To find the trajectory of the E-vector tip, eliminate time $t$. Let $\phi = \omega t - kx$. Then:

$$E_y = E_{0y} \cos\phi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \cos\phi = \frac{E_y}{E_{0y}}$$

$$E_z = E_{0z} \cos(\phi - \delta)$$

**Step 2:** Expand $\cos(\phi - \delta)$ using the identity:

$$\cos(\phi - \delta) = \cos\phi \cos\delta + \sin\phi \sin\delta$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $\cos\phi = E_y / E_{0y}$ and $\sin\phi = \sqrt{1 - \cos^2\phi} = \sqrt{1 - (E_y/E_{0y})^2}$:

$$\frac{E_z}{E_{0z}} = \frac{E_y}{E_{0y}} \cos\delta + \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{E_y}{E_{0y}}\right)^2} \cdot \sin\delta$$

**Step 4:** Rearranging and squaring gives the equation of an **ellipse** in general. This proves that the superposition of two perpendicular harmonic waves with arbitrary amplitudes and phase differences yields an **elliptically polarized wave**.

**Special Case Analysis (for board-exam style questions):**

| Condition | Resulting Polarization |
|-----------|------------------------|
| $\delta = 0$ or $2\pi$ (no phase difference) | **Linearly polarized** (along a straight line) |
| $\delta = \pi$ (HWP) | **Linearly polarized** (rotated by 90° relative to original) |
| $\delta = \pi/2$ (QWP) and $E_{0y} = E_{0z}$ | **Circularly polarized** |
| $\delta = \pi/2$ (QWP) and $E_{0y} \neq E_{0z}$ | **Elliptically polarized** |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** Unpolarized light of intensity $I_0$ is incident on a stack of two polarizers. The angle between their transmission axes is 30°. Find the intensity of the transmitted light.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** When unpolarized light passes through the first polarizer, its intensity is halved (only one direction of vibration is selected out of two orthogonal ones, averaged over all incoming orientations):

$$I_1 = \frac{I_0}{2}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Malus's law for the second polarizer (analyzer) at angle $\theta = 30°$:

$$I_2 = I_1 \cos^2(30°) = \frac{I_0}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{I_0}{2} \cdot \frac{3}{4} = \frac{3 I_0}{8}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{I_2 = \frac{3 I_0}{8} = 0.375 \, I_0}$$

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions (Short Answer / Conceptual)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Set B]

> **Q1.** Define polarization of light. Why are sound waves in air not affected by polarization?

**Model Answer:**

Polarization is the phenomenon of restricting the vibrations of the electric field vector of a light wave to a particular direction perpendicular to the direction of propagation.

Polarization is exhibited only by **transverse waves**, where the vibration direction is perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation. **Sound waves are longitudinal waves** — the particle oscillation is along the direction of propagation, so the concept of an "orientation of vibration" is meaningless. Therefore, sound waves cannot be polarized.

> **Marking Scheme (KTU 2024 pattern):**
> - Definition of polarization: **1.5 marks**
> - Identification of sound as longitudinal + justification: **1.5 marks**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> **Q2.** State and explain Brewster's law. What is the value of Brewster's angle for a glass surface of refractive index 1.5?

**Model Answer:**

**Brewster's Law:** When unpolarized light is incident on a transparent dielectric surface at a particular angle (the polarizing angle or Brewster's angle $i_p$), the reflected light is completely plane-polarized. At this angle, the reflected and refracted rays are mutually perpendicular, and:

$$\tan i_p = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Calculation for glass surface** (light going from air, $n_1 = 1$, to glass, $n_2 = 1.5$):

$$\tan i_p = \frac{1.5}{1} = 1.5$$

$$i_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) \approx 56.31°$$

> **Marking Scheme (KTU 2024 pattern):**
> - Statement of Brewster's law: **1 mark**
> - Formula: **1 mark**
> - Final numerical answer: **1 mark**

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **ESE Pattern Note (KTU 2024):** Module 3 questions typically carry 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each. Students answer **one full question** with internal choice between Option A and Option B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]

> **Q. (a)** What is Malus's law? Derive the expression for the intensity of light transmitted through two polaroids kept at an angle $\theta$ with each other when unpolarized light of intensity $I_0$ is incident on the first polaroid. **[7 Marks]**

> **Q. (b)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **Nicol prism**. Mention its drawbacks. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Q.(a):**

**Statement of Malus's Law:**
When plane-polarized light of intensity $I_0$ is incident on an analyzer whose transmission axis makes an angle $\theta$ with the plane of polarization of the incident light, the intensity of the transmitted light is given by:

$$I = I_0 \cos^2 \theta$$

**Derivation:**

**Step 1:** Consider plane-polarized light traveling along the X-axis. Let the plane of vibration of the electric field vector $\mathbf{E}$ make an angle $\theta$ with the Y-axis (transmission axis of the analyzer).

The electric field can be written as:

$$E = E_0 \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 2:** Resolve the electric field into two components — $E_y$ along the transmission axis of the analyzer, and $E_z$ perpendicular to it:

$$E_y = E_0 \cos\theta \cdot \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

$$E_z = E_0 \sin\theta \cdot \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 3:** The analyzer transmits only the component parallel to its transmission axis. So the transmitted electric field is:

$$E_{\text{trans}} = E_0 \cos\theta \cdot \cos(\omega t - kx)$$

**Step 4:** Intensity is proportional to the square of the amplitude:

$$I_0 \propto E_0^2 \quad \text{and} \quad I \propto (E_0 \cos\theta)^2 = E_0^2 \cos^2\theta$$

**Step 5:** Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = \cos^2\theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad I = I_0 \cos^2\theta$$

This proves Malus's law.

**Numerical Application:** For unpolarized light of intensity $I_0$ incident on the **first polaroid**, the transmitted intensity is halved because only one polarization direction is selected from the random distribution:

$$I_1 = \frac{I_0}{2}$$

After the second polaroid (analyzer) at angle $\theta$:

$$I_2 = I_1 \cos^2\theta = \frac{I_0}{2} \cos^2\theta$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{I_{\text{transmitted}} = \frac{I_0}{2} \cos^2\theta}$$

---

#### **Solution to Q.(b):**

**Nicol Prism — Construction:**

A Nicol prism is an optical device made from a naturally birefringent crystal of **calcite** ($\text{CaCO}_3$). It is used to produce and analyze plane-polarized light.

**Construction Steps:**

1. A calcite crystal of length about 3 times its width is taken.
2. The end faces are ground to an angle of **68°** (instead of the natural 71°).
3. The crystal is then cut diagonally along a plane perpendicular to the new end faces.
4. The two halves are polished and cemented together using **Canada balsam** (a transparent resin with refractive index ≈ 1.55).

**Working Principle:**

- Unpolarized light enters the calcite crystal through the slanted end face.
- Due to double refraction, it splits into the **ordinary ray (O-ray)** and the **extraordinary ray (E-ray)**.
- For the O-ray: $n_o = 1.658$ (calcite). For Canada balsam: $n_b = 1.55$.
- Since $n_o > n_b$, the O-ray travels from a denser to a rarer medium at the Canada balsam layer. At a suitable angle, it undergoes **total internal reflection** and is absorbed by the blackened side surface.
- For the E-ray: $n_e = 1.486 < n_b = 1.55$. The E-ray travels from rarer to denser medium and is **transmitted** through the Canada balsam layer without total internal reflection.
- The emergent light is **purely plane-polarized**, vibrating along the principal section of the prism.

**Merits:**
- Produces highly polarized light.
- Used as both polarizer and analyzer.

**Demerits (Drawbacks):**
- The complete light cone cannot be polarized — only a narrow beam is polarized.
- The prism is expensive due to natural calcite scarcity.
- The Canada balsam cement can be damaged by heat and UV light.
- The emergent ray is laterally displaced from the incident ray.

**Working Diagram (Mermaid Block):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L[Unpolarized Light] --> N[Calcite Crystal]
    N --> O[O-ray n greater than Canada balsam]
    N --> E[E-ray n less than Canada balsam]
    O --> TIR[Total Internal Reflection at Canada balsam]
    TIR --> AB[Absorbed by blackened side]
    E --> OUT[Plane Polarized Light Emerges]
    style L fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style N fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style O fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style TIR fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style AB fill:#696969,stroke:#FFF,color:#000
    style OUT fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Nicol Prism):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students write the E-ray is "totally internally reflected" and O-ray is transmitted. **This is BACKWARDS.** The O-ray has the higher refractive index ($n_o = 1.658 > n_b = 1.55$) and undergoes TIR; the E-ray has lower index ($n_e = 1.486$) and passes through. **Lose 2 marks if reversed.**
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to mention the **refractive index of Canada balsam** explicitly. This is a key KTU valuation point — lose 1 mark if missing.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Listing merits/demerits incorrectly. State at least **2 demerits** to score full marks in the construction/working sub-part.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> **Q. (a)** Explain the phenomenon of double refraction. Derive the expression for the thickness of a **quarter wave plate**. **[7 Marks]**

> **Q. (b)** A beam of unpolarized light is incident on a glass surface ($n = 1.5$) at the Brewster's angle. Find: **(i)** the angle of incidence, **(ii)** the angle of refraction, **(iii)** the angle between the reflected and refracted rays. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### **Solution to Q.(a):**

**Phenomenon of Double Refraction:**

When a ray of unpolarized light enters a birefringent (anisotropic) crystal such as calcite, quartz, or tourmaline, it splits into **two refracted rays**:
1. **Ordinary Ray (O-ray):** Obeys Snell's law ($n = \sin i / \sin r$ is constant). Polarized perpendicular to the principal plane. Travels with the same velocity in all directions inside the crystal.
2. **Extraordinary Ray (E-ray):** Does **not** obey Snell's law. Polarized in the principal plane. Travels with a velocity that depends on the direction of propagation.

**Optic Axis:** The direction inside the crystal along which the O-ray and E-ray travel with the same velocity (no double refraction occurs) is called the optic axis.

**Principal Section/Plane:** A plane containing the optic axis and the incident ray.

**Derivation of Quarter Wave Plate Thickness:**

Let linearly polarized light be incident normally on a birefringent crystal slab of thickness $t$, with its plane of vibration at an angle $\alpha$ (typically 45°) to the optic axis. Inside the crystal, the wave splits into O-ray and E-ray components.

**Step 1:** The optical path difference between the E-ray and O-ray after traversing the crystal is:

$$\Delta x = (n_e - n_o) t$$

**Step 2:** The corresponding phase difference is:

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot (n_e - n_o) t$$

**Step 3:** For a Quarter Wave Plate, the required phase difference is $\pi/2$:

$$\frac{2\pi}{\lambda} (n_e - n_o) t = \frac{\pi}{2}$$

**Step 4:** Solving for thickness:

$$t = \frac{\pi/2 \cdot \lambda}{2\pi (n_e - n_o)} = \frac{\lambda}{4(n_e - n_o)}$$

**Step 5:** Taking the absolute value:

$$\boxed{t = \frac{\lambda}{4 \vert n_e - n_o \vert}}$$

**Function of QWP:**
- When plane-polarized light is incident at 45° to the optic axis, the O-ray and E-ray emerge with equal amplitudes but a 90° phase difference, producing **circularly polarized** light.
- Conversely, circularly polarized light incident on a QWP emerges as plane-polarized light.

---

#### **Solution to Q.(b):**

**Given:** $n_1 = 1$ (air), $n_2 = 1.5$ (glass).

**(i) Angle of Incidence (Brewster's Angle):**

By Brewster's law:

$$\tan i_p = \frac{n_2}{n_1} = \frac{1.5}{1} = 1.5$$

$$i_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) = 56.31°$$

$$\boxed{i_p \approx 56.31°}$$

**(ii) Angle of Refraction:**

By the geometric condition at Brewster's angle, the reflected and refracted rays are mutually perpendicular:

$$i_p + r_p = 90°$$

$$r_p = 90° - 56.31° = 33.69°$$

**Verification using Snell's law:**

$$n_1 \sin i_p = n_2 \sin r_p$$

$$1 \times \sin(56.31°) = 1.5 \times \sin(33.69°)$$

$$0.832 = 1.5 \times 0.555 = 0.832 \quad \checkmark$$

$$\boxed{r_p \approx 33.69°}$$

**(iii) Angle Between Reflected and Refracted Rays:**

From the geometry of incidence at a plane interface, the angle between the incident ray and the refracted ray is $(i_p - r_p)$. The angle between the **reflected** ray (which makes angle $i_p$ with the normal on the same side as the incident ray) and the refracted ray (which makes angle $r_p$ with the normal on the other side) is:

$$\theta = 180° - i_p - r_p = 180° - 90° = 90°$$

$$\boxed{\theta = 90°}$$

This confirms the defining property of Brewster's angle — the reflected and refracted rays are always mutually perpendicular.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Brewster's Numerical):**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Using $n_1 = 1.5$ and $n_2 = 1$ (reversed) → wrong Brewster's angle. Always confirm the direction of light travel.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to add the units of the angle (degrees) → lose 0.5 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Not stating the geometric condition $i_p + r_p = 90°$ explicitly. The KTU valuation key specifically awards **1 mark** for this statement. Skip it and lose marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Polarization** is the orientation of the electric field vector of a transverse wave.
- [x] **Only transverse waves can be polarized.** Longitudinal waves (sound) cannot.
- [x] **Malus's Law:** $I = I_0 \cos^2 \theta$ — describes intensity after analyzer.
- [x] For unpolarized light through one polaroid: $I = I_0 / 2$ (always).
- [x] **Brewster's Law:** $\tan i_p = n_2/n_1$ — reflected light is completely polarized at $i_p$.
- [x] At Brewster's angle, **reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular** ($i_p + r_p = 90°$).
- [x] **Double refraction** = splitting of light into O-ray and E-ray in birefringent crystals (calcite, quartz).
- [x] $n_o$ and $n_e$ are the two principal refractive indices of a birefringent crystal.
- [x] **Quarter Wave Plate (QWP):** thickness $t = \lambda / (4 \vert n_e - n_o \vert)$; introduces $\pi/2$ phase shift; converts linear ↔ circular polarization.
