- [x] **Half Wave Plate (HWP):** thickness $t = \lambda / (2 \vert n_e - n_o \vert)$; introduces $\pi$ phase shift; rotates the plane of polarization by $2\alpha$.
- [x] **Nicol Prism:** calcite crystal + Canada balsam cement. O-ray TIR'd, E-ray transmitted. Output is plane-polarized.
- [x] **Degree of Polarization:** $P = (I_{\text{max}} - I_{\text{min}}) / (I_{\text{max}} + I_{\text{min}})$.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** LCD displays, polaroid sunglasses, 3D cinema, optical fiber PMD, glucose sensors, astronomy.
- [x] **Circular polarization** results only when QWP input is at **45°** to the optic axis AND amplitudes are equal.
- [x] **Elliptical polarization** is the most general polarized state; linear and circular are special cases.
- [x] **Mnemonic for Brewster's:** "**T**an Brewster = **R**atio of indices" → $\tan i_p = n_2/n_1$.
- [x] **Mnemonic for QWP thickness:** "**4** in the **denominator**, **absolute difference** in the numerator" → $\lambda / 4 \vert n_e - n_o \vert$.
- [x] **Common pitfall:** In Nicol prism, O-ray (not E-ray) is the one that is totally internally reflected. Memorize: **O** for **O**stracized (kicked out), **E** for **E**mitted.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Permittivity - Relative Permittivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Permittivity - Relative Permittivity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permittivity (ε)** is a fundamental physical constant of a dielectric medium that quantifies the medium's ability to permit (allow) the formation of an electric field within it, or more precisely, the amount of electric flux (electric displacement) generated per unit electric field intensity in that medium.

Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio of the **electric displacement field (D)** to the **electric field intensity (E)** at any point in an isotropic, linear, homogeneous dielectric:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\vec{D}}{\vec{E}}$$

The SI unit of permittivity is **Farad per meter (F/m)**.

**Relative Permittivity (εᵣ)**, also historically called the **dielectric constant (K)**, is a dimensionless quantity that measures the permittivity of a given material relative to the permittivity of free space (vacuum):

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **ε₀ (Permittivity of Free Space / Vacuum Permittivity)**: This is a universal physical constant with the value **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m ≈ (1/36π × 10⁻⁹) F/m**. It represents the permittivity of an absolute vacuum where there is no material medium.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Rubber Tube Hose"

Imagine you are squeezing a tube of toothpaste:
- **An empty bottle (vacuum)**: Offers a certain "resistance" to the paste flow — this is **ε₀**.
- **A bottle filled with a thick gel (dielectric medium)**: The gel offers a different (usually higher) "capacitance for charge storage." The **ratio** of how much charge this new bottle can hold compared to the empty bottle is **εᵣ**.

In simple terms: **A high εᵣ material allows more electric flux to pass through for the same applied electric field than a vacuum would.** This is why εᵣ ≥ 1 for all materials, with εᵣ = 1 exactly for free space.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook**: Think **"Per-MIT-tivity"** = **"How much electric field is permitted"**. A material with high permittivity is like a wide highway for electric field lines — many lines can pass through easily.

## 1.3 Why This Matters in Electrical Engineering

The concept of permittivity governs the design of:
- **Capacitors** (C = εA/d)
- **PCB dielectric substrates**
- **Insulating materials** in transformers
- **Cables and transmission lines**
- **Antenna substrates**
- **Semiconductor devices (MOSFET gate oxides)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Origin: From Coulomb's Law to Permittivity

The concept of permittivity emerges naturally from the **Coulomb's Law** for point charges placed inside a dielectric medium.

In free space, the force between two charges q₁ and q₂ separated by a distance r is:

$$F_{vacuum} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$$

When the same charges are placed inside a dielectric medium, the force is **reduced** by a factor εᵣ because the medium polarizes and partially shields the charges:

$$F_{medium} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon}\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the key insight**: A higher εᵣ means the electrostatic force between charges is weaker. This is because the dielectric polarizes, creating bound charges that oppose the original field.

## 2.2 The Three-Vector Relationship (D, E, P)

In any dielectric, three fundamental field vectors describe the electric state:

| Vector | Symbol | Definition | SI Unit |
|--------|--------|------------|---------|
| Electric Field Intensity | **E** | Force per unit positive test charge | V/m |
| Electric Displacement (Flux Density) | **D** | Free charge per unit area responsible for field | C/m² |
| Polarization | **P** | Dipole moment per unit volume | C/m² |

The governing relationship is:

$$\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \vec{P}$$

For **linear, isotropic, homogeneous (LIH) dielectrics**:

$$\vec{P} = \varepsilon_0 \chi_e \vec{E}$$

where **χₑ** is the **electric susceptibility** (dimensionless). Substituting:

$$\vec{D} = \varepsilon_0 \vec{E} + \varepsilon_0 \chi_e \vec{E} = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) \vec{E}$$

Since **ε = ε₀(1 + χₑ)**, we conclude:

$$\chi_e = \varepsilon_r - 1$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (2024 Scheme)

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations** — they appear every year in KTU exams for this module.

| # | Formula | Description | Constraints |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ | Absolute permittivity of a medium | ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m |
| 2 | $\varepsilon_r = \varepsilon / \varepsilon_0$ | Relative permittivity definition | Dimensionless, εᵣ ≥ 1 |
| 3 | $\vec{D} = \varepsilon \vec{E}$ | Constitutive relation (LIH media) | Linear media only |
| 4 | $C = \varepsilon A / d$ | Capacitance of parallel plate capacitor | A = area, d = separation |
| 5 | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | Capacitance in terms of εᵣ | Standard exam form |
| 6 | $F = (1/4\pi\varepsilon)(q_1q_2/r^2)$ | Coulomb's law in a medium | Spherical symmetry |
| 7 | $u = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2$ | Electrostatic energy density (J/m³) | Valid for any medium |
| 8 | $\chi_e = \varepsilon_r - 1$ | Susceptibility to relative permittivity | Linear dielectrics |
| 9 | $C_n = nC_0$ | Capacitance with n dielectrics (series add inverse) | For stack dielectrics |
| 10 | $\varepsilon_{eff} = (\sum d_i) / (\sum d_i/\varepsilon_i)$ | Effective permittivity (series) | Used in multi-layer caps |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Capacitor Sizing**: To make a small, high-capacitance device, engineers choose high-εᵣ ceramics (e.g., BaTiO₃ with εᵣ ~ 1000–10000).
- **Insulation Design**: High-εᵣ materials confine electric field lines, reducing breakdown risk.
- **PCB Materials**: FR-4 (εᵣ ~ 4.5) is standard; high-frequency boards use Rogers substrates (εᵣ ~ 2.2–3.5) for signal integrity.
- **MOSFET Gate**: SiO₂ (εᵣ ~ 3.9) governs gate capacitance; high-κ dielectrics (HfO₂) replaced it at nanoscale nodes.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Permittivity from Coulomb's Law (The Core KTU Derivation)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU 2024 scheme exams for this module.

**Step 1 — Start with Coulomb's Law in free space (vacuum)**

The electrostatic force between two point charges q₁ and q₂ separated by distance r in vacuum is given by Coulomb's experimentally derived law:

$$F_0 = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that ε₀ is the proportionality constant**

Coulomb's law is the product of an experimental observation (inverse-square dependence on r) and a proportionality constant. In SI units, this constant is defined as:

$$\frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9 \text{ N·m}^2/\text{C}^2$$

**Step 3 — Introduce a dielectric medium**

Now consider the same two charges immersed in a homogeneous, isotropic, linear dielectric medium (e.g., oil, mica, water). Experimentally, the force is observed to **decrease** by some factor that depends on the medium. We write the new force as:

$$F = \frac{1}{4\pi K}\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$$

where K is the new proportionality constant that must depend on the medium.

**Step 4 — Define the relative permittivity**

By definition, the ratio of the new proportionality constant to the vacuum constant is the relative permittivity:

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{K}{\varepsilon_0}$$

Equivalently:

$$K = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back into the force equation**

$$F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$$

**Step 6 — Define the absolute permittivity**

Let the product ε₀εᵣ be defined as the **absolute permittivity** of the medium:

$$\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$$

Therefore, the most general form of Coulomb's law in a dielectric medium is:

$$\boxed{F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon}\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}}$$

**Physical Interpretation**: The dielectric medium reduces the effective force between charges because the medium polarizes — bound surface charges appear that partially cancel the original field. The factor 1/εᵣ captures this reduction.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Capacitance of a Parallel Plate Capacitor (Deriving εᵣ's Role)

**Step 1 — Setup**

Consider two parallel conducting plates of area **A**, separated by a small distance **d**, with a dielectric of permittivity ε filling the gap. A charge **+Q** is on the upper plate, **−Q** on the lower plate.

**Step 2 — Surface charge density**

$$\sigma = \frac{Q}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Electric field between the plates (LIH dielectric)**

$$\vec{E} = \frac{\vec{D}}{\varepsilon} = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \frac{Q}{A\varepsilon}$$

**Step 4 — Potential difference between the plates**

$$V = \int_0^d E \, dx = E \cdot d = \frac{Qd}{A\varepsilon}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the definition of capacitance**

By definition, **C = Q/V**:

$$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{Q}{\left(\frac{Qd}{A\varepsilon}\right)} = \frac{A\varepsilon}{d}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute ε = ε₀εᵣ**

$$\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}}$$

**Conclusion**: The capacitance is directly proportional to the relative permittivity. **Doubling εᵣ doubles the capacitance** for the same geometry — this is the entire reason capacitor manufacturers hunt for high-κ materials.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Energy Stored in a Dielectric Medium

**Step 1 — Energy density in vacuum**

For an electric field E in vacuum, the electrostatic energy density is:

$$u_{vacuum} = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 E^2$$

**Step 2 — Energy density in a dielectric**

When a dielectric is inserted, the total energy must account for both the field energy and the polarization work. The energy density in the dielectric is:

$$u = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon E^2 = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E^2$$

**Step 3 — Total energy stored in a capacitor**

For a parallel plate capacitor of volume V = A·d:

$$U = u \cdot V = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E^2 \cdot Ad = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \left(\frac{V_{cap}}{d}\right)^2 \cdot Ad = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}V_{cap}^2 = \frac{1}{2}CV_{cap}^2$$

This recovers the familiar result: **U = ½CV²**, confirming consistency.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Dielectrics (Exam-Relevant Data)

| Material | Relative Permittivity (εᵣ) | Breakdown Strength (MV/m) | Common Application |
|----------|---------------------------|---------------------------|--------------------|
| Vacuum | 1 (exact) | ∞ (no medium) | Reference standard |
| Air (STP) | 1.0006 | 3.0 | Power line insulation |
| Polystyrene | 2.6 | 20 | High-frequency capacitors |
| FR-4 (PCB) | 4.3 – 4.8 | 20 | PCB substrate |
| Glass | 5 – 10 | 10 – 25 | Insulators |
| Mica | 6 – 7 | 100 | Precision capacitors |
| Distilled Water | 80 | — | Reference liquid |
| Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃) | 1000 – 10000 | 4 | MLCC capacitors |
| Strontium Titanate | ~ 300 | — | Experimental high-κ |
| TiO₂ (Rutile) | ~ 100 | 6 | Thick-film capacitors |
| Silicon Dioxide (SiO₂) | 3.9 | 1000 | MOSFET gate oxide |
| Hafnium Dioxide (HfO₂) | ~ 25 | — | Modern high-κ gate |
| Paraffin Wax | 2.1 – 2.5 | 10 | Cable insulation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: How Permittivity Arises in a Dielectric

The following block diagram maps the chain of physical phenomena that lead to the macroscopic quantity called "permittivity" — from the microscopic polarization of atoms to the measurable capacitance of a device.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Applied Electric Field E] --> nodeB[Forces on Atomic Electron Cloud]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Electron Cloud Distorts relative to Nucleus]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Microscopic Dipole Forms]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Each Atom becomes a Tiny Dipole p]
    nodeE --> nodeF[All Dipoles Align with E Field]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Macroscopic Polarization P develops]
    nodeG --> nodeH[P = epsilon_0 chi_e E]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Total Electric Displacement D = epsilon_0 E + P]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[D = epsilon E]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Effective Constant epsilon = epsilon_0 1 + chi_e]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Relative Permittivity epsilon_r = epsilon / epsilon_0]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Measured via Capacitance C = epsilon_r epsilon_0 A / d]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Engineering Device Capacitor]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Relationship Between ε₀, χₑ, εᵣ, and the D / E / P Vectors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[epsilon_0 permittivity of free space] --> nodeC[Combine with chi_e]
    nodeB[chi_e electric susceptibility] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD[epsilon = epsilon_0 1 + chi_e]
    nodeD --> nodeE[epsilon_r = 1 + chi_e]
    nodeD --> nodeF[D vector]
    nodeG[E vector] --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeH[Capacitance C = epsilon A / d]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Energy U = half C V squared]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Polarization P = epsilon_0 chi_e E]
    nodeJ --> nodeF
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Effect of Inserting Dielectric in a Capacitor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Vacuum Capacitor C_0 = epsilon_0 A / d] --> nodeB[Insert Dielectric of permittivity epsilon]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Dielectric Polarizes]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Bound Charges appear on Dielectric Surfaces]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Net Internal Field reduces by factor epsilon_r]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Voltage V reduces for same Q]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Capacitance increases C = epsilon_r C_0]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Energy Storage increases U = half C V squared]
```

## 4.4 Decision Map: When to Use Which Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Given Problem Type] --> nodeB{Geometry Specified}
    nodeB -- Parallel Plates --> nodeC[Use C = epsilon_r epsilon_0 A / d]
    nodeB -- Point Charges --> nodeD[Use F = q1 q2 over 4 pi epsilon_r epsilon_0 r squared]
    nodeB -- Field and Energy --> nodeE[Use u = half epsilon_r epsilon_0 E squared]
    nodeB -- Multiple Dielectric Layers --> nodeF[Use Series Combination: 1 by C_total = sum of 1 by C_i]
    nodeC --> nodeG[Convert to C in Farads]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define permittivity and relative permittivity. State the relation between them.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Permittivity (ε):** It is the physical property of a dielectric medium that measures its ability to permit the passage of electric field lines through it, or equivalently, it is the ratio of electric displacement D to electric field intensity E in the medium.

$$\varepsilon = \frac{D}{E} \quad \text{(SI unit: F/m)}$$

**Relative Permittivity (εᵣ):** It is the ratio of the permittivity of a given medium to the permittivity of free space. It is a dimensionless quantity.

$$\varepsilon_r = \frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Relation:** $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$, where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m is the permittivity of free space.

*(Awarded: 1 mark for definition of ε, 1 mark for definition of εᵣ, 1 mark for the relation and constant.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the physical significance of relative permittivity? Why is it always greater than or equal to one?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Physical Significance:**
1. Relative permittivity (εᵣ) indicates how many times the electric flux density (or capacitance) of a capacitor increases when a dielectric material is inserted between the plates, compared to vacuum.
2. It also represents the factor by which the electrostatic force between two charges is reduced when the charges are placed in the medium instead of vacuum.

**Why εᵣ ≥ 1:**
For vacuum (or free space), εᵣ = 1 exactly. For all other materials, the polarization of bound charges creates an opposing field, which always **reduces** the net internal field. This reduction can never be less than the vacuum case, so εᵣ is always greater than (for real materials) or equal to 1 (for vacuum).

$$\varepsilon_r = 1 \text{ (vacuum)}, \quad \varepsilon_r > 1 \text{ (any material medium)}$$

*(Awarded: 1.5 marks for significance, 1.5 marks for εᵣ ≥ 1 justification.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**: In 14-mark derivations, students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** for: (1) Not defining what each new symbol represents before using it, (2) Skipping the final substitution step, and (3) Forgetting to specify SI units in the final answer. **Always write units explicitly.**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Set A]`

**a)** Derive the expression for the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor partially filled with a dielectric slab of thickness **t** and relative permittivity **εᵣ**, the remaining space **(d − t)** being air. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A parallel plate capacitor has plates of area 200 cm² separated by a distance of 2 mm. The space between the plates is filled with a dielectric of relative permittivity 4.5. Calculate: (i) the capacitance, (ii) the charge stored when connected to a 100 V battery, and (iii) the energy stored. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Setup:** Consider a parallel plate capacitor with plate area **A** and plate separation **d**. A dielectric slab of thickness **t** and relative permittivity **εᵣ** is placed between the plates. The remaining gap (d − t) is filled with air (εᵣ ≈ 1).

**Step 2 — Treat as series combination:** The system is equivalent to two capacitors in series:
- C₁ (dielectric region): thickness t, permittivity εᵣε₀
- C₂ (air region): thickness (d − t), permittivity ε₀

**Step 3 — Express individual capacitances:**

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{t}$$

$$C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - t}$$

**Step 4 — Apply series formula:**

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{C_1} + \frac{1}{C_2} = \frac{t}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A} + \frac{d - t}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$\frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{\varepsilon_0 A}\left[\frac{t}{\varepsilon_r} + (d - t)\right]$$

$$\boxed{C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\left[\dfrac{t}{\varepsilon_r} + (d - t)\right]}}$$

**Special Cases:**
- If t = d (fully filled): $C = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A / d = \varepsilon_r C_0$ ✓
- If t = 0 (no dielectric): $C = \varepsilon_0 A / d = C_0$ ✓

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- A = 200 cm² = 200 × 10⁻⁴ m² = 2 × 10⁻² m²
- d = 2 mm = 2 × 10⁻³ m
- εᵣ = 4.5
- V = 100 V
- ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m

**(i) Capacitance:**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12})(4.5)(2 \times 10^{-2})}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C = \frac{7.9686 \times 10^{-13}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 3.9843 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F}$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 398.43 \text{ pF} \approx 0.398 \text{ nF}}$$

**(ii) Charge stored:**

$$Q = CV = (3.9843 \times 10^{-10})(100) = 3.9843 \times 10^{-8} \text{ C}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 39.84 \text{ nC}}$$

**(iii) Energy stored:**

$$U = \frac{1}{2}CV^2 = \frac{1}{2}(3.9843 \times 10^{-10})(100)^2$$

$$U = \frac{1}{2}(3.9843 \times 10^{-10})(10^4) = 1.992 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J}$$

$$\boxed{U \approx 1.99 \text{ μJ} = 1.99 \times 10^{-6} \text{ J}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Set B]`

**a)** Starting from Coulomb's law, derive the expression for the force between two point charges immersed in a dielectric medium. Define the terms absolute permittivity and relative permittivity in the process. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Two point charges of magnitude +4 μC and −2 μC are placed 30 cm apart in a medium of relative permittivity 5.0. Calculate: (i) the force between them, (ii) the electric field at the midpoint between them, and (iii) the electric displacement at the midpoint. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation:** *(See Section 3.1 above for the complete step-by-step derivation. Recap of key result:)*

$$\boxed{F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2} = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon}\frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}}$$

where:
- **ε = ε₀εᵣ** is the **absolute permittivity** of the medium (F/m)
- **εᵣ = ε/ε₀** is the **relative permittivity** (dimensionless)
- **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** is the permittivity of free space

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- q₁ = +4 μC = +4 × 10⁻⁶ C
- q₂ = −2 μC = −2 × 10⁻⁶ C
- r = 30 cm = 0.3 m
- εᵣ = 5.0
- ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m

**(i) Force between the charges:**

$$F = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\frac{\vert q_1 \vert \vert q_2 \vert}{r^2}$$

Using $1/(4\pi\varepsilon_0) = 9 \times 10^9$ N·m²/C²:

$$F = \frac{(9 \times 10^9)(4 \times 10^{-6})(2 \times 10^{-6})}{(5.0)(0.3)^2}$$

$$F = \frac{72 \times 10^{-3}}{5.0 \times 0.09} = \frac{0.072}{0.45} = 0.16 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F = 0.16 \text{ N (attractive, since charges are opposite)}}$$

**(ii) Electric field at the midpoint:**

Midpoint distance from each charge: r/2 = 0.15 m

Electric field due to +4 μC (pointing away from it, towards the −2 μC side):
$$E_1 = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}\frac{\vert q_1 \vert}{(r/2)^2} = \frac{(9 \times 10^9)(4 \times 10^{-6})}{(5.0)(0.15)^2}$$

$$E_1 = \frac{36000}{0.1125} = 3.2 \times 10^5 \text{ V/m}$$

Electric field due to −2 μC (pointing towards it, i.e., same direction as E₁):
$$E_2 = \frac{(9 \times 10^9)(2 \times 10^{-6})}{(5.0)(0.15)^2} = \frac{18000}{0.1125} = 1.6 \times 10^5 \text{ V/m}$$

Since both fields point in the same direction (from +4 μC towards −2 μC):

$$E_{net} = E_1 + E_2 = 3.2 \times 10^5 + 1.6 \times 10^5$$

$$\boxed{E_{net} = 4.8 \times 10^5 \text{ V/m}}$$

**(iii) Electric displacement at the midpoint:**

$$D = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E = (8.854 \times 10^{-12})(5.0)(4.8 \times 10^5)$$

$$D = (4.427 \times 10^{-11})(4.8 \times 10^5) = 2.125 \times 10^{-5} \text{ C/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{D \approx 2.125 \times 10^{-5} \text{ C/m}^2 \approx 21.25 \text{ μC/m}^2}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Use this checklist in the last 10 minutes before your KTU exam.**

- ✅ **Permittivity ε** = D/E has units of **F/m** (Farad per meter).
- ✅ **Relative permittivity εᵣ** = ε/ε₀ is **dimensionless**, **always ≥ 1**.
- ✅ **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** — memorize this number; it appears in 90% of numericals.
- ✅ Coulomb's law in a medium: $F = q_1 q_2 / (4\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r^2)$.
- ✅ Capacitance scaling: $C_{medium} = \varepsilon_r \cdot C_{vacuum}$.
- ✅ Energy density in a dielectric: $u = \frac{1}{2}\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 E^2$.
- ✅ Susceptibility–permittivity link: $\chi_e = \varepsilon_r - 1$.
- ✅ For series dielectrics, the effective permittivity is: $\varepsilon_{eff} = \text{Total thickness} / \text{Sum of } (d_i/\varepsilon_i)$.
- ✅ High-εᵣ materials (e.g., BaTiO₃) → high capacitance → smaller physical capacitors.
- ✅ Vector relation: **D = ε₀E + P**, and in linear media: **D = εE**.
- ✅ A higher εᵣ implies a **weaker** force between charges (don't get this backwards!).
- ✅ **Units to write explicitly in every numerical**: V/m for E, F for C, J for U, C/m² for D, N for F.
- ✅ **KTU-specific tip**: Always state the **Coulomb's law** form *first*, then introduce the dielectric. Examiners reward the logical flow of derivation, not just the final formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relation between Polarization and Dielectric Constant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relation between Polarization and Dielectric Constant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Polarization (P)** in a dielectric medium is defined as the **electric dipole moment per unit volume** of the material. When a dielectric is subjected to an external electric field **E**, the bound charges (electrons and nuclei) within the material experience small relative displacements, producing aligned dipoles throughout the bulk. This collective alignment constitutes the **polarization vector**.

**Dielectric Constant (ε_r)** — also called the **relative permittivity** — is the dimensionless factor by which the capacitance of a vacuum capacitor increases when the vacuum gap is completely filled with the dielectric material. Mathematically, it quantifies the ability of a material to store electrical energy in an electric field by reducing the internal field through polarization.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> The dielectric constant (relative permittivity) is **not** a free parameter — it is a direct macroscopic manifestation of the microscopic polarization of the medium. The relation ε_r = 1 + P / (ε₀ E) bridges microscopic dipoles (P) with the macroscopic measurable quantity (ε_r).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a crowd of people (representing dielectric molecules) standing in an open field on a calm day. Now, a strong wind begins to blow (this is the **external electric field E**). The crowd leans uniformly in one direction, all aligned with the wind. The collective leaning of the crowd is analogous to **polarization P** — every individual leans, but the group acts as one.

Now, here is the crucial part: because everyone is leaning forward, the wind's effect is *reduced* behind the crowd (because the bodies block some of the flow). The wind inside the crowd is weaker than the wind outside. The ratio of the "outside wind" to the "inside wind" is exactly the **dielectric constant ε_r**.

A material that leans *strongly* (high polarization) will shield the interior field more effectively (high ε_r). A material that barely leans (low polarization) gives ε_r ≈ 1, which is the vacuum case.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway (High-Yield for KTU Board Exams):**
> - ε_r = 1 → Vacuum (no polarization, no shielding)
> - ε_r > 1 → Dielectric (polarization reduces the internal field)
> - The larger the ε_r, the more the material "cancels" the external field inside itself.

### 1.3 Physical Constants Used in This Module

- **ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m** (permittivity of free space)
- **ε_r** is dimensionless (relative permittivity / dielectric constant)
- **P** has units of **C/m²** (same as surface charge density)
- **E** has units of **V/m** (electric field)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Fundamental Vector Relations in a Dielectric

The complete electromagnetic description of a linear, isotropic, homogeneous dielectric requires three coupled vector fields:

1. **Electric Field (E):** The actual microscopic field experienced inside the dielectric material.
2. **Electric Displacement (D):** A derived auxiliary field that depends only on the *free* charges (not bound charges). It is the same inside and outside the dielectric.
3. **Polarization (P):** The dipole moment density of the bound charges.

The defining equations, in the order required for the KTU derivation, are:

- **Constitutive Relation (Linear Dielectric):** 
 $$\mathbf{P} = \chi_e \, \varepsilon_0 \, \mathbf{E}$$

- **Definition of Displacement Field:** 
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \mathbf{P}$$

- **Dielectric Response:** 
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \mathbf{E}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **χ_e** is the **electric susceptibility** — a dimensionless scalar for linear isotropic dielectrics. It measures *how strongly* a material polarizes in response to an applied field. The bridge between microscopic polarization and macroscopic dielectric constant is built using χ_e.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of the Bridge Equation

Step 1 — Start with the definition of D:
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \mathbf{P}$$

Step 2 — Substitute the linear constitutive relation **P = χ_e ε₀ E**:
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \chi_e \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E}$$

Step 3 — Factor out ε₀ E:
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) \, \mathbf{E}$$

Step 4 — Compare with the dielectric response **D = ε₀ ε_r E**:
 $$\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \mathbf{E} = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) \, \mathbf{E}$$

Step 5 — Cancel common factors (ε₀ and **E** are non-zero inside the material):
 $$\boxed{\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e}$$

Step 6 — Solve for the polarization explicitly:
 $$\mathbf{P} = \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1) \, \mathbf{E}$$

### 2.3 The Most Important Form (Directly Tested in KTU Boards)

The single most exam-relevant expression is:
 $$\varepsilon_r = 1 + \frac{P}{\varepsilon_0 E}$$

This shows that the dielectric constant is **unity plus the ratio of bound-charge response to vacuum response**. It is the form KTU examiners most frequently use to test whether a student understands the physical origin of ε_r.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|--------------|-------------------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | Polarization (microscopic) | $\mathbf{P} = N \, \mathbf{p}$ | Dipole moment per unit volume (N = number density) | C/m² |
| 2 | Constitutive relation | $\mathbf{P} = \chi_e \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E}$ | Linear response of bound charges | C/m² |
| 3 | Electric displacement | $\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \mathbf{P}$ | Field due to free charges alone | C/m² |
| 4 | Dielectric response | $\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \mathbf{E}$ | Macroscopic constitutive law | C/m² |
| 5 | Susceptibility–permittivity link | $\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$ | Bridge equation (core result) | dimensionless |
| 6 | Polarization from ε_r | $\mathbf{P} = \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1) \mathbf{E}$ | Direct P–E relation | C/m² |
| 7 | Relative permittivity from P | $\varepsilon_r = 1 + \dfrac{P}{\varepsilon_0 E}$ | Inverse of above | dimensionless |
| 8 | Absolute permittivity | $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ | Material permittivity | F/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL BOARD EXAM WARNING:** Use $\vert \mathbf{E} \vert$ or scalar magnitudes when writing $P = \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1) E$. Never use the bare vertical pipe character $\vert$ inside a markdown table — always use the LaTeX escape $\vert$ to preserve table rendering.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Capacitor Design:** High-ε_r ceramics (e.g., BaTiO₃ with ε_r ≈ 10³ to 10⁴) enable miniaturized capacitors for smartphone motherboards and SMPS circuits.
- **Insulation in Power Cables:** XLPE (cross-linked polyethylene) with ε_r ≈ 2.3 is used in underground HV cables; lower ε_r reduces capacitive charging currents.
- **MOSFET and CMOS Technology:** The gate dielectric (historically SiO₂ with ε_r ≈ 3.9, now HfO₂ with ε_r ≈ 25) controls the threshold voltage — directly dependent on the polarization relation.
- **Microwave Engineering:** Substrates with controlled ε_r (PTFE, alumina) determine the propagation velocity and impedance of microstrip lines.
- **Transformers and Motors:** Insulation papers and oils are selected for specific ε_r values to manage field distribution in windings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — From Microscopic Plate Charges to the P–ε_r Relation

**Setup:** Consider a parallel plate capacitor with plate area **A** and plate separation **d**.

**Step 1: Vacuum Capacitance (no dielectric)**

For a vacuum capacitor, the capacitance is:
 $$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

The charge on each plate is **Q₀**, giving a surface charge density:
 $$\sigma_0 = \frac{Q_0}{A}$$

The electric field between the plates is:
 $$E_0 = \frac{\sigma_0}{\varepsilon_0} = \frac{Q_0}{\varepsilon_0 A}$$

**Step 2: Insert a Dielectric Slab (no free charge added)**

When the dielectric fully fills the gap, the capacitance becomes:
 $$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \varepsilon_r \, C_0$$

For the **same applied voltage V** (the capacitor is connected to a battery, i.e., the E field source is fixed), the new free charge on the plates is:
 $$Q = C V = \varepsilon_r C_0 V = \varepsilon_r Q_0$$

The new free surface charge density is:
 $$\sigma_f = \frac{Q}{A} = \varepsilon_r \sigma_0 = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E_0$$

Note: The free charge on the plates **increases** by a factor of ε_r, but the field **inside the dielectric** is **unchanged** (it is still E₀) because the battery maintains the voltage. The extra free charge is drawn from the battery.

**Step 3: Bound Surface Charge Density on the Dielectric**

Inside the dielectric, the dipoles that form have their positive ends pointing toward the negative plate and negative ends toward the positive plate. This creates a bound surface charge density σ_b at the two surfaces of the slab.

The relationship between polarization and bound surface charge is:
 $$\sigma_b = P$$

where **P** is the magnitude of the polarization vector (which is uniform and perpendicular to the plates in this geometry).

**Step 4: Net Field Inside the Dielectric (Microscopic View)**

The actual field inside the dielectric is the superposition of the field from the free charges and the field from the bound charges. In vector form:
 $$\mathbf{E} = \frac{\sigma_f - \sigma_b}{\varepsilon_0} \, \hat{\mathbf{n}}$$

Substituting the expressions:
 $$E = \frac{\sigma_f - P}{\varepsilon_0}$$

**Step 5: Solve for P**

Rearrange the previous equation to isolate P:
 $$P = \sigma_f - \varepsilon_0 E$$

Substitute the value of σ_f from Step 2 (which equals ε₀ ε_r E₀) and recognize that E = E₀ (the field inside is the same as the vacuum case for a constant-V battery connection):
 $$P = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E - \varepsilon_0 E$$

Factor out ε₀ E:
 $$\boxed{P = \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1) \, E}$$

Dividing both sides by ε₀ E yields the canonical bridge equation:
 $$\boxed{\varepsilon_r = 1 + \frac{P}{\varepsilon_0 E}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation B — From Susceptibility to the Same Bridge

This is the more abstract (but elegant) derivation using only the three defining vector equations.

**Step 1:** Write the definition of the electric displacement field:
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \mathbf{P}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the linear constitutive relation **P = χ_e ε₀ E** into the displacement definition:
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \chi_e \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E}$$

**Step 3:** Factor ε₀ from both terms:
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) \, \mathbf{E}$$

**Step 4:** By definition of the dielectric response, **D = ε₀ ε_r E**:
 $$\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \mathbf{E} = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) \, \mathbf{E}$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by ε₀ E (which is non-zero inside the material):
 $$\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$$

**Step 6:** Substitute back χ_e = P / (ε₀ E):
 $$\varepsilon_r = 1 + \frac{P}{\varepsilon_0 E}$$

This confirms the result of Derivation A by an independent route.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A dielectric material of relative permittivity **ε_r = 4.5** is placed in a uniform electric field of magnitude **E = 2 × 10⁵ V/m**. Find:
(i) The electric susceptibility χ_e.
(ii) The polarization P.
(iii) The displacement field D.

**Given:**
- ε_r = 4.5
- E = 2 × 10⁵ V/m
- ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m

**Solution:**

(i) From ε_r = 1 + χ_e:
 $$\chi_e = \varepsilon_r - 1 = 4.5 - 1 = 3.5$$

(ii) From P = ε₀ (ε_r − 1) E:
 $$P = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times (4.5 - 1) \times (2 \times 10^{5})$$

Calculate step by step:
 $$P = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 3.5 \times (2 \times 10^{5})$$

 $$P = (8.854 \times 3.5) \times 10^{-12} \times (2 \times 10^{5})$$

 $$P = 30.989 \times (2 \times 10^{-7})$$

 $$P = 61.978 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C/m}^2$$

 $$\boxed{P \approx 6.20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C/m}^2}$$

(iii) From D = ε₀ ε_r E:
 $$D = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 4.5 \times (2 \times 10^{5})$$

 $$D = (8.854 \times 4.5) \times 10^{-12} \times (2 \times 10^{5})$$

 $$D = 39.843 \times 2 \times 10^{-7}$$

 $$D = 79.686 \times 10^{-7}$$

 $$\boxed{D \approx 7.97 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C/m}^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification (Cross-Check):** D should equal ε₀ E + P. 
> ε₀ E = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² × 2 × 10⁵ = 17.708 × 10⁻⁷ = 1.77 × 10⁻⁶ C/m² 
> ε₀ E + P = 1.77 × 10⁻⁶ + 6.20 × 10⁻⁶ = 7.97 × 10⁻⁶ C/m² ✓ 

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The Three Field Vectors

| Aspect | **E** (Electric Field) | **D** (Displacement) | **P** (Polarization) |
|--------|------------------------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Origin | Free + bound charges | Free charges only | Bound charges only |
| Exists outside dielectric? | Yes | Yes (continuous at boundary) | No (zero in vacuum) |
| Exists inside dielectric? | Yes (reduced by polarization) | Yes | Yes |
| Units | V/m | C/m² | C/m² |
| Vacuum limit | $\mathbf{E}_0$ | $\varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E}_0$ | 0 |
| Reduced inside dielectric? | Yes (factor of ε_r) | No (same as outside) | N/A |
| Boundary condition | Tangential component continuous | Normal component has jump from σ_free | Normal component has jump from σ_bound |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Microscopic Dipoles to Macroscopic ε_r

The following block-level diagram maps the logical flow by which microscopic dipole alignment produces the macroscopic dielectric constant.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["External E Field Applied"] --> B["Bound Charges Experience Force"]
    B --> C["Microscopic Dipoles Form p per molecule"]
    C --> D["Number Density N of Dipoles per m3"]
    D --> E["Polarization Vector P equals N times p"]
    A --> F["Constitutive Relation P equals chi_e times epsilon_0 times E"]
    F --> G["Define D as epsilon_0 E plus P"]
    G --> H["D equals epsilon_0 epsilon_r E"]
    H --> I["Bridge Equation epsilon_r equals 1 plus chi_e"]
    I --> J["Final Result P equals epsilon_0 times epsilon_r minus 1 times E"]
    E --> J
```

### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — Vacuum vs. Dielectric

This diagram illustrates the parallel-plate capacitor scenario and the difference between vacuum and dielectric cases, which is the most-tested configuration in KTU board exams.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VACUUM_CASE["VACUUM CASE"]
        V1["Plate Charge sigma_0"] --> V2["Field E_0 equals sigma_0 over epsilon_0"]
        V2 --> V3["Capacitance C_0 equals epsilon_0 A over d"]
    end
    subgraph DIELECTRIC_CASE["DIELECTRIC CASE with same V"]
        D1["Plate Charge sigma_f equals epsilon_r sigma_0"] --> D2["Bound Charge sigma_b equals P"]
        D2 --> D3["Net Field inside E equals sigma_f minus P over epsilon_0"]
        D3 --> D4["P equals epsilon_0 times epsilon_r minus 1 times E"]
        D4 --> D5["Capacitance C equals epsilon_r C_0"]
    end
    VACUUM_CASE --> DIELECTRIC_CASE
```

### 4.3 The Three-Vector Coupling Schematic

This block diagram shows the three-way coupling among **E**, **D**, and **P** that underpins the entire relation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    E_in["Electric Field E inside dielectric"] --> D_def["D equals epsilon_0 E plus P"]
    P_in["Polarization P from dipoles"] --> D_def
    D_def --> D_out["D equals epsilon_0 epsilon_r E"]
    E_in --> P_const["P equals chi_e epsilon_0 E"]
    P_const --> P_in
    D_out --> bridge["epsilon_r equals 1 plus chi_e"]
    P_const --> bridge
    bridge --> final["P equals epsilon_0 times epsilon_r minus 1 times E"]
```

### 4.4 Physical Setup Schematic — Polarization Inside a Dielectric Slab

The following is a Mermaid representation of the parallel-plate capacitor with a dielectric inserted, showing the bound surface charges that arise from polarization.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PLATE_TOP["TOP PLATE positive free charge sigma_f"]
        T1["+ + + + + + + + +"]
    end
    subgraph DIELECTRIC_REGION["DIELECTRIC SLAB polarized dipoles"]
        D1a["+ - + - + - + -"]
        D1b["+ - + - + - + -"]
        D1c["+ - + - + - + -"]
    end
    subgraph BOUND_CHARGES["INDUCED BOUND SURFACE CHARGES"]
        B1["Top surface of slab negative bound charge minus P"]
        B2["Bottom surface of slab positive bound charge plus P"]
    end
    subgraph PLATE_BOTTOM["BOTTOM PLATE negative free charge minus sigma_f"]
        B3["- - - - - - - - -"]
    end
    PLATE_TOP --> DIELECTRIC_REGION
    DIELECTRIC_REGION --> PLATE_BOTTOM
    BOUND_CHARGES --> DIELECTRIC_REGION
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for the Diagrams:** The dipoles in the dielectric align such that their negative ends face the positive plate, creating a layer of **negative bound charge** on the top surface of the slab and **positive bound charge** on the bottom surface. This bound charge partially cancels the free charge on the plates, reducing the net field inside the dielectric — this is the microscopic origin of ε_r.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]
**Define polarization and relative permittivity. Establish the relation between them.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition of Polarization (1 Mark):** 
Polarization **P** in a dielectric is defined as the electric dipole moment per unit volume of the material. It is a vector quantity given by **P** = N**p**, where N is the number of dipoles per unit volume and **p** is the average dipole moment. Its SI unit is C/m².

**Definition of Relative Permittivity (1 Mark):** 
Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) ε_r of a material is the ratio of the permittivity of the medium ε to the permittivity of free space ε₀. It is dimensionless and represents the factor by which the capacitance of a vacuum capacitor increases when the dielectric is inserted.

**Relation (1 Mark):**
Starting from **D** = ε₀**E** + **P** and substituting **P** = χ_e ε₀**E**, we get **D** = ε₀(1 + χ_e)**E** = ε₀ε_r**E**. Therefore ε_r = 1 + χ_e. Equivalently:
 $$P = \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1) E$$

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Exam]
**What is electric susceptibility? How is it related to the dielectric constant?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition of Susceptibility (1 Mark):** 
Electric susceptibility χ_e is a dimensionless constant that measures the degree of polarization of a dielectric material in response to an applied electric field. It is defined by the linear constitutive relation **P** = χ_e ε₀**E**.

**Physical Significance (1 Mark):** 
A material with χ_e = 0 (such as vacuum) does not polarize at all. Materials with large χ_e (such as ferroelectrics) polarize strongly and are used in high-value capacitors.

**Relation to Dielectric Constant (1 Mark):** 
Substituting the constitutive relation into the displacement definition and comparing with **D** = ε₀ε_r**E**:
 $$\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Starting from the definition of the electric displacement vector, derive the relation between polarization **P**, electric field **E**, and dielectric constant ε_r. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A parallel plate capacitor with plate area 200 cm² and plate separation 2 mm is filled with a dielectric of relative permittivity 5.2. If a potential difference of 100 V is applied across the plates, calculate (i) the capacitance, (ii) the magnitude of the electric displacement **D**, (iii) the polarization **P**, and (iv) the bound surface charge density on the dielectric. (7 Marks)

##### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** The electric displacement vector **D** is defined as:
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \mathbf{P}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** For a linear, isotropic, homogeneous dielectric, the polarization is proportional to the applied field:
 $$\mathbf{P} = \chi_e \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E}$$

where χ_e is the dimensionless electric susceptibility.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Substituting into the definition of **D**:
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \chi_e \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) \mathbf{E}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** The dielectric response equation is **D** = ε₀ε_r**E**. Equating the two expressions:
 $$\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \mathbf{E} = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) \mathbf{E}$$

Therefore:
 $$\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Since χ_e = P / (ε₀ E), the direct relation between **P** and ε_r is:
 $$\boxed{\mathbf{P} = \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1) \mathbf{E}}$$

##### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- A = 200 cm² = 200 × 10⁻⁴ m² = 2 × 10⁻² m²
- d = 2 mm = 2 × 10⁻³ m
- ε_r = 5.2
- V = 100 V
- ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m

**(i) Capacitance (2 Marks):**
 $$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d} = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 5.2 \times (2 \times 10^{-2})}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Compute step by step:
 $$C = \frac{(8.854 \times 5.2 \times 2) \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

 $$C = \frac{92.08 \times 10^{-14}}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{92.08 \times 10^{-14}}{0.002}$$

 $$C = 46.04 \times 10^{-11} = 4.604 \times 10^{-10} \text{ F}$$

 $$\boxed{C \approx 460.4 \text{ pF}}$$

**(ii) Electric Displacement D (2 Marks):**
The electric field in the dielectric is:
 $$E = \frac{V}{d} = \frac{100}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 5 \times 10^{4} \text{ V/m}$$

Then:
 $$D = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 5.2 \times (5 \times 10^{4})$$

 $$D = (8.854 \times 5.2 \times 5) \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{4} = 230.20 \times 10^{-8}$$

 $$\boxed{D \approx 2.302 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C/m}^2}$$

**(iii) Polarization P (2 Marks):**
 $$P = \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1) E = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times (5.2 - 1) \times (5 \times 10^{4})$$

 $$P = (8.854 \times 4.2 \times 5) \times 10^{-8} = 185.93 \times 10^{-8}$$

 $$\boxed{P \approx 1.859 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C/m}^2}$$

**(iv) Bound Surface Charge Density (1 Mark):**
 $$\sigma_b = P = 1.859 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C/m}^2$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Part b-i):** A common mistake is to forget to convert the area from **cm² to m²**. Many students write A = 200 directly in the formula and get an answer 10⁴ times too large. The examiners **will** deduct 1 full mark for unit-conversion errors. Always write A = 2 × 10⁻² m² explicitly.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Internal Choice Alternative]

**(a)** Define the dielectric constant. Derive the relation between dielectric constant and electric susceptibility. Show that the polarization is given by **P** = ε₀(ε_r − 1)**E**. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A dielectric material has ε_r = 6.8. When placed in a uniform electric field of 5 × 10⁵ V/m, calculate (i) the polarization vector **P**, (ii) the electric displacement **D**, and (iii) the energy density stored in the dielectric. (7 Marks)

##### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Dielectric Constant (2 Marks):**
The dielectric constant (relative permittivity) ε_r of a medium is defined as the ratio of the permittivity of the medium ε to the permittivity of free space ε₀:
 $$\varepsilon_r = \frac{\varepsilon}{\varepsilon_0}$$

It is a dimensionless number. For vacuum, ε_r = 1. For any other material, ε_r > 1. It is the factor by which the capacitance of a parallel plate capacitor increases when the gap is filled with the dielectric (with the same geometry and applied voltage).

**Derivation of the Relation (5 Marks):**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** For a linear dielectric, the polarization is proportional to the applied field. The proportionality constant is the electric susceptibility χ_e:
 $$\mathbf{P} = \chi_e \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** The displacement field is defined as the sum of vacuum and polarization responses:
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \mathbf{P}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Substituting the expression for **P**:
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} + \chi_e \varepsilon_0 \mathbf{E} = \varepsilon_0 (1 + \chi_e) \mathbf{E}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** By the dielectric response equation:
 $$\mathbf{D} = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \mathbf{E}$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Comparing both expressions for **D**, and canceling ε₀**E** (non-zero inside the material):
 $$\varepsilon_r = 1 + \chi_e$$

Rearranging for **P**:
 $$\mathbf{P} = \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1) \mathbf{E}$$

##### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- ε_r = 6.8
- E = 5 × 10⁵ V/m
- ε₀ = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m

**(i) Polarization P (2 Marks):**
 $$P = \varepsilon_0 (\varepsilon_r - 1) E = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times (6.8 - 1) \times (5 \times 10^{5})$$

 $$P = (8.854 \times 5.8 \times 5) \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{5} = 256.77 \times 10^{-7}$$

 $$\boxed{P \approx 2.568 \times 10^{-5} \text{ C/m}^2}$$

**(ii) Electric Displacement D (2 Marks):**
 $$D = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E = (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 6.8 \times (5 \times 10^{5})$$

 $$D = (8.854 \times 6.8 \times 5) \times 10^{-7} = 301.04 \times 10^{-7}$$

 $$\boxed{D \approx 3.010 \times 10^{-5} \text{ C/m}^2}$$

**(iii) Energy Density (3 Marks):**
The energy density stored in an electric field inside a dielectric is:
 $$u = \frac{1}{2} \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r E^2$$

Substituting values:
 $$u = \frac{1}{2} \times (8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \times 6.8 \times (5 \times 10^{5})^2$$

 $$u = \frac{1}{2} \times 8.854 \times 6.8 \times 25 \times 10^{-12} \times 10^{10}$$

 $$u = \frac{1}{2} \times 8.854 \times 6.8 \times 25 \times 10^{-2}$$

 $$u = \frac{1}{2} \times 1505.18 \times 10^{-2} = 7.526 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^{-2}$$

Wait — recompute carefully. (5 × 10⁵)² = 25 × 10¹⁰. So ε₀ E² = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² × 25 × 10¹⁰ = 221.35 × 10⁻² = 2.2135.

Then:
 $$u = \frac{1}{2} \times 6.8 \times 2.2135 = 3.4 \times 2.2135$$

 $$\boxed{u \approx 7.526 \text{ J/m}^3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert (Part b-iii):** Many students write u = (1/2) ε₀ E² and **forget the ε_r factor**. The energy density in a dielectric is enhanced by ε_r, not reduced. Losing this factor is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction. Also, students often confuse the energy density formula with that for an inductor (½ B²/μ₀) — remember the dielectric formula has ε_r in the numerator.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision — Must Memorize:**

- **Definition anchor:** Polarization **P** is dipole moment per unit volume; SI unit is C/m².
- **Susceptibility formula:** χ_e = ε_r − 1 (this single equation unlocks ~40% of the marks in this module).
- **Core bridge equation:** 
 $$\varepsilon_r = 1 + \frac{P}{\varepsilon_0 E}$$
- **Dielectric response:** **D** = ε₀ ε_r **E** (this is what examiners quote in the question paper; know it cold).
- **Displacement definition:** **D** = ε₀**E** + **P** (the **+ P** is *additive*, not subtractive — a frequent error).
- **Energy density:** u = (1/2) ε₀ ε_r E² (note the presence of ε_r — the dielectric *enhances* energy storage).
- **Three field vectors — never confuse:** **E** is reduced inside the dielectric, **D** is unchanged (continuous normal component), **P** exists only inside the dielectric.
- **Capacitance scaling:** C = ε_r C₀ (filling the gap multiplies capacitance by ε_r, *not* by ε₀).
- **Bound charge identity:** σ_bound = P (the surface density of bound charge equals the magnitude of polarization).
- **Boundary condition for D:** The normal component of **D** jumps by σ_free; the normal component of **P** jumps by −σ_bound.
- **Vacuum limit check:** If ε_r = 1, then P = 0, D = ε₀ E, χ_e = 0. Always verify your final answer by setting ε_r = 1.
- **Numerical trap:** Plate area must be in m², separation in m, E in V/m — unit conversion is worth 1 mark per KTU question.
- **The relation ε_r = 1 + χ_e is the central result of this entire topic.** Every other formula in Module 3 follows from it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Polarization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Types of Polarization ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Dielectric Polarization? **Dielectric Polarization** is the phenomenon in which the centres of positive and negative charges of an atom, molecule, or dielectric material are displaced in opposite directions under the influence of an external electric field, producing induced dipoles. The vector sum of all such dipole moments per unit volume is defined as the **polarization vector** $\vec{P}$. Mathematically, the total polarization is the resultant of all microscopic polarization mechanisms that exist in a dielectric material: $$ \vec{P} = \vec{P_e} + \vec{P_i} + \vec{P_o} + \vec{P_s} $$ where the subscripts $e$, $i$, $o$, and $s$ correspond to **electronic, ionic, orientational, and space-charge** polarizations respectively. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Under the action of a static or alternating electric field, four fundamental microscopic mechanisms contribute to the total dielectric response. Each mechanism is dominant over a specific frequency range, which is a key concept for KTU examination problems. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition) Imagine a **sponge made of soft rubber** sitting in still water. If you gently tilt the water surface, the sponge will absorb some water (orientational/electronic response). If you push the sponge from one side, water inside shifts, creating tiny current-like flows (ionic response). If you suddenly drop a stone nearby, ripples (waves) of different sizes travel through the sponge at different speeds — some ripples (high frequency) only vibrate the surface atoms, while slow ripples (low frequency) can push the entire sponge structure. Each **type of polarization** behaves like a different "response mode" of a dielectric to the applied electric field: | Mechanism | Analogy | | :--- | :--- | | Electronic | Stretching the electron cloud of an atom (like pulling a balloon out of shape) | | Ionic | Displacing +ve and -ve ions in a crystal lattice (like a spring-mass system) | | Orientational | Rotating permanent dipoles like compass needles aligning with a magnetic field | | Space Charge | Pile-up of charges at interfaces/grain boundaries (like traffic jam at a toll booth) | > [!NOTE] > **Fundamental Constants Used:** > - $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\, \text{F/m}$ (permittivity of free space) > - $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\, \text{J/K}$ (Boltzmann constant) > - $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\, \text{C}$ (elementary charge) ### 1.3 Why is Polarization Important in Electrical Engineering? Polarization determines the **dielectric constant (relative permittivity, $\epsilon_r$)** of a material, which directly controls: - The **capacitance** of capacitors: $C = \epsilon_r \epsilon_0 \dfrac{A}{d}$ - The **speed of signal propagation** in PCBs and transmission lines - The **loss tangent** and quality of high-frequency insulators - The **energy density** of electric fields in transformer oils and insulators ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four Types of Polarization — Operational Logic When an external electric field $\vec{E}$ is applied, four distinct microscopic mechanisms may be activated depending on the structure of the dielectric and the **frequency** of the applied field. #### 2.1.1 Electronic (Atomic) Polarization ($P_e$) **Mechanism:** The electron cloud surrounding a positively charged nucleus gets **displaced** relative to the nucleus, creating an induced dipole moment. - **Why it occurs:** The electron cloud is loosely bound; the field exerts opposite forces on electrons ($-e$) and the nucleus ($+e$). - **How it scales:** Induced dipole moment $\vec{p}_e = \alpha_e \vec{E}_l$, where $\alpha_e$ is the **electronic polarizability**. - **Characteristic time:** $\sim 10^{-15}$ s — present at **all frequencies up to ultraviolet** (optical range). $$ \alpha_e = 4\pi \epsilon_0 R^3 $$ where $R$ is the effective radius of the atom/ion. #### 2.1.2 Ionic Polarization ($P_i$) **Mechanism:** In a molecule like NaCl, the positive Na$^+$ and negative Cl$^-$ ions are displaced from their equilibrium positions in **opposite directions**, producing a net dipole. - **Why it occurs:** Cations and anions experience equal and opposite Coulomb forces. - **How it scales:** Induced dipole moment $\vec{p}_i = \alpha_i \vec{E}_l$, where $\alpha_i = \dfrac{q^2}{K}$ with $K$ the **spring/restoring force constant** of the ionic bond. - **Characteristic time:** $\sim 10^{-12}$ to $10^{-13}$ s — vanishes above **infrared frequencies** because ions are too heavy to follow the field. $$ \alpha_i = \frac{q^2}{K} $$ #### 2.1.3 Orientational (Dipolar) Polarization ($P_o$) **Mechanism:** Materials like H$_2$O, HCl possess **permanent dipoles** ($\mu$) that are randomly oriented by thermal agitation. The applied field tries to align them along $\vec{E}$. - **Why it occurs:** Thermal agitation ($k_B T$) randomizes dipole orientations; the field provides the ordering torque. - **How it scales:** Average dipole moment $\langle p_o \rangle = \alpha_o E_l$, governed by the **Langevin–Debye equation**. $$ \alpha_o = \frac{\mu^2}{3 k_B T} $$ - **Characteristic time:** $\sim 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-10}$ s — dominant in **radio and audio frequency** ranges. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Temperature Dependence:** Unlike $\alpha_e$ and $\alpha_i$, orientational polarizability **decreases with increasing temperature** because thermal agitation disrupts dipole alignment. This is a frequently tested KTU concept. #### 2.1.4 Space-Charge (Interfacial) Polarization ($P_s$) **Mechanism:** In **heterogeneous, impure, or polycrystalline** dielectrics, free charge carriers (ions/electrons) travel some distance under the field but get **trapped at interfaces, grain boundaries, or electrode surfaces**, producing macroscopic dipoles. - **Why it occurs:** Mismatch in conductivity between regions causes charge accumulation. - **How it scales:** Dominant at **very low frequencies** ($< 10^3$ Hz); vanishes at high frequencies because carriers cannot drift far enough in a short half-cycle. > [!NOTE] > **Key Engineering Insight:** Space-charge polarization is responsible for the **high effective $\epsilon_r$** of ceramics, biological tissues, and composite insulators at power-line frequencies. ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Applicable Range | | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Electronic polarizability | $\alpha_e = 4\pi \epsilon_0 R^3$ | F·m² | All dielectrics (UV range) | | 2 | Ionic polarizability | $\alpha_i = \dfrac{q^2}{K}$ | F·m² | Ionic crystals (IR range) | | 3 | Orientational polarizability | $\alpha_o = \dfrac{\mu^2}{3 k_B T}$ | F·m² | Polar molecules (RF range) | | 4 | Total polarizability | $\alpha = \alpha_e + \alpha_i + \alpha_o + \alpha_s$ | F·m² | — | | 5 | Local field (Lorentz) | $E_l = E + \dfrac{P}{3\epsilon_0}$ | V/m | Spherical cavity (cubic symmetry) | | 6 | Clausius–Mossotti relation | $\dfrac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2} = \dfrac{N \alpha_e}{3 \epsilon_0}$ | dimensionless | Non-polar dielectrics | | 7 | Debye equation | $\dfrac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2} = \dfrac{N}{3 \epsilon_0}\left(\alpha_e + \alpha_i + \dfrac{\mu^2}{3 k_B T}\right)$ | dimensionless | Polar dielectrics | | 8 | Polarization vector | $\vec{P} = N \alpha \vec{E}_l = \epsilon_0 (\epsilon_r - 1) \vec{E}$ | C/m² | Linear dielectrics | | 9 | Frequency-dependent $\epsilon_r$ | $\epsilon_r(\omega) = \epsilon_\infty + \dfrac{\epsilon_s - \epsilon_\infty}{1 + i\omega \tau}$ | dimensionless | Debye relaxation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Pitfall:** In the Debye equation, the orientational term $\dfrac{\mu^2}{3 k_B T}$ has its **3 in the numerator** (not denominator), and $3 k_B T$ must be written in **Joules**. Examiners frequently deduct marks for writing $3k_BT$ in eV without conversion. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Capacitor design:** High-$\epsilon_r$ ceramics (e.g., BaTiO$_3$) exploit electronic + ionic + domain polarization to achieve $\epsilon_r > 10^4$. - **Transformer oils:** Non-polar liquids — only electronic and ionic polarizations exist, giving stable $\epsilon_r \approx 2.2$ across frequencies. - **Biological tissue modeling:** Space-charge polarization dominates at MHz–GHz (medical imaging, hyperthermia). - **Microwave substrates:** Low-loss materials must minimize orientational polarization to reduce dielectric heating. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Electronic Polarizability ($\alpha_e$) for a Hydrogen-like Atom **Setup:** Consider a single atom of radius $R$ with a nucleus of charge $+Ze$ at the centre and a uniformly distributed electron cloud of total charge $-Ze$. When an external field $\vec{E}$ is applied, the nucleus is displaced by a small distance $x$ relative to the electron cloud. **Step 1 — Equate the restoring force to the applied Coulomb force.** The electron cloud exerts a Hooke's-law-like restoring force on the nucleus: $$ F_{\text{restoring}} = K x = \frac{Ze}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 R^3} \cdot x $$ Here, $K$ is determined by the uniform charge-sphere model. The potential energy of a charge inside a uniform sphere of radius $R$ is $U(r) = \dfrac{Ze^2}{8 \pi \epsilon_0 R^3}\left(3R^2 - r^2\right)$, and its derivative gives the restoring force constant $K = \dfrac{Ze^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 R^3}$. **Step 2 — Set up the equilibrium equation.** At equilibrium, the external force on the nucleus equals the restoring force: $$ Ze E = K x $$ **Step 3 — Solve for the displacement $x$.** $$ x = \frac{Ze E}{K} = \frac{Ze E \cdot 4 \pi \epsilon_0 R^3}{Z e^2} = \frac{4 \pi \epsilon_0 R^3 E}{e} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the induced dipole moment.** The induced dipole moment is the product of charge magnitude and displacement: $$ p_e = (Ze) \cdot x = (Ze) \cdot \frac{4 \pi \epsilon_0 R^3 E}{e} = 4 \pi \epsilon_0 R^3 E $$ **Step 5 — Identify the polarizability.** Since $p_e = \alpha_e E$, we have: $$ \boxed{\alpha_e = 4 \pi \epsilon_0 R^3} $$ > [!NOTE] > **For hydrogen (Z=1):** $\alpha_e = 4\pi\epsilon_0 a_0^3 \approx 0.667 \times 10^{-30}$ F·m², where $a_0$ is the Bohr radius. This value is often used as a benchmark in KTU numericals. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Ionic Polarizability ($\alpha_i$) for a Diatomic Ionic Crystal **Setup:** Consider a NaCl-type crystal. The Na$^+$ and Cl$^-$ ions are separated by an equilibrium distance $r_0$. An applied field $\vec{E}$ displaces them by $\Delta r = x_1 + x_2$ from their original positions. **Step 1 — Express the restoring force constant $K$.** If the short-range repulsive potential between ions varies as $U_r(r) = \dfrac{B}{r^n}$, the net equilibrium inter-ionic spacing is determined by the balance: $$ K = \frac{(n-1) \, e^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 r_0^3} $$ For NaCl, $n \approx 9.4$ giving $K \approx 14.3$ N/m. **Step 2 — Apply equilibrium for each ion.** For the positive ion (mass $m_1$), the field pushes it in the direction of $\vec{E}$: $$ m_1 \ddot{x}_1 = eE - K(x_1 + x_2) $$ For the negative ion, the field pushes it in the opposite direction: $$ m_2 \ddot{x}_2 = eE - K(x_1 + x_2) $$ **Step 3 — Add the two equations to obtain the relative displacement.** For a static field ($\ddot{x}_1 = \ddot{x}_2 = 0$): $$ 2K(x_1 + x_2) = 2eE \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_1 + x_2 = \frac{eE}{K} $$ **Step 4 — Calculate the induced dipole moment per ion pair.** $$ p_i = e \cdot (x_1 + x_2) = \frac{e^2 E}{K} $$ **Step 5 — Identify the ionic polarizability.** $$ \boxed{\alpha_i = \frac{e^2}{K}} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Orientational Polarizability ($\alpha_o$) — Langevin–Debye Theory **Setup:** A dielectric contains $N$ molecules per unit volume, each having a permanent dipole moment $\mu$, randomly oriented due to thermal agitation. The applied field $\vec{E}$ tends to align them against the randomizing thermal energy $k_B T$. **Step 1 — Express the potential energy of a dipole in the field.** $$ U(\theta) = -\vec{\mu} \cdot \vec{E} = -\mu E \cos\theta $$ where $\theta$ is the angle between $\vec{\mu}$ and $\vec{E}$. **Step 2 — Apply Boltzmann statistics.** The probability of finding a dipole at angle $\theta$ is proportional to $e^{-U/k_B T} = e^{\mu E \cos\theta / k_B T}$. Define $a = \dfrac{\mu E}{k_B T}$. **Step 3 — Compute the average dipole moment component along $\vec{E}$.** $$ \langle \mu \cos\theta \rangle = \frac{\int_0^{\pi} \mu \cos\theta \, e^{a\cos\theta} \sin\theta \, d\theta}{\int_0^{\pi} e^{a\cos\theta} \sin\theta \, d\theta} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals (substitution $u = \cos\theta$).** The numerator becomes: $$ \int_{-1}^{1} \mu u \, e^{a u} \, du = \frac{\mu}{a}(e^a - e^{-a}) \cdot \text{(integration factor)} = \frac{\mu}{a} \left[ e^a + e^{-a} - \frac{e^a - e^{-a}}{a} \right] $$ Wait — performing the integration more cleanly: Let $I = \int_{-1}^{1} e^{au} du = \dfrac{e^a - e^{-a}}{a}$, and $J = \int_{-1}^{1} u e^{au} du = \dfrac{e^a + e^{-a}}{a} - \dfrac{e^a - e^{-a}}{a^2}$. **Step 5 — Form the ratio.** $$ \langle \mu \cos\theta \rangle = \frac{J}{I} = \frac{(e^a + e^{-a})/a - (e^a - e^{-a})/a^2}{(e^a - e^{-a})/a} $$ Rearranging: $$ \langle \mu \cos\theta \rangle = \left[ \frac{e^a + e^{-a}}{e^a - e^{-a}} \right] - \frac{1}{a} = \coth(a) - \frac{1}{a} = L(a) $$ This is the **Langevin function** $L(a)$. **Step 6 — Apply the high-temperature approximation ($a \ll 1$).** For typical fields $E \sim 10^6$ V/m and $T \sim 300$ K, $a \sim 10^{-3} \ll 1$. Using the Taylor expansion $\coth(a) \approx \dfrac{1}{a} + \dfrac{a}{3} - \dfrac{a^3}{45} + \cdots$: $$ L(a) \approx \frac{a}{3} = \frac{\mu E}{3 k_B T} $$ **Step 7 — Identify the average dipole moment and polarizability.** $$ \langle p_o \rangle = \mu L(a) \approx \frac{\mu^2 E}{3 k_B T} $$ Comparing with $p_o = \alpha_o E$: $$ \boxed{\alpha_o = \frac{\mu^2}{3 k_B T}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "3" appears in the denominator:** The factor of 3 emerges from the 3D angular integration over the unit sphere ($4\pi$ steradians), which yields an average value of $\langle \cos^2\theta \rangle = 1/3$ for an isotropic distribution. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4: The Clausius–Mossotti Relation **Step 1 — Define polarization and the local (Lorentz) field.** Total polarization: $$ \vec{P} = N \alpha \vec{E}_l $$ Local Lorentz field inside a spherical cavity of the dielectric: $$ \vec{E}_l = \vec{E} + \frac{\vec{P}}{3 \epsilon_0} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $\vec{E}_l$ into the polarization equation.** $$ \vec{P} = N \alpha \left( \vec{E} + \frac{\vec{P}}{3 \epsilon_0} \right) $$ **Step 3 — Replace $\vec{P}$ using $\vec{P} = \epsilon_0(\epsilon_r - 1)\vec{E}$.** $$ \epsilon_0 (\epsilon_r - 1) \vec{E} = N \alpha \vec{E} + \frac{N \alpha}{3 \epsilon_0} \cdot \epsilon_0 (\epsilon_r - 1) \vec{E} $$ Cancel $\vec{E}$ on both sides: $$ \epsilon_0 (\epsilon_r - 1) = N \alpha + \frac{N \alpha (\epsilon_r - 1)}{3} $$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate $\epsilon_r$.** $$ \epsilon_0 (\epsilon_r - 1) - \frac{N \alpha (\epsilon_r - 1)}{3} = N \alpha $$ $$ (\epsilon_r - 1) \left( \epsilon_0 - \frac{N \alpha}{3} \right) = N \alpha $$ $$ \epsilon_r - 1 = \frac{N \alpha}{\epsilon_0 - \dfrac{N \alpha}{3}} $$ **Step 5 — Divide numerator and denominator by $\epsilon_0$.** $$ \epsilon_r - 1 = \frac{\dfrac{N \alpha}{\epsilon_0}}{1 - \dfrac{N \alpha}{3 \epsilon_0}} $$ **Step 6 — Divide top and bottom by $1 - \dfrac{N \alpha}{3 \epsilon_0}$ and add 1 to both sides.** This manipulation yields the standard form. First, write $\epsilon_r = 1 + \dfrac{N \alpha}{\epsilon_0 - N\alpha/3}$. Now compute $\epsilon_r + 2$: $$ \epsilon_r + 2 = 3 + \frac{N \alpha}{\epsilon_0 - N \alpha/3} = \frac{3 \epsilon_0 - N \alpha + N \alpha}{\epsilon_0 - N \alpha/3} = \frac{3 \epsilon_0}{\epsilon_0 - N \alpha/3} $$ **Step 7 — Form the ratio $\dfrac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2}$.** $$ \frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2} = \frac{\dfrac{N \alpha}{\epsilon_0 - N \alpha/3}}{\dfrac{3 \epsilon_0}{\epsilon_0 - N \alpha/3}} = \frac{N \alpha}{3 \epsilon_0} $$ $$ \boxed{\frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2} = \frac{N \alpha}{3 \epsilon_0}} $$ This is the celebrated **Clausius–Mossotti relation** connecting microscopic polarizability to the macroscopic dielectric constant. --- ### 3.5 The Debye Equation (for Polar Dielectrics) For polar molecules, the total polarizability includes the orientational term: $$ \alpha = \alpha_e + \alpha_i + \alpha_o = \alpha_e + \alpha_i + \frac{\mu^2}{3 k_B T} $$ Substituting into Clausius–Mossotti: $$ \boxed{\frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2} = \frac{N}{3 \epsilon_0}\left( \alpha_e + \alpha_i + \frac{\mu^2}{3 k_B T} \right)} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **Debye equation** and is the most-asked formula in KTU Module 3 derivations. Students are expected to derive it from the Langevin + Clausius–Mossotti results. --- ### 3.6 Frequency-Dependent Behaviour of $\epsilon_r$ As frequency increases, slower polarization mechanisms fail to "follow" the alternating field. The dielectric constant is given by the **Debye relaxation equation**: $$ \epsilon_r(\omega) = \epsilon_\infty + \frac{\epsilon_s - \epsilon_\infty}{1 + i \omega \tau} $$ | Frequency Range | Active Polarization | Engineering Example | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | DC / Power ($< 10^3$ Hz) | All four types | Transformer oil characterization | | Audio / RF ($10^3$–$10^9$ Hz) | Electronic, Ionic, Orientational | Capacitor dielectrics | | Microwave / IR ($10^9$–$10^{12}$ Hz) | Electronic, Ionic | Radar substrates | | Optical / UV ($> 10^{12}$ Hz) | Electronic only | Optical fibres, lenses | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Polarization Mechanisms Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Applied Electric Field E] --> B[Dielectric Material] B --> C[Electronic Polarization] B --> D[Ionic Polarization] B --> E[Orientational Polarization] B --> F[Space-Charge Polarization] C --> C1[Electron cloud displaces
from nucleus] D --> D1[Positive and negative ions
shift relative to each other] E --> E1[Permanent dipoles
align with E] F --> F1[Free charges accumulate
at interfaces] C1 --> G[Total Polarization P] D1 --> G E1 --> G F1 --> G G --> H[Macroscopic Dielectric
Constant epsilon_r] H --> I[Engineering Applications:
Capacitors, Insulators,
Transmission Lines] ``` ### 4.2 Frequency-Response Architecture of Polarization Mechanisms ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LF[Low Frequency DC to kHz] P1[Space-Charge] P2[Orientational] end subgraph MF[Medium Frequency kHz to MHz] P3[Ionic Polarization] end subgraph HF[High Frequency GHz to PHz] P4[Electronic Polarization] end LF --> MF MF --> HF P1 --> R1[Trapped carriers at grain boundaries] P2 --> R2[Permanent dipoles reorient] P3 --> R3[Ions vibrate in lattice] P4 --> R4[Electron cloud distorts] R1 --> OUT[Total epsilon_r omega] R2 --> OUT R3 --> OUT R4 --> OUT ``` ### 4.3 Polarizability Contribution Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is the molecule polar?} Q1 -- No --> Q2{Has ionic bonding?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q3[Orientational active] Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Ionic active] Q2 -- No --> Q5[Electronic only] Q3 --> Q6{N is dielectric
homogeneous?} Q4 --> Q7{At high frequency?} Q5 --> Q8[All frequencies
up to UV] Q6 -- No --> Q9[Space-charge active] Q6 -- Yes --> Q10[No space-charge] Q7 -- Yes --> Q11[Ionic drops out] Q7 -- No --> Q12[All active except
space-charge] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]** *Distinguish between electronic and orientational polarization. Mention the frequency range over which each is significant.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Property | Electronic Polarization | Orientational Polarization | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Origin | Displacement of electron cloud from nucleus | Rotation of permanent dipoles | | Time scale | $\sim 10^{-15}$ s | $\sim 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-10}$ s | | Frequency range | Optical to UV ($\sim 10^{14}$–$10^{16}$ Hz) | RF to microwave ($10^3$–$10^9$ Hz) | | Temperature dependence | Independent of $T$ | $\alpha_o \propto 1/T$ | | Occurs in | All dielectrics | Polar molecules only (H$_2$O, HCl, etc.) | | Polarizability | $\alpha_e = 4\pi\epsilon_0 R^3$ | $\alpha_o = \mu^2 / 3k_B T$ | **(3 marks: 1 mark for distinction, 1 mark for frequency range, 1 mark for correct formula/temperature dependence.)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]** *Define space-charge polarization. Why does it vanish at high frequencies?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1.5 marks):** Space-charge (interfacial) polarization is the accumulation of free charge carriers (ions or electrons) at the interfaces, grain boundaries, or electrode surfaces of heterogeneous or impure dielectrics under an applied electric field, producing macroscopic dipoles aligned with the field. **Reason for vanishing at high frequencies (1.5 marks):** At high frequencies, the alternating field reverses direction so rapidly that the free charge carriers do not have sufficient time within a half-cycle to drift across the material and accumulate at the interfaces. Hence the macroscopic dipoles cannot form, and the contribution of space-charge polarization to the total polarization drops to zero. --- ### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — With Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]** **(a) Derive the expression for the electronic polarizability of an atom using the classical model. Hence obtain an expression for the total polarization of a dielectric containing $N$ non-polar atoms per unit volume.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Classical model (1 mark):** Consider an atom of radius $R$ with a nucleus of charge $+Ze$ surrounded by a uniformly distributed electron cloud of total charge $-Ze$. The atomic model assumes the electron cloud behaves as a charged fluid held by a Hooke's-law restoring force. **Step 2 — Restoring force constant (1 mark):** The potential energy of the nucleus inside a uniform charge sphere is $U(r) = \dfrac{Ze^2}{8\pi\epsilon_0 R^3}\left(3R^2 - r^2\right)$. The derivative gives the restoring force: $$ F = -\frac{dU}{dr} = -\frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 R^3} \cdot r = -Kr $$ where the spring constant is: $$ K = \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 R^3} $$ **Step 3 — Equilibrium under external field (1 mark):** Applying an external field $\vec{E}$ displaces the nucleus by distance $x$ from the cloud's centre. At equilibrium: $$ Ze \cdot E = Kx = \frac{Ze^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 R^3} \cdot x $$ **Step 4 — Solving for displacement and induced dipole (2 marks):** $$ x = \frac{4\pi\epsilon_0 R^3 E}{Ze} $$ The induced dipole moment is: $$ p_e = (Ze) \cdot x = 4\pi\epsilon_0 R^3 E $$ Comparing with $p_e = \alpha_e E$: $$ \boxed{\alpha_e = 4\pi\epsilon_0 R^3} $$ **Step 5 — Total polarization (2 marks):** For $N$ non-polar atoms per unit volume, the total polarization is the vector sum of all induced dipole moments per unit volume: $$ \vec{P} = N \vec{p}_e = N \alpha_e \vec{E}_l $$ where $\vec{E}_l = \vec{E} + \dfrac{\vec{P}}{3\epsilon_0}$ is the **local Lorentz field**. Substituting: $$ \boxed{\vec{P} = N \alpha_e \left( \vec{E} + \frac{\vec{P}}{3\epsilon_0} \right)} $$ --- **(b) State and derive the Clausius–Mossotti relation. Discuss its significance.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Statement (1 mark):** The Clausius–Mossotti relation connects the macroscopic relative permittivity $\epsilon_r$ of a non-polar dielectric to the microscopic electronic polarizability $\alpha_e$ and the number density $N$: $$ \frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2} = \frac{N \alpha_e}{3 \epsilon_0} $$ **Derivation (5 marks):** Step 1 — Local Lorentz field in a dielectric with cubic symmetry: $$ E_l = E + \frac{P}{3 \epsilon_0} $$ Step 2 — Total polarization: $$ P = N \alpha_e E_l = N \alpha_e \left( E + \frac{P}{3\epsilon_0} \right) $$ Step 3 — Replace $P = \epsilon_0(\epsilon_r - 1)E$: $$ \epsilon_0(\epsilon_r - 1)E = N\alpha_e E + \frac{N\alpha_e(\epsilon_r - 1)E}{3} $$ Step 4 — Cancel $E$ and rearrange: $$ \epsilon_0(\epsilon_r - 1) - \frac{N\alpha_e(\epsilon_r - 1)}{3} = N\alpha_e $$ Step 5 — Factor and solve: $$ (\epsilon_r - 1)\left(\epsilon_0 - \frac{N\alpha_e}{3}\right) = N\alpha_e $$ $$ \epsilon_r - 1 = \frac{N\alpha_e}{\epsilon_0 - N\alpha_e/3} $$ Step 6 — Compute $\epsilon_r + 2$ by adding 3 to both sides and simplifying: $$ \epsilon_r + 2 = \frac{3\epsilon_0}{\epsilon_0 - N\alpha_e/3} $$ Step 7 — Form the ratio: $$ \boxed{\frac{\epsilon_r - 1}{\epsilon_r + 2} = \frac{N\alpha_e}{3\epsilon_0}} $$ **Significance (1 mark):** - Bridges **microscopic** polarizability $\alpha_e$ with **macroscopic** dielectric constant $\epsilon_r$. - Explains why $\epsilon_r$ depends on density (and hence temperature) of the dielectric. - Fails for polar molecules — extended by Debye to include orientational polarization. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]** **(a) With the help of Langevin's theory of paramagnetism (analogy), derive the expression for the orientational polarizability of a polar dielectric.** **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Set up the model (1 mark):** Consider a dielectric with $N$ molecules per unit volume, each possessing a permanent dipole moment $\mu$. The random thermal motion orients them randomly; an external field $\vec{E}$ tends to align them. **Step 2 — Potential energy of a dipole (1 mark):** $$ U(\theta) = -\mu E \cos\theta $$ **Step 3 — Boltzmann distribution (1 mark):** The probability that a dipole lies at angle $\theta$ to $\vec{E}$: $$ P(\theta) \, d\theta \propto e^{-U/k_B T} \sin\theta \, d\theta = e^{\mu E \cos\theta / k_B T} \sin\theta \, d\theta $$ **Step 4 — Average dipole moment component along $\vec{E}$ (2 marks):** Let $a = \dfrac{\mu E}{k_B T}$.
