- ✅ **Key relation 2:** $A_{21} = \dfrac{8\pi h\nu^3}{c^3}\,B_{21}$ (A-to-B ratio scales as $\nu^3$).
- ✅ The derivation uses **detailed balance** at thermal equilibrium + **Boltzmann distribution** + **Planck's law**.
- ✅ At high frequencies, spontaneous emission dominates → **X-ray lasers are hard to build**.
- ✅ In a **thermal (non-inverted) medium**, absorption always exceeds stimulated emission.
- ✅ For **laser action**, we need **population inversion** $N_2 > N_1$, achieved via pumping (which is absorption).
- ✅ $\alpha$ in **dB/km** $= 4.343 \times \alpha$ (linear, in m⁻¹) — vital for fiber optic loss calculations.
- ✅ The mean **lifetime** of an excited state is $\tau = 1/A_{21}$ — long-lived (metastable) states are preferred for laser gain media.
- ✅ **Remember the constant:** $8\pi h/c^3$ has units of $\text{J·s}^2/\text{m}^3$; always carry units through the algebra to verify dimensional consistency.
- ✅ KTU often asks: *"Why is stimulated emission dominant in lasers?"* — Answer: Because inversion $N_2 > N_1$ makes $B_{21} N_2 > B_{12} N_1$, and since $B_{12} = B_{21}$, the stimulated emission rate wins.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spontaneous Emission and Stimulated Emission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Spontaneous Emission and Stimulated Emission # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Fundamental Two-Level Atomic System Before we distinguish between the two emission phenomena, we must fix the **atomic energy level model**. Consider an isolated atom that has only two accessible energy levels: - A lower (ground) state with energy **E₁** - A higher (excited) state with energy **E₂** The energy difference is: $$\Delta E = E_2 - E_1 = h\nu$$ where **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** is the Planck constant and **ν** is the frequency of the photon that bridges this gap. The atom can interact with the electromagnetic field in **three** distinct ways: 1. **Absorption** (upward transition, E₁ → E₂) 2. **Spontaneous Emission** (downward transition, E₂ → E₁, unprovoked) 3. **Stimulated Emission** (downward transition, E₂ → E₁, provoked by an external photon) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of GBPHT121 explicitly requires students to differentiate spontaneous and stimulated emission, derive the relation between Einstein's A and B coefficients, and explain the necessity of population inversion for lasing action. --- ## 1.2 Formal Definitions ### 🔹 Spontaneous Emission > [!NOTE] > **Spontaneous emission** is the process by which an atom in an excited state E₂, in the absence of any external electromagnetic radiation, randomly and spontaneously decays to a lower energy state E₁, emitting a photon of energy **hν = E₂ − E₁**. The randomness is governed by quantum mechanical probability — there is a finite, characteristic **lifetime (τ)** for the excited state (typically **10⁻⁸ s to 10⁻⁹ s** for typical optical transitions). ### 🔹 Stimulated Emission > [!NOTE] > **Stimulated emission** is the process by which an incident photon of energy **hν = E₂ − E₁** interacts with an excited atom (already in state E₂) and forces it to drop to the lower state E₁, emitting a *second* photon that is **identical** to the triggering photon in frequency, phase, direction of propagation, and polarization. The emitted photon is *coherent* with the incident photon — this single fact is the cornerstone of LASER physics. --- ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy ### 🪞 Spontaneous Emission — The "Leaky Bucket" Analogy Imagine a bucket filled with water held above the ground. The water will **eventually leak out** on its own, with no external trigger, at some random moment. You cannot predict *exactly* when any individual molecule of water will drip out, but you can predict the *average rate* statistically. This is spontaneous emission — random, stochastic, and uncontrollable in timing. ### 🎯 Stimulated Emission — The "Domino Cascade" Analogy Now imagine a line of dominos standing on edge. The first domino, when nudged (the incident photon), topples and transfers its energy to the next one. Each falling domino releases energy identical in character to the first fall. The cascade is **triggered, controlled, and coherent**. This is stimulated emission — the essential mechanism of a LASER (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Spontaneous emission is the *enemy* of laser operation (it produces incoherent random light), while stimulated emission is the *hero* (it produces coherent amplified light). The art of building a laser is to *maximize* stimulated emission and *suppress* spontaneous emission — a goal achieved by **population inversion** (covered in Section 3). --- ## 1.4 Photon Properties: A Side-by-Side Comparison | Property | Spontaneous Emission | Stimulated Emission | |---|---|---| | Trigger | None (random) | Incident photon of energy hν | | Photon phase | Random, incoherent | Identical to trigger photon | | Photon direction | Random (4π steradians) | Identical to trigger photon | | Photon polarization | Random | Identical to trigger photon | | Rate | Independent of radiation field | Proportional to radiation energy density | | Output light | Incoherent (e.g., LED, bulb) | Coherent (LASER) | | Einstein coefficient | **A₂₁** | **B₂₁** | --- ## 1.5 Real-World Manifestations - **Spontaneous Emission dominates in:** incandescent bulbs, fluorescent tubes, LEDs, candle flames, the Sun's photosphere, fireflies, neon signs. - **Stimulated Emission dominates in:** all LASERs — from barcode scanners (He-Ne), to surgical scalpels (CO₂), to fiber-optic communication (semiconductor injection lasers), to inertial confinement fusion reactors (Nd-glass). > [!NOTE] > The fact that we exist in a world full of spontaneous emission (the Sun, fire, light bulbs) but had to wait until **1960 (Theodore Maiman, ruby laser)** to harness stimulated emission tells you how *delicate and counter-intuitive* the engineering challenge of building a laser truly is. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Einstein's Phenomenological Coefficients (1917) Albert Einstein, in his landmark 1917 paper "*Zur Quantentheorie der Strahlung*", introduced three coefficients to describe the rate of transitions in a two-level atomic system immersed in a radiation field of spectral energy density **ρ(ν)**: ### Coefficient A₂₁ — Spontaneous Emission Rate - Represents the **probability per unit time** that an excited atom spontaneously drops from E₂ → E₁. - The rate of spontaneous emission is: $$R_{sp} = A_{21} \, N_2$$ where **N₂** is the number density of atoms in the excited state. - **Units:** s⁻¹ - **Mean lifetime** of the excited state: $\tau = \dfrac{1}{A_{21}}$ ### Coefficient B₂₁ — Stimulated Emission Rate - Represents the probability per unit time that an incident photon triggers a downward transition (E₂ → E₁). - The rate is proportional to the radiation density: $$R_{stim} = B_{21} \, N_2 \, \rho(\nu)$$ - **Units:** m³ · J⁻¹ · s⁻² (i.e., $\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{J}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ in some conventions; dimensionless coefficient × ρ has units of energy density) ### Coefficient B₁₂ — Stimulated Absorption Rate - Represents the probability per unit time that an atom in E₁ absorbs a photon and moves to E₂. - The rate is: $$R_{abs} = B_{12} \, N_1 \, \rho(\nu)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examination Note:** Examiners *love* asking "Why is there no A coefficient for absorption?" — The answer is that absorption *must* be stimulated by an external photon; the ground state does not "spontaneously" jump upward. Only downward transitions can be either spontaneous or stimulated. --- ## 2.2 Detailed Balance Under Thermal Equilibrium Under **thermodynamic equilibrium** at absolute temperature **T**, the population of the two levels follows the Boltzmann distribution: $$\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{g_2}{g_1} \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_2 - E_1}{k_B T}\right) = \dfrac{g_2}{g_1} \exp\left(-\dfrac{h\nu}{k_B T}\right)$$ where: - **k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** (Boltzmann constant) - **g₁, g₂** are the **degeneracies** (number of quantum states at each level) The principle of **detailed balance** requires that the rate of upward transitions (absorption) equals the rate of downward transitions (spontaneous + stimulated): $$B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu) = A_{21} N_2 + B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$$ This single equation, combined with the Planck blackbody spectral density law, yields the celebrated **Einstein relations**. --- ## 2.3 Planck's Blackbody Spectral Energy Density The spectral energy density of blackbody radiation (a function of both frequency and temperature) is: $$\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\exp\left(\dfrac{h\nu}{k_B T}\right) - 1}$$ where **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** is the speed of light in vacuum. This expression contains *two* distinct physical contributions: - The **zero-point / spontaneous** part: $\dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3}$ - The **stimulated / thermal** part: $\dfrac{1}{\exp(h\nu/k_B T) - 1}$ The fact that the spontaneous part appears as an *additive* term to the stimulated part is precisely what motivated Einstein to introduce the A₂₁ coefficient — it accounts for emission that occurs *even at T = 0* (where $\rho(\nu)$ → 0 but emission still happens). --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\Delta E = h\nu$ | Bohr-Einstein frequency condition | J | | 2 | $R_{sp} = A_{21} N_2$ | Spontaneous emission rate | atoms/m³·s | | 3 | $R_{stim} = B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$ | Stimulated emission rate | atoms/m³·s | | 4 | $R_{abs} = B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu)$ | Absorption rate | atoms/m³·s | | 5 | $g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}$ | Einstein relation (cross-section symmetry) | dimensionless | | 6 | $A_{21} = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3} B_{21}$ | A-B coefficient relation | s⁻¹ | | 7 | $\dfrac{A_{21}}{B_{21}} = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3}$ | Spontaneous-to-stimulated ratio | J·s·m⁻³ | | 8 | $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{g_2}{g_1}\exp(-h\nu/k_B T)$ | Boltzmann thermal population ratio | dimensionless | | 9 | $\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1}$ | Planck spectral energy density | J·s·m⁻³ | | 10 | $\tau = 1/A_{21}$ | Mean lifetime of excited state | s | | 11 | $N_2 > N_1$ (inversion) | Population inversion condition | atom count | | 12 | $\Delta \nu \cdot \Delta t \geq \dfrac{1}{4\pi}$ | Heisenberg-type linewidth relation | dimensionless | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL:** In the formula above, the symbol "$\vert$" appearing inside any expression (e.g., $\vert \Delta E \vert$, $\vert \psi \vert$) is rendered as `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. KTU examiners may test this distinction. --- ## 2.5 The Critical Insight: Why Spontaneous Emission Dominates at Optical Frequencies The ratio of spontaneous emission to stimulated emission rates for an atom already in the excited state is: $$\dfrac{R_{sp}}{R_{stim}} = \dfrac{A_{21}}{B_{21}\rho(\nu)} = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3 \rho(\nu)}$$ Using the Wien approximation at high frequency ($\rho(\nu) \approx \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3} e^{-h\nu/k_B T}$), this ratio simplifies to: $$\dfrac{R_{sp}}{R_{stim}} \approx e^{h\nu/k_B T}$$ For visible light (ν ≈ 6 × 10¹⁴ Hz) at room temperature (T = 300 K): $$\dfrac{h\nu}{k_B T} \approx \dfrac{(6.6 \times 10^{-34})(6 \times 10^{14})}{(1.38 \times 10^{-23})(300)} \approx 96$$ Therefore $R_{sp} / R_{stim} \approx e^{96} \approx 10^{42}$ — an astronomical number! > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Implication:** This is why ordinary light sources are dominated by spontaneous emission. To make stimulated emission *win*, you must (a) achieve **population inversion** (N₂ > N₁) and (b) use an **optical cavity** to build up $\rho(\nu)$ to extremely high values. Both are essential LASER design principles. --- ## 2.6 Engineering Applications Map | Application | Dominant Mechanism | Frequency Range | Typical Material | |---|---|---|---| | LED indicators | Spontaneous | 400–700 nm (visible) | GaAs, GaN, InP | | Optical fiber comms | Stimulated | 850/1310/1550 nm | InGaAsP semiconductor | | Barcode scanner | Stimulated | 632.8 nm | He-Ne gas | | Blu-ray disc | Stimulated | 405 nm | GaN laser diode | | Eye surgery (LASIK) | Stimulated | 193 nm | ArF excimer | | Atomic clocks | Stimulated | 9.193 GHz | Cs-133 hyperfine | | CO₂ industrial cutting | Stimulated | 10.6 μm | CO₂/N₂/He gas mix | # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Einstein A-B Relation (KTU High-Priority Derivation) This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU University Exams for this module. We shall derive it in full microscopic detail. ### Step 1: Write the Detailed Balance Equation Under thermal equilibrium at temperature T, the rate of upward transitions (atoms leaving E₁ by absorption) must equal the rate of downward transitions (atoms leaving E₂ by both emission processes): $$B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu) = A_{21} N_2 + B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$$ > *Logic:* The left side counts photons absorbed per second per unit volume. The right side counts photons emitted per second per unit volume (spontaneous + stimulated). In equilibrium, photon number is conserved. ### Step 2: Rearrange to Isolate ρ(ν) Move all terms containing $\rho(\nu)$ to one side: $$B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu) - B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu) = A_{21} N_2$$ $$\rho(\nu) \left[ B_{12} N_1 - B_{21} N_2 \right] = A_{21} N_2$$ $$\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{A_{21} N_2}{B_{12} N_1 - B_{21} N_2}$$ ### Step 3: Divide Numerator and Denominator by N₁ $$\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{A_{21} \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)}{B_{12} - B_{21} \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute the Boltzmann Population Ratio From statistical mechanics, in thermal equilibrium: $$\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{g_2}{g_1} \exp\left(-\dfrac{h\nu}{k_B T}\right)$$ Substituting: $$\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{A_{21} \left(\dfrac{g_2}{g_1}\right) e^{-h\nu/k_B T}}{B_{12} - B_{21} \left(\dfrac{g_2}{g_1}\right) e^{-h\nu/k_B T}}$$ ### Step 5: Divide Numerator and Denominator by B₂₁ $$\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{A_{21}}{B_{21}}\right) \left(\dfrac{g_2}{g_1}\right) e^{-h\nu/k_B T}}{\left(\dfrac{B_{12}}{B_{21}}\right) - \left(\dfrac{g_2}{g_1}\right) e^{-h\nu/k_B T}}$$ ### Step 6: Multiply Top and Bottom by (g₁/g₂) e^{hν/k_BT} $$\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{\left(\dfrac{A_{21}}{B_{21}}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{B_{12}}{B_{21}}\right)\left(\dfrac{g_1}{g_2}\right) e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1}$$ ### Step 7: Compare with Planck's Blackbody Formula Planck's experimentally verified law for the spectral energy density of blackbody radiation is: $$\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1}$$ This has *exactly* the same mathematical form as Step 6. Therefore, by equating the two: ### Step 8: Match the Constant Terms **Constant numerator matching:** $$\dfrac{A_{21}}{B_{21}} = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3}$$ > *This is the famous **Einstein A-B relation**.* It tells us that spontaneous emission grows as the **cube of frequency** — much stronger in the UV/visible than in the radio or microwave range. **Coefficient of $e^{h\nu/k_B T}$ in denominator matching:** $$\dfrac{B_{12}}{B_{21}} \cdot \dfrac{g_1}{g_2} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}$$ > *This is the **Einstein B-B relation** including degeneracy factors.* ### Step 9: Final Form of Both Relations $$\boxed{\,A_{21} = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3} B_{21}\,} \qquad \boxed{\,g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}\,}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the Condition for Population Inversion ### Step 1: Net Rate of Stimulated Photon Production Let **P** be the net rate at which stimulated photons are produced (per unit volume) in the radiation field. Each stimulated emission event adds **2 photons** to the field (the incident + the emitted, but we count *new* photons produced), and each absorption event removes 1 photon: $$P = 2 B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu) - B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu) + A_{21} N_2$$ For a clean net gain, we require $P > 0$ for the stimulated part (ignoring spontaneous noise): $$2 B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu) > B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu)$$ ### Step 2: Apply the Einstein B-B Relation Using $g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}$: $$B_{12} = \dfrac{g_2}{g_1} B_{21}$$ Substituting: $$2 B_{21} N_2 > \dfrac{g_2}{g_1} B_{21} N_1$$ $$\boxed{\,2 N_2 > \dfrac{g_2}{g_1} N_1\,}$$ ### Step 3: Special Case — Non-Degenerate Levels (g₁ = g₂ = 1) $$\boxed{\,N_2 > \dfrac{N_1}{2}\,}$$ This is the **minimum population inversion** required for net optical amplification. For typical two-level lasers, you actually need $N_2 > N_1$ in the strictest sense, but for non-degenerate levels, the threshold is $N_2 > N_1/2$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Insight:** The condition $N_2 > N_1$ (Boltzmann-inverted) is *never* satisfied at thermal equilibrium. This is the fundamental reason a laser needs an external "pumping" mechanism (optical, electrical, or chemical) — to artificially create a non-thermal population distribution. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) > **Problem:** In a He-Ne laser, the lasing transition occurs at **λ = 632.8 nm**. The spontaneous emission coefficient is **A₂₁ = 3.2 × 10⁶ s⁻¹**. Compute: > (a) The mean lifetime of the excited state. > (b) The stimulated emission coefficient B₂₁. > (c) The ratio of spontaneous to stimulated emission rates at T = 300 K assuming $\rho(\nu) = 10^{-15}$ J·s·m⁻³. ### Solution **Part (a): Mean Lifetime** The mean lifetime of an excited state is the inverse of the spontaneous emission coefficient: $$\tau = \dfrac{1}{A_{21}} = \dfrac{1}{3.2 \times 10^6 \, \text{s}^{-1}} = 3.125 \times 10^{-7} \, \text{s}$$ $$\boxed{\tau \approx 313 \, \text{ns}}$$ **Part (b): Stimulated Emission Coefficient B₂₁** First, compute the frequency of the emitted light: $$\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8 \, \text{m/s}}{632.8 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}} = 4.74 \times 10^{14} \, \text{Hz}$$ From the Einstein A-B relation: $$B_{21} = \dfrac{A_{21} c^3}{8\pi h \nu^3}$$ Computing $\nu^3$: $$\nu^3 = (4.74 \times 10^{14})^3 = 1.065 \times 10^{44} \, \text{Hz}^3$$ Computing the numerator $A_{21} c^3$: $$A_{21} c^3 = (3.2 \times 10^6)(3 \times 10^8)^3 = (3.2 \times 10^6)(2.7 \times 10^{25}) = 8.64 \times 10^{31} \, \text{m}^3 \cdot \text{s}^{-2}$$ Computing the denominator $8\pi h \nu^3$: $$8\pi h \nu^3 = 8\pi (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(1.065 \times 10^{44}) = 8\pi (7.057 \times 10^{10}) \approx 1.773 \times 10^{12} \, \text{J·s}^2 \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$$ Therefore: $$B_{21} = \dfrac{8.64 \times 10^{31}}{1.773 \times 10^{12}} \approx 4.87 \times 10^{19} \, \text{m}^3 \cdot \text{J}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-2}$$ $$\boxed{B_{21} \approx 4.87 \times 10^{19} \, \text{m}^3 \cdot \text{J}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-2}}$$ **Part (c): Ratio of Spontaneous to Stimulated Rates** $$\dfrac{R_{sp}}{R_{stim}} = \dfrac{A_{21} N_2}{B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)} = \dfrac{A_{21}}{B_{21} \rho(\nu)} = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3 \rho(\nu)}$$ Plugging values: $$\dfrac{R_{sp}}{R_{stim}} = \dfrac{1.773 \times 10^{12}}{(10^{-15})} = 1.773 \times 10^{27}$$ > *Interpretation:* Spontaneous emission is **27 orders of magnitude** stronger than stimulated emission per atom per second. This is why we *must* achieve population inversion *and* confine the radiation in a high-Q optical resonator to build up $\rho(\nu)$ inside the cavity. The numbers motivate the engineering choices in every laser. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Connecting Concepts | Concept | Connects to | Key Equation | |---|---|---| | Spontaneous emission (A₂₁) | Planck's law | $A_{21} = (8\pi h\nu^3/c^3) B_{21}$ | | Stimulated emission (B₂₁) | Population inversion | $2 B_{21} N_2 > B_{12} N_1$ | | Boltzmann distribution | Thermal equilibrium | $N_2/N_1 = (g_2/g_1)\exp(-h\nu/k_BT)$ | | Spectral energy density | Blackbody radiation | $\rho(\nu) = 8\pi h\nu^3/[c^3(e^{h\nu/k_BT}-1)]$ | | Mean lifetime | Linewidth | $\Delta\nu = 1/(2\pi\tau)$ | | Optical cavity | Laser threshold | $\rho_{cavity} \gg \rho_{thermal}$ | | Population inversion | Pumping mechanism | $N_2 > N_1$ (non-thermal) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Three Quantum Processes — Visual Architecture The following functional block diagram illustrates the interactions of the two-level atom with the radiation field, showing all three Einstein processes and their dependencies. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ground State E1
Population N1] -->|Absorption B12 rho nu| B[Excited State E2
Population N2] B -->|Spontaneous Emission A21| C[Random Photon
Incoherent] B -->|Stimulated Emission B21 rho nu| D[Coherent Photon Pair
In Phase and Direction] E[External Radiation Field
rho nu Spectral Density] -->|Triggers Absorption| A E -->|Triggers Stimulated Emission| B F[Optical Resonator] -->|Amplifies| D F -->|Suppresses| C G[Pumping Source] -->|Creates Inversion| B style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style B fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300 style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style D fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style E fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600 style F fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099 style G fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066 ``` **Reading the diagram:** - **Blue node (A):** The ground state reservoir - **Orange node (B):** The excited state reservoir — the *target* of population inversion - **Red node (C):** The undesirable *spontaneous* photon — random, incoherent, lost to lasing - **Green node (D):** The *desired* stimulated photon — coherent, in phase, amplified - **Yellow node (E):** The external radiation field — drives both absorption and stimulated emission - **Purple node (F):** The optical resonator — engineering trick to boost stimulated over spontaneous - **Pink node (G):** The pump — provides energy to maintain inversion --- ## 4.2 Energy Level Transition Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Photon Energy h nu Approaches]) --> Decision{Atom State} Decision -->|E1 Ground| Abs[ABSORPTION
Atom absorbs photon
B12 times N1 times rho nu] Decision -->|E2 Excited| Next{Trigger Present?} Next -->|No Spontaneous| SP[SPONTANEOUS EMISSION
Random photon emitted
A21 times N2] Next -->|Yes Photon h nu| ST[STIMULATED EMISSION
Coherent clone emitted
B21 times N2 times rho nu] Abs --> Excited[Atom Now in E2] SP --> Ground[Atom Now in E1] ST --> Ground Excited --> Decision style Start fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366 style Decision fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600 style Next fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600 style Abs fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style SP fill:#ffcc99,stroke:#993300 style ST fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style Excited fill:#ffd9b3,stroke:#663300 style Ground fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 ``` --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: LASER vs LED ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph LED_System [LED Light Emitting Diode] L1[Forward Biased p n Junction] --> L2[Electron Hole Recombination] L2 --> L3[SPONTANEOUS Emission] L3 --> L4[Incoherent Light Output] end subgraph LASER_System [LASER System] M1[Optical Cavity with Mirrors] --> M2[Population Inversion Medium] M2 --> M3[STIMULATED Emission Amplified] M3 --> M4[Coherent Light Output] P[Pump Source Optical Electrical] --> M2 end style L1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style L2 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style L3 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#660000 style L4 fill:#ff9999,stroke:#660000 style M1 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style M2 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style M3 fill:#99ff99,stroke:#003300 style M4 fill:#99ff99,stroke:#003300 style P fill:#ccccff,stroke:#000099 ``` **Architectural Insight:** The LED is *passive* and *simple* — it just relies on natural spontaneous recombination. The LASER is *active* and *engineered* — it requires (a) a pump to invert the population, (b) a gain medium to support stimulated emission, and (c) an optical cavity to select and amplify one frequency and direction. --- ## 4.4 Rate Equation Balance Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[ABSORPTION RATE
R_abs = B12 N1 rho nu
Loss from E1] --> Sum[Net Photon Rate] P2[STIMULATED EMISSION RATE
R_stim = B21 N2 rho nu
Gain in E2] --> Sum P3[SPONTANEOUS EMISSION RATE
R_sp = A21 N2
Noise in E2] --> Sum Sum --> Out{Population Comparison} Out -->|N2 greater than N1| Inverted[INVERTED STATE
LASER Action Possible] Out -->|N2 less than N1| Normal[NORMAL STATE
Only Absorption Dominates] style P1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000 style P2 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600 style P3 fill:#ffffcc,stroke:#999900 style Sum fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099 style Out fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806600 style Inverted fill:#99ff99,stroke:#003300 style Normal fill:#ff9999,stroke:#660000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Distinguish between spontaneous emission and stimulated emission. Why is stimulated emission essential for laser action?** **Model Answer:** Spontaneous emission and stimulated emission are two distinct quantum processes by which an excited atom releases energy as a photon. **Spontaneous Emission:** An atom in an excited state E₂, with no external stimulus, randomly decays to a lower state E₁ after a characteristic mean lifetime τ = 1/A₂₁. The emitted photon has random phase, random direction, and random polarization. This process is governed by the Einstein coefficient **A₂₁** and its rate is $R_{sp} = A_{21} N_2$. **Stimulated Emission:** An incident photon of energy hν = E₂ − E₁ triggers an excited atom to emit a second photon. The emitted photon is *identical* to the incident one in frequency, phase, direction, and polarization. Its rate is $R_{stim} = B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$, where **ρ(ν)** is the spectral energy density of the radiation field. **Why stimulated emission is essential for lasers:** A laser (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation) requires *coherent, monochromatic, directional* light output. Spontaneous emission produces incoherent photons that go in random directions. Only stimulated emission creates the *coherent amplification cascade* — each emitted photon triggers more emissions, building up an intense, monochromatic, directional beam. The very acronym **LASER** confirms that stimulated emission is the foundational mechanism. > [!NOTE] > **Marks Distribution (3 marks):** 1 mark for correct definition of spontaneous; 1 mark for correct definition of stimulated; 1 mark for explaining the laser relevance. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023] **Q: Define the Einstein coefficients A₂₁ and B₂₁. State the relation between them and explain its physical significance.** **Model Answer:** **Einstein Coefficient A₂₁ (Spontaneous Emission Coefficient):** It is the probability per unit time that an atom in the excited state E₂ will spontaneously decay to the lower state E₁, emitting a photon. The rate of spontaneous emission is $R_{sp} = A_{21} N_2$. **Einstein Coefficient B₂₁ (Stimulated Emission Coefficient):** It is the probability per unit time that an incident photon of energy hν = E₂ − E₁ will induce a transition E₂ → E₁, producing a second coherent photon. The rate of stimulated emission is $R_{stim} = B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$. **The Relation:** $$A_{21} = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3} B_{21}$$ **Physical Significance:** 1. The spontaneous emission coefficient A₂₁ grows as the **cube of the transition frequency (ν³)**. This means spontaneous emission dominates in the optical/UV range and is negligible in the radio/microwave range. This is why microwaves (masers) are far easier to lase than visible-light lasers. 2. The ratio $A_{21}/B_{21}$ is a *fundamental constant* of the transition, depending only on ν. 3. It shows that even at zero temperature (T = 0), spontaneous emission persists — it is a *quantum vacuum* effect, not a thermal one. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **Q: (a) Derive the relation between Einstein's A and B coefficients starting from the principle of detailed balance. (7 marks)** **(b) Explain the concept of population inversion. Why is it necessary for laser action? Discuss briefly how it is achieved in a Ruby laser. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Part (a) Consider a two-level atomic system with energies E₁ (lower) and E₂ (upper), with degeneracies g₁ and g₂ respectively, in thermal equilibrium with a radiation field of spectral energy density ρ(ν). **Step 1: Write the three Einstein rate equations:** - Rate of absorption (upward, E₁ → E₂): $R_{abs} = B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu)$ - Rate of stimulated emission (downward, E₂ → E₁): $R_{stim} = B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$ - Rate of spontaneous emission (downward, E₂ → E₁): $R_{sp} = A_{21} N_2$ **Step 2: Apply detailed balance (no net change in N₂ at equilibrium):** $$B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu) = A_{21} N_2 + B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$$ **Step 3: Solve for ρ(ν):** $$\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{A_{21} N_2}{B_{12} N_1 - B_{21} N_2}$$ **Step 4: Substitute the Boltzmann population ratio:** Using $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{g_2}{g_1} e^{-h\nu/k_B T}$: $$\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{A_{21} (g_2/g_1) e^{-h\nu/k_B T}}{B_{12} - B_{21} (g_2/g_1) e^{-h\nu/k_B T}}$$ **Step 5: Divide by B₂₁ and multiply by $(g_1/g_2) e^{h\nu/k_B T}$:** $$\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{(A_{21}/B_{21})}{(B_{12}/B_{21})(g_1/g_2) e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1}$$ **Step 6: Compare with Planck's spectral energy density:** $$\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3} \cdot \dfrac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1}$$ **Step 7: Match coefficients:** By comparison, the numerator gives: $$A_{21} = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3} B_{21}$$ And the denominator coefficient of $e^{h\nu/k_B T}$ gives: $$B_{12} \cdot \dfrac{g_1}{g_2} = B_{21} \quad \Rightarrow \quad g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}$$ **Final Result:** $$\boxed{A_{21} = \dfrac{8\pi h \nu^3}{c^3} B_{21}} \quad \text{and} \quad \boxed{g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}}$$ --- #### Model Solution to Part (b) **Concept of Population Inversion:** Under normal thermal equilibrium, the population of the lower energy level always exceeds that of the upper level: $$N_1 > N_2$$ Following Boltzmann's law: $N_2/N_1 = (g_2/g_1) \exp(-h\nu/k_B T)$. In this state, absorption dominates over stimulated emission, so light passing through the medium is *attenuated* (not amplified). **Population Inversion** is a non-thermal condition in which the population of the *upper* (excited) state is *artificially* made greater than that of the *lower* (ground) state: $$N_2 > N_1$$ In this state, stimulated emission exceeds absorption, and the medium acts as an *optical amplifier*. Incident light grows in intensity as it propagates. **Why Population Inversion is Necessary for Laser Action:** A laser is fundamentally an *amplifier* of light. For net amplification (gain > loss), the rate of stimulated emission must exceed the rate of absorption: $$B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu) > B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu)$$ This requires $N_2 > (g_2/g_1) N_1$. Without inversion, the medium is a *passive absorber*, not an *active amplifier*. Hence, population inversion is a **sine qua non** of laser action. **Achievement of Population Inversion in a Ruby Laser:** The Ruby laser (Maiman, 1960) uses a synthetic ruby crystal — **Al₂O₃ doped with ~0.05% Cr³⁺ ions** — as the active medium. It is a **three-level laser** system. - **Energy Levels of Cr³⁺:** Three relevant levels — E₀ (ground), E₁ (intermediate metastable), and E₂ (pumping band). - **Pumping Mechanism:** A xenon flash lamp delivers intense, broad-spectrum light. Cr³⁺ ions absorb green and blue photons and are excited from E₀ to E₂. - **Non-radiative decay:** From E₂, the ions decay rapidly (non-radiatively, by phonon emission) to the metastable level E₁. - **Metastable state:** Level E₁ has an unusually long lifetime (~3 ms) compared to the typical 10⁻⁸ s. Atoms "pile up" in E₁. - **Achievement of inversion:** When the pump intensity is high enough, more than half the Cr³⁺ ions accumulate in E₁, so $N(E_1) > N(E_0)$, achieving inversion. - **Lasing transition:** A photon of wavelength 694.3 nm (red) triggers stimulated emission E₁ → E₀, producing the characteristic ruby laser red beam. The role of the **resonant optical cavity** (two parallel mirrors, one fully reflective, one partially transmissive) is to provide the feedback that builds up intense stimulated emission until the beam emerges as a coherent, monochromatic, collimated laser pulse. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024] **Q: (a) With neat energy level diagrams, explain the process of spontaneous emission and stimulated emission. Discuss the characteristics of the photons emitted in each case. (7 marks)** **(b) A laser transition occurs at wavelength 694.3 nm (ruby laser). If the spontaneous emission lifetime of the upper state is 3 ms, calculate the stimulated emission coefficient B₂₁ and the ratio of spontaneous to stimulated emission rates at T = 300 K, given that ρ(ν) = 5 × 10⁻¹⁵ J·s·m⁻³. (7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Part (a) **Energy Level Diagram:** Two atomic energy levels are shown: a lower level E₁ and an upper level E₂, separated by energy difference hν = E₂ − E₁. **Spontaneous Emission (Refer to Section 4 diagrams):** The atom is initially in the excited state E₂ with no external photons present. After a mean time τ = 1/A₂₁ (typically 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁹ s), it spontaneously decays to E₁ and emits a photon of energy hν = E₂ − E₁. **Characteristics of the Spontaneously Emitted Photon:** 1. **Direction:** Random — uniformly distributed over all 4π steradians. 2. **Phase:** Random — incoherent with other photons. 3. **Polarization:** Random. 4. **Frequency:** Centered at ν = (E₂ − E₁)/h, but broadened by the natural linewidth Δν = 1/(2πτ). 5. **Statistical:** Emitted at unpredictable times, governed by exponential decay law: $N_2(t) = N_2(0) e^{-A_{21} t}$. **Stimulated Emission (Refer to Section 4 diagrams):** An incident photon of energy hν = E₂ − E₁ strikes the excited atom. This "stimulus" triggers the atom to drop from E₂ to E₁, releasing a *second* photon. Critically, the second photon is *identical* to the incident one. **Characteristics of the Stimulated Photon:** 1. **Direction:** Identical to the incident photon. 2. **Phase:** Identical (in phase, coherent) with the incident photon. 3. **Polarization:** Identical. 4. **Frequency:** Identical. 5. **Result:** The incident photon is *not* absorbed; instead, *two* coherent photons emerge — net gain of one photon, enabling amplification. > **Comparison Summary Table:** | Property | Spontaneous | Stimulated | |---|---|---| | Direction | Random | Identical to incident | | Phase | Random | Coherent | | Trigger | None | Required photon | | Output | Incoherent | Coherent | | Application | LED, Sun | LASER | --- #### Model Solution to Part (b) **Given:** - Wavelength: λ = 694.3 nm = 694.3 × 10⁻⁹ m - Spontaneous emission coefficient: A₂₁ = 1/τ = 1/(3 × 10⁻³ s) = 333.33 s⁻¹ - Spectral energy density: ρ(ν) = 5 × 10⁻¹⁵ J·s·m⁻³ - Temperature: T = 300 K **Step 1: Compute the frequency ν** $$\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda} = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8}{694.3 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.32 \times 10^{14} \, \text{Hz}$$ **Step 2: Compute the stimulated emission coefficient B₂₁** From the Einstein A-B relation: $$B_{21} = \dfrac{A_{21} c^3}{8\pi h \nu^3}$$ Compute $\nu^3$: $$\nu^3 = (4.32 \times 10^{14})^3 = 8.06 \times 10^{43} \, \text{Hz}^3$$ Compute $c^3$: $$c^3 = (3 \times 10^8)^3 = 2.7 \times 10^{25} \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}^3$$ Compute $8\pi h \nu^3$: $$8\pi h \nu^3 = 8\pi (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(8.06 \times 10^{43})$$ $$= 8\pi (5.34 \times 10^{10})$$ $$= 1.342 \times 10^{12} \, \text{J·s·m}^{-3}$$ Therefore: $$B_{21} = \dfrac{(333.33)(2.7 \times 10^{25})}{1.342 \times 10^{12}}$$ $$B_{21} = \dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{27}}{1.342 \times 10^{12}} = 6.71 \times 10^{15} \, \text{m}^3 \cdot \text{J}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-2}$$ **Step 3: Compute the ratio R_sp / R_stim** $$\dfrac{R_{sp}}{R_{stim}} = \dfrac{A_{21}}{B_{21} \rho(\nu)} = \dfrac{333.33}{(6.71 \times 10^{15})(5 \times 10^{-15})}$$ $$= \dfrac{333.33}{33.55} = 9.93$$ **Interpretation:** Spontaneous emission is approximately **10 times** stronger than stimulated emission per atom. To achieve laser action, the ruby laser uses the metastable E₁ state (long lifetime 3 ms) to accumulate population, *and* the optical cavity builds up $\rho(\nu)$ inside to enhance stimulated emission by orders of magnitude. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting degeneracy factors:** In the B-B relation, students often write $B_{12} = B_{21}$ without including $g_1, g_2$. **Lose 1 mark** if degeneracies are missing when the question involves non-degenerate systems. > 2. **Unit confusion in B₂₁:** Students often forget that B₂₁ has units involving $\rho(\nu)$ in the denominator. Always show the unit cancellation explicitly. > 3. **Population inversion defined wrongly:** "More atoms in upper level" is *necessary but not sufficient*. The rigorous condition is $N_2 > (g_2/g_1) N_1$. > 4. **Confusing the rate and the coefficient:** $A_{21}$ is a coefficient (s⁻¹) and $A_{21} N_2$ is the *rate* (atoms/m³·s). Examiners deduct marks for this interchange. > 5. **Forgetting to multiply by 2 in stimulated emission:** When you count the photons *after* a stimulated emission event, there are *two* photons (the original + the clone), not one. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:** - [x] **Spontaneous emission** = random, no trigger, incoherent photons, governed by A₂₁; rate = A₂₁ N₂. - [x] **Stimulated emission** = triggered by incident photon, *coherent clone* emitted, governed by B₂₁; rate = B₂₁ N₂ ρ(ν). - [x] **Absorption** = only stimulated (no A coefficient for absorption); rate = B₁₂ N₁ ρ(ν). - [x] **Einstein A-B relation:** $A_{21} = (8\pi h \nu^3 / c^3) B_{21}$ — A₂₁ dominates at high frequency (visible, UV). - [x] **Einstein B-B relation with degeneracy:** $g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}$. - [x] **Boltzmann population ratio:** $N_2/N_1 = (g_2/g_1)\exp(-h\nu/k_BT)$ — always less than 1 at thermal equilibrium. - [x] **Population inversion** = non-thermal condition N₂ > N₁; required for laser amplification. - [x] **LASER acronym** = Light Amplification by **S**timulated **E**mission of **R**adiation. - [x] **LED** vs **LASER**: LED = spontaneous (incoherent); LASER = stimulated (coherent). - [x] **Mean excited state lifetime:** $\tau = 1/A_{21}$. - [x] **Ruby laser** = 3-level system, Cr³⁺ in Al₂O₃, 694.3 nm red output, optical pumping by xenon flash lamp. - [x] **He-Ne laser** = 4-level system, 632.8 nm red output, electrical discharge pumping. - [x] **Optical cavity** = two parallel mirrors (one fully reflective, one partially transmissive) that build up ρ(ν) and select one frequency and direction. - [x] **Why masers are easier than lasers:** Spontaneous emission scales as ν³, so microwave transitions have negligible A₂₁, making population inversion easy to maintain. - [x] **Coherence** = the defining output property of stimulated emission — both temporal (single frequency) and spatial (single direction). - [x] **Remember the magic numbers:** h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s, c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s, k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K. - [x] **At thermal equilibrium, absorption rate = spontaneous emission rate + stimulated emission rate** — this is detailed balance. > **End of Topic Notes — Spontaneous Emission and Stimulated Emission, Module 4, GBPHT121, KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Properties of Laser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Properties of Laser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **LASER** (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation) is a coherent, monochromatic, and highly directional source of optical radiation produced by **stimulated emission** within an optically pumped resonant cavity. The unique properties of laser light distinguish it fundamentally from thermal (incoherent) sources such as tungsten lamps or sodium vapor lamps.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> The properties of laser light that must be mastered at the B.Tech level are:
> 1. **Monochromaticity** (spectral purity)
> 2. **Coherence** — *Temporal* and *Spatial*
> 3. **Directionality** (low beam divergence)
> 4. **Brightness / High Intensity**

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Why a Laser is NOT Just a Bright Bulb"

Imagine a stadium crowd doing "the wave." Each person stands up independently at random times — that is **ordinary (incoherent) light**: photons are emitted randomly, in random directions, at random phases. Now imagine a *single conductor* who has all 50,000 people stand and sit at the *exact same instant, in the exact same direction, at the exact same wavelength* — that is a **laser**. Every photon is a *twin* of every other photon: same color, same phase, same direction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** The extraordinary properties of laser light are *not* independent — they all arise from the same physical mechanism: **stimulated emission in a resonant optical cavity**. Once you understand stimulated emission, every property (monochromaticity, coherence, directionality) is a logical consequence.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics at a Glance

| Property | Conventional Source (e.g., Na lamp) | Laser Source (e.g., He–Ne) |
|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth $\Delta\lambda$ | ~ **1 nm – 100 nm** | ~ **10⁻⁶ nm – 10⁻³ nm** |
| Coherence Length $L_c$ | ~ **0.1 mm – 1 mm** | ~ **10 cm – 30 cm** (can reach km range) |
| Divergence $\theta$ | Radiates in **4π sr** | ~ **1 mrad** (0.057°) |
| Output Power (CW) | Few Watts | mW to kW |
| Spatial Coherence | Poor | Excellent (TEM₀₀ mode) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Property 1 — Monochromaticity

**Definition:** Monochromaticity is the degree to which the emitted light consists of a single wavelength (or a very narrow band of wavelengths).

**Why does a laser achieve it?**
The optical cavity of the laser supports only certain discrete resonant frequencies (longitudinal modes) given by:

$$\nu_q = \frac{q \cdot c}{2 n L}$$

where:
- $q$ = mode number (positive integer)
- $c$ = speed of light in vacuum
- $n$ = refractive index of cavity medium
- $L$ = length of the resonant cavity

**Logic Flow:**
- Step 1 → In spontaneous emission (ordinary sources), atoms emit independently across a broad spectrum.
- Step 2 → In stimulated emission, an incoming photon of energy $E = h\nu$ forces an excited atom to emit a *second photon identical in phase, frequency, polarization, and direction*.
- Step 3 → The resonant cavity acts as a *frequency filter*: only frequencies matching $\nu_q$ survive through multiple reflections between the mirrors.
- Step 4 → Result: Output spectrum is restricted to a few narrow lines → extremely high monochromaticity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantitative Measure of Monochromaticity:**
> $$\text{Degree of Monochromaticity} = \frac{\Delta\lambda}{\lambda}$$
> Lower this ratio → more monochromatic. For a He–Ne laser: $\frac{\Delta\lambda}{\lambda} \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$.

### 2.2 Property 2 — Coherence

Coherence is the **correlation between light waves at different points in space or time**. It is a direct measure of how "twin-like" two photons in the beam are.

#### 2.2.1 Temporal Coherence
Temporal coherence describes the correlation of the wave with **itself at different times** (i.e., along the direction of propagation). It is governed by the **spectral bandwidth** $\Delta\nu$ of the source.

$$\tau_c = \frac{1}{\Delta\nu} \quad ; \quad L_c = c \cdot \tau_c = \frac{c}{\Delta\nu} = \frac{\lambda^2}{\Delta\lambda}$$

where:
- $\tau_c$ = **Coherence Time** — the duration over which the phase of the wave is predictable
- $L_c$ = **Coherence Length** — the propagation distance over which the wave maintains a fixed phase relationship with itself

#### 2.2.2 Spatial Coherence
Spatial coherence describes the correlation between two **different points on the wavefront at the same instant** (perpendicular to the direction of propagation). It is governed by the **size of the source** $d$:

$$d \cdot \theta \le \lambda \quad ; \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \theta_c = \frac{\lambda}{2d}$$

A laser operating in the fundamental **TEM₀₀ (Gaussian) mode** behaves as a *point source* → perfect spatial coherence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temporal vs Spatial Coherence — Exam-Ready Distinction:**
> - **Temporal coherence** = "How long does the photon remember its phase?" → fixed by $\Delta\lambda$
> - **Spatial coherence** = "How well do two different points on the wavefront agree?" → fixed by source size / beam geometry

### 2.3 Property 3 — Directionality

A laser emits a **narrow, almost perfectly collimated beam** that spreads very slowly over long distances.

**Physical Origin:**
The resonant cavity (two parallel mirrors) forces all photons to travel *back and forth* along the cavity axis. Only those photons moving **exactly parallel to the axis** are amplified. Photons emitted at an angle are quickly lost through the sides of the cavity.

**Quantitative Measure — Beam Divergence:**
The minimum divergence due to diffraction alone is:

$$\theta = \frac{\lambda}{D}$$

where $D$ is the diameter of the beam at the output aperture.

**Comparison:**
- A torchlight has divergence $\sim 30°$ (0.5 rad).
- A typical He–Ne laser: $\theta \approx 1 \text{ mrad}$.
- A pointer laser: $\theta \approx 0.5 \text{ mrad}$.

### 2.4 Property 4 — Brightness / High Intensity

Brightness $B$ is the **power emitted per unit area per unit solid angle**:

$$B = \frac{P}{A \cdot \Omega}$$

where $A$ is the beam cross-sectional area and $\Omega$ is the solid angle of divergence ($\Omega \approx \pi\theta^2$ for small angles).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why lasers are absurdly bright:** Even a 1 mW He–Ne laser can outshine the Sun in narrow-angle brightness because $\Omega_{Sun} \approx 7 \times 10^{-5}$ sr, while $\Omega_{laser} \approx 10^{-6}$ sr — and the laser's beam cross-section is microscopically small.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resonant cavity frequency | $\nu_q$ | $\nu_q = \dfrac{qc}{2nL}$ | Hz |
| 2 | Coherence time | $\tau_c$ | $\tau_c = \dfrac{1}{\Delta\nu}$ | s |
| 3 | Coherence length | $L_c$ | $L_c = c\,\tau_c = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{\Delta\lambda}$ | m |
| 4 | Spectral line width | $\Delta\lambda$ | $\Delta\lambda = \dfrac{\lambda^2}{L_c}$ | m |
| 5 | Coherence condition | — | $d\,\theta \le \lambda$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Beam divergence (diffraction limit) | $\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{\lambda}{D}$ | rad |
| 7 | Solid angle of beam | $\Omega$ | $\Omega = \pi\theta^2$ | sr |
| 8 | Brightness | $B$ | $B = \dfrac{P}{A\,\Omega}$ | W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹ |
| 9 | Intensity (average) | $I$ | $I = \dfrac{P}{A} = \dfrac{1}{2}c\,\varepsilon_0\,E_0^2$ | W·m⁻² |
| 10 | Monochromaticity ratio | — | $\dfrac{\Delta\lambda}{\lambda}$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Applications that exploit these properties:**
> - **Monochromaticity** → Holography, Raman spectroscopy, interferometry
> - **Temporal Coherence** → Optical coherence tomography (OCT) in medical imaging
> - **Spatial Coherence** → Speckle interferometry, Schlieren photography
> - **Directionality** → LIDAR, surveying, free-space optical communication, laser cutting
> - **Brightness** → Laser surgery, nuclear fusion (NIF), materials processing

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Coherence Length from Spectral Bandwidth

**Statement to Prove:**
$$L_c = \frac{c}{\Delta\nu} = \frac{\lambda^2}{\Delta\lambda}$$

**Step 1 — Start with the definition of coherence time.**
Temporal coherence is measured by the time interval $\tau_c$ over which the phase of the light wave at a fixed point in space remains predictable (correlated). It is inversely proportional to the spectral bandwidth:

$$\tau_c \propto \frac{1}{\Delta\nu}$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the constant of proportionality.**
For a *naturally broadened* line (Lorentzian profile), the rigorous relationship is:

$$\tau_c = \frac{1}{\Delta\nu}$$

**Step 3 — Convert coherence time to coherence length.**
Since light travels at speed $c$ in vacuum, the distance $L_c$ it covers in time $\tau_c$ is:

$$L_c = c \cdot \tau_c = \frac{c}{\Delta\nu}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of wavelength.**
We know that for any wave, $\nu = c/\lambda$, therefore:

$$\Delta\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda^2}\,\Delta\lambda \quad \text{(using calculus: } d\nu = -\frac{c}{\lambda^2}d\lambda\text{)}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the coherence length formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_c &= \frac{c}{\Delta\nu} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{c}{\dfrac{c}{\lambda^2}\,\Delta\lambda} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\lambda^2}{\Delta\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{L_c = \frac{\lambda^2}{\Delta\lambda}}$$

**Step 6 — Physical Interpretation:**
- Smaller bandwidth $\Delta\lambda$ → larger coherence length
- This is why **single-mode lasers** (e.g., stabilized He–Ne) can have $L_c$ up to kilometers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Beam Divergence from Diffraction

**Statement to Prove:**
The minimum angular spread of a laser beam emerging from a circular aperture of diameter $D$ is:

$$\theta = \frac{\lambda}{D}$$

**Step 1 — Model the laser output mirror as a circular aperture.**
When the planar wave inside the cavity strikes the partially reflecting output mirror, it passes through a circular opening of diameter $D$.

**Step 2 — Apply Fraunhofer diffraction theory.**
The far-field diffraction pattern of a circular aperture is the **Airy disk**, with the first diffraction minimum at angular radius:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{1.22\,\lambda}{D}$$

**Step 3 — Approximate for small angles.**
Since the divergence of a laser is very small ($\theta \ll 1$ rad), we use $\sin\theta \approx \theta$:

$$\theta \approx \frac{1.22\,\lambda}{D}$$

**Step 4 — Drop the numerical factor for order-of-magnitude estimate.**
The numerical factor $1.22$ arises from the first zero of the Bessel function. For a uniform beam profile, the *full-angle* divergence to the first minimum is $\sim 1.22\,\lambda/D$. The angular radius to the first minimum is:

$$\theta = \frac{\lambda}{D}$$

**Result:**
$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{\lambda}{D}}$$

**Step 5 — Practical insight:**
- Doubling the mirror diameter $D$ halves the divergence.
- Switching from a red laser ($\lambda = 633$ nm) to a blue laser ($\lambda = 450$ nm) reduces divergence by $\sim 30\%$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Brightness Comparison: Laser vs Sun

**Problem Setup:**
Compare the brightness of a 5 mW He–Ne laser ($\lambda = 632.8$ nm, $D = 1$ mm) with that of the Sun as seen from Earth.

**Step 1 — Compute the laser's divergence.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \frac{\lambda}{D} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{632.8 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{m}}{1 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}} \\[4pt]
&= 6.328 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{rad}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the solid angle of the beam.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Omega_{laser} &= \pi \theta^2 \\[4pt]
&= \pi \times (6.328 \times 10^{-4})^2 \\[4pt]
&= 1.258 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{sr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the laser's beam area at the aperture.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (10^{-3})^2}{4} \\[4pt]
&= 7.854 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the laser's brightness.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{laser} &= \frac{P}{A \cdot \Omega} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W}}{(7.854 \times 10^{-7}) \times (1.258 \times 10^{-6})\ \text{m}^2\cdot\text{sr}} \\[4pt]
&\approx 5.06 \times 10^{9}\ \text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{sr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Sun's brightness (solar constant $\approx 1361$ W/m² at Earth, angular diameter $\approx 0.53°$).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Omega_{Sun} &= \pi \theta_{Sun}^2 = \pi \times \left(4.63 \times 10^{-3}\right)^2 \approx 6.74 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{sr}
\end{aligned}
$$

Considering the Sun radiates as a blackbody at ~5778 K, the brightness of the Sun at visible wavelengths (using Planck's law integrated over a narrow band) is approximately:

$$B_{Sun} \sim 2 \times 10^{7}\ \text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{sr}^{-1} \quad \text{(visible band)}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison.**

$$
\frac{B_{laser}}{B_{Sun}} \approx \frac{5.06 \times 10^{9}}{2 \times 10^{7}} \approx 253
$$

**Result:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** Even a 5 mW He–Ne pointer laser is ~**250 times brighter** than the Sun in narrow-angle visible brightness. This is the most striking illustration of laser directionality + monochromaticity.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Properties

| Property | Physical Origin | Quantitative Measure | He–Ne Laser Value | Sun / Tungsten Lamp |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Monochromaticity | Resonant cavity mode selection | $\Delta\lambda/\lambda$ | $\sim 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ | $\sim 10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| Temporal Coherence | Narrow spectral bandwidth | $L_c = \lambda^2/\Delta\lambda$ | $\sim 30$ cm (single mode → km) | $\sim 0.5$ mm |
| Spatial Coherence | TEM₀₀ mode emission | $d\,\theta \le \lambda$ | Excellent (point-like source) | Poor (extended source) |
| Directionality | Collimated cavity emission | $\theta = \lambda/D$ | $\sim 1$ mrad | $\sim$ full $4\pi$ sr |
| Brightness | Combined effect | $B = P/(A\Omega)$ | $\sim 10^{9}$ W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹ | $\sim 10^{7}$ W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹ |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
**Define temporal coherence. Write the expression for coherence length and explain why a He–Ne laser has higher temporal coherence than a sodium vapor lamp.**

**Model Answer:**

**Temporal Coherence:** It is the correlation of the phase of a light wave with itself at different instants of time. It is a measure of how "predictable" the phase of the wave is as it propagates.

**Expression for Coherence Length:**

$$L_c = c \cdot \tau_c = \frac{\lambda^2}{\Delta\lambda}$$

**Why He–Ne > Na lamp:**
- A He–Ne laser has a very narrow bandwidth ($\Delta\lambda \approx 0.0001$ nm) because the optical cavity permits only the resonant longitudinal modes.
- A sodium vapor lamp emits via spontaneous emission with $\Delta\lambda \approx 0.6$ nm.
- Since $L_c \propto 1/\Delta\lambda$, the laser's coherence length is **~6000 times larger** than the lamp's.

**Mark Distribution (3 marks):**
- Definition of temporal coherence — 1 mark
- Expression $L_c = \lambda^2/\Delta\lambda$ — 1 mark
- Comparison of $\Delta\lambda$ values and conclusion — 1 mark

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**What is meant by directionality of a laser beam? Show that the divergence of a laser beam is $\theta = \lambda/D$, where $D$ is the beam diameter.**

**Model Answer:**

**Directionality:** The property of a laser beam to travel as a narrow, highly collimated ray with very little angular spread, in contrast to ordinary sources which radiate in all directions ($4\pi$ steradians).

**Derivation of $\theta = \lambda/D$:**

When the plane wave inside the laser cavity emerges from the output mirror (aperture diameter $D$), it undergoes **Fraunhofer diffraction**. The angular radius of the central Airy maximum is given by the condition for the first minimum of the Bessel function $J_1$:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{1.22\,\lambda}{D}$$

For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \theta$, giving the minimum achievable divergence:

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{\lambda}{D}}$$

This divergence is *extremely small* because $\lambda$ (a few hundred nm) is much smaller than $D$ (a few mm), yielding $\theta \sim 10^{-4}$ rad.

**Mark Distribution (3 marks):**
- Definition of directionality — 1 mark
- Diffraction-based reasoning — 1 mark
- Final expression and physical insight — 1 mark

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Full internal choice — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model]**

**(a) Derive an expression for the coherence length of a laser beam in terms of its wavelength and spectral bandwidth. A He–Ne laser operating at 632.8 nm has a spectral bandwidth of $1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ nm. Calculate its coherence length.** (7 marks)

**(b) Explain the concepts of temporal and spatial coherence. Why is spatial coherence of a laser better than that of a tungsten lamp? Give two applications that rely on coherence.** (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Step 1 — Definition of Coherence Time:**
Coherence time $\tau_c$ is the time interval over which the phase of the light wave remains correlated with itself:

$$\tau_c = \frac{1}{\Delta\nu}$$

**Step 2 — Relation to Wavelength:**
Since $\nu = c/\lambda$, taking the differential:

$$d\nu = -\frac{c}{\lambda^2}\,d\lambda \quad\Rightarrow\quad \Delta\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda^2}\,\Delta\lambda$$

**Step 3 — Coherence Length Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_c &= c \cdot \tau_c = \frac{c}{\Delta\nu} = \frac{c}{\dfrac{c}{\lambda^2}\,\Delta\lambda} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\lambda^2}{\Delta\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Calculation:**
Given: $\lambda = 632.8 \text{ nm} = 632.8 \times 10^{-9}$ m, and $\Delta\lambda = 1.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ nm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-18}$ m.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_c &= \frac{(632.8 \times 10^{-9})^2}{1.5 \times 10^{-18}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{4.004 \times 10^{-13}}{1.5 \times 10^{-18}} \\[6pt]
&= 2.67 \times 10^{5}\ \text{m} \\[6pt]
&\approx 267\ \text{km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The He–Ne laser has a coherence length of approximately **267 km**, demonstrating its extraordinarily high temporal coherence.

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Derivation setup (1) + Final formula (1) = 2 marks
- Wavelength-to-frequency conversion (1) + final formula $L_c = \lambda^2/\Delta\lambda$ (1) = 2 marks
- Numerical substitution (1) + correct answer with units (1) = 2 marks
- Final boxed result and physical conclusion (1) = 1 mark

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Temporal Coherence:** Correlation of a light wave with *itself at different times* (along the direction of propagation). It is governed by the **spectral bandwidth** $\Delta\lambda$ of the source.

$$L_c = \frac{\lambda^2}{\Delta\lambda}, \qquad \tau_c = \frac{1}{\Delta\nu}$$

**Spatial Coherence:** Correlation between light waves at *two different points on the same wavefront* (perpendicular to the direction of propagation). It is governed by the **size of the source** $d$ and the angle $\theta$ of observation. The coherence condition is:

$$d\,\theta \le \lambda$$

A smaller source $d$ → better spatial coherence. An ideal point source has perfect spatial coherence.

**Why laser > tungsten lamp in spatial coherence:**

- A tungsten lamp is an **extended source** — every point on the filament acts as an independent emitter. The wavefronts are an incoherent superposition of contributions from millions of points.
- A laser operating in the **TEM₀₀ Gaussian mode** behaves as an *effective point source* because stimulated emission locks all atoms to emit in phase from a small region.
- Hence, any two points on a laser wavefront have a fixed phase relationship, while on a lamp's wavefront, the phase relationship fluctuates randomly.

**Two Applications Relying on Coherence:**

1. **Holography** — Requires both temporal and spatial coherence to record the full amplitude and phase information of a 3D object onto a photographic plate.
2. **Optical Coherence Tomography (OCT)** — Used in ophthalmology to image the retina. It uses short-coherence-length broadband light to achieve micrometer axial resolution.
3. *(Optional third)* Michelson / Mach–Zehnder interferometry in metrology and surface profiling.

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Temporal coherence definition + formula — 1.5 marks
- Spatial coherence definition + condition $d\theta \le \lambda$ — 1.5 marks
- Comparison of laser vs tungsten (2 points) — 2 marks
- Two applications with brief explanation — 2 marks

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, Model Alternative]**

**(a) Define brightness of a laser beam. A 5 mW He–Ne laser ($\lambda = 632.8$ nm) emits a beam of diameter 1 mm with divergence of 1 mrad. Calculate its brightness and compare it with that of the Sun (solar constant = 1361 W/m², angular diameter = 0.53°).** (7 marks)

**(b) Explain the term monochromaticity. Show that the degree of monochromaticity of a laser is extremely high compared to a conventional source. Mention two engineering applications that exploit the monochromaticity of laser light.** (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Brightness Definition:**
Brightness is the radiated power per unit area per unit solid angle emitted by a source:

$$B = \frac{P}{A \cdot \Omega}$$

**Step 1 — Compute the beam area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (10^{-3})^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the solid angle:**
For small divergence, $\Omega = \pi \theta^2$:

$$\Omega = \pi \times (10^{-3})^2 = \pi \times 10^{-6} \approx 3.14 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{sr}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the brightness of the laser:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{laser} &= \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{W}}{(7.854 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{m}^2)\,(3.14 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{sr})} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{2.466 \times 10^{-12}} \\[6pt]
&\approx 2.03 \times 10^{9}\ \text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{sr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the solid angle subtended by the Sun:**
Angular diameter $\alpha = 0.53° = 9.25 \times 10^{-3}$ rad, so half-angle $\theta_{Sun} = 4.63 \times 10^{-3}$ rad.

$$\Omega_{Sun} = \pi\,\theta_{Sun}^2 = \pi \times (4.63 \times 10^{-3})^2 \approx 6.74 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{sr}$$

**Step 5 — Sun's brightness (visible-band estimate):**
The solar constant gives total irradiance. The visible-band (380–700 nm) fraction is ~40% of total, so $I_{vis} \approx 540$ W/m². Brightness $B_{Sun}$:

$$B_{Sun} = \frac{I_{vis}}{\pi\,\sin^2(\theta_{Sun})} \approx \frac{540}{6.74 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 8.0 \times 10^{6}\ \text{W}\cdot\text{m}^{-2}\cdot\text{sr}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Comparison:**

$$\frac{B_{laser}}{B_{Sun}} \approx \frac{2.03 \times 10^{9}}{8.0 \times 10^{6}} \approx 254$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The 5 mW He–Ne laser is approximately **254 times brighter** than the Sun in the visible band, even though its total power is $\sim 10^{10}$ times less than the Sun's total output. This dramatic ratio arises purely from the laser's tiny beam cross-section and tiny solid angle of emission.

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Definition of brightness — 1 mark
- Beam area calculation — 1 mark
- Solid angle of laser — 1 mark
- Brightness of laser (numerical) — 1 mark
- Sun's solid angle — 1 mark
- Sun's brightness — 1 mark
- Comparison ratio + conclusion — 1 mark

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**Monochromaticity Definition:**
Monochromaticity is the property of a light source to emit radiation confined to an extremely narrow range of wavelengths. Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio:

$$\text{Monochromaticity} = \frac{\Delta\lambda}{\lambda}$$

A smaller value of $\Delta\lambda/\lambda$ implies a more monochromatic source.

**Step 1 — Why lasers are highly monochromatic:**
The optical cavity of a laser supports only certain discrete longitudinal modes given by:

$$\nu_q = \frac{q\,c}{2nL}, \qquad q = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

Only frequencies matching these resonant modes are amplified; all other frequencies are suppressed by destructive interference. Additionally, mode competition inside the cavity often forces single-mode operation.

**Step 2 — Numerical comparison:**

| Source | $\lambda$ (nm) | $\Delta\lambda$ (nm) | $\Delta\lambda/\lambda$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sunlight (yellow band) | 550 | ~200 | $\sim 3.6 \times 10^{-1}$ |
| Sodium vapor lamp | 589.3 | $\sim 0.6$ | $\sim 10^{-3}$ |
| White LED | 550 | $\sim 30$ | $\sim 5.5 \times 10^{-2}$ |
| Standard He–Ne laser | 632.8 | $\sim 0.001$ | $\sim 1.6 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Stabilized single-mode He–Ne | 632.8 | $\sim 1.5 \times 10^{-9}$ | $\sim 2.4 \times 10^{-12}$ |

A laser thus has a monochromaticity ratio that is **6 to 11 orders of magnitude smaller** than any conventional source.

**Step 3 — Two Engineering Applications Exploiting Monochromaticity:**

1. **Holography:** The reference and object beams must be highly monochromatic to produce stable interference fringes (otherwise, the fringes wash out). A pulsed ruby laser ($\Delta\lambda \approx 0.01$ nm) is the standard source.
2. **Raman Spectroscopy:** The Raman shift is measured as a tiny wavelength difference ($\Delta\lambda \sim 0.1$–$1$ nm) from the excitation wavelength. A narrow excitation line is essential to resolve the closely spaced vibrational/rotational lines of molecules.
3. *(Optional third)* Laser-induced fluorescence in biomedical imaging and barcode scanning.

**Mark Split (7 marks):**
- Definition + expression — 1 mark
- Cavity mode equation + physical reasoning — 2 marks
- Comparative table of $\Delta\lambda/\lambda$ — 2 marks
- Two applications with explanation — 2 marks

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up temporal and spatial coherence.** Temporal = *time* (bandwidth), Spatial = *space* (source size). Examiners *specifically deduct* 1–2 marks for this swap.
> 2. **Forgetting units in numerical answers.** Coherence length MUST be reported in **meters or km**, brightness in **W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹**. Missing units = lose 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Sign error in $\Delta\nu = c\Delta\lambda/\lambda^2$.** The relationship between $\nu$ and $\lambda$ involves a minus sign, but since we deal with magnitudes of *spreads*, drop the sign. Writing $d\nu = -c\,d\lambda/\lambda^2$ *with* the minus sign in a magnitude calculation is marked wrong.
> 4. **Confusing "divergence" with "solid angle."** Divergence $\theta$ is an *angle* (rad), solid angle $\Omega = \pi\theta^2$ (sr). Both must appear correctly in brightness calculations.
> 5. **Omitting the factor of $1.22$ in the rigorous diffraction formula.** Use $\theta = 1.22\,\lambda/D$ for the *first minimum* of the Airy pattern; use $\theta = \lambda/D$ for an *order-of-magnitude* estimate. Examiners will accept both only if the context is clear.
> 6. **Skipping the physical interpretation** ("smaller bandwidth → longer coherence") at the end of derivations. Always close with a one-line insight sentence.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullet Points — KTU 2024 Scheme**

- [ ] **LASER** = Light Amplification by **S**timulated **E**mission of **R**adiation.
- [ ] The four pillars of laser light: **Monochromaticity, Coherence, Directionality, Brightness**.
- [ ] **Monochromaticity** measured by $\Delta\lambda/\lambda$. Lasers: $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-12}$. Conventional: $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-1}$.
- [ ] **Coherence time** $\tau_c = 1/\Delta\nu$. **Coherence length** $L_c = c\,\tau_c = \lambda^2/\Delta\lambda$.
- [ ] **Temporal coherence** is governed by spectral bandwidth $\Delta\lambda$.
- [ ] **Spatial coherence** is governed by source size $d$, with the condition $d\,\theta \le \lambda$.
- [ ] **Directionality** comes from the cavity geometry; minimum divergence is $\theta = \lambda/D$ (diffraction limit).
- [ ] **Solid angle of beam** $\Omega = \pi\theta^2$ (small-angle approximation).
- [ ] **Brightness** $B = P / (A\,\Omega)$ — units: W·m⁻²·sr⁻¹.
- [ ] A 5 mW He–Ne laser is ~250× brighter than the Sun in visible-band narrow-angle brightness.
- [ ] He–Ne laser typical values: $\lambda = 632.8$ nm, $\Delta\lambda \approx 0.001$ nm, $\theta \approx 1$ mrad, $L_c \approx 30$ cm (multi-mode) to ~hundreds of km (single-mode).
- [ ] **TEM₀₀ mode** = fundamental Gaussian mode, ideal for maximum spatial coherence and minimum beam divergence.
- [ ] **Two key formulas to memorize cold:** $L_c = \lambda^2/\Delta\lambda$ and $\theta = \lambda/D$.
- [ ] All four properties trace back to a single cause: **stimulated emission + resonant optical cavity**.
- [ ] Examiner's hot-button: Always distinguish *temporal* vs *spatial* coherence clearly in any answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principle of Laser - Conditions for Sustained Lasing - Population Inversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Principle of Laser - Conditions for Sustained Lasing - Population Inversion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of LASER **LASER** stands for **Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation**. It is a device that produces an intense, highly directional, monochromatic, and coherent beam of light through a process called **stimulated emission**, in which an external photon of a specific frequency triggers an excited atom to emit a second photon identical in phase, frequency, direction, and polarization. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (GBPHT121 – Module 4):** > The principle of laser operation rests on **three essential pillars**: > 1. **Stimulated Emission** (the quantum mechanism) > 2. **Population Inversion** (the non-equilibrium state) > 3. **Optical Resonant Cavity** (the feedback mechanism) > > All three must be satisfied *simultaneously* for **sustained lasing** (continuous laser action). ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Photon Chain Reaction Imagine a perfectly still **water slide pool** at an amusement park. Normally, only a few people are at the top of the slide (excited state) and most are in the pool below (ground state). If someone at the top is gently pushed (stimulated by a photon), they slide down and create a splash that pushes another person at the top — and so on. This is **stimulated emission**: one photon triggers another identical photon. Now, to keep the splash going, we need *more people at the top than in the pool* — this unnatural, "upside-down" condition is the **population inversion**. Finally, two large mirrors at opposite ends of the pool reflect the splashes back and forth, multiplying the effect. These mirrors form the **optical resonator cavity**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Three Conditions for Sustained Lasing (the "Golden Triangle"):** > 1. **Population Inversion:** $N_2 > N_1$ (more atoms in the excited state than the ground state). > 2. **Metastable Upper Level:** The excited state must have a relatively long lifetime (~10⁻³ s) so that atoms accumulate there. > 3. **Optical Resonator Cavity:** Two mirrors (one fully reflective, one partially reflective) provide positive feedback to amplify light. ## 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants - Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8 \ \text{m/s}$ - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \ \text{J·s}$ - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \ \text{J/K}$ - Typical metastable state lifetime: $\tau \sim 10^{-3} \ \text{s}$ (compared to ordinary excited state lifetime of $\sim 10^{-8}$ s) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Einstein's Coefficients — The Quantum Foundation Einstein (1917) introduced three fundamental coefficients to describe the interaction between light and matter: ### 2.1.1 The Three Processes | Process | Coefficient | Description | |---|---|---| | **Spontaneous Emission** | $A_{21}$ | An excited atom drops to a lower energy level on its own, emitting a photon of random phase and direction. | | **Stimulated Absorption** | $B_{12}$ | An atom in the lower state absorbs an incident photon and jumps to the higher energy level. | | **Stimulated Emission** | $B_{21}$ | An incident photon triggers an excited atom to emit a second photon identical in all respects (coherent). | ### 2.1.2 The Einstein Rate Equations The number of transitions per unit time per unit volume: - Spontaneous emission rate: $R_{sp} = A_{21} N_2$ - Stimulated emission rate: $R_{se} = B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$ - Stimulated absorption rate: $R_{ab} = B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu)$ where $\rho(\nu)$ is the **spectral energy density** of the radiation field. ### 2.1.3 Einstein's Thermodynamic Relations At thermal equilibrium, the rates of upward and downward transitions must balance, and the spectral energy density follows the **Planck's blackbody radiation law**: $$\rho(\nu) = \frac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1}$$ By equating the rates, Einstein derived two universal relations that are **independent of temperature**: $$\frac{A_{21}}{B_{21}} = \frac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3}$$ $$g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}$$ where $g_1$ and $g_2$ are the **degeneracies** of the lower and upper levels, respectively. ## 2.2 Population Inversion — The Heart of the Laser In **thermal equilibrium**, the population of two energy levels follows the **Boltzmann distribution**: $$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{g_2}{g_1} e^{-(E_2 - E_1)/k_B T}$$ Since $E_2 > E_1$, we have $N_2 < N_1$ — the *opposite* of what is needed for lasing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Population Inversion** is a **non-equilibrium, thermodynamically inverted** state in which: > $$N_2 > N_1 \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{N_2}{N_1} > 1$$ > > This **cannot be achieved with a simple two-level system** under normal pumping because stimulated absorption always equals stimulated emission at equilibrium. ## 2.3 Why a Metastable State is Essential A **metastable state** is an excited electronic state with an unusually long lifetime ($\sim 10^{-3}$ s, compared to $\sim 10^{-8}$ s for ordinary excited states). Because atoms "linger" in this state, they accumulate, making population inversion **practically achievable**. ## 2.4 The Three-Level Laser System A **three-level system** uses: - **Level 1:** Ground state (long-lived, full of atoms initially) - **Level 3:** Pump level (short-lived, optical pumping excites atoms here) - **Level 2:** Metastable laser upper level (atoms accumulate here) The transition for lasing is $E_3 \to E_2$ (pump), followed by $E_2 \to E_1$ (laser emission). **More than 50% of ground-state atoms must be pumped** — a major practical disadvantage (e.g., Ruby laser). ## 2.5 The Four-Level Laser System — The Engineering Solution A **four-level system** solves the three-level problem by introducing an intermediate lower laser level: - **Level 1:** Ground state - **Level 4:** Pump level - **Level 3:** Metastable upper laser level - **Level 2:** Lower laser level (rapidly depopulated to ground state) Since Level 2 is essentially empty at any time, **population inversion between Levels 3 and 2 is easy to achieve** — only a small pump power is needed. This is why most modern lasers (He-Ne, Nd:YAG, CO₂) are four-level systems. ## 2.6 Optical Resonator Cavity Two mirrors facing each other form a **Fabry–Pérot resonator**: - One mirror is **100% reflective** (the rear mirror) - The other is **partially reflective** (~95–98%), called the **output coupler** Photons bounce back and forth, stimulating more emissions. A small fraction escapes as the **laser beam**. The cavity length $L$ must support **standing wave modes**: $$L = q \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2}, \quad q = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$ ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Meaning / Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Planck's radiation law | $\rho(\nu) = \dfrac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3} \dfrac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1}$ | Energy density at frequency $\nu$ | | 2 | Boltzmann population ratio | $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{g_2}{g_1} e^{-(E_2-E_1)/k_B T}$ | Equilibrium distribution (always $N_2 < N_1$) | | 3 | Einstein relation 1 | $\dfrac{A_{21}}{B_{21}} = \dfrac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3}$ | Links spontaneous to stimulated emission | | 4 | Einstein relation 2 | $g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}$ | Cross-section symmetry | | 5 | Population inversion | $N_2 > N_1$ | Non-equilibrium requirement for lasing | | 6 | Spontaneous emission rate | $R_{sp} = A_{21} N_2$ | Random, incoherent emission | | 7 | Stimulated emission rate | $R_{se} = B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$ | Coherent amplification | | 8 | Absorption rate | $R_{ab} = B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu)$ | Loss of photons | | 9 | Net amplification condition | $B_{21} N_2 > B_{12} N_1 \Rightarrow N_2 > N_1 \cdot \dfrac{g_2}{g_1}$ | Lasing threshold | | 10 | Resonator mode condition | $L = q \dfrac{\lambda}{2}$ | Allowed standing-wave modes | | 11 | Photon energy | $E = h \nu = \dfrac{h c}{\lambda}$ | Quantum of light | | 12 | Threshold gain condition | $G \ge \alpha + \dfrac{1}{2L} \ln \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2} \right)$ | Gain must exceed losses | ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Optical fiber communication** (1.55 µm semiconductor lasers for long-distance data transmission) - **Medical surgery** (LASIK, tumor ablation, dental drills) - **Industrial cutting and welding** (CO₂ and fiber lasers) - **Barcode scanners, LIDAR, spectroscopy** (He-Ne lasers) - **Nuclear fusion research** (NIF's 192 high-power laser beams) - **Holography, Blu-ray disc reading** (GaN blue lasers) # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I: Einstein's First Relation $A_{21}/B_{21}$ **Step 1 — Set up the equilibrium balance.** At thermal equilibrium, the rate of upward (absorptive) transitions must equal the rate of downward (emissive) transitions: $$B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu) = A_{21} N_2 + B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$$ **Step 2 — Solve for the energy density $\rho(\nu)$.** Rearrange to isolate $\rho(\nu)$ on one side: $$B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu) - B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu) = A_{21} N_2$$ Factor out $\rho(\nu)$ from the left-hand side: $$\rho(\nu) \left[ B_{12} N_1 - B_{21} N_2 \right] = A_{21} N_2$$ Divide both sides by the bracketed term: $$\rho(\nu) = \frac{A_{21} N_2}{B_{12} N_1 - B_{21} N_2}$$ **Step 3 — Divide numerator and denominator by $N_2$.** $$\rho(\nu) = \frac{A_{21}}{B_{12} \frac{N_1}{N_2} - B_{21}}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute the Boltzmann ratio.** At thermal equilibrium: $$\frac{N_1}{N_2} = \frac{g_1}{g_2} e^{(E_2 - E_1)/k_B T}$$ Substituting: $$\rho(\nu) = \frac{A_{21}}{B_{12} \cdot \dfrac{g_1}{g_2} e^{h\nu/k_B T} - B_{21}}$$ **Step 5 — Match with Planck's law.** Planck's blackbody spectral energy density is: $$\rho(\nu) = \frac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1}$$ For both expressions to be identical for *all* temperatures $T$, the coefficients must match precisely. Comparing the two forms yields: $$\boxed{\frac{A_{21}}{B_{21}} = \frac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3}}$$ and $$\boxed{g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation II: Why a Two-Level System Cannot Lase **Step 1 — Write the net rate of stimulated emission.** The net rate of coherent (stimulated) emission per unit volume is: $$R_{\text{net}} = B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu) - B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu)$$ **Step 2 — Factor and apply Einstein's second relation.** Using $g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}$ (and assuming $g_1 = g_2$ for simplicity): $$R_{\text{net}} = B_{21} \rho(\nu) \left[ N_2 - N_1 \right]$$ **Step 3 — Apply thermal equilibrium.** In a two-level system at thermal equilibrium: $$N_2 - N_1 = N_1 \left[ e^{-h\nu/k_B T} - 1 \right] < 0$$ This means $R_{\text{net}} < 0$ — **absorption always wins over stimulated emission** in a simple two-level system. **Step 4 — Pumping argument.** If we pump atoms from level 1 to level 2, the *maximum* we can ever achieve in a two-level system is $N_2 = N_1$ (saturation), which gives $R_{\text{net}} = 0$.
