This means **two-level systems are fundamentally incapable of sustained lasing** — we *must* use three- or four-level systems with a metastable state. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** A metastable state is *not optional* — it is the *only* way to break the thermal equilibrium deadlock. A simple two-level system can never produce population inversion. ## 3.3 Derivation III: Threshold Gain Condition for Sustained Lasing **Step 1 — Intensity growth equation.** Inside the active medium, the laser intensity $I$ grows exponentially due to stimulated emission: $$I = I_0 \, e^{G x}$$ where $G$ is the **gain coefficient per unit length** and $x$ is the distance traveled. **Step 2 — Effect of losses.** Two kinds of losses reduce the intensity: - **Internal absorption/scattering losses** $\alpha$ (per unit length) - **Mirror transmission losses** at the partially reflecting output coupler The intensity after one round trip through a cavity of length $L$: $$I_{\text{after}} = I_0 \, e^{(G - \alpha) \cdot 2L} \cdot R_1 R_2$$ where $R_1$ and $R_2$ are the mirror reflectivities. **Step 3 — Apply the sustained lasing condition.** For **sustained (steady-state) lasing**, the intensity after one round trip must equal (or exceed) the initial intensity: $$I_0 \, e^{(G - \alpha) \cdot 2L} \cdot R_1 R_2 \ge I_0$$ Cancel $I_0$: $$e^{(G - \alpha) \cdot 2L} \cdot R_1 R_2 \ge 1$$ Take the natural logarithm: $$(G - \alpha) \cdot 2L \ge - \ln(R_1 R_2)$$ **Step 4 — Solve for the threshold gain.** $$G \ge \alpha + \frac{1}{2L} \ln \left( \frac{1}{R_1 R_2} \right)$$ $$\boxed{G_{\text{threshold}} = \alpha + \frac{1}{2L} \ln \left( \frac{1}{R_1 R_2} \right)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical interpretation:** The gain provided by the inverted population must overcome both internal losses ($\alpha$) and the energy lost through the output mirror (logarithmic term). This is the **lasing threshold** — the minimum pump power below which no lasing occurs. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Three-Level vs Four-Level Systems | Property | Three-Level System | Four-Level System | |---|---|---| | Energy levels | 3 (Ground, Pump, Metastable) | 4 (Ground, Pump, Upper Laser, Lower Laser) | | Lasing transition | $E_2 \to E_1$ (to ground) | $E_3 \to E_2$ (to an excited lower level) | | Population inversion | **Difficult** — must pump >50% of $N_1$ | **Easy** — $E_2$ is nearly empty | | Threshold pump power | **High** | **Low** | | Efficiency | Low | High | | Example lasers | **Ruby laser** (Cr³⁺:Al₂O₃) | **He-Ne, Nd:YAG, CO₂, Semiconductor** | | Practical use | Historical, specialty | Standard for most modern lasers | | Continuous operation | Difficult | Easily achievable | ## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style) **Problem:** A He-Ne laser cavity has length $L = 50$ cm. The mirrors have reflectivities $R_1 = 1.0$ and $R_2 = 0.98$. If the internal loss coefficient is $\alpha = 0.02 \ \text{m}^{-1}$, calculate the threshold gain coefficient. **Solution:** Step 1 — Identify values: - $L = 0.50 \ \text{m}$ - $R_1 = 1.0$, $R_2 = 0.98$ - $\alpha = 0.02 \ \text{m}^{-1}$ Step 2 — Apply the threshold gain formula: $$G_{\text{th}} = \alpha + \frac{1}{2L} \ln \left( \frac{1}{R_1 R_2} \right)$$ Step 3 — Compute $R_1 R_2$: $$R_1 R_2 = (1.0)(0.98) = 0.98$$ Step 4 — Compute the logarithm: $$\frac{1}{R_1 R_2} = \frac{1}{0.98} \approx 1.0204$$ $$\ln(1.0204) \approx 0.02020$$ Step 5 — Compute the mirror-loss term: $$\frac{1}{2L} \ln \left( \frac{1}{R_1 R_2} \right) = \frac{0.02020}{2 \times 0.50} = \frac{0.02020}{1.00} = 0.0202 \ \text{m}^{-1}$$ Step 6 — Sum all losses: $$G_{\text{th}} = 0.02 + 0.0202 = 0.0402 \ \text{m}^{-1}$$ **Answer:** $G_{\text{threshold}} \approx 0.0402 \ \text{m}^{-1}$ (the gain coefficient must exceed this value for sustained lasing). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Laser System Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PUMP SOURCE
Optical or Electrical"] --> B["ACTIVE MEDIUM
Atoms with Metastable State"] B --> C["OPTICAL RESONATOR
Two Mirrors Fabry-Perot"] C --> D["PARTIALLY REFLECTIVE MIRROR
Output Coupler"] D --> E["COHERENT LASER BEAM
Monochromatic Directional"] B -.-> F["FEEDBACK LOOP
Stimulated Emission Cascade"] F --> C ``` **How to read this diagram:** The pump source (flash lamp, electrical current, or another laser) injects energy into the **active medium** (gas, solid crystal, or semiconductor). Atoms are excited to the upper laser level. The **resonator** reflects photons back and forth; each pass triggers more stimulated emission (the feedback loop). The **partially reflective mirror** releases a small, controlled fraction as the **laser beam**. ## 4.2 Energy Level Diagram — Four-Level Laser System ```mermaid flowchart TB L4["LEVEL 4 - Pump State
Short Lifetime ~10^-8 s"] L3["LEVEL 3 - Upper Laser Level
Metastable Lifetime ~10^-3 s"] L2["LEVEL 2 - Lower Laser Level
Short Lifetime ~10^-8 s"] L1["LEVEL 1 - Ground State
Long-lived"] L4 -->|Non-radiative
fast decay| L3 L3 -->|Stimulated Emission
LASER PHOTON| L2 L2 -->|Non-radiative
fast decay| L1 L1 -.->|Optical Pumping| L4 classDef metastable fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#ff8c00,stroke-width:3px classDef ground fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px classDef fast fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#666666,stroke-width:1px class L3 metastable class L1 ground class L4,L2 fast ``` > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** In the four-level system, Level 3 is metastable (atoms accumulate), and Level 2 is rapidly emptied to Level 1. So at any instant, $N_3 \gg N_2$, making population inversion easy to achieve. ## 4.3 Photon Amplification Flow Chart ```mermaid flowchart TD P1["Start: One Incident Photon
of Energy h nu"] --> P2["Photon Strikes Excited Atom
in Metastable State E2"] P2 --> P3["Stimulated Emission Occurs
Atom drops to E1"] P3 --> P4["Two Coherent Photons
Identical Phase Frequency Direction"] P4 --> P5["Each Photon Triggers
Another Excited Atom"] P5 --> P6["Four Coherent Photons
Geometric Multiplication"] P6 --> P7["Mirror Reflection
Continues the Cascade"] P7 --> P1 style P1 fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900 style P4 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900 style P6 fill:#66ff66,stroke:#006600,color:#fff style P7 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000 ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Schematic: Two-Level vs Three-Level vs Four-Level ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph TwoLevel T1[Ground] -.No lasing possible.-> T2[Excited] end subgraph ThreeLevel S1[Ground] -->|Pump| S3[Pump Level] S3 -->|Fast decay| S2[Metastable] S2 -->|Laser photon
HARD inversion| S1 end subgraph FourLevel F1[Ground] -->|Pump| F4[Pump Level] F4 -->|Fast decay| F3[Metastable] F3 -->|Laser photon
EASY inversion| F2[Lower Laser] F2 -->|Fast decay| F1 end ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Population Inversion. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Model Answer:** Population inversion is a non-equilibrium condition in a laser medium in which the number density of atoms in the excited (upper) energy level exceeds the number density of atoms in the lower energy level, i.e., $N_2 > N_1$. In thermal equilibrium, the Boltzmann distribution ensures $N_2 < N_1$. Achieving $N_2 > N_1$ requires an external **pumping mechanism** and a **metastable state** to accumulate atoms at the upper level. Population inversion is the *fundamental prerequisite* for light amplification by stimulated emission, because only then does the rate of stimulated emission exceed the rate of absorption. ### Question 2: What is a Metastable State? Why is it Essential for Lasing? [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Model Answer:** A metastable state is an excited electronic energy level of an atom that has an unusually long lifetime (typically $\tau \sim 10^{-3}$ s) compared to ordinary excited states ($\tau \sim 10^{-8}$ s). It is "metastable" because the transition to the lower state is *forbidden* by quantum mechanical selection rules (e.g., $\Delta l = 0$ for $S \to S$ transitions). **Essential for lasing because:** 1. Atoms pumped to higher levels rapidly decay to the metastable state, where they "linger." 2. This accumulation makes $N_2 > N_1$ (population inversion) practically achievable. 3. Without a metastable state, atoms would decay too quickly to allow population inversion to build up. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022] **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain Einstein's coefficients $A_{21}$, $B_{12}$, and $B_{21}$. Derive the relation $\dfrac{A_{21}}{B_{21}} = \dfrac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3}$. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the conditions necessary for sustained laser action. With a neat energy level diagram, explain the working of a four-level laser system. --- ### **Model Solution for Question A:** #### Part (a) — Einstein's Coefficients and Their Relation **Einstein's Three Coefficients:** Einstein introduced three coefficients to describe the interaction of radiation with matter: 1. **$A_{21}$ — Spontaneous Emission Coefficient:** Represents the probability per unit time that an excited atom in state $E_2$ spontaneously drops to $E_1$, emitting a photon of energy $h\nu = E_2 - E_1$. The rate is $R_{sp} = A_{21} N_2$. The emitted photon has random phase and direction. 2. **$B_{12}$ — Stimulated Absorption Coefficient:** Represents the probability per unit time per unit spectral energy density that an atom in state $E_1$ absorbs a photon and is excited to $E_2$. The rate is $R_{ab} = B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu)$. 3. **$B_{21}$ — Stimulated Emission Coefficient:** Represents the probability per unit time per unit spectral energy density that an incident photon stimulates an excited atom in $E_2$ to drop to $E_1$, emitting a second photon *identical* in phase, frequency, direction, and polarization. The rate is $R_{se} = B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$. **Derivation of the Relation:** At thermal equilibrium, the rate of upward transitions (absorption) equals the rate of downward transitions (spontaneous + stimulated emission): $$B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu) = A_{21} N_2 + B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$$ Rearranging: $$\rho(\nu) \left[ B_{12} N_1 - B_{21} N_2 \right] = A_{21} N_2$$ $$\rho(\nu) = \frac{A_{21} N_2}{B_{12} N_1 - B_{21} N_2} = \frac{A_{21}}{B_{12} \frac{N_1}{N_2} - B_{21}}$$ Using the Boltzmann distribution $N_1 / N_2 = (g_1/g_2) e^{h\nu/k_B T}$: $$\rho(\nu) = \frac{A_{21}}{B_{12} \cdot \dfrac{g_1}{g_2} e^{h\nu/k_B T} - B_{21}}$$ Multiplying numerator and denominator by $(g_2 / B_{12})$: $$\rho(\nu) = \frac{A_{21} (g_2 / B_{12})}{g_1 e^{h\nu/k_B T} - (B_{21} g_2 / B_{12})}$$ Setting $B_{12} g_1 = B_{21} g_2$ (Einstein's second relation, derived from thermodynamic consistency): $$\rho(\nu) = \frac{A_{21} g_2 / B_{12}}{g_1 \left( e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1 \right)}$$ Comparing with Planck's law: $$\rho(\nu) = \frac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h\nu/k_B T} - 1}$$ We get: $$\boxed{\frac{A_{21}}{B_{21}} = \frac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3}}$$ This shows that the ratio of spontaneous to stimulated emission probability scales as $\nu^3$ — explaining why optical lasers (high $\nu$) require careful design, while microwave masers (low $\nu$) lase more easily. #### Part (b) — Conditions for Sustained Laser Action & Four-Level System **The Three Essential Conditions:** 1. **Population Inversion ($N_2 > N_1$):** A non-equilibrium state where the upper laser level is more populated than the lower level. This is achieved through *pumping* (optical, electrical, or chemical). 2. **Metastable Upper Level:** The atoms in the upper laser level must have a long lifetime so that they accumulate. Otherwise, atoms decay too fast for inversion to build up. 3. **Optical Resonator Cavity:** Two mirrors (one fully reflective, one partially reflective) provide *positive feedback* by reflecting stimulated photons back through the medium, triggering more stimulated emission. A small fraction escapes through the partial mirror as the laser output. **Additional Condition — Threshold Pump Power:** The gain provided by the inverted population must exceed all losses (internal absorption and mirror transmission). This defines the lasing threshold. **Working of a Four-Level Laser System:** (See energy level diagram in Section 4.2.) - **Level 1 (Ground State):** Atoms are initially in this state. ($N_1$ is large.) - **Level 4 (Pump State):** External pumping (e.g., flash lamp for solid-state lasers, electrical discharge for gas lasers) excites atoms from Level 1 to Level 4. - **Level 4 → Level 3:** Atoms in Level 4 undergo *fast, non-radiative* decay to Level 3 (the metastable upper laser level). - **Level 3 (Metastable):** Atoms accumulate here due to its long lifetime. This is the **upper laser level**. - **Level 3 → Level 2 (Stimulated Emission):** This is the **laser transition**. A photon of energy $h\nu = E_3 - E_2$ triggers the emission of an identical photon. This is the *coherent* laser light. - **Level 2 → Level 1:** Level 2 (the lower laser level) is *above* the ground state but has a very short lifetime. Atoms quickly drop to Level 1 (non-radiative decay), keeping $N_2 \approx 0$ at all times. **Why four-level is superior to three-level:** Since $N_2 \approx 0$ always, even a tiny population in Level 3 creates inversion. Thus, the threshold pump power is **very low**, and **continuous-wave (CW) operation** is easily achieved. Examples: He-Ne laser (632.8 nm red), Nd:YAG laser (1064 nm infrared), CO₂ laser (10.6 µm far-infrared). --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative):** [KTU University Exam - May 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** What is population inversion? Explain why it cannot be achieved in a simple two-level system. How is it achieved using a three-level pumping scheme? **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the construction and working of a Ruby laser (three-level system) with a neat energy level diagram. State its wavelength and applications. --- ### **Model Solution for Question B:** #### Part (a) — Population Inversion and Its Achievability **Definition:** Population inversion is a non-equilibrium condition in which the number of atoms in the excited (higher) energy level $E_2$ is *greater* than the number of atoms in the lower energy level $E_1$, i.e., $N_2 > N_1$. **Why a Two-Level System Fails:** In thermal equilibrium, the Boltzmann distribution gives: $$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{g_2}{g_1} e^{-(E_2 - E_1)/k_B T} < 1$$ So $N_2 < N_1$ always. If we pump atoms from Level 1 to Level 2, the stimulated absorption rate $B_{12} N_1 \rho(\nu)$ increases, but so does the stimulated emission rate $B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$. The two are equal when $N_2 = N_1$ (assuming equal degeneracies), and **saturation** occurs. The system reaches $N_2 = N_1$ at best — *never* $N_2 > N_1$. Therefore, **sustained lasing is impossible in a pure two-level system**. **Achieving Inversion via Three-Level Pumping:** The three-level scheme introduces a *third* level (a metastable state) above the ground state: - **Level 1:** Ground state (initially all atoms here, $N_1 \approx N_{\text{total}}$). - **Level 3:** Pump state (high-energy short-lived state). - **Level 2:** Metastable state (long-lived, intermediate energy). **Pumping process:** 1. External pump (flash lamp) excites atoms from Level 1 → Level 3. 2. Atoms in Level 3 undergo *fast, non-radiative* decay to Level 2. 3. Because Level 2 is metastable ($\tau \sim 10^{-3}$ s), atoms accumulate there. 4. As pumping continues, $N_2$ grows while $N_1$ shrinks. When $N_2 > N_1$, **population inversion is achieved**. 5. The lasing transition is $E_2 \to E_1$ (back to ground state). **Drawback:** Since the lower laser level is the ground state, more than **50% of the atoms** must be pumped out of Level 1 before inversion is reached. This requires *very high pump power*, making three-level lasers inefficient and difficult to operate continuously. #### Part (b) — Ruby Laser: Construction and Working **Ruby Laser (Three-Level Solid-State Laser):** - **Active medium:** A cylindrical rod of *synthetic ruby*, which is aluminum oxide (Al₂O₃) doped with about **0.03% to 0.05% chromium ions (Cr³⁺)**. The Cr³⁺ ions are the actual lasing species; the Al₂O₃ crystal is the host matrix. - **Dimensions:** Typically 2–20 cm long, 0.5–2 cm in diameter. The ends are polished to optical flatness (flat to within $\lambda/10$). - **Pumping source:** A *helical xenon flash lamp* surrounds the ruby rod, providing intense white-light pulses. - **Optical resonator:** The two flat ends of the ruby rod are silvered — one end is 100% reflective, the other is ~95% reflective (the output coupler). **Energy Level Diagram (Cr³⁺ in Ruby):** - **Level 1 (⁴A₂):** Ground state of Cr³⁺ ion. - **Level 3 (⁴F₂ and ⁴F₁):** Two broad pump bands (green and violet absorption). - **Level 2 (²E):** Metastable state, actually consisting of two closely-spaced sublevels $\bar{E}$ and $2\bar{A}$ separated by $\Delta E \approx 29 \ \text{cm}^{-1}$. **Working:** 1. **Optical Pumping:** The xenon flash lamp excites Cr³⁺ ions from the ground state (⁴A₂) to the broad pump bands (⁴F₂ and ⁴F₁). 2. **Non-radiative Decay:** Excited ions quickly drop (in $\sim 10^{-8}$ s) to the metastable state ²E via non-radiative phonon emission. 3. **Population Inversion:** Because ²E has a long lifetime ($\tau \approx 3 \times 10^{-3}$ s), ions accumulate. When $N_{^{2}E} > N_{^{4}A_2}$, inversion is achieved. 4. **Stimulated Emission (Lasing):** Spontaneously emitted photons trigger stimulated emission from ²E to ⁴A₂. The emitted photon has energy corresponding to the ²E → ⁴A₂ transition. 5. **Output:** The photons bounce back and forth between the mirrors, amplifying. A coherent beam exits through the partial mirror. **Wavelength:** The Ruby laser emits at **694.3 nm** (red light), corresponding to the $R_1$ line ($^{2}E \to {}^{4}A_2$ transition). **Applications:** - **Pulsed holography** (the first laser used for holography) - **Military range-finders and target designators** - **Medical procedures** (tattoo removal, dermatology) - **Scientific research** (the first working laser, demonstrated by Maiman in 1960) - **Q-switching** for generating *giant pulses* (nanosecond durations, megawatt peak power) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse spontaneous and stimulated emission rates.** Spontaneous ($A_{21} N_2$) is independent of $\rho(\nu)$; stimulated ($B_{21} N_2 \rho(\nu)$) depends on it. (2-mark penalty if swapped.) > 2. **The lasing transition in a 3-level system ends at the GROUND state**, not at an intermediate level. (1-mark penalty if wrong.) > 3. **Always mention the metastable state** when explaining population inversion — it is the *enabling mechanism*. (1-mark penalty if missing.) > 4. **Do not forget the optical resonator** when listing the three conditions. Many students list only population inversion and metastable state. (2-mark penalty.) > 5. **In the threshold gain derivation**, ensure the factor is $\frac{1}{2L}$ (for round trip), not $\frac{1}{L}$ (one-way). (3-mark penalty if wrong.) --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Summary — Read Before the Exam:** - **LASER** = Light Amplification by **S**timulated **E**mission of **R**adiation. - **Three Einstein coefficients:** $A_{21}$ (spontaneous), $B_{21}$ (stimulated emission), $B_{12}$ (absorption). - **Einstein's two key relations:** $\dfrac{A_{21}}{B_{21}} = \dfrac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3}$ and $g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}$. - **Stimulated emission** produces coherent photons — *this* is the quantum heart of the laser. - **Population inversion** $N_2 > N_1$ is *non-thermal* and requires a **metastable state**. - **Two-level systems CANNOT lase** — only 3-level and 4-level systems can. - **Three-level systems** (e.g., Ruby) need >50% atoms pumped; hard to achieve continuous operation. - **Four-level systems** (e.g., He-Ne, Nd:YAG) have an intermediate lower laser level → easy inversion, low threshold, CW operation possible. - **Three conditions for sustained lasing:** (1) Population inversion, (2) Metastable state, (3) Optical resonator cavity. - **Optical resonator** = Fabry–Pérot cavity with $R_1 = 1$ and $R_2 < 1$; condition $L = q \lambda / 2$. - **Threshold gain:** $G_{\text{th}} = \alpha + \dfrac{1}{2L} \ln \left( \dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2} \right)$. - **Threshold condition** = gain ≥ total losses (internal + mirror transmission). - **Properties of laser light:** (1) Monochromatic, (2) Directional, (3) Coherent, (4) High intensity/brightness. - **Ruby laser wavelength:** 694.3 nm (red); invented by **Theodore Maiman** in 1960. - **He-Ne laser wavelength:** 632.8 nm (red), four-level system, common in labs. - **Nd:YAG laser:** 1064 nm (infrared), four-level, used in surgery and industry. - **CO₂ laser:** 10.6 µm (far-infrared), high power, used in cutting and welding. - **Pumping methods:** Optical (flash lamp, another laser), Electrical (gas discharge), Chemical, Nuclear. - **Population inversion formula** (when $g_1 = g_2$): $N_2 > N_1$ — for non-degenerate levels. - **Boltzmann population ratio** (always at equilibrium): $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{g_2}{g_1} e^{-\Delta E / k_B T}$ — *less than 1*. > **End of Module 4 Notes — Principle of Laser, Conditions for Sustained Lasing, Population Inversion**

topics.title: Pumping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pumping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Pumping

In the context of laser physics and photonics, **Pumping** is the process of supplying energy from an external source to the active medium (lasing material) in order to excite its atoms, ions, or molecules from their lower energy states to higher energy states. The primary goal of pumping is to create a condition called **Population Inversion**, where the number of particles in the higher energy level exceeds the number of particles in the lower energy level. Without this non-equilibrium state, **stimulated emission** — the fundamental mechanism behind laser action — cannot dominate over absorption.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *Physics for Electrical Science*, pumping is treated as the *first and most critical prerequisite* for achieving coherent light generation, and is foundational to the operation of devices such as **LASERs (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation)** and **optical amplifiers** used in fiber-optic communication systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Pumping is the *engine* of any laser. Without an effective pumping mechanism, no lasing action can occur, regardless of how perfect the optical cavity is. The KTU board expects students to clearly distinguish between the *types* of pumping and justify *why* a particular scheme is chosen for a given laser.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a multi-story water tank system:

- **Ground floor tank** = Lower energy level (E₁) of the laser medium
- **Top floor tank** = Higher energy level (E₂) of the laser medium
- **Water pump** = Pumping source (e.g., flash lamp, electric current)

In *natural* conditions, the lower tank is always fuller (more atoms in the ground state — this is **Boltzmann equilibrium**). To get a laser beam out, you need the *top tank* to have **more water than the bottom tank** — which is unnatural, like water flowing *uphill*. The pump does this job by forcefully lifting water (energy) to the top floor.

> **Real-world example:** A **flash lamp** in a ruby laser is like a giant camera flash that delivers a sudden, intense burst of light. The atoms of the ruby crystal absorb this light energy and jump to a higher energy state, just like rainwater being pumped uphill into a reservoir.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are repeatedly used in pumping-related derivations and are **bold-marked** for emphasis:

- **Planck's constant: h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- **Speed of light in vacuum: c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Boltzmann constant: k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **Threshold pump power (P_th)**: The minimum pumping power required to initiate lasing action — a parameter critical to laser design.
- **Pump efficiency (η_p)**: The fraction of input pump power actually absorbed by the active medium.
- **Einstein coefficients**: A₂₁ (spontaneous emission), B₁₂ (absorption), B₂₁ (stimulated emission) — these govern the *rates* at which atomic transitions occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Valuation:** Always state the units of pumping power (Watts), energy (eV or Joules), and population (dimensionless count or atoms/m³) in your final answers. Marks are deducted if units are missing in numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Pumping is Necessary: The Population Inversion Problem

Under normal thermal equilibrium, the population of two energy levels E₁ and E₂ (with E₂ > E₁) is governed by the **Boltzmann distribution law**:

$$N_2 = N_1 \cdot e^{-\frac{(E_2 - E_1)}{k_B T}}$$

This means **N₁ >> N₂** at room temperature — most atoms sit in the lower level, and any incoming photon is more likely to be *absorbed* than to trigger stimulated emission. To overcome this, an external energy source must be used to **artificially** invert the population.

## 2.2 The Three Fundamental Requirements for Pumping

For a successful pumping process (and subsequent lasing), the following three conditions must be satisfied:

- **Metastable Upper Level:** The pumped atoms must accumulate in an energy state that has a *long lifetime* (typically 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁸ seconds). This allows the population to build up.
- **Continuous Energy Supply:** The pump must provide energy at a rate greater than the rate at which atoms decay from the upper level. This is the **threshold condition**.
- **Suitable Active Medium:** The material must have energy levels that permit population inversion with available pump sources.

## 2.3 Types / Methods of Pumping

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically emphasizes the classification of pumping methods. Each is briefly described below:

- **Optical Pumping (Photonic Pumping):** Uses an intense light source (flash lamp, another laser, LED) to excite atoms. *Example: Ruby laser, Nd:YAG laser.*
- **Electrical Pumping (Injection Pumping):** Uses an electric current to inject electrons and holes into a semiconductor junction, causing recombination that emits light. *Example: Semiconductor laser diode (GaAs).*
- **Chemical Pumping:** Energy is released by a chemical reaction. *Example: Chemical oxygen-iodine laser (COIL).*
- **Thermal Pumping:** Rapid cooling of a pre-heated gas causes population inversion. *Example: Gas-dynamic laser (CO₂).*
- **Electron Beam Pumping:** High-energy electron beam excites the lasing medium. *Example: Excimer lasers.*

## 2.4 Pumping in Two-Level, Three-Level, and Four-Level Systems

### (a) Two-Level System — *Not Used in Practice*

In a two-level system, the pump excites atoms from E₁ to E₂. However, as soon as the population reaches N₂ = N₁ (equal populations — called *saturation*), the rate of absorption equals the rate of stimulated emission, and **no net amplification occurs**. The system is said to be "transparent." Hence, **two-level lasers cannot work**.

### (b) Three-Level System — *Used in Ruby Laser*

Atoms are pumped from the ground state E₁ to the highest state E₃. They then decay *rapidly* (non-radiatively) to a metastable state E₂. Lasing occurs from E₂ → E₁. Since the lower level E₁ is the *ground state*, you must pump **more than 50%** of all atoms out of E₁ to achieve inversion — this requires very high pump power.

### (c) Four-Level System — *Used in He-Ne, CO₂, Nd:YAG Lasers*

Atoms are pumped from E₁ to E₄, decay rapidly to a metastable state E₃ (upper laser level), lase from E₃ → E₂, and the lower laser level E₂ is *rapidly depopulated* to E₁. Because E₂ is virtually empty at room temperature, only a **tiny** pump power is needed. This is the most efficient scheme.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description / Meaning** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $N_2 = N_1 \cdot e^{-\Delta E / k_B T}$ | Boltzmann population distribution (thermal equilibrium) | atoms/m³ |
| $R_{12} = B_{12} \cdot \rho(\nu)$ | Rate of stimulated absorption (E₁ → E₂) | transitions/s/m³ |
| $R_{21} = B_{21} \cdot \rho(\nu)$ | Rate of stimulated emission (E₂ → E₁) | transitions/s/m³ |
| $A_{21}$ | Rate of spontaneous emission (Einstein A coefficient) | s⁻¹ |
| $\frac{dN_2}{dt} = R_p - \frac{N_2}{\tau_{21}}$ | Rate equation for upper level under pumping | atoms/m³/s |
| $N_2 > \frac{g_2}{g_1} N_1$ | Condition for population inversion (with degeneracy g) | dimensionless |
| $P_{th} = \frac{h \nu \cdot V \cdot \Delta N}{\eta_p \cdot \tau_{sp}}$ | Threshold pump power for lasing threshold | Watts |
| $\Delta N = N_2 - \frac{g_2}{g_1} N_1$ | Population inversion density | atoms/m³ |
| $B_{12} \cdot g_1 = B_{21} \cdot g_2$ | Einstein relation between B coefficients | dimensionless |
| $A_{21} = \frac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3} B_{21}$ | Relation between Einstein A and B coefficients | s⁻¹ |
| $\tau_{sp} = \frac{1}{A_{21}}$ | Spontaneous emission lifetime of the upper level | seconds |

> **Engineering Utility:** Pumping schemes are at the heart of every modern photonics device — from barcode scanners (He-Ne laser, four-level), to fiber-optic transceivers (semiconductor lasers, electrical pumping), to medical surgery tools (CO₂ laser, electrical pumping). Choosing the right pump type determines the laser's efficiency, cost, and applicability.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Einstein's A and B Coefficient Relation (Foundation for Pumping Theory)

This derivation is the **mathematical foundation** for understanding why pumping is needed and what conditions must be satisfied.

**Step 1 — Setup in Thermal Equilibrium:**

Consider a cavity filled with atoms having two energy levels E₁ and E₂, in equilibrium with blackbody radiation of energy density $\rho(\nu)$. Three processes occur simultaneously:

- Stimulated absorption (rate ∝ $B_{12} \rho(\nu)$)
- Stimulated emission (rate ∝ $B_{21} \rho(\nu)$)
- Spontaneous emission (rate ∝ $A_{21}$)

**Step 2 — Write the Detailed Balance Equation:**

In equilibrium, the total upward transitions must equal the total downward transitions:

$$N_1 B_{12} \rho(\nu) = N_2 B_{21} \rho(\nu) + N_2 A_{21}$$

This equation states: *the rate at which atoms absorb photons (left side) equals the rate at which atoms lose energy via both stimulated and spontaneous emission (right side).*

**Step 3 — Apply the Boltzmann Distribution:**

From statistical mechanics, the populations are:

$$N_1 = N \cdot \frac{g_1}{Z} e^{-E_1 / k_B T}, \quad N_2 = N \cdot \frac{g_2}{Z} e^{-E_2 / k_B T}$$

where $g_1, g_2$ are the degeneracies and Z is the partition function. Taking their ratio:

$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{g_2}{g_1} e^{-(E_2 - E_1)/k_B T} = \frac{g_2}{g_1} e^{-h\nu / k_B T}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Rearrange:**

Insert the population ratio into the balance equation:

$$\rho(\nu) \cdot B_{12} \cdot N_1 = N_2 [B_{21} \rho(\nu) + A_{21}]$$

Divide both sides by $N_2$:

$$B_{12} \rho(\nu) \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_2} = B_{21} \rho(\nu) + A_{21}$$

Substitute $N_1/N_2$ from Step 3:

$$B_{12} \rho(\nu) \cdot \frac{g_1}{g_2} e^{h\nu / k_B T} = B_{21} \rho(\nu) + A_{21}$$

Rearrange to isolate $\rho(\nu)$:

$$\rho(\nu) \left[ \frac{g_1}{g_2} B_{12} e^{h\nu / k_B T} - B_{21} \right] = A_{21}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Planck's Law:**

Planck's law of blackbody radiation states:

$$\rho(\nu) = \frac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h\nu / k_B T} - 1}$$

For the equation in Step 4 to reduce to Planck's form, the coefficients must satisfy:

$$g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}$$

$$A_{21} = \frac{8 \pi h \nu^3}{c^3} B_{21}$$

These are the **Einstein relations** — they are *universal* and hold for *all* atomic systems, not just those in equilibrium. The second relation is the famous link that shows **spontaneous emission is fundamentally a stimulated process driven by the zero-point energy of the electromagnetic field**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Condition for Population Inversion

**Step 1 — Define Net Stimulated Emission Rate:**

The net rate of stimulated emission is the difference between stimulated emission and absorption:

$$R_{net} = (N_2 B_{21} - N_1 B_{12}) \rho(\nu)$$

**Step 2 — Apply Einstein Relation:**

From $B_{12} = (g_2/g_1) B_{21}$, substitute:

$$R_{net} = B_{21} \rho(\nu) \left[ N_2 - \frac{g_2}{g_1} N_1 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Condition for Positive Net Gain:**

For light amplification (laser action), $R_{net} > 0$:

$$N_2 - \frac{g_2}{g_1} N_1 > 0$$

$$\boxed{N_2 > \frac{g_2}{g_1} N_1}$$

This is the **fundamental condition for population inversion**. If degeneracies are equal ($g_1 = g_2$), the condition simplifies to $N_2 > N_1$ — i.e., more atoms in the upper level than the lower level. **This can NEVER occur in thermal equilibrium**, hence the need for external pumping.

## 3.3 Derivation: Threshold Pump Power for a Three-Level Laser

This is a classic KTU board problem and shows up in nearly every exam.

**Step 1 — Rate Equation for the Upper Level:**

The population $N_2$ of the upper laser level grows due to pumping and decays due to spontaneous emission (with lifetime $\tau_{sp}$):

$$\frac{dN_2}{dt} = R_p - \frac{N_2}{\tau_{sp}}$$

where $R_p$ is the pumping rate (atoms/m³/s).

**Step 2 — Steady-State Condition:**

At steady state, $dN_2/dt = 0$, so:

$$N_2 = R_p \cdot \tau_{sp}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Threshold Condition for Three-Level System:**

In a three-level laser, the threshold for inversion requires $N_2 = N_1 = N_{total}/2$ (assuming equal degeneracy). Therefore:

$$\frac{N_{total}}{2} = R_{p,th} \cdot \tau_{sp}$$

$$R_{p,th} = \frac{N_{total}}{2 \tau_{sp}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Pump Power:**

Each pump photon has energy $h \nu_p$, and the pump efficiency is $\eta_p$. Hence:

$$P_{th} = \frac{R_{p,th} \cdot h \nu_p \cdot V}{\eta_p} = \frac{N_{total} \cdot h \nu_p \cdot V}{2 \eta_p \tau_{sp}}$$

where V is the volume of the active medium.

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

This shows that the threshold power is *inversely proportional* to the spontaneous lifetime $\tau_{sp}$ — a longer-lived metastable state dramatically reduces the pump power needed. This is why **metastable states are the holy grail of laser design**.

## 3.4 Comparative Analysis: Pumping Power Required (Three-Level vs. Four-Level)

| **Parameter** | **Three-Level System** | **Four-Level System** |
|---|---|---|
| Lower laser level | Ground state E₁ (highly populated) | Excited state E₂ (nearly empty) |
| Atoms to be pumped | $> 50\%$ of total | Very small fraction |
| Threshold pump power | **Very high** | **Low** (factor of 10–100× lower) |
| Efficiency | Low (5–10%) | High (20–50%) |
| Example | Ruby laser | He-Ne, CO₂, Nd:YAG |
| CW operation | Difficult | Easily possible |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The four-level system is the *preferred* design for continuous-wave (CW) lasers because the lower laser level is essentially unpopulated at room temperature. The ruby laser (three-level) can only be operated in *pulsed* mode because the ground state is initially full and must be depleted before inversion is achieved.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Level Diagram: Three-Level Pumping System (Ruby Laser)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the energy transitions involved in a typical three-level laser pumping cycle:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E1["E1 Ground State (Lower Laser Level)"]:::ground
    E3["E3 Pump Band (Upper Excited State)"]:::pump
    E2["E2 Metastable State (Upper Laser Level)"]:::meta
    L1["Stimulated Emission: Photon of Energy h nu = E2 minus E1"]:::laser

    E1 -- "Absorption: Pump Photon Energy h nu_p" --> E3
    E3 -- "Fast Non-Radiative Decay" --> E2
    E2 -- "Spontaneous and Stimulated Emission" --> L1
    L1 -- "Atom Returns to Ground" --> E1

    classDef ground fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pump fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#e67e22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef meta fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef laser fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#2980b9,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Energy Level Diagram: Four-Level Pumping System (He-Ne Laser)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1["E1 Ground State"]:::ground
    F4["E4 Pump Band (Highest Excited State)"]:::pump
    F3["E3 Metastable Upper Laser Level"]:::meta
    F2["E2 Lower Laser Level (Nearly Empty)"]:::lower
    FL["Laser Photon h nu = E3 minus E2"]:::laser

    F1 -- "Pump Absorption h nu_p" --> F4
    F4 -- "Fast Non-Radiative Decay" --> F3
    F3 -- "Stimulated Emission" --> FL
    FL -- "Atom Lands in E2" --> F2
    F2 -- "Rapid Non-Radiative Decay to Ground" --> F1

    classDef ground fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef pump fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#e67e22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef meta fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef lower fill:#f0e5ff,stroke:#8e44ad,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef laser fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#2980b9,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Pumping Subsystem in a Laser System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PS["Pump Source\nFlash Lamp / Diode / Current Source"]:::pumpSource
    COU["Coupling Optics\nLens / Reflector Cavity"]:::couple
    AM["Active Medium\nRuby / He-Ne / Semiconductor"]:::active
    OC["Optical Cavity\nTwo Mirrors with R close to 1 and R less than 1"]:::cavity
    OUT["Coherent Laser Output Beam"]:::output

    PS -- "Pump Energy In" --> COU
    COU -- "Focused Energy" --> AM
    AM -- "Stimulated Emission" --> OC
    OC -- "Amplified Light" --> OUT
    OC -. "Feedback" .-> AM

    classDef pumpSource fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#e67e22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef couple fill:#e8daef,stroke:#8e44ad,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef active fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cavity fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#2980b9,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Conceptual Flow: Population Dynamics Under Continuous Pumping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Pump Energy Switched ON"]:::start
    ABS["Atoms Excited from E1 to E3"]:::abs
    DEC["Non-Radiative Decay to E2"]:::dec
    ACC["Population Accumulates in Metastable E2"]:::acc
    CHK{"Is N2 greater than threshold N_th?"}:::check
    INV["Population Inversion Achieved"]:::inv
    LASER["Lasing Action Begins"]:::laser
    NOINV["No Lasing, More Pumping Required"]:::noinv

    START --> ABS --> DEC --> ACC --> CHK
    CHK -- Yes --> INV --> LASER
    CHK -- No --> NOINV
    NOINV -. "Increase Pump Power" .-> START

    classDef start fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#27ae60,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef abs fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#e67e22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#d68910,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef acc fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#2874a6,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef check fill:#f4ecf7,stroke:#6c3483,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef inv fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#1e8449,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef laser fill:#f1948a,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef noinv fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read these diagrams in your answer sheet (KTU 2024 Exam Tip):** Always label the **E1, E2, E3, E4** levels in ascending order of energy. Use *horizontal arrows* for radiative transitions (absorption/emission) and *wavy arrows* for non-radiative transitions. This is the standard KTU board convention and earns full marks in diagrams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define pumping in the context of laser action. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

Pumping is the process of supplying external energy to the active medium of a laser to excite its atoms from a lower energy level to a higher energy level. The objective of pumping is to create **population inversion**, a non-equilibrium condition in which the number of atoms in the higher energy level exceeds the number in the lower energy level. Without population inversion, stimulated emission cannot dominate over absorption, and laser action cannot occur. Common pumping methods include **optical pumping** (using flash lamps), **electrical pumping** (using current injection in semiconductors), and **chemical pumping**.

> **Marking Note (3 marks):** Definition (1 mark) + Objective: population inversion (1 mark) + At least one example of pumping method (1 mark).

---

### Question 2: Why is a two-level laser system not feasible in practice? [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

In a two-level system, atoms are pumped from the ground state E₁ to the excited state E₂. As pumping continues, the population of E₂ increases while that of E₁ decreases. The system reaches a condition called **saturation** when $N_2 = N_1$ (equal populations). At this point, the rate of absorption exactly equals the rate of stimulated emission, resulting in zero net amplification. To achieve $N_2 > N_1$ (population inversion), one would need an infinite pump power. Therefore, **two-level lasers cannot work in practice**, and real lasers use three-level or four-level systems where a metastable state allows accumulation of population in the upper laser level.

> **Marking Note (3 marks):** Saturation condition N₂ = N₁ (1 mark) + Zero net gain explanation (1 mark) + Need for metastable level (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1): Pumping Mechanisms and Population Inversion

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain with necessary energy level diagrams, the pumping mechanism in a **three-level laser system** (e.g., Ruby laser). Derive the condition for population inversion. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Energy Level Description:** A three-level laser system consists of:
- **E₁**: Ground state (lower laser level)
- **E₂**: Metastable state (upper laser level)
- **E₃**: Pump band (high-energy excited state)

**Step 2 — Pumping Cycle:**
1. Atoms in E₁ absorb pump photons of energy $h \nu_p = E_3 - E_1$ and are excited to E₃ (transition 1).
2. Atoms in E₃ decay *rapidly* and *non-radiatively* (within ~10⁻⁹ s) to the metastable state E₂ (transition 2).
3. Atoms accumulate in E₂ because the metastable state has a long lifetime (~10⁻³ s).
4. When $N_2 > N_1$, lasing occurs via stimulated emission from E₂ to E₁ (transition 3), emitting a photon of energy $h \nu = E_2 - E_1$.

**Step 3 — Derivation of Population Inversion Condition:**

The rate of stimulated absorption from E₁ to E₂ is:

$$R_{12} = B_{12} \rho(\nu) N_1$$

The rate of stimulated emission from E₂ to E₁ is:

$$R_{21} = B_{21} \rho(\nu) N_2$$

The rate of spontaneous emission from E₂ to E₁ is $A_{21} N_2$.

For **net amplification** (laser action), stimulated emission must exceed absorption:

$$B_{21} N_2 > B_{12} N_1$$

Using the Einstein relation $g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}$ (assuming equal degeneracy $g_1 = g_2$, so $B_{12} = B_{21}$):

$$N_2 > N_1$$

This is the **population inversion condition** for a non-degenerate three-level system. Since E₁ is the ground state with a large initial population, more than 50% of all atoms must be pumped out of E₁, requiring **high threshold pump power**.

**Step 4 — Threshold Condition:**

At threshold, $N_2 = N_1 = N_{total}/2$. The required pumping rate is:

$$R_{p,th} = \frac{N_{total}}{2 \tau_{sp}}$$

where $\tau_{sp}$ is the spontaneous lifetime of the metastable level. The corresponding pump power is:

$$P_{th} = \frac{N_{total} \cdot h \nu_p \cdot V}{2 \eta_p \tau_{sp}}$$

> **Marks Distribution:** Diagram (2 marks) + Step-by-step pumping cycle (2 marks) + Derivation of inversion condition (2 marks) + Threshold formula (1 mark).

---

**(b)** With a neat energy level diagram, describe the working of a **four-level laser system** (e.g., He-Ne laser). Compare its performance with a three-level system. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Energy Levels:**
- **E₁**: Ground state
- **E₂**: Lower laser level (just above ground, very short lifetime ~10⁻⁸ s)
- **E₃**: Metastable upper laser level (long lifetime ~10⁻³ s)
- **E₄**: Pump band (high-energy excited state)

**Step 2 — Pumping Cycle:**
1. Atoms in E₁ absorb pump energy and are excited to E₄.
2. Atoms in E₄ undergo fast non-radiative decay to the metastable state E₃.
3. Population accumulates in E₃ because of its long lifetime.
4. Lasing transition: E₃ → E₂, emitting photon of energy $h\nu = E_3 - E_2$.
5. Atoms in E₂ decay *very rapidly* and non-radiatively to E₁, ensuring E₂ remains virtually empty.

**Step 3 — Population Inversion Condition:**

Since E₂ is empty at room temperature, even a small population in E₃ is enough to achieve $N_3 > N_2$. The threshold condition is:

$$N_3 > N_2 \approx 0$$

This is **trivially satisfied** for even modest pump powers.

**Step 4 — Comparison with Three-Level System:**

| **Aspect** | **Three-Level** | **Four-Level** |
|---|---|---|
| Threshold power | Very high | Low |
| Efficiency | Poor | High |
| CW operation | Difficult | Easy |
| Example | Ruby (694 nm) | He-Ne (632.8 nm), CO₂ (10.6 μm) |
| Lower level population | Large (ground state) | Negligible (rapid depopulation) |

**Conclusion:** Four-level lasers are **far more efficient** and are the preferred design for continuous-wave operation. The Ruby laser, being three-level, is restricted to pulsed operation.

> **Marks Distribution:** Diagram (2 marks) + Cycle description (2 marks) + Inversion condition (1 mark) + Comparison table (2 marks).

---

### Question B (Option 2): Threshold Pump Power and Rate Equations

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the **threshold pump power** for a four-level laser system, clearly stating the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rate Equation for Upper Laser Level (E₃):**

The rate of change of population in the upper level is:

$$\frac{dN_3}{dt} = R_p - \frac{N_3}{\tau_{32}}$$

where $R_p$ is the pump rate (atoms/m³/s) and $\tau_{32}$ is the lifetime of the upper level.

**Step 2 — Steady-State:**

At steady state, $dN_3/dt = 0$:

$$N_3 = R_p \cdot \tau_{32}$$

**Step 3 — Threshold Condition for Four-Level System:**

In a four-level laser, the lower level E₂ is essentially empty. Hence threshold is reached as soon as $N_3$ becomes a small positive value. Taking the threshold inversion as $N_{th}$:

$$R_{p,th} \cdot \tau_{32} = N_{th}$$

$$R_{p,th} = \frac{N_{th}}{\tau_{32}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Pump Power:**

Each absorbed pump photon has energy $h\nu_p$. The pump power absorbed is:

$$P_{abs} = R_{p,th} \cdot h\nu_p \cdot V$$

Accounting for the pump efficiency $\eta_p$ (the fraction of input power actually absorbed):

$$P_{th} = \frac{R_{p,th} \cdot h \nu_p \cdot V}{\eta_p} = \frac{N_{th} \cdot h \nu_p \cdot V}{\eta_p \cdot \tau_{32}}$$

**Step 5 — Key Assumptions:**
- Single-mode operation
- No losses in the optical cavity
- Homogeneous broadening of the laser line
- The lower level E₂ has infinite depopulation rate (so $N_2 = 0$)

**Step 6 — Comparison with Three-Level:**

For a three-level system, the threshold is:

$$P_{th}^{(3)} = \frac{N_{total} \cdot h\nu_p \cdot V}{2 \eta_p \tau_{sp}}$$

For a four-level system:

$$P_{th}^{(4)} = \frac{N_{th} \cdot h\nu_p \cdot V}{\eta_p \tau_{32}}$$

Since $N_{th} \ll N_{total}/2$, we get $P_{th}^{(4)} \ll P_{th}^{(3)}$, confirming that four-level lasers need much less pump power.

> **Marks Distribution:** Rate equation setup (2 marks) + Steady-state solution (1 mark) + Threshold derivation (2 marks) + Assumptions and final formula (2 marks).

---

**(b)** A **Ruby laser** has the following specifications: lasing wavelength $\lambda = 694.3$ nm, spontaneous lifetime $\tau_{sp} = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ s, total chromium ion density $N_{total} = 1.58 \times 10^{25}$ m⁻³, pump wavelength $\lambda_p = 550$ nm, and pump efficiency $\eta_p = 0.5$. The active medium volume is $V = 10^{-6}$ m³. Calculate the **threshold pump power** for this laser. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Lasing wavelength: $\lambda = 694.3$ nm $\Rightarrow \nu = c/\lambda = (3 \times 10^8) / (694.3 \times 10^{-9}) = 4.32 \times 10^{14}$ Hz
- Pump wavelength: $\lambda_p = 550$ nm $\Rightarrow \nu_p = (3 \times 10^8) / (550 \times 10^{-9}) = 5.45 \times 10^{14}$ Hz
- $\tau_{sp} = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ s
- $N_{total} = 1.58 \times 10^{25}$ m⁻³
- $\eta_p = 0.5$
- $V = 10^{-6}$ m³

**Formula (Three-Level Threshold):**

$$P_{th} = \frac{N_{total} \cdot h \nu_p \cdot V}{2 \eta_p \tau_{sp}}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $h \nu_p$:**

$$h \nu_p = (6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \times (5.45 \times 10^{14}) = 3.61 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Numerator:**

$$N_{total} \cdot h \nu_p \cdot V = (1.58 \times 10^{25}) \times (3.61 \times 10^{-19}) \times (10^{-6})$$

$$= 1.58 \times 3.61 \times 10^{25 - 19 - 6} = 5.70 \times 10^{0} = 5.70 \text{ W (J/s)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Denominator:**

$$2 \eta_p \tau_{sp} = 2 \times 0.5 \times 3 \times 10^{-3} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Final Calculation:**

$$P_{th} = \frac{5.70}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.9 \times 10^{3} \text{ W} = 1900 \text{ W}$$

**Answer:** The threshold pump power for the Ruby laser is approximately **1900 W (1.9 kW)**.

**Interpretation:** This is a very high pump power — characteristic of three-level systems — which is why Ruby lasers require pulsed flash lamps. A four-level system with similar parameters would require only a few watts.

> **Marks Distribution:** Formula selection (2 marks) + Intermediate step $h\nu_p$ (1 mark) + Numerator and denominator (2 marks) + Final numerical answer with units (1 mark) + Comment on interpretation (1 mark).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mistakes:** Many students forget to convert nm to m before computing frequency. Always write: $\nu = c/\lambda$ with $\lambda$ in **meters**, not nm. Marks lost: 1–2.
> 2. **Confusing Three-Level vs. Four-Level Formula:** The factor of **2 in the denominator** appears *only* for three-level systems. For four-level, the formula is $P_{th} = N_{th} h \nu_p V / (\eta_p \tau_{sp})$ *without* the factor of 2. Mixing these up is a common KTU board mistake — 2 marks deducted.
> 3. **Missing Pump Efficiency $\eta_p$:** If the problem mentions $\eta_p$ or "useful fraction," include it in the denominator. Forgetting it loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Forgetting the Metastable Lifetime $\tau_{sp}$:** This is *not* the same as the lasing transition lifetime. Use the metastable level's spontaneous lifetime in the threshold formula.
> 5. **No Energy Level Diagram:** KTU board examiners *expect* a diagram in any pumping-related question. Even a neatly drawn box diagram earns 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Pumping** = supplying external energy to a laser medium to achieve **population inversion**.
- **Population inversion** = $N_2 > (g_2/g_1) N_1$ (or simply $N_2 > N_1$ for equal degeneracy).
- **Two-level systems do NOT work** — they saturate at $N_2 = N_1$ and give zero net gain.
- **Three-level lasers** (Ruby, 694.3 nm) need *more than 50%* of atoms pumped out of the ground state — *very high threshold power*.
- **Four-level lasers** (He-Ne 632.8 nm, CO₂ 10.6 μm, Nd:YAG 1064 nm) have an almost empty lower level — *low threshold power*, *high efficiency*.
- **Einstein relations** (must be memorized): $g_1 B_{12} = g_2 B_{21}$ and $A_{21} = (8\pi h \nu^3 / c^3) B_{21}$.
- **Metastable state** is essential — long lifetime (10⁻³ to 10⁻⁸ s) allows atoms to accumulate in the upper level.
- **Threshold pump power formula** (Three-level): $P_{th} = N_{total} h\nu_p V / (2 \eta_p \tau_{sp})$.
- **Threshold pump power formula** (Four-level): $P_{th} = N_{th} h\nu_p V / (\eta_p \tau_{32})$.
- **Types of pumping to remember:** Optical, Electrical (Injection), Chemical, Thermal, Electron-beam.
- **Rate equation for upper level** under continuous pumping: $N_2 = R_p \cdot \tau_{sp}$ (steady state).
- **Spontaneous lifetime** $\tau_{sp} = 1/A_{21}$.
- **Always include units** in numerical answers (Hz, W, J, m, m³, atoms/m³).
- **Always draw an energy level diagram** for any 7-mark question on pumping.
- **Boltzmann distribution** governs *thermal* populations — pumping violates this to achieve inversion.
- **Stimulated emission rate** $\propto B_{21} \rho(\nu)$; **spontaneous rate** $\propto A_{21}$ (independent of $\rho$).
- The **Planck relation** $A_{21}/B_{21} = 8\pi h \nu^3 / c^3$ shows that *spontaneous emission increases rapidly with frequency* — explains why X-ray lasers are harder to build than IR lasers.

> **Final Note:** For the KTU 2024 Scheme, expect at least one 7-mark question from this topic every semester. Master the energy-level diagrams and the threshold formula — these alone cover 70% of the marks in the exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metastable States
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Metastable States

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Metastable State** is a discrete excited energy level of an atom, ion, or molecule whose lifetime is **abnormally long** (typically ranging from **10⁻³ seconds to several seconds**, and in some cases up to **hours or years**) compared to ordinary excited states, which decay by spontaneous emission in about **10⁻⁸ seconds**.

In rigorous quantum mechanical terms, a metastable state is an energy eigenstate from which all electric dipole (E1) transitions to **lower-lying states are forbidden by the quantum mechanical selection rules**. Since the dipole transition probability is the dominant decay mechanism, the absence of an allowed dipole pathway forces the atom to rely on much slower higher-order processes such as **magnetic dipole (M1)** transitions, **electric quadrupole (E2)** transitions, or **collisional de-excitation**, thereby extending the lifetime dramatically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Metastable states are the *physical foundation* of all laser action. Without them, achieving **population inversion** (a non-thermal distribution where more atoms exist in a higher energy state than a lower one) would be virtually impossible using optical pumping alone.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **ball sitting on a small shelf inside a deep well**.

* An **ordinary excited state** is like a ball balanced on the *rim* of the well. It is unstable and rolls back down almost immediately (in ~10⁻⁸ s).
* A **metastable state** is like the ball having dropped onto a *small intermediate ledge* halfway down the well. It is genuinely lower in energy than the rim, so it wants to fall further, but the ledge is small and has a tricky geometry — the ball gets "stuck" there for a long time before eventually finding a way to roll all the way down.

Because the ball lingers on the ledge, we can **accumulate many balls on that ledge** by pouring more in from above. Once we have more balls on the ledge than on the floor of the well, we have a **population inversion** — the prerequisite for **stimulated emission** and **laser action**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> Key benchmark numbers for KTU numerical problems and MCQs:
>
> | Metric | Typical Excited State | Metastable State |
> |---|---|---|
> | Lifetime (τ) | ~**10⁻⁸ s** | **10⁻³ s** to **10⁰ s** (or longer) |
> | Decay channel | Allowed electric dipole (E1) | Forbidden — M1, E2, collisions |
> | Transition probability (A) | ~**10⁸ s⁻¹** | ~**10³ to 10⁻³ s⁻¹** |
> | Selection rule status | Δl = ±1 satisfied | Δl = 0, ±2 (forbidden) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Metastable States Exist — The Quantum Selection Rules

The allowedness of an electric dipole transition between two quantum states $i$ and $f$ is governed by the **electric dipole transition matrix element**:

$$
\langle f \mid \hat{\mu} \mid i \rangle = \int \psi_f^{*} \, (e \mathbf{r}) \, \psi_i \, d\tau
$$

A transition is **allowed** only if this integral is *non-zero*. This imposes the **Laporte selection rule** and the resulting constraints on quantum numbers:

$$
\Delta l = \pm 1, \quad \Delta m_l = 0, \pm 1, \quad \Delta j = 0, \pm 1 \quad (j = 0 \to j' = 0 \text{ forbidden}), \quad \Delta s = 0
$$

where $l$ is the orbital angular momentum quantum number, $m_l$ its projection, $j$ the total angular momentum, and $s$ the spin quantum number.

When an excited state has **no lower state** for which all of these rules are *simultaneously* satisfied, the dipole transition probability vanishes to first order. The state is then **metastable**.

## 2.2 The Three-Step Logic of Metastability

1. **Excitation (Pumping):** An atom in the ground state absorbs a photon (or collides with an energetic particle) and is promoted to a high-energy "pump" state. This state has a *short* lifetime (~$10^{-8}$ s).
2. **Rapid Non-Radiative Decay to the Metastable Level:** The pump state decays — usually via a fast non-radiative or allowed radiative transition — into the metastable level. This step happens so quickly that the metastable level **fills up like a reservoir**.
3. **Slow Forbidden Decay:** From the metastable level, the atom cannot decay by a fast dipole transition. It must wait for a much slower M1/E2 transition or a collision. During this waiting time, **atoms accumulate**, allowing population inversion to build up if a lower "laser lower state" exists.

## 2.3 Population Inversion — The Role of Metastable States

The steady-state population of an energy level $E_n$ under pumping rate $R$ and total decay rate $A_n^{\text{total}}$ is:

$$
N_n = \frac{R}{A_n^{\text{total}}}
$$

For an ordinary excited state, $A_n^{\text{total}} \approx 10^8 \text{ s}^{-1}$, so $N_n$ remains tiny. For a metastable state, $A_n^{\text{total}} \approx 10^0$–$10^3 \text{ s}^{-1}$, so $N_n$ becomes **8 to 10 orders of magnitude larger** for the same pumping rate. This enormous enhancement is what makes laser operation feasible.

The **population inversion condition** between an upper laser level (2) and lower laser level (1) is:

$$
N_2 > N_1
$$

Achieving this in thermal equilibrium is impossible (Boltzmann distribution always gives $N_1 > N_2$), so we use metastable states to artificially "trap" atoms in level 2.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Einstein A coefficient (spontaneous emission rate) | $A_{21} = \dfrac{\omega^3 \vert \mu_{21} \vert^2}{3\pi \varepsilon_0 \hbar c^3}$ | Sets the natural linewidth $\Delta \nu = A_{21}/(2\pi)$ |
| 2 | Radiative lifetime of an excited state | $\tau = \dfrac{1}{A_{21}}$ | For metastable: $\tau \gg 10^{-8}$ s |
| 3 | Energy of metastable level above ground | $E_m = h \nu_m$ | Determines pump photon energy |
| 4 | Population inversion threshold | $N_2 - N_1 \ge \dfrac{1}{\sigma L}$ | $\sigma$ = stimulated emission cross-section, $L$ = cavity length |
| 5 | Boltzmann population (no inversion possible in equilibrium) | $\dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = e^{-(E_2 - E_1)/k_B T} < 1$ | Shows why non-equilibrium pumping is mandatory |
| 6 | Forbidden transition parity rule | Parity **must change** for E1: $\pi_i \cdot \pi_f = -1$ | States of same parity cannot undergo dipole decay |
| 7 | Critical pump rate for inversion | $R_{cr} = \dfrac{N_{\text{total}}}{\tau_m}$ | $\tau_m$ = metastable lifetime |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never use the `|` character inside any KTU table cell.** Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. Example: write $\vert \mu_{21} \vert^2$ rather than $\vert\mu_{21}\vert^2$ to avoid markdown table parsing failures.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

* **Helium-Neon (He-Ne) laser:** The $^1S_0$ and $^3S_1$ states of helium are metastable and resonantly transfer energy to neon — the textbook example used in KTU textbooks.
* **Ruby laser:** The $^2E$ state of Cr³⁺ in Al₂O₃ is metastable (lifetime ~**3 ms**), enabling the famous 694.3 nm red laser line.
* **Nd:YAG solid-state laser:** The $^4F_{3/2}$ metastable level of Nd³⁺ produces 1064 nm output, used in industrial cutting, range finding, and medical surgery.
* **Gas discharge tubes and fluorescent lamps:** Mercury metastable states (6³P₀, 6³P₂) are responsible for the efficient 254 nm UV emission that excites the phosphor coating.
* **Atomic clocks (Cs, Rb standards):** The hyperfine ground-state splitting is effectively metastable, allowing the long coherence times that define the SI second.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lifetime of an Excited State from the Einstein A Coefficient

We derive the explicit relationship between the spontaneous emission rate and the lifetime, which is the heart of the metastability concept.

**Step 1 — Start with the time-dependent population of an excited level undergoing only spontaneous emission.**

Let $N_2(t)$ be the number of atoms in the excited level 2 at time $t$. Each atom has a probability $A_{21} \, dt$ of decaying to level 1 in a small time interval $dt$. Therefore, the rate of loss of excited atoms is:

$$
\frac{dN_2}{dt} = -A_{21} \, N_2(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Recognise this as a first-order linear differential equation and solve.**

Separating variables and integrating from $t = 0$ (when $N_2 = N_0$) to time $t$:

$$
\int_{N_0}^{N_2(t)} \frac{dN_2}{N_2} = -A_{21} \int_0^t dt
$$

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{N_2(t)}{N_0}\right) = -A_{21} \, t
$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate to obtain the explicit decay law.**

$$
N_2(t) = N_0 \, e^{-A_{21} \, t}
$$

**Step 4 — Define the mean (radiative) lifetime as the time at which the population falls to $1/e$ of its initial value.**

By definition, $N_2(\tau) = N_0/e$, so:

$$
e^{-A_{21}\tau} = e^{-1} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \tau = \frac{1}{A_{21}}
$$

This is the **defining equation** of lifetime. *Logic check:* the larger $A_{21}$ (i.e., the faster the allowed decay), the shorter the lifetime. Conversely, in a metastable state, the *allowed* $A_{21} \approx 0$, so the lifetime is dominated by the much smaller $A_{21}^{\text{forbidden}}$ (M1 or E2), giving $\tau_m \gg 10^{-8}$ s.

## 3.2 Derivation: Population Inversion Threshold in a Two-Level Laser Medium

**Step 1 — Write the threshold condition for net optical gain in a laser cavity of length $L$ with mirror reflectivity $R$.**

The gain per pass must equal the losses per pass:

$$
R_1 R_2 \, e^{2(\gamma - \alpha)L} = 1
$$

where $\gamma$ is the gain coefficient and $\alpha$ is the internal loss coefficient. Taking logarithms:

$$
\gamma_{\text{th}} = \alpha + \frac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Relate the gain coefficient to the population difference.**

The gain coefficient at frequency $\nu$ for a transition with line-shape function $g(\nu)$ is:

$$
\gamma(\nu) = (N_2 - N_1) \, \frac{\lambda^2}{8\pi n^2 \tau_{\text{sp}}} \, g(\nu)
$$

where $n$ is the refractive index of the medium and $\tau_{\text{sp}}$ is the spontaneous lifetime of the upper level.

**Step 3 — Equate the gain at line centre ($g(\nu_0) = 1/\Delta\nu$) to the threshold gain.**

$$
(N_2 - N_1)_{\text{th}} = \frac{8\pi n^2 \tau_{\text{sp}}}{\lambda^2} \left[ \alpha + \frac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right) \right] \Delta\nu
$$

**Step 4 — Critical inversion in terms of metastable lifetime.**

Because the upper laser level *is* the metastable level, $\tau_{\text{sp}} = \tau_m$ (the metastable lifetime). The required threshold is therefore *inversely* sensitive to $\tau_m$ — the longer the metastable lifetime, the *easier* it is to reach threshold for a given pump power. This is the central engineering reason metastable states are indispensable.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Metastable States

| Atom/Ion | Metastable Level | Configuration | Lifetime | Laser Wavelength Produced |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Helium (He) | $2\,^1S_0$, $2\,^3S_1$ | $1s2s$ | $\sim 10^{-2}$ s (forbidden to $1s^2$) | — (transfers to Ne) |
| Neon (Ne) | $3s, 2s$ (Paschen/IUPAC) | $2p^5 3s$ | $\sim 10^{-7}$ to $10^{-8}$ s (quasi-meta) | 632.8 nm (He-Ne) |
| Cr³⁺ in Ruby | $^2E$ | $3d^3$ | **3 ms** | 694.3 nm |
| Nd³⁺ in YAG | $^4F_{3/2}$ | $4f^3$ | **230 µs** | 1064 nm |
| Argon (Ar⁺) | $3p^4 4s$ | metastable lower | $\sim 10^{-9}$ s (quasi) | 488.0, 514.5 nm |
| CO₂ | $00^01$ bending mode | vibrational | **several ms** | 10.6 µm |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Energy Flow in a Metastable-State Laser

The diagram below maps the *functional flow of energy and population* during laser pumping, accumulation, and stimulated emission. It avoids any restricted Mermaid keywords and uses only alphanumeric node IDs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[GROUND STATE Level 0] -->|Optical Pumping h nu_pump| A1[PUMP STATE Level 3 short lifetime 10 ns]
    A1 -->|Fast Allowed E1 Decay| A2[METASTABLE STATE Level 2 lifetime ms to s]
    A2 -->|SLOW FORBIDDEN M1 or E2 Decay| A3[LOWER LASER LEVEL Level 1]
    A3 -->|Fast Non Radiative Decay| A0
    A2 -->|STIMULATED EMISSION photon h nu_laser| A3
    A3 -.->|Replenishes ground| A0
    PUMP[Pumping Source Flashlamp or Discharge] -. Provides energy .-> A1
    CAV1[Optical Cavity Mirror 1 High R] -. Reflects photons .-> A2
    CAV2[Output Coupler Mirror 2 Partial R] -. Transmits laser beam .-> OUT[COHERENT LASER OUTPUT]
    A2 -. Accumulation Reservoir .-> INV[POPULATION INVERSION N2 greater than N1]
    INV -. Achieved when .-> A2
    OUT[COHERENT LASER OUTPUT]
```

## 4.2 Schematic Block Diagram — Four-Level vs Three-Level Laser Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FOURLEVEL[FOUR LEVEL LASER]
        F0[Ground 0] -->|Pump| F3[Level 3 Pump]
        F3 -->|Fast decay| F2[Level 2 Metastable Upper Laser]
        F2 -->|Stimulated Emission| F1[Level 1 Lower Laser]
        F1 -->|Fast decay| F0
    end
    subgraph THREELEVEL[THREE LEVEL LASER]
        T0[Ground 0 also Lower Laser] -->|Pump| T3[Level 3 Pump]
        T3 -->|Fast decay| T2[Level 2 Metastable Upper Laser]
        T2 -->|Stimulated Emission| T0
    end
    FOURLEVEL --- NOTE[Four level is easier to invert because Level 1 empties rapidly to Ground]
    THREELEVEL --- NOTE2[Three level needs more than fifty percent inversion because Lower Level equals Ground]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In both schematics, the *thickness* of the implicit population is what we care about. The metastable level (Level 2) is the *storage reservoir* in both cases. In the three-level system, the lower laser level *is* the ground state, which is already heavily populated at room temperature — this is why three-level lasers (like ruby) need very intense pumping. Four-level lasers (like He-Ne and Nd:YAG) sidestep this by having a quickly-emptied lower laser level (Level 1), making population inversion dramatically easier.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024 Similar]`
**Define a metastable state. Why is it essential for laser action?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A metastable state is an excited energy level of an atom whose lifetime is unusually long, typically **10⁻³ s to several seconds**, because transitions from it to lower-lying levels are *forbidden* by the electric dipole selection rules (such as $\Delta l = 0, \pm 2$ instead of $\Delta l = \pm 1$).

It is essential for laser action because **(1 mark)** it acts as a *reservoir* in which excited atoms can accumulate, and **(1 mark)** this accumulation enables **population inversion** ($N_2 > N_1$) between the metastable upper laser level and a lower level. **(1 mark)** Without metastable states, atoms would decay from the pump level almost as fast as they are excited, and stimulated emission could never overcome absorption to produce net optical gain.

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Similar]`
**State the electric dipole selection rules. Explain how violation of these rules leads to metastability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The principal electric dipole (E1) selection rules are: **(1 mark)**

$$
\Delta l = \pm 1, \quad \Delta m_l = 0, \pm 1, \quad \Delta j = 0, \pm 1 \; (0 \to 0 \text{ forbidden}), \quad \Delta s = 0
$$

An excited state whose wavefunction has the *same parity* as all lower-lying states, and for which no lower state simultaneously satisfies $\Delta l = \pm 1$ and the other rules, has a *vanishing* electric dipole matrix element. **(1 mark)** Such a state cannot decay by the dominant dipole mechanism; it must decay by much weaker magnetic dipole (M1) or electric quadrupole (E2) transitions, with transition probabilities lower by factors of $10^4$ to $10^{10}$. **(1 mark)** This gives rise to lifetimes of milliseconds to seconds — i.e., metastability.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**(a)** With a neat energy level diagram, explain the principle and working of a **Helium-Neon (He-Ne) laser**. Discuss the role of metastable states in achieving population inversion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A He-Ne laser operates at **632.8 nm** with a cavity length of **50 cm** and one mirror of reflectivity 100% and the other 95%. The neon upper laser level has a spontaneous lifetime of **$1.7 \times 10^{-7}$ s** and the linewidth is **1.5 GHz**. The refractive index of the gain medium is $n = 1.0$. Calculate the **threshold population inversion per unit volume**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**1. Energy levels of He and Ne.** Helium has two metastable levels — $2\,^1S_0$ (singlet) at 20.61 eV and $2\,^3S_1$ (triplet) at 19.82 eV above the ground state. **(1 mark)**

**2. Pumping.** A high-voltage discharge excites helium atoms from the ground state $1\,^1S_0$ into these metastable levels via electron impact. **(1 mark)**

**3. Resonant energy transfer.** The $2\,^1S_0$ He level is nearly coincident with the neon $5s$ level, and the $2\,^3S_1$ He level is nearly coincident with the neon $4s$ level. Because both He levels are metastable, excited He atoms live long enough to collide with ground-state Ne atoms and *resonantly transfer* their excitation energy. **(1 mark)**

**4. Population of Ne upper laser level.** The neon $5s$ and $4s$ levels thus become heavily populated. They are also quasi-metastable (slow dipole decay to lower levels). **(1 mark)**

**5. Lower laser level.** The lower laser levels are the $3p$ and $2p$ neon states. They have very short lifetimes (~$10^{-8}$ s) and rapidly decay to the ground state, keeping them nearly empty. **(1 mark)**

**6. Population inversion.** Because the upper laser level (5s or 4s) fills rapidly via He → Ne transfer, and the lower level empties rapidly to the ground state, the condition $N(\text{upper}) > N(\text{lower})$ is easily achieved. **(1 mark)**

**7. Lasing.** A photon of wavelength 632.8 nm (from the $5s \to 3p$ transition) stimulates further emission, and the optical cavity formed by two mirrors (one 100% and one partially transmitting) selects and amplifies this wavelength into a coherent beam. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Use the threshold inversion formula derived in Section 3.2:**

$$
(N_2 - N_1)_{\text{th}} = \frac{8\pi n^2 \tau_{\text{sp}}}{\lambda^2} \left[ \alpha + \frac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right) \right] \Delta\nu
$$

**Step 2 — Assume internal loss $\alpha \approx 0$ (negligible for gas laser).** Then:

$$
(N_2 - N_1)_{\text{th}} = \frac{8\pi n^2 \tau_{\text{sp}}}{\lambda^2} \cdot \frac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right) \cdot \Delta\nu
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the given numerical values.**

* $n = 1.0$
* $\tau_{\text{sp}} = 1.7 \times 10^{-7}$ s
* $\lambda = 632.8 \times 10^{-9}$ m
* $L = 0.50$ m
* $R_1 = 1.00$, $R_2 = 0.95$
* $\Delta\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{9}$ Hz

**Step 4 — Evaluate the mirror loss term:**

$$
\frac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right) = \frac{1}{2(0.50)}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1.00 \times 0.95}\right) = \frac{1}{1.00} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.95}\right) = 1.00 \times 0.05129 = 0.05129 \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the wavelength factor:**

$$
\frac{8\pi n^2 \tau_{\text{sp}}}{\lambda^2} = \frac{8\pi (1)^2 (1.7 \times 10^{-7})}{(632.8 \times 10^{-9})^2} = \frac{8\pi \times 1.7 \times 10^{-7}}{4.004 \times 10^{-13}}
$$

$$
= \frac{4.273 \times 10^{-6}}{4.004 \times 10^{-13}} = 1.067 \times 10^{7} \text{ s/m}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Combine everything:**

$$
(N_2 - N_1)_{\text{th}} = 1.067 \times 10^{7} \times 0.05129 \times 1.5 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
= 1.067 \times 10^{7} \times 7.694 \times 10^{7} = 8.21 \times 10^{14} \text{ m}^{-3}
$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**

$$
\boxed{(N_2 - N_1)_{\text{th}} \approx 8.2 \times 10^{14} \text{ atoms/m}^3}
$$

This is the *minimum* inversion per unit volume needed to overcome the 5% transmission loss at the output coupler and sustain laser oscillation.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A) — 14 Marks
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Expected Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **population inversion** with the aid of an energy level diagram. Why is a four-level laser system preferred over a three-level system? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ruby laser has a metastable upper level with lifetime **$\tau = 3 \text{ ms}$** and emits at **694.3 nm**. The cavity has length **10 cm** and both mirrors have reflectivity **98%**. The linewidth of the transition is **330 GHz** and the refractive index is **1.76**. Estimate the **minimum pump power per unit volume** required to maintain threshold inversion. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**1. Definition of population inversion.** Under thermal equilibrium, the Boltzmann distribution always gives $N_1 > N_2$ for any two levels with $E_2 > E_1$. Population inversion is the *non-equilibrium* condition $N_2 > N_1$. **(1 mark)**

**2. Two-level system — impossible.** In a true two-level system, optical pumping cannot achieve inversion because the pump-induced upward transitions are exactly cancelled by stimulated downward transitions as soon as $N_2$ approaches $N_1$. **(1 mark)**

**3. Three-level system.** Ground state (0) → pump level (3) [fast decay] → metastable upper laser level (2) → lower laser level (1) which is the *ground state*. The lower laser level has a huge thermal population, so more than 50% of the total atoms must be pumped to level 2 to achieve inversion. **(2 marks)**

**4. Four-level system.** Add an extra level 1 between the lower laser level and the ground state, with a *very fast* non-radiative decay $1 \to 0$. This keeps level 1 essentially empty, so even a small population in level 2 yields inversion. **(2 marks)**

**5. Why four-level is preferred.** Lower threshold pump power, continuous-wave (CW) operation is feasible, and less waste heat — leading to higher efficiency. **(1 mark)**

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Re-use the threshold inversion formula:**

$$
(N_2 - N_1)_{\text{th}} = \frac{8\pi n^2 \tau_{\text{sp}}}{\lambda^2} \cdot \frac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right) \cdot \Delta\nu
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in the values.**

* $n = 1.76$
* $\tau_{\text{sp}} = 3 \times 10^{-3}$ s
* $\lambda = 694.3 \times 10^{-9}$ m
* $L = 0.10$ m
* $R_1 = R_2 = 0.98$
* $\Delta\nu = 330 \times 10^{9}$ Hz

**Step 3 — Mirror loss term:**

$$
\frac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right) = \frac{1}{0.20}\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.98^2}\right) = 5.0 \times \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{0.9604}\right) = 5.0 \times 0.04041 = 0.2020 \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Wavelength factor:**

$$
\frac{8\pi n^2 \tau_{\text{sp}}}{\lambda^2} = \frac{8\pi (1.76)^2 (3 \times 10^{-3})}{(694.3 \times 10^{-9})^2} = \frac{8\pi \times 3.098 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{4.821 \times 10^{-13}}
$$

$$
= \frac{8\pi \times 9.295 \times 10^{-3}}{4.821 \times 10^{-13}} = \frac{0.2335}{4.821 \times 10^{-13}} = 4.844 \times 10^{11} \text{ s/m}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$
(N_2 - N_1)_{\text{th}} = 4.844 \times 10^{11} \times 0.2020 \times 330 \times 10^{9}
$$

$$
= 4.844 \times 10^{11} \times 6.667 \times 10^{10} = 3.23 \times 10^{22} \text{ m}^{-3}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to minimum pump rate per unit volume.** The minimum pump rate per unit volume must replenish the metastable state at a rate equal to the loss rate from it. At threshold, the pump rate $R_{p,\text{th}}$ must satisfy:

$$
R_{p,\text{th}} \geq \frac{(N_2 - N_1)_{\text{th}}}{\tau_m} = \frac{3.23 \times 10^{22}}{3 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.08 \times 10^{25} \text{ m}^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 7 — Convert to pump *power* per unit volume using photon energy $E_p \approx h\nu_p$.** If the pump photons have wavelength ~550 nm (typical xenon flashlamp green), $E_p = hc/\lambda_p$:

$$
E_p = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{550 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.614 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}
$$

$$
P_{\text{pump,th}} = R_{p,\text{th}} \times E_p = 1.08 \times 10^{25} \times 3.614 \times 10^{-19} = 3.90 \times 10^{6} \text{ W/m}^3
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{P_{\text{pump,th}} \approx 3.9 \times 10^{6} \text{ W/m}^3 \;\; \text{(i.e., about 3.9 kW per litre of ruby)}}
$$

This huge power density requirement is precisely why three-level ruby lasers are typically operated in pulsed mode with very intense flashlamp excitation, whereas four-level lasers like He-Ne or Nd:YAG can run continuously.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit mistakes on $\Delta\nu$:** Students often write linewidth in nm instead of Hz. *Always* convert nm $\to$ Hz using $\Delta\nu = c \Delta\lambda / \lambda^2$ before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** The $1/(2L)$ term comes from the round-trip gain in a Fabry–Pérot cavity. Omitting the factor of 2 gives an answer off by exactly a factor of 2 — a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Mixing $A_{21}$ and $\tau$:** Some students write $\tau = A_{21}$ instead of $\tau = 1/A_{21}$. The lifetime is the *inverse* of the Einstein coefficient. Examiners will deduct.
> 4. **Selection rule violation:** When asked *why* a state is metastable, merely stating "forbidden transition" is incomplete (2 marks). You must *list* the selection rules and show *which one* is violated (full 3 marks).
> 5. **Confusing three-level and four-level diagrams:** In the four-level diagram, the lower laser level is *not* the ground state. Drawing it as the ground state loses 2 marks immediately.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Metastable States**
