* A **metastable state** is a *long-lived* excited state (τ ≈ **10⁻³ s – 10⁰ s**) because its decay to lower levels is **forbidden** by the electric dipole selection rules $\Delta l = \pm 1$, $\Delta m_l = 0, \pm 1$, $\Delta j = 0, \pm 1$.
* Metastable states decay only via weak **magnetic dipole (M1)** or **electric quadrupole (E2)** transitions, or via **collisions**, with rates lower by factors of $10^4$ to $10^{10}$.
* They are the **energy reservoir** that makes **population inversion** possible in any laser system.
* **Population inversion** is the *non-thermal* condition $N_2 > N_1$ — impossible under Boltzmann equilibrium.
* **Three-level lasers** (e.g., ruby) use the ground state as the lower laser level — *hard* to invert, need > 50% excitation, operate pulsed.
* **Four-level lasers** (e.g., He-Ne, Nd:YAG) have a fast-emptying lower laser level *separate* from the ground state — *easy* to invert, can operate CW.
* The **threshold population inversion** scales as $(N_2 - N_1)_{\text{th}} \propto \tau_{\text{sp}} \Delta\nu / (L \lambda^2) \cdot \ln(1/R_1R_2)$.
* **Longer metastable lifetime ⇒ lower threshold pump power** (engineering rule of thumb).
* He-Ne laser uses **resonant collisional energy transfer** from He metastable states to Ne — textbook KTU example.
* Cr³⁺ in ruby: metastable $^2E$ state, lifetime **3 ms**, emission at **694.3 nm** (red).
* Nd³⁺ in YAG: metastable $^4F_{3/2}$ state, lifetime **230 µs**, emission at **1064 nm** (infrared).
* **Parity rule:** Electric dipole transitions require a *change* in parity ($\pi_i \pi_f = -1$). Same-parity states are dipole-forbidden, contributing to metastability.
* **Remember the units:** lifetime in seconds, wavelength in metres, linewidth in Hz — never mix them up in the threshold formula.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Components of Laser - Active Medium - Optical Resonant Cavity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basic Components of Laser - Active Medium - Optical Resonant Cavity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔦 Module 4: Laser Physics — Basic Components of a Laser

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a LASER?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LASER** stands for **Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation**. A laser is a device that produces a highly directional, monochromatic, coherent, and intense beam of light through a process of optical amplification based on the stimulated emission of photons from an excited atomic system.

A laser is fundamentally different from ordinary light sources. The word *LASER* in Malayalam engineering textbooks is treated as a noun describing the device as well as the beam it produces. The defining properties of laser radiation are:

- **Monochromaticity** – extremely narrow spectral bandwidth (single wavelength).
- **Coherence** – both temporal (long coherence length) and spatial coherence.
- **Directionality** – extremely low beam divergence (nearly parallel rays).
- **High Intensity / Brightness** – power concentrated into a tiny cross-section and narrow bandwidth.

### 1.2 The Three Basic Components of a Laser

Any laser system, irrespective of its type (Ruby, He–Ne, CO₂, semiconductor), consists of **three indispensable building blocks**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of a Laser System**
> 1. **Active (Gain) Medium** – the material that amplifies light by stimulated emission.
> 2. **Optical Resonant Cavity (Resonator)** – provides positive optical feedback and selects the mode of oscillation.
> 3. **Pumping Source (Energy Pump)** – supplies external energy to achieve population inversion.

Think of these three as an inseparable triangle: removing any one collapses the laser action.

### 1.3 Active Medium — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Active Medium (Gain Medium):** A specially chosen material (solid, liquid, gas, or semiconductor) containing atoms, ions, or molecules that can be excited to higher energy states, and within which **population inversion** and **stimulated emission** can be sustained to provide optical gain at the lasing wavelength.

The active medium is the "heart" of the laser — without it, no photons can be amplified. It must possess at least one **metastable state** (a long-lived excited state, typically lifetime ~10⁻³ s) where excited atoms can accumulate.

#### Examples of Active Media

| Type | Example | Lasing Wavelength |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| Solid (doped crystal) | Ruby (Al₂O₃ : Cr³⁺) | **694.3 nm** (red) |
| Solid (doped crystal) | Nd:YAG (Y₃Al₅O₁₂ : Nd³⁺) | **1064 nm** (IR) |
| Gas | Helium–Neon (He–Ne) | **632.8 nm** (red) |
| Gas | Carbon dioxide (CO₂) | **10.6 μm** (far IR) |
| Liquid (dye) | Rhodamine 6G in methanol | tunable 570–650 nm |
| Semiconductor | GaAs (Gallium Arsenide) | **850–900 nm** (IR) |

### 1.4 Optical Resonant Cavity — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optical Resonant Cavity (Fabry–Pérot Resonator):** An arrangement of two mirrors (one highly reflective, R₁ ≈ 99.9 %; the other partially transmissive, R₂ ≈ 95–98 %) placed parallel to each other at the two ends of the active medium, which traps the photons and forces them to pass repeatedly through the gain medium, thereby providing optical feedback and selecting specific resonant frequencies.

#### Conceptual Analogy — The "Whispering Tunnel"

Imagine a long, narrow, perfectly straight hallway with a mirror on each end. You clap your hands once. Most of the sound dissipates into the walls, but a tiny fraction travels back and forth between the two mirrors, getting slightly amplified each time it passes a special "amplifying zone" in the middle. If the hallway length happens to "fit" an exact number of half-wavelengths, the reflections add constructively — building a strong, steady, in-step hum. The partially silvered mirror at one end lets a fraction of this built-up wave escape as the laser beam.

**In this analogy:**
- The hallway = optical cavity
- The amplifying zone = active medium
- The clap = pumping energy
- The partially silvered mirror = output coupler
- The steady hum = laser beam

### 1.5 Pumping Source — Brief Note

> [!NOTE]
> The pumping source is *not* a "basic component" in the strict optical sense, but it is the **energy input** required to achieve population inversion. Pumping can be **optical** (flash lamp, another laser), **electrical** (gas discharge, current injection in semiconductors), or **chemical**.

| Laser Type | Typical Pump |
|------------|--------------|
| Ruby laser | Xenon flash lamp (optical) |
| He–Ne laser | Electrical gas discharge |
| Semiconductor laser | Forward-biased current injection |

### 1.6 Physical Constants Relevant to Laser Cavities

- Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
- Planck's constant: **h = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- Boltzmann constant: **k_B = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Role of the Active Medium — Step-by-Step Logic

The active medium is selected based on the following **functional requirements**:

- **Step 1 — Existence of suitable energy levels:** The material must have at least two well-defined energy levels with a transition that produces photons in the desired wavelength range (typically in the visible, IR, or UV).
- **Step 2 — Metastable upper laser level:** The upper laser level must have a **long radiative lifetime** (a metastable state, τ ≈ 10⁻³ s to 10⁻⁶ s) so that atoms can accumulate there. The lower level must decay rapidly (lifetime ~10⁻⁸ s).
- **Step 3 — Achievable population inversion:** Pumping must be strong enough to push more than half the atoms into the upper state: **N₂ > N₁** (against the natural Boltzmann distribution).
- **Step 4 — Optical gain exceeds losses:** The gain per unit length *g* must satisfy **g × L ≥ losses**, where *L* is the cavity length.
- **Step 5 — Pumping wavelength compatibility:** The material must efficiently absorb pump radiation at the wavelength emitted by the pump source.

### 2.2 Three-Level vs Four-Level Laser Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three-Level System (e.g., Ruby laser):**
> Ground state (E₁) → Pump band (E₃) → Metastable upper laser level (E₂) → Lower laser level is the **ground state (E₁)**.
> Difficult to achieve population inversion because more than 50 % of ground-state atoms must be pumped up — requires very intense pumping.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four-Level System (e.g., He–Ne, Nd:YAG, CO₂):**
> Ground state (E₀) → Pump band (E₃) → Metastable upper laser level (E₂) → Lower laser level (E₁) → quickly decays to ground state E₀.
> Much easier to achieve population inversion because the lower laser level is essentially empty at thermal equilibrium — only a few % of ground-state atoms need to be excited.

### 2.3 Role of the Optical Resonant Cavity — Step-by-Step Logic

The cavity is not a passive container; it actively *shapes* the laser beam. Its functions are:

- **Step 1 — Optical feedback:** The two mirrors reflect photons back into the active medium, allowing each photon to stimulate the emission of additional photons many times, producing a chain-reaction amplification.
- **Step 2 — Mode selection (frequency selectivity):** Only those wavelengths that form standing waves inside the cavity survive — this is the resonance condition. All other wavelengths interfere destructively and die out.
- **Step 3 — Directionality:** Photons travelling exactly along the cavity axis are reflected back and forth; off-axis photons walk out of the cavity after a few bounces. The output beam is therefore nearly parallel.
- **Step 4 — Threshold reduction:** Cavity feedback dramatically reduces the pumping power required to reach threshold compared to a single-pass amplifier.
- **Step 5 — Coherence enhancement:** The standing-wave pattern enforces a fixed phase relationship, producing temporal and spatial coherence.

### 2.4 Types of Optical Resonators

| Resonator Type | Mirror Geometry | Stability | Typical Use |
|----------------|-----------------|-----------|-------------|
| Planar–Planar (Fabry–Pérot) | Two flat mirrors | Marginally stable (sensitive to alignment) | He–Ne lab lasers |
| Confocal | Two spherical mirrors, R = L, foci coincide at centre | Highly stable | Gas lasers |
| Concentric (Spherical) | Two mirrors, R = L/2, centres coincide | Marginally stable | Low-gain systems |
| Plano-Concave | One flat, one concave (R > L) | Stable | Most practical lasers |
| Confocal Unstable | Magnifying telescope geometry | Unstable (used for high-power) | CO₂ industrial lasers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Condition for a Two-Mirror Cavity:**
> A cavity is stable only if the paraxial ray remains bounded after many round trips. For mirrors of radii of curvature R₁ and R₂ separated by distance L:
> **0 ≤ g₁ · g₂ ≤ 1**, where **gᵢ = 1 − L/Rᵢ**.

### 2.5 Longitudinal and Transverse Modes

- **Longitudinal modes** differ in frequency along the optical axis (different values of integer *q*).
- **Transverse modes (TEM_{mn})** differ in the field distribution across the beam cross-section, with integers *m*, *n* ≥ 0. The lowest-order mode is **TEM_{00}** — a perfect Gaussian beam.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula you must memorise for the KTU 2024 University Exam on this topic. Master this thoroughly.**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|--------------------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | Resonant wavelength | $\lambda_q = \dfrac{2L}{q}$ | Wavelengths that form standing waves; *q* = integer (mode number) |
| 2 | Resonant frequency | $\nu_q = \dfrac{q \, c}{2L}$ | Allowed oscillation frequencies |
| 3 | Mode spacing (Free Spectral Range) | $\Delta \nu = \dfrac{c}{2L}$ | Frequency difference between two adjacent longitudinal modes |
| 4 | Number of longitudinal modes in a gain bandwidth $\Delta \nu_{\text{gain}}$ | $N_{\text{modes}} = \dfrac{\Delta \nu_{\text{gain}}}{\Delta \nu} = \dfrac{2L \, \Delta \nu_{\text{gain}}}{c}$ | Integer part only |
| 5 | Cavity stability | $0 \le g_1 g_2 \le 1$, with $g_i = 1 - L/R_i$ | Required for bounded rays |
| 6 | Quality factor (Q-factor) | $Q = \dfrac{\nu_0}{\Delta \nu_{\text{cav}}} = 2\pi \, \nu_0 \, \tau_p$ | Sharpness of resonance; $\tau_p$ = photon lifetime in cavity |
| 7 | Photon lifetime | $\tau_p = \dfrac{L}{c \, (1 - R)} \quad \text{(approx. for } R = \sqrt{R_1 R_2}\text{)}$ | Time photon remains in cavity |
| 8 | Threshold gain condition | $g_{\text{th}} L = -\dfrac{1}{2} \ln(R_1 R_2)$ | Round-trip gain must equal round-trip losses |
| 9 | Round-trip phase condition | $\phi = 2 \pi \, q \quad \Rightarrow \quad 2 L = q \lambda$ | Constructive interference |
| 10 | Coherence length | $L_{\text{coh}} = \dfrac{c}{\Delta \nu}$ | Inversely related to spectral width |
| 11 | Coherence time | $\tau_{\text{coh}} = \dfrac{1}{\Delta \nu}$ | Inverse of spectral bandwidth |
| 12 | Beam divergence (full angle) | $\theta = \dfrac{\lambda}{\pi w_0}$ | For TEM_{00} Gaussian beam; $w_0$ = waist radius |
| 13 | Spot size at waist | $w_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\lambda L R}{2 \pi}}$ | For symmetric cavity (R = radius of curvature) |

> **Note:** In the table above, $L$ is the **cavity length** and $R_i$ is the **radius of curvature** of mirror *i*. Always use the same physical units on both sides of any equation (typically metres for L, Hz for ν, seconds for τ).

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical fibre communication** — semiconductor lasers (InGaAsP) with carefully designed Fabry–Pérot or DFB cavities form the backbone of long-distance data transmission.
- **Medical surgery (LASIK, dermatology)** — CO₂ and Er:YAG lasers require stable cavities producing 10.6 µm and 2.94 µm beams.
- **Industrial cutting & welding** — high-power CO₂ lasers use unstable resonators to extract maximum power.
- **LIDAR & 3D scanning** — narrow-linewidth, single-mode lasers rely on the mode-selection property of the cavity.
- **Holography & interferometry** — long-coherence-length He–Ne lasers owe their coherence to a well-stabilised cavity.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Expectation:** When a derivation is asked (e.g., "Derive the condition for optical resonant cavity"), you must show **every single algebraic step** with a one-line physical explanation. Skipping a step is the most common reason for losing 2–3 marks. Follow the structure below religiously.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Resonant Condition of an Optical Cavity

### Statement
The optical length of the cavity must contain an **integral number of half-wavelengths** so that a standing wave is established.

### Given
- Cavity length = $L$
- Wavelength of light inside the cavity = $\lambda$
- Speed of light in the medium = $c$ (assumed vacuum, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s)

### Derivation

**Step 1 —** Consider a photon travelling from mirror M₁ to mirror M₂ (both highly reflective) and back. The round-trip distance covered is:

$$
D_{\text{rt}} = 2L
$$

**Step 2 —** For the wave to interfere constructively with itself after one round trip, the round-trip optical path length must equal an integer number of wavelengths:

$$
2L = q \, \lambda
$$

where $q = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$ is a positive integer called the **longitudinal mode number**.

**Step 3 —** Solving for the wavelength:

$$
\lambda_q = \frac{2L}{q}
$$

**Step 4 —** The corresponding frequency (using $\nu = c / \lambda$):

$$
\nu_q = \frac{c}{\lambda_q} = \frac{c \, q}{2L} = \frac{q \, c}{2L}
$$

**Step 5 —** Two adjacent longitudinal modes ($q$ and $q+1$) have a frequency separation:

$$
\Delta \nu = \nu_{q+1} - \nu_q = \frac{(q+1) c}{2L} - \frac{q \, c}{2L} = \frac{c}{2L}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** This separation is called the **Free Spectral Range (FSR)** or **longitudinal mode spacing**. It is the *fundamental* frequency scale of the cavity. For $L = 0.5$ m, $\Delta \nu = 3 \times 10^8 / (2 \times 0.5) = 3 \times 10^8$ Hz = **300 MHz**.

> **Conclusion Box:** A cavity of length $L$ supports only those frequencies $\nu_q = q c / (2L)$. The mode spacing is $\Delta \nu = c / (2L)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Longitudinal Modes within the Gain Bandwidth

### Statement
The number of oscillating longitudinal modes is limited by the **gain bandwidth** $\Delta \nu_{\text{gain}}$ of the active medium.

### Given
- Gain bandwidth of the medium = $\Delta \nu_{\text{gain}}$ (e.g., for Ruby ≈ 330 GHz; for He–Ne ≈ 1.5 GHz; for Nd:YAG ≈ 120 GHz)
- Mode spacing = $\Delta \nu = c / (2L)$

### Derivation

**Step 1 —** The total number of modes that can be sustained is the integer part of the ratio of the gain bandwidth to the mode spacing:

$$
N_{\text{modes}} = \left\lfloor \frac{\Delta \nu_{\text{gain}}}{\Delta \nu} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 2 —** Substituting $\Delta \nu = c / (2L)$:

$$
N_{\text{modes}} = \left\lfloor \frac{2 L \, \Delta \nu_{\text{gain}}}{c} \right\rfloor
$$

**Step 3 —** Example: For a He–Ne laser with $L = 0.5$ m and $\Delta \nu_{\text{gain}} \approx 1.5 \times 10^9$ Hz:

$$
N_{\text{modes}} = \frac{2 \times 0.5 \times 1.5 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} = 5
$$

So only **5 longitudinal modes** lie within the Doppler-broadened gain curve of the He–Ne medium — typically a single mode dominates after mode competition.

**Physical Interpretation:** A *narrow* gain bandwidth (like He–Ne) leads to single-frequency operation, while a *broad* bandwidth (like Ti:sapphire) supports many modes and is the basis for **ultrashort pulse (femtosecond) lasers**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Quality Factor (Q-Factor) of the Optical Resonator

### Statement
The Q-factor of a cavity measures how sharply the cavity selects frequencies — it is the ratio of stored energy to energy lost per radian of oscillation.

### Given
- Resonant (central) frequency = $\nu_0$
- FWHM linewidth of the cavity resonance = $\Delta \nu_{\text{cav}}$

### Derivation

**Step 1 —** Definition of Q-factor (general, applies to any resonant system):

$$
Q = 2 \pi \, \frac{\text{Energy stored in cavity}}{\text{Energy lost per cycle}}
$$

**Step 2 —** Equivalent and most-used form:

$$
Q = \frac{\nu_0}{\Delta \nu_{\text{cav}}}
$$

**Step 3 —** In time-domain, if $\tau_p$ is the **photon lifetime** in the cavity (the average time a photon survives before escaping or being absorbed), then:

$$
\Delta \nu_{\text{cav}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \, \tau_p}
$$

**Step 4 —** Substituting into the Q-definition:

$$
Q = 2 \pi \, \nu_0 \, \tau_p
$$

**Step 5 —** Derive the photon lifetime $\tau_p$ for a simple Fabry–Pérot cavity with mean mirror reflectivity $R = \sqrt{R_1 R_2}$:

- The fraction of photon energy lost per round trip is $(1 - R)$.
- The round-trip time is $T = 2L / c$.
- The fractional energy loss per unit time is $(1 - R) / T$.
- The photon lifetime is the inverse:

$$
\tau_p = \frac{T}{1 - R} = \frac{2 L}{c \, (1 - R)}
$$

**Step 6 —** Substituting back:

$$
Q = 2 \pi \, \nu_0 \, \frac{2L}{c \, (1 - R)} = \frac{4 \pi \, \nu_0 \, L}{c \, (1 - R)}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** A *high-Q* cavity (large $Q$) means sharp frequency selectivity, long photon lifetime, narrow linewidth, and a long coherence length. For instance, a stable He–Ne cavity can achieve $Q \sim 10^9$ to $10^{10}$.

> **Conclusion Box:** $Q = \nu_0 / \Delta \nu_{\text{cav}} = 2 \pi \nu_0 \tau_p = 4 \pi \nu_0 L / [c(1-R)]$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Threshold Gain Condition

### Statement
Laser oscillation begins only when the optical gain experienced by a wave in one round trip exactly compensates the losses.

### Given
- Single-pass gain coefficient = $g$ (per metre)
- Cavity length = $L$
- Mirror reflectivities = $R_1$ and $R_2$

### Derivation

**Step 1 —** A wave of intensity $I_0$ after one round trip (length $2L$) is amplified by a factor $\exp(2 g L)$:

$$
I_{\text{after gain}} = I_0 \, e^{2 g L}
$$

**Step 2 —** It then reflects off both mirrors, losing a factor $R_1 R_2$:

$$
I_{\text{after mirrors}} = I_0 \, e^{2 g L} \, R_1 R_2
$$

**Step 3 —** For sustained (steady-state) oscillation, the intensity after one round trip must equal the initial intensity:

$$
I_0 = I_0 \, e^{2 g L} \, R_1 R_2
$$

**Step 4 —** Cancelling $I_0$ (non-zero) and taking the natural logarithm:

$$
2 g L + \ln(R_1 R_2) = 0
$$

**Step 5 —** Solving for the threshold gain coefficient:

$$
g_{\text{th}} = -\frac{1}{2L} \ln(R_1 R_2)
$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Higher mirror reflectivities (larger $R_1 R_2$) reduce the required threshold gain, allowing low-gain media (like He–Ne) to lase. Lower reflectivities demand higher gain, which is possible in semiconductor or dye lasers.

---

## 3.5 Analytic Summary Matrix

| Quantity | Symbol | Expression | Depends On |
|----------|--------|------------|------------|
| Resonant frequency | $\nu_q$ | $q c / (2L)$ | Mode number, cavity length |
| Mode spacing (FSR) | $\Delta \nu$ | $c / (2L)$ | Cavity length only |
| Number of modes | $N_{\text{modes}}$ | $2 L \Delta \nu_{\text{gain}} / c$ | L, gain bandwidth |
| Q-factor | $Q$ | $4 \pi \nu_0 L / [c(1-R)]$ | Frequency, L, R |
| Photon lifetime | $\tau_p$ | $2 L / [c(1-R)]$ | L, R |
| Threshold gain | $g_{\text{th}}$ | $- \ln(R_1 R_2) / (2L)$ | L, mirror reflectivities |
| Coherence length | $L_{\text{coh}}$ | $c / \Delta \nu$ | Spectral width |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Note:** A well-labelled diagram in your answer sheet scores you a minimum of **2 marks** even if the rest of the explanation is average. Always draw the block diagram of a laser system whenever asked about "components of a laser" or "construction and working".

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — A Generic Laser System

The Mermaid diagram below maps the *functional interaction* between the three basic components and the energy flow within a generic laser (Ruby, He–Ne, or semiconductor).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pump1[External Pumping Source<br>Flash lamp / Electrical discharge / Current injection]
    active1[Active Gain Medium<br>Solid / Liquid / Gas / Semiconductor<br>Provides optical gain via stimulated emission]
    cavity1[Optical Resonant Cavity<br>Mirror M1 - R1 approx 99.9 percent<br>Mirror M2 - R2 approx 95 percent partially transmissive]
    out1[Coherent Laser Output Beam<br>Monochromatic, directional, intense]
    fb1[Optical Feedback Loop<br>Photons reflected back and forth]
    inv1[Population Inversion<br>N2 greater than N1]
    stim1[Stimulated Emission Cascade<br>Exponential photon multiplication]

    pump1 -->|energy input| active1
    active1 -->|creates| inv1
    inv1 -->|enables| stim1
    stim1 -->|photons travel along axis| cavity1
    cavity1 -->|reflects majority| fb1
    fb1 -->|re-enters| active1
    active1 -->|amplified photons| cavity1
    cavity1 -->|transmits fraction R2| out1
    cavity1 -->|reflects fraction R1| fb1
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **pump** injects energy into the **active medium**, producing **population inversion**.
- **Stimulated emission** inside the active medium generates photons in phase with the stimulating photon.
- These photons enter the **optical cavity** between two mirrors. M1 (highly reflective) reflects nearly all light back; M2 (partially transmissive) reflects most but allows a small fraction to escape as the **laser beam**.
- The reflected photons re-enter the active medium, stimulating more emission — a positive-feedback loop that produces an exponentially growing coherent field, limited only by cavity losses and gain saturation.

## 4.2 Energy-Level Schematic of a Four-Level Laser System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ground1[E0 - Ground State]
    pumpLvl1[E3 - Pump Band - fast decay approx 10 to power minus 8 s]
    metaLvl1[E2 - Metastable Upper Laser Level - long life approx 10 to power minus 3 s]
    lowLvl1[E1 - Lower Laser Level - fast decay approx 10 to power minus 8 s]
    pumpArrow1[Pumping - upward red arrow]
    relaxArrow1[Non-radiative decay - downward dashed]
    laserArrow1[Stimulated emission - laser photon - solid green arrow]
    decayArrow1[Fast non-radiative decay to E0 - downward dashed]

    ground1 -->|pumpArrow1| pumpLvl1
    pumpLvl1 -->|relaxArrow1| metaLvl1
    metaLvl1 -->|laserArrow1| lowLvl1
    lowLvl1 -->|decayArrow1| ground1
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- Atoms in the **ground state E₀** absorb pump energy and jump to the **pump band E₃**.
- They decay rapidly (non-radiatively) to the **metastable level E₂**, where they accumulate.
- A passing photon of energy $h\nu = E_2 - E_1$ triggers **stimulated emission** to **E₁**.
- The lower laser level E₁ decays quickly back to E₀, keeping it nearly empty — this is what makes population inversion easy.

## 4.3 Longitudinal Mode Structure Inside the Cavity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    m1A[Mirror M1 - Highly Reflective - R1 approx 100 percent]
    m2A[Mirror M2 - Partially Transmissive - R2 approx 98 percent]
    modeA[Longitudinal Mode q1 - one half-wavelength fits]
    modeB[Longitudinal Mode q2 - two half-wavelengths fit]
    modeC[Longitudinal Mode q3 - three half-wavelengths fit]
    gainA[Gain Curve of Active Medium - bell shaped]

    m1A -->|standing wave region| m2A
    modeA -. lives under .-> gainA
    modeB -. lives under .-> gainA
    modeC -. lives under .-> gainA
    m2A -->|laser output| outA[Coherent Output]
```

**Physical meaning:** Only those longitudinal modes whose frequencies fall under the gain curve receive enough amplification to oscillate. The others are suppressed. The free spectral range $\Delta \nu = c / (2L)$ is the *pitch* of the comb of allowed modes.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Define active medium. State any two essential properties it must possess for laser action."** [3 marks]

**Model Answer:**

An *active medium* (or gain medium) is a specially chosen material — solid, liquid, gas, or semiconductor — containing atoms/ions/molecules that can be excited to higher energy levels and sustain population inversion, providing optical gain through stimulated emission at the lasing wavelength.

**Two essential properties:**

1. **Presence of a metastable state:** The upper laser level must have a long radiative lifetime (≈ 10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ s) so that excited atoms can accumulate and population inversion can be achieved.
2. **Suitable energy-level structure:** The material must have a pair of energy levels separated by an energy difference corresponding to the desired lasing wavelength, with a fast-decaying lower laser level (especially in four-level systems).

*(Any other valid property — high quantum efficiency, broad pump absorption, optical homogeneity, thermal/chemical stability — also accepted.)*

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"What are the functions of an optical resonant cavity in a laser? Mention the stability condition."** [3 marks]

**Model Answer:**

**Functions of the optical resonant cavity:**

1. **Optical feedback:** The two mirrors reflect photons back into the active medium multiple times, producing a chain of stimulated emissions and amplifying the beam.
2. **Mode selection (frequency selectivity):** Only standing-wave frequencies satisfying $\nu_q = q c / (2L)$ oscillate; this produces a narrow, monochromatic output.
3. **Directionality:** Only photons travelling along the cavity axis are reflected back and forth; off-axis photons escape, yielding a highly directional output.

**Stability condition:**

$$
0 \le g_1 \, g_2 \le 1 \quad \text{where} \quad g_i = 1 - \frac{L}{R_i}
$$

This ensures that paraxial rays remain bounded after many round trips.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Module questions with 14 marks usually have two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The structure below mirrors the official question paper.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the three basic components of a laser. Discuss the role of the active medium and pumping source in achieving population inversion.** [7 marks]

**(b) Derive the resonant condition of an optical cavity. Hence obtain the expression for longitudinal mode spacing. For a He–Ne laser with cavity length 50 cm, calculate the number of longitudinal modes (gain bandwidth of He–Ne ≈ 1.5 GHz).** [7 marks]

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

**Introduction (1 mark):** A laser consists of three essential components:
1. Active (gain) medium
2. Optical resonant cavity
3. Pumping (energy) source

**Block diagram (1 mark):** A clearly labelled diagram (similar to Section 4.1) showing pump → active medium → cavity (two mirrors) → output beam, with feedback loop.

**Role of the active medium (2.5 marks):** The active medium contains the atoms that undergo stimulated emission. To achieve lasing, more atoms must be in the excited state (E₂) than in the lower state (E₁) — this is **population inversion**, which contradicts the natural Boltzmann distribution. The active medium must possess:
- A metastable upper level for accumulation of excited atoms
- A lower level that decays rapidly
- A transition energy matching the desired laser wavelength

**Role of the pumping source (2.5 marks):** The pump supplies external energy (optical, electrical, or chemical) to lift atoms from the ground state to the excited state. Without a sufficiently strong pump, the rate of stimulated emission cannot exceed the rate of spontaneous emission and absorption, and lasing will not occur. Three-level systems require a much stronger pump than four-level systems because the lower laser level is the ground state.

**Conclusion:** Population inversion is the necessary precondition for laser action, and the active medium and pumping source together establish and maintain it.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a Fabry–Pérot cavity of length $L$ with light of wavelength $\lambda$ bouncing between two mirrors. For a standing wave to be established, the round-trip optical path must equal an integer number of wavelengths:

$$
2L = q \lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_q = \frac{2L}{q}
$$

The corresponding frequency is:

$$
\nu_q = \frac{c}{\lambda_q} = \frac{q c}{2L}, \quad q = 1, 2, 3, \ldots
$$

The frequency separation between adjacent longitudinal modes is:

$$
\Delta \nu = \nu_{q+1} - \nu_q = \frac{c}{2L}
$$

**Numerical part (2 marks):**

Given $L = 0.5$ m, $\Delta \nu_{\text{gain}} = 1.5 \times 10^9$ Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

$$
\Delta \nu = \frac{c}{2L} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2 \times 0.5} = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ Hz} = 300 \text{ MHz}
$$

$$
N_{\text{modes}} = \left\lfloor \frac{\Delta \nu_{\text{gain}}}{\Delta \nu} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1.5 \times 10^9}{3 \times 10^8} \right\rfloor = \lfloor 5 \rfloor = 5
$$

**Answer:** **5 longitudinal modes** can oscillate in this He–Ne cavity. (In practice, mode competition often leaves only one dominant mode — single-frequency operation.)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

**(a) Compare three-level and four-level laser systems with suitable examples. Why are four-level systems preferred for continuous-wave (CW) operation?** [7 marks]

**(b) What is the Q-factor of an optical cavity? Derive its expression in terms of photon lifetime. A He–Ne cavity has $L = 0.6$ m, $\lambda = 632.8$ nm, and mean mirror reflectivity $R = 0.98$. Calculate the Q-factor and the coherence length of the laser.** [7 marks]

---

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks

| Feature | Three-Level System | Four-Level System |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Lower laser level | Ground state (E₁) | Excited state E₁, above ground state |
| Population inversion | Difficult; need > 50 % of all atoms in upper level | Easy; lower level is nearly empty |
| Pumping requirement | Very intense (pulsed operation) | Moderate (CW possible) |
| Threshold | High | Low |
| Example | Ruby laser (Cr³⁺ in Al₂O₃) | He–Ne, Nd:YAG, CO₂, semiconductor lasers |

**Why four-level systems are preferred for CW (3 marks):** In a four-level system, the lower laser level E₁ lies above the ground state E₀ and is essentially unpopulated at thermal equilibrium. It decays rapidly (non-radiatively) to E₀. Therefore, as soon as atoms accumulate in E₂, the condition N₂ > N₁ is achieved with only a small fraction of ground-state atoms being excited. This drastically reduces the threshold pump power, allowing continuous-wave (CW) operation. In contrast, three-level systems require pumping more than half the atoms out of the ground state, demanding very intense flash-lamp pulses and making CW operation practically impossible for most cases.

**Examples:** Ruby (694.3 nm, three-level, pulsed); He–Ne (632.8 nm, four-level, CW); Nd:YAG (1064 nm, four-level, both pulsed and CW).

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The **Quality factor (Q)** of an optical cavity is the ratio of the resonant (central) frequency $\nu_0$ to the full width at half-maximum (FWHM) linewidth $\Delta \nu_{\text{cav}}$ of the cavity resonance:

$$
Q = \frac{\nu_0}{\Delta \nu_{\text{cav}}}
$$

**Derivation (3.5 marks):** The energy stored in the cavity decays exponentially due to mirror transmission and internal losses. If $\tau_p$ is the photon lifetime (time for stored energy to decay to $1/e$ of its initial value), then the decay rate is $1/\tau_p$, and the corresponding spectral linewidth (Fourier transform of an exponential decay) is:

$$
\Delta \nu_{\text{cav}} = \frac{1}{2 \pi \, \tau_p}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
Q = 2 \pi \, \nu_0 \, \tau_p
$$

For a Fabry–Pérot cavity with mean mirror reflectivity $R$ and length $L$, the photon lifetime is:

$$
\tau_p = \frac{2L}{c(1-R)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
Q = \frac{4 \pi \, \nu_0 \, L}{c(1-R)}
$$

**Numerical part (2 marks):**

Given $L = 0.6$ m, $\lambda = 632.8$ nm, $R = 0.98$, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.

Central frequency:

$$
\nu_0 = \frac{c}{\lambda} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{632.8 \times 10^{-9}} = 4.74 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}
$$

Photon lifetime:

$$
\tau_p = \frac{2L}{c(1-R)} = \frac{2 \times 0.6}{3 \times 10^8 \times 0.02} = \frac{1.2}{6 \times 10^6} = 2.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}
$$

Q-factor:

$$
Q = 2 \pi \, \nu_0 \, \tau_p = 2 \pi \times 4.74 \times 10^{14} \times 2.0 \times 10^{-7} \approx 5.96 \times 10^{8}
$$

Coherence length:

$$
L_{\text{coh}} = c \, \tau_p = 3 \times 10^8 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-7} = 60 \text{ m}
$$

**Answer:** $Q \approx 5.96 \times 10^{8}$ and coherence length $L_{\text{coh}} \approx 60$ m. (Coherence length can also be obtained as $c / \Delta \nu_{\text{cav}}$ with $\Delta \nu_{\text{cav}} = \nu_0 / Q$.)

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make — and where you LOSE marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the resonant condition** — The round-trip distance is $2L$, not $L$. Writing $\lambda = 2L / q$ is correct; writing $\lambda = L / q$ is a 1-mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing up resonant frequency and free spectral range** — $\nu_q = q c / (2L)$ is the resonant frequency of mode $q$; $\Delta \nu = c / (2L)$ is the *spacing* between modes. They are not the same.
> 3. **Confusing "active medium" with "pumping source"** — The active medium is the *material* in which stimulated emission occurs. The pumping source is the *external energy input* (lamp, discharge, current). Examiners deduct 1 mark for swapping them.
> 4. **Three-level vs four-level confusion** — In Ruby, the *ground state* itself is the lower laser level (three-level). In He–Ne, the lower laser level is *above* the ground state (four-level). Many students incorrectly say Ruby is four-level.
> 5. **Forgetting units in numerical problems** — Always show units: $\Delta \nu$ in Hz, $L$ in m, $\tau_p$ in s. Unit-less answers are penalised.
> 6. **Skipping the block diagram** — A diagram is worth a minimum of 2 marks in any "construction and working" question. If you only write text, you lose those marks.
> 7. **Stability condition sign errors** — The condition is $0 \le g_1 g_2 \le 1$, NOT $g_1 g_2 \ge 1$. A stable cavity is *not* the one with "very large" $g_1 g_2$.
> 8. **Q-factor definition** — Some students write $Q = \nu_0 \Delta \nu$ (product) instead of $Q = \nu_0 / \Delta \nu$ (ratio). This is a fundamental error.
> 9. **"Population inversion" as a vague statement** — Always state it as a numerical inequality: **N₂ > N₁**, where N₂ is the population of the upper laser level and N₁ that of the lower level. Words like "more atoms in excited state" are considered imprecise.
> 10. **Threshold gain sign** — In the threshold condition $g_{\text{th}} = -\ln(R_1 R_2) / (2L)$, $R_1 R_2 < 1$, so $\ln(R_1 R_2) < 0$, and $g_{\text{th}}$ is positive. Always verify the sign at the end of the derivation.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-revision checklist — memorise this before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ **LASER** = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.
- ✅ Three basic components: **Active medium + Optical cavity + Pumping source**.
- ✅ **Active medium** provides optical gain; needs a metastable state for population inversion.
- ✅ **Optical cavity** provides feedback, mode selection, directionality, and threshold reduction.
- ✅ **Population inversion** is the condition $N_2 > N_1$ — a non-thermal, externally pumped state.
- ✅ **Resonant condition:** $\lambda_q = 2L / q$, i.e., $2L = q \lambda$.
- ✅ **Mode spacing (FSR):** $\Delta \nu = c / (2L)$.
- ✅ **Q-factor:** $Q = \nu_0 / \Delta \nu_{\text{cav}} = 2 \pi \nu_0 \tau_p$.
- ✅ **Photon lifetime:** $\tau_p = 2L / [c(1-R)]$.
- ✅ **Threshold gain:** $g_{\text{th}} = -\ln(R_1 R_2) / (2L)$.
- ✅ **Coherence length:** $L_{\text{coh}} = c \tau_p = c / \Delta \nu$.
- ✅ **Stability condition:** $0 \le g_1 g_2 \le 1$ with $g_i = 1 - L/R_i$.
- ✅ **Three-level system** (e.g., Ruby) needs very intense pumping; **four-level system** (e.g., He–Ne) supports CW operation.
- ✅ **TEM_{00}** is the lowest-order transverse (Gaussian) mode — most desirable for applications.
- ✅ Always draw a **labelled block diagram** in construction-type questions — it guarantees at least 2 marks.
- ✅ Numerical problems: keep $L$ in metres, $\nu$ in Hz, $\tau$ in seconds; show the formula before substitution.
- ✅ When asked for "expression for resonant frequency of an optical cavity," write both the *derivation* (4–5 steps) and the *final formula* $\nu_q = q c / (2L)$ — derivations fetch full marks, mere formula does not.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Construction and Working of Ruby Laser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Construction and Working of Ruby Laser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ruby Laser:** A solid-state, optically pumped, **three-level pulsed laser** in which the active medium is a synthetic ruby crystal (aluminium oxide, Al₂O₃, doped with approximately **0.05 % chromium(III) ions, Cr³⁺**) that produces a coherent, monochromatic red beam at a wavelength of **λ = 694.3 nm**. It was the **first working laser ever demonstrated**, built by Theodore H. Maiman at Hughes Research Laboratories in **July 1960**.

The term *LASER* is an acronym for **L**ight **A**mplification by **S**timulated **E**mission of **R**adiation. The ruby laser belongs to the family of **solid-state lasers** because the gain medium is an insulating crystalline solid (as opposed to gas, liquid, or semiconductor lasers).

### 1.2 Physical Layout of a Ruby Crystal

| Component | Description |
|---|---|
| Host lattice | Aluminium oxide (Al₂O₃), also called corundum or sapphire |
| Dopant | Cr³⁺ ions substituting roughly **0.03 %–0.05 %** of the Al³⁺ sites |
| Geometry | Cylindrical rod, typical dimensions: length **2–10 cm**, diameter **5–15 mm** |
| Colour | Pink/red (due to absorption in the green–yellow region) |

> [!NOTE]
> Pure Al₂O₃ is colourless. The pinkish-red colour of ruby arises because Cr³⁺ ions absorb strongly in the green (~550 nm) and yellow regions, transmitting red light.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water tank with a leak at the bottom** sitting on a platform.

- **The water (excited electrons)** fills the upper region of the tank when pumped up.
- **The leak (metastable state)** is much smaller than the inlet, so water accumulates faster than it drains.
- Once the water level (population) in the upper region **exceeds** the level in the lower basin, you have achieved **population inversion**.
- The instant a single drop spills through a special valve (a photon), it triggers a **chain reaction** — every drop above it follows in lockstep — and a steady, coherent stream of water (the laser beam) gushes out.

In a ruby laser, the xenon flash lamp **"lifts"** Cr³⁺ ions up; the metastable ²E level **"holds"** them briefly; the silvered ends **"reflect"** photons back and forth to trigger stimulated emission; and the partially silvered end **"lets the beam escape"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The ruby laser is the textbook archetype of a **three-level laser system**, contrasted with the four-level systems used in most modern lasers (e.g., He–Ne, Nd:YAG). Understanding why population inversion is *harder* in three-level systems is a recurring KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

A ruby laser works in **four sequential physical stages**:

**Step 1 — Optical Pumping (Population Excitation)**
A high-power **xenon (Xe) flash lamp** (typically delivering several kilojoules over a few hundred microseconds) discharges an intense burst of broadband white light onto the ruby rod. Photons in the **green (~550 nm)** and **violet (~400 nm)** bands are absorbed by Cr³⁺ ions, raising them from the ground state **⁴A₂** to the broad absorption bands **⁴F₂** and **⁴F₁**.

**Step 2 — Non-Radiative Decay (Relaxation to Metastable State)**
The excited Cr³⁺ ions undergo **rapid non-radiative (phonon) transitions** down to the metastable state **²E**. This state is split by spin–orbit coupling into two closely spaced levels: **2Ā (E₂)** at higher energy and **Ē (E₁)** at slightly lower energy. The lifetime of this metastable state is unusually long, about **τ ≈ 3 × 10⁻³ s (3 ms)** — extraordinarily long for an excited electronic state.

**Step 3 — Population Inversion & Spontaneous Emission**
Because the metastable state is long-lived, excited ions accumulate faster than they leave. When the population of **²E exceeds** the population of **⁴A₂**, population inversion is achieved. A few ions decay spontaneously, emitting photons of energy equal to the **²E → ⁴A₂** gap.

**Step 4 — Stimulated Emission & Amplification**
These initial photons travel along the ruby rod, reflect off the silvered end faces, and trigger other excited Cr³⁺ ions to emit **identical, in-phase photons** (same wavelength, phase, polarisation, direction). The resonant optical cavity formed by the two silvered ends amplifies the beam exponentially. A fraction (≈ 1–2 %) leaks through the partially silvered output coupler as the **coherent laser output at 694.3 nm**.

### 2.2 Why Two Silvered Ends?

| Mirror | Reflectivity | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Rear (fully silvered) | **R ≈ 100 %** | Reflects all photons back into the rod for further amplification |
| Front (partially silvered) | **R ≈ 98 %** | Reflects most photons back, transmits ≈ 2 % as the output beam |

Together they form an **optical resonator cavity** of length *L*. The resonant standing-wave condition requires integer multiples of half-wavelengths to fit between the mirrors:

$$ 2L = q\lambda \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nu_q = \frac{qc}{2L} $$

where **q** is an integer (longitudinal mode number) and **c** is the speed of light in the ruby medium.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Law | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| Photon energy | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of each laser photon; *h* is Planck's constant |
| Frequency–wavelength relation | $\nu = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$ | Converts wavelength to frequency of the emitted photon |
| Population inversion condition (3-level) | $N_2 > N_1$ (ground) | Harder than 4-level because ground state must be *depleted* |
| Threshold pump condition | $R_{pump} \geq R_{threshold} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_{sp}}$ | Pump rate must exceed inverse of spontaneous lifetime |
| Photon flux in cavity | $\phi = \dfrac{n_{ph}}{V}$ | Number of photons per unit cavity volume |
| Gain coefficient | $G = \sigma \Delta N$ | $\sigma$ = stimulated emission cross-section, $\Delta N$ = inversion density |
| Ruby laser wavelength | $\lambda = 694.3 \text{ nm}$ | Dominant R₁ line: 2Ā → ⁴A₂ transition |
| Metastable lifetime | $\tau \approx 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$ | Long lifetime makes population inversion feasible |
| Refractive index of ruby | $n \approx 1.76$ | Determines cavity optical path length and Brewster's angle |
| Resonant cavity condition | $2nL = q\lambda$ | *n* is refractive index, *q* is integer mode number |
| Threshold gain condition | $G_{th} = \dfrac{1}{2L}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)$ | Minimum gain to overcome mirror + internal losses |
| Pulse energy (typical) | $E_{pulse} \approx 1\text{–}10 \text{ J}$ | Output energy per flash |
| Pulse duration | $\Delta t \approx 0.5\text{–}5 \text{ ms}$ | Time width of each laser pulse |
| Peak power | $P_{peak} = \dfrac{E_{pulse}}{\Delta t}$ | Can reach several kilowatts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** The threshold-gain equation $G_{th} = \tfrac{1}{2L}\ln(1/R_1 R_2)$ is *the* most-tested formula in laser-physics numericals. Memorise it.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Ruby Laser

| Application Area | Use |
|---|---|
| Dermatology & medicine | Tattoo removal, birthmark treatment, pigmented lesion ablation (694 nm strongly absorbed by melanin) |
| Holography | First widely used source for recording reflection holograms (deep red) |
| Range finding | Early military and surveying LIDAR; pulsed beam enables time-of-flight distance measurement |
| Non-linear optics | Q-switched ruby laser used to pump dye lasers and Raman-shifting crystals |
| Alignment & surveying | Beam visible in daylight due to red colour |
| Plasma research | Focusing high-power Q-switched ruby pulses to ignite fusion plasmas (early inertial confinement fusion studies) |

The ruby laser, although largely replaced today by more efficient Nd:YAG, fibre, and diode lasers, remains the **pedagogical cornerstone** of all laser physics courses — including the KTU 2024 syllabus — because it cleanly illustrates population inversion, optical pumping, cavity resonance, and stimulated emission in a single, well-documented device.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Threshold Gain Condition

**Statement to derive:** The minimum gain coefficient required to sustain laser oscillation in a cavity of length *L* with mirror reflectivities *R₁* and *R₂* is

$$ G_{th} = \frac{1}{2L} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right) $$

**Step 1 — Define the round-trip intensity amplification.**
Consider a photon of intensity *I* starting at mirror M₁. It traverses the gain medium of length *L*, is amplified by a factor *e^{GL}*, hits mirror M₂, is multiplied by *R₂*, traverses the medium back, is amplified by another *e^{GL}*, hits M₁, and is multiplied by *R₁*. For sustained oscillation, the round-trip net gain must equal unity.

$$
I \cdot e^{GL} \cdot R_2 \cdot e^{GL} \cdot R_1 \;=\; I
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute and simplify the exponential terms.**
The two gain segments combine to give a total gain of *e^{2GL}*, so the equation reduces to:

$$
R_1 R_2 \, e^{2 G_{th} L} \;=\; 1
$$

**Step 3 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**
Apply $\ln(\cdot)$ to eliminate the exponential term:

$$
\ln(R_1 R_2) + 2 G_{th} L \;=\; 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the threshold gain coefficient $G_{th}$.**
Rearrange to isolate $G_{th}$:

$$
2 G_{th} L \;=\; -\ln(R_1 R_2) \;=\; \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,G_{th} \;=\; \frac{1}{2L} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)\,}
$$

**Physical interpretation:**
- The longer the cavity *L*, the *smaller* the required threshold gain — longer cavities are easier to lase.
- The closer the mirror reflectivities are to 1, the *smaller* the required threshold gain — high-reflectivity mirrors lower the lasing threshold.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Energy of the Ruby Laser Photon

**Step 1 — Recall the Planck relation.**
The energy of a single photon is given by:

$$
E \;=\; h\nu
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the frequency in terms of wavelength.**
Using $\nu = c/\lambda$:

$$
E \;=\; \frac{hc}{\lambda}
$$

**Step 3 — Insert the ruby laser wavelength $\lambda = 694.3 \text{ nm}$.**
Constants: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$, $\lambda = 694.3 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$.

$$
E \;=\; \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}) (3 \times 10^{8})}{694.3 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute numerator and denominator separately.**

$$
\text{Numerator} \;=\; (6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8}) \;=\; 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}
$$

$$
\text{Denominator} \;=\; 694.3 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}
$$

**Step 5 — Final numerical value.**

$$
E \;=\; \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{6.943 \times 10^{-7}} \;=\; 2.863 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to electron-volts (1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J).**

$$
E \;=\; \frac{2.863 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \;\approx\; 1.787 \text{ eV}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_{photon} \;\approx\; 2.86 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \;\approx\; 1.79 \text{ eV}\,}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Why a Three-Level System is "Hard" to Pump

| Property | Three-Level System (Ruby) | Four-Level System (e.g., He–Ne, Nd:YAG) |
|---|---|---|
| Lower laser level | **Ground state ⁴A₂** (most populated at thermal equilibrium) | Excited state *E₁* (essentially empty at thermal equilibrium) |
| Population inversion requirement | $N(²E) > N(⁴A₂)$, i.e., pump must *deplete* > 50 % of ground state | $N(E_2) > N(E_1)$, automatically satisfied by *any* small pump |
| Threshold pump power | **High** | **Low** (typically 10–100× smaller) |
| Efficiency | Low (1–3 %) | Higher (10–30 %) |
| Example | Ruby (Cr³⁺:Al₂O₃) | Nd:YAG, CO₂, He–Ne, diode lasers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Insight:** In a three-level laser, the *final* state of the lasing transition is the ground state, which starts out almost completely full. To achieve inversion you must pump more than half of all atoms out of the ground state — an enormous energy investment. The ruby laser works only because the **metastable ²E** state has a uniquely long lifetime (3 ms), allowing ions to pile up.

### 3.4 Derivation: Resonant Frequencies of the Optical Cavity

**Step 1 — Standing-wave boundary condition.**
For constructive interference between a wave and its mirror-reflected counterpart, an integer number of half-wavelengths must fit inside the optical path:

$$
q \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2} \;=\; L
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the wavelength.**

$$
\lambda_q \;=\; \frac{2L}{q}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to frequency using $c = \nu \lambda$.**

$$
\nu_q \;=\; \frac{c}{\lambda_q} \;=\; \frac{qc}{2L}
$$

**Step 4 — Free spectral range (mode spacing).**
The frequency separation between two adjacent longitudinal modes ($q$ and $q+1$) is:

$$
\Delta\nu \;=\; \nu_{q+1} - \nu_q \;=\; \frac{c}{2L}
$$

**Numerical example:** For *L* = 5 cm = 0.05 m:

$$
\Delta\nu \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 0.05} \;=\; 3 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz} \;=\; 3 \text{ GHz}
$$

This means **3 GHz of mode spacing** in a 5 cm ruby cavity — much smaller than the ruby fluorescence linewidth (~330 GHz at room temperature), so several longitudinal modes can lase simultaneously, producing a slightly multi-mode output.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Physical Construction — Block Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the physical construction and energy-flow architecture of a ruby laser.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PS[High Voltage Power Supply] -->|Charges| CAP[Energy Storage Capacitor Bank]
    CAP -->|Triggers discharge| XEL[Xenon Flash Lamp Xe]
    XEL -->|Emits intense white light pulse| REF[Elliptical Metallic Reflector]
    REF -->|Focuses light onto rod| RUBY[Ruby Rod Cr3+ doped Al2O3]
    RUBY -->|Cr3+ ions excited to 4F1 4F2 pump bands| ABS[Optical Absorption]
    ABS -->|Non radiative decay| META[Metastable State 2E lifetime 3 ms]
    META -->|Spontaneous + stimulated emission 694.3 nm| CAV[Optical Resonator Cavity]
    CAV -->|Round trip amplification| MR1[Fully Silvered Rear Mirror R 100 percent]
    CAV -->|Output coupling| MR2[Partially Silvered Front Mirror R 98 percent]
    MR2 -->|Coherent red beam emerges| OUT[Laser Output Beam lambda 694.3 nm]
    RUBY -.->|Heat removal| COOL[Water or Air Cooling Jacket]
    CAP -.->|Trigger pulse synchronisation| TRG[External Trigger Circuit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The elliptical reflector is geometrically designed so that the xenon lamp sits at one focus and the ruby rod at the other — ensuring maximum optical coupling between the lamp and the gain medium.

### 4.2 Energy-Level Diagram of the Cr³⁺ Ion in Ruby

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    G["Ground State 4A2"]
    P1["Pump Band 4F2 green 550 nm"]
    P2["Pump Band 4F1 violet 400 nm"]
    M1["Metastable 2A Bar upper 2E doublet"]
    M2["Metastable E Bar lower 2E doublet"]

    P1 -->|Non radiative fast| M1
    P2 -->|Non radiative fast| M1
    M1 -->|Fast relaxation| M2
    G -->|Optical pumping 550 400 nm| P1
    G -->|Optical pumping 400 nm| P2
    M2 -->|Stimulated emission 694.3 nm R1 line| G
    M1 -->|Spontaneous weak 692.8 nm R2 line| G
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Upward arrows:** Optical pumping — xenon-lamp photons lift Cr³⁺ from ⁴A₂ to ⁴F₁/⁴F₂.
- **Wavy downward arrows:** Non-radiative (phonon) decay — energy released as lattice vibrations.
- **Solid downward arrow:** The dominant laser transition **R₁: 2Ā → ⁴A₂** at **694.3 nm**.
- **Dashed downward arrow:** The much weaker R₂: Ē → ⁴A₂ line at 692.8 nm (non-lasing fluorescence).

### 4.3 Functional Sequence of Laser Action

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[1 Optical Pumping] --> B[2 Population of Pump Bands]
    B --> C[3 Rapid Non Radiative Decay to Metastable 2E]
    C --> D[4 Population Inversion Achieved]
    D --> E[5 Spontaneous Photon Triggers Stimulated Emission]
    E --> F[6 Amplification by Round Trips in Cavity]
    F --> G[7 Coherent Beam Emerges via Partial Mirror]
```

### 4.4 Cross-Sectional Schematic of the Ruby Laser Head

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ELL[Elliptical Cavity Cross Section]
        F1[F1 Focus] --- ROD[Ruby Rod Cylinder]
        F2[F2 Focus] --- XEL2[Xenon Flash Lamp]
        F1 ---|Light path via reflection| F2
    end
    ROD -->|Output beam| LENS[Collimating Lens]
    LENS -->|Parallel beam| TARGET[TARGET or APPLICATION]
    XEL2 -.->|Cooling fins| AIR[Ambient Air or Water Jacket]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
**Q1.** Define the term *population inversion* and explain why it is necessary for laser action. Mention the population inversion type in a ruby laser.

**Model Answer:**

> Population inversion is a non-equilibrium condition in which the number of atoms (or ions) in an *excited* energy level $N_2$ exceeds the number in a *lower* energy level $N_1$, i.e., $N_2 > N_1$.

> It is necessary because stimulated emission — the process that *amplifies* light coherently — has a probability proportional to the population of the upper level minus the population of the lower level. Under normal (thermal-equilibrium) conditions, $N_1 \gg N_2$, so absorption dominates over stimulated emission and *no* amplification is possible.

> In a ruby laser, population inversion is achieved between the metastable state **²E** (upper laser level) and the ground state **⁴A₂** (lower laser level). Since the lower laser level is the ground state, this is a **three-level inversion**, which requires pumping more than 50 % of the Cr³⁺ ions out of ⁴A₂.

*(Self-explanatory, awarding 3 marks.)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
**Q2.** Distinguish between spontaneous emission and stimulated emission. Which of the two is responsible for the coherent nature of the laser beam?

**Model Answer:**

> **Spontaneous emission** is the random, probabilistic decay of an excited atom to a lower energy state, emitting a photon in a *random direction*, with *random phase* and *random polarisation*. Each event is independent. It follows an exponential decay law with a characteristic lifetime $\tau_{sp}$.

> **Stimulated emission** occurs when an *incoming photon* of energy exactly matching the atomic transition energy $E = h\nu$ induces an excited atom to drop to the lower state, emitting a *second photon* that is **identical** to the incident one in wavelength, phase, direction, and polarisation — *coherent* with it.

> **Stimulated emission** is responsible for the *coherent* nature of the laser beam, because every emitted photon is a clone of the stimulating photon, leading to phase-locked, monochromatic, directional light. Spontaneous emission merely *seeds* the process; amplification is performed by stimulated emission.

*(Self-explanatory, awarding 3 marks.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

#### QUESTION A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**
**Q.A.** With a neat diagram, describe the **construction and working of a ruby laser**. Explain the energy-level diagram of Cr³⁺ in ruby and justify why the ²E state is called the *metastable* state. **(14 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline:**

**(a) Construction of the Ruby Laser [7 marks]**

The main components are:

1. **Active medium — Ruby rod**: A cylindrical single crystal of Al₂O₃ doped with about **0.05 %** of Cr³⁺ ions, typically 4 cm long and 0.5 cm diameter. The two end faces are polished optically flat and parallel to within a fraction of a wavelength. One face is *fully silvered* (reflectivity ≈ 100 %) and the other is *partially silvered* (reflectivity ≈ 98 %) — together they form the **optical resonator cavity**.

2. **Pumping source — Xenon flash lamp**: A coiled or linear xenon-filled discharge tube that emits a few-hundred-microsecond burst of intense white light, peaking in the green (~550 nm) and violet (~400 nm) regions. Energy is supplied by a capacitor bank charged to a few kilovolts.

3. **Optical coupling — Elliptical reflector**: A polished metallic reflector in the shape of a *half-ellipse* (or full ellipse) is used. The ruby rod and the xenon lamp are placed at the **two foci** of the ellipse, so that light emanating from one focus is concentrated onto the other by total internal reflection inside the cavity.

4. **Cooling system**: The ruby rod and the lamp are surrounded by a water jacket or forced-air cooling because a significant fraction of the input energy is converted into heat.

5. **Triggering circuit**: Synchronises the capacitor discharge to produce a controlled pulsed output.

**(b) Working of the Ruby Laser — Energy-Level Diagram [7 marks]**

The relevant energy levels of the Cr³⁺ ion in the Al₂O₃ host are:

- **⁴A₂** — Ground state
- **⁴F₁ and ⁴F₂** — Broad absorption pump bands (lying in the violet and green regions respectively)
- **²E** — Metastable state, split into two sub-levels **2Ā** (upper) and **Ē** (lower)

**Working sequence:**

1. **Optical pumping**: Photons from the xenon flash lamp lift Cr³⁺ ions from the ground state ⁴A₂ to the broad absorption bands ⁴F₁ and ⁴F₂.

2. **Non-radiative decay**: The excited ions lose energy rapidly (~10⁻⁹ s) by *phonon emission* to the lattice, dropping to the long-lived **²E** metastable state. Because this decay is non-radiative, no photon is emitted — energy simply heats the crystal locally.

3. **Population inversion**: With continued pumping, ions accumulate in ²E because its spontaneous lifetime is extraordinarily long ($\tau \approx 3$ ms). When $N(²E) > N(⁴A₂)$, inversion is achieved.

4. **Spontaneous emission (seeding)**: A few ions decay spontaneously from ²E → ⁴A₂, emitting photons of wavelength 694.3 nm (the **R₁ line**, 2Ā → ⁴A₂ transition).

5. **Stimulated emission and amplification**: These photons reflect back and forth between the silvered mirrors, triggering other excited Cr³⁺ ions to emit *identical* photons. The intensity grows exponentially along the rod.

6. **Output coupling**: After many round trips, a fraction of the beam escapes through the partially silvered mirror as a narrow, coherent, monochromatic, intense red beam at **694.3 nm**.

**Justification — Why ²E is *metastable*:**
The ²E state is metastable because the electric-dipole transition from ²E to ⁴A₂ is **spin-forbidden** (it would require $\Delta S = 0$, but the spin multiplicities differ: ²E has S = 1/2 while ⁴A₂ has S = 3/2 — a true dipole transition requires $\Delta S = 0$). The transition is allowed only via weak magnetic-dipole or electric-quadrupole coupling, giving a lifetime of ~3 ms — about **10⁶ times longer** than typical excited electronic states. This long lifetime is what makes population inversion feasible.

*(Self-explanatory, awarding 14 marks.)*

---

#### QUESTION B — (14 Marks) [INTERNAL CHOICE]

**[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Style]**
**Q.B.** (a) Derive the **threshold gain condition** for laser oscillation in an optical resonator of length *L* with mirror reflectivities *R₁* and *R₂*. (b) Calculate the photon energy (in eV) of the ruby laser and the **free spectral range** for a cavity length of 6 cm. **(7 + 7 = 14 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Threshold Gain Condition [7 marks]**

Consider a photon of intensity *I* starting at mirror M₁. After one **round trip** through the cavity:

- Traverses gain medium of length *L* (amplified by $e^{GL}$)
- Reflects from M₂ (multiplied by $R_2$)
- Traverses gain medium back (amplified by another $e^{GL}$)
- Reflects from M₁ (multiplied by $R_1$)

For sustained (steady-state) oscillation, the net round-trip multiplication must equal unity:

$$
R_1 R_2 \, e^{2 G_{th} L} \;=\; 1
$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
\ln(R_1 R_2) + 2 G_{th} L \;=\; 0
$$

Solving for the threshold gain:

$$
\boxed{\,G_{th} \;=\; \frac{1}{2L} \ln\!\left(\frac{1}{R_1 R_2}\right)\,}
$$

This is the minimum gain coefficient required to overcome the mirror losses and sustain laser oscillation.

**(b) Numerical Calculations [7 marks]**

**Photon energy at λ = 694.3 nm:**

Using $E = hc/\lambda$ with $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s:

$$
E \;=\; \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^{8})}{694.3 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
E \;=\; \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{6.943 \times 10^{-7}} \;\approx\; 2.863 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}
$$

Converting to electron-volts:

$$
E \;=\; \frac{2.863 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \;\approx\; 1.787 \text{ eV}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_{photon} \approx 1.79 \text{ eV}\,}
$$

**Free spectral range (FSR) for L = 6 cm:**

$$
\Delta\nu \;=\; \frac{c}{2L} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{2 \times 0.06} \;=\; \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{0.12}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta\nu \;=\; 2.5 \times 10^{9} \text{ Hz} \;=\; 2.5 \text{ GHz}\,}
$$

*(Self-explanatory, awarding 14 marks.)*

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing pump bands with metastable level.** Many students draw the metastable ²E as a pump band. The correct picture: pump bands ⁴F₁/⁴F₂ → *non-radiative decay* → metastable ²E → *radiative* laser transition → ground state ⁴A₂.
>
> 2. **Wrong laser wavelength.** The ruby laser emits at **694.3 nm** (the R₁ line). A common mistake is to write 632.8 nm (which is the He–Ne laser). Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to convert J to eV.** Photon energy questions almost always require the value *in electron-volts*. Skipping the conversion loses 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Missing the "why metastable?" justification.** Simply naming ²E as metastable is worth only 1 mark; the *spin-forbidden* explanation (ΔS ≠ 0) is worth the remaining marks.
>
> 5. **Threshold-gain sign errors.** The formula is $G_{th} = \frac{1}{2L}\ln(1/R_1 R_2)$, *not* $\ln(R_1 R_2)/2L$. The argument of the log is the reciprocal.
>
> 6. **Confusing stimulated emission cross-section σ with absorption cross-section.** Always use the *emission* σ in the gain formula $G = \sigma \Delta N$.
>
> 7. **Omitting cooling system in construction diagrams.** The cooling jacket is worth at least 1 mark in a 14-mark construction question.
>
> 8. **Calling ruby a four-level laser.** Ruby is a textbook *three-level* system. Mixing this up is a 2-mark penalty.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Revision Checklist**
>
> - Ruby laser = **first laser** demonstrated by Maiman in **1960**; emits at **694.3 nm** (red).
> - Active medium: **Al₂O₃ doped with Cr³⁺ (~0.05 %)** — a *solid-state, optically pumped* laser.
> - Pumping source: **Xenon flash lamp**, coupled via **elliptical reflector** (lamp and rod at the two foci).
> - Three-level system: ground state **⁴A₂**, pump bands **⁴F₁ / ⁴F₂**, metastable state **²E** (split into **2Ā** and **Ē**).
> - Laser transition: **R₁ line: 2Ā → ⁴A₂ at 694.3 nm**.
> - Metastable lifetime: **τ ≈ 3 ms** — extremely long because the ²E → ⁴A₂ transition is **spin-forbidden** (ΔS = 1).
> - Cavity: one fully silvered (R ≈ 100 %) + one partially silvered (R ≈ 98 %) end face.
> - Resonant condition: $2L = q\lambda \;\Rightarrow\; \nu_q = qc / 2L$.
> - Threshold gain: $G_{th} = \tfrac{1}{2L}\ln(1/R_1 R_2)$.
> - Photon energy: $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda \approx 1.79$ eV at 694.3 nm.
> - Mode spacing (FSR): $\Delta\nu = c / 2L$.
> - Output is **pulsed** (typical pulse: 1–10 J, 0.5–5 ms duration, several kW peak power).
> - Three-level systems have **high threshold** because the lower laser level is the ground state; inversion requires pumping > 50 % of all ions.
> - Applications: **holography, dermatology, range-finding, LIDAR, plasma studies**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
