# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Patterned on KTU 2024 End-Semester Evaluation (ESE), Part A consists of 2-mark and 3-mark short-answer questions. Each answer should be ~50–80 words and may include one supporting equation or diagram.

---

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fiber. Mention its physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fiber is a dimensionless parameter that quantifies its *light-gathering ability*. It is defined as the sine of the maximum acceptance angle $\theta_a$:

$$NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Physical significance:**
- NA determines the cone of light that can be coupled into the fiber.
- A higher NA means easier coupling and greater tolerance to mechanical misalignment at splices.
- However, higher NA increases intermodal dispersion in multi-mode fibers, reducing bandwidth.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Formula (1M) + Significance (1M) = **3 Marks**

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is Total Internal Reflection (TIR)? State the two conditions necessary for TIR to occur.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Total Internal Reflection is the phenomenon in which a light ray travelling from a *denser* medium to a *rarer* medium is completely reflected back into the denser medium at the interface, with **no** light escaping into the rarer medium.

**Two Necessary Conditions:**
1. Light must travel from an optically denser medium to an optically rarer medium (i.e., $n_1 > n_2$).
2. The angle of incidence at the interface must be **greater than or equal to the critical angle** $\theta_c$, where $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$.

**Engineering Use:** TIR is the foundational principle that allows light to be guided along the core of an optical fiber over thousands of kilometers with negligible loss.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1M) + Two conditions (1M) + Example (1M) = **3 Marks**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations: each Part B question is for **14 marks** with **internal choice** (OR option between two questions). Sub-parts are typically 7 marks each. Every step must be shown explicitly to obtain full marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **acceptance angle** and **numerical aperture** of a step-index optical fiber. Explain the physical significance of the acceptance angle. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of neat block diagrams, explain the **different types of optical fibers** based on (i) refractive index profile and (ii) number of modes. Compare their key characteristics. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Statement of Geometry (1 Mark):**
Consider a step-index optical fiber with core RI $n_1$, cladding RI $n_2$ ($n_2 < n_1$), surrounded by air ($n_0 = 1$). A meridional ray enters the fiber end face from air at an external angle $\theta_0$ to the fiber axis, refracts into the core at angle $\theta_1$, and strikes the core–cladding interface at an angle $\theta$ measured from the normal.

**Step 2 — Geometric Relationship (1 Mark):**
From the geometry of the right triangle formed by the ray and the cylindrical interface:

$$\theta + \theta_1 = 90° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1 = 90° - \theta$$

**Step 3 — TIR Condition (1 Mark):**
For light to be guided by TIR, $\theta \geq \theta_c$. The corresponding minimum value of $\theta_1$ inside the core is:

$$\theta_1^{min} = 90° - \theta_c$$

This minimum $\theta_1$ corresponds to the **maximum external angle $\theta_0$** in air, which by definition is the **acceptance angle $\theta_a$**.

**Step 4 — Snell's Law at the End Face (2 Marks):**

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_1^{min} = n_1 \sin(90° - \theta_c) = n_1 \cos\theta_c$$

With $n_0 = 1$:

$$\sin\theta_a = n_1 \cos\theta_c$$

Using $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$ and $\cos\theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2\theta_c} = \sqrt{1 - n_2^2/n_1^2}$:

$$\sin\theta_a = n_1 \sqrt{\frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{n_1^2}} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

$$\boxed{\sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

The numerical aperture is defined as $NA = \sin\theta_a$:

$$\boxed{NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Significance (2 Marks):**
The acceptance angle $\theta_a$ is the *maximum angle* (with respect to the fiber axis) at which an incoming ray in air can be successfully coupled into the fiber and guided by TIR. Rays entering at angles greater than $\theta_a$ will refract through the cladding and be lost. The half-angle $\theta_a$ defines the **acceptance cone** of the fiber. The NA quantifies this light-gathering capacity: a higher NA fiber accepts light from a wider cone, making source-to-fiber coupling easier and more tolerant to misalignment — at the cost of increased intermodal dispersion.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Classification Based on Refractive Index Profile (4 Marks):**

**(i) Step-Index Fiber:**
The core has a uniform refractive index $n_1$, which drops abruptly to $n_2$ at the core–cladding boundary. Light rays travel in straight-line zig-zag paths inside the core.

- *Single-mode step-index:* Core diameter ~ 9 μm; one mode propagates; minimal dispersion; used for long-haul telecom.
- *Multi-mode step-index:* Core diameter ~ 50–200 μm; hundreds to thousands of modes; suffers high modal dispersion; used only for very short links.

**(ii) Graded-Index Fiber:**
The refractive index of the core varies *parabolically* with radial distance $r$ from the axis:

$$n(r) = n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta\left(\frac{r}{a}\right)^2}$$

Maximum on-axis at $r = 0$; minimum at the core-cladding boundary. Light rays follow curved (sinusoidal) paths. Higher-angle rays travel through lower-index regions, hence at higher speed — automatically compensating for their longer path length. Modal dispersion is reduced by a factor of ~1000 compared to step-index MM fiber.

**Classification Based on Number of Modes (3 Marks):**

**(i) Single-Mode Fiber (SMF):** Only the fundamental HE₁₁ mode propagates ($V < 2.405$). Core ~ 9 μm; NA ~ 0.10; standard telecom fiber at 1310/1550 nm; bandwidth-distance product > 100 GHz·km.

**(ii) Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF):** Hundreds to thousands of modes propagate. Core ~ 50–100 μm; NA ~ 0.20–0.40; bandwidth-distance product ~ 200–2000 MHz·km; used in LANs, data centers, endoscopy.

**Comparison Table (Verbal):**

| Parameter | SMF | MMF (Step-Index) | MMF (Graded-Index) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Core Size | 9 μm | 50–200 μm | 50–100 μm |
| NA | ~ 0.10 | 0.20–0.40 | 0.20–0.30 |
| Modal Dispersion | Negligible | Very High | Low |
| Bandwidth | Very High | Low | High |
| Cost | High | Low | Moderate |
| Application | Telecom | Short LANs | Data Centers |

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Discuss the important **applications of lasers** in the fields of (i) industry, (ii) medicine, and (iii) communication. Specify suitable laser types for each application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **attenuation and dispersion** in optical fibers. Discuss the three transmission windows used in silica fibers and identify the wavelength at which attenuation is minimum. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Introduction (1 Mark):**
A laser (Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation) produces a beam of light with unique properties: high **monochromaticity**, high **spatial and temporal coherence**, high **directionality**, and high **brightness**. These properties enable a wide range of engineering applications.

**(i) Industrial Applications (2 Marks):**
- **CO₂ laser (λ ≈ 10.6 μm):** Used for cutting, drilling, and welding metals, plastics, and ceramics. Output powers up to tens of kW.
- **Nd:YAG laser (λ ≈ 1.064 μm):** Preferred for cutting thick metals and for deep-penetration welding in the automotive and ship-building industries. Fiber-coupled Nd:YAG lasers enable flexible beam delivery.
- **Excimer laser (λ = 193 nm):** Used in photolithography for semiconductor chip fabrication — defining nanometer-scale features on silicon wafers.
- **3D Printing:** Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) of metal powders to fabricate complex aerospace components.

**(ii) Medical Applications (2 Marks):**
- **Excimer laser (193 nm, UV):** LASIK eye surgery — precisely ablates corneal tissue without thermal damage to surrounding cells.
- **CO₂ laser (10.6 μm):** Surgical cutting and cauterization of tissues (highly absorbed by water in tissues).
- **Argon-ion laser (488/514 nm):** Photocoagulation of retinal blood vessels in diabetic retinopathy.
- **Pulsed dye laser (585 nm):** Treatment of port-wine stains and tattoo removal.
- **Fiber-coupled diode lasers:** Used in endoscopic surgery and dental procedures.

**(iii) Communication Applications (2 Marks):**
- **Semiconductor (laser diode) at 1310 nm:** Used as the optical source in single-mode fiber links; matches the zero-dispersion window of silica.
- **Semiconductor laser at 1550 nm:** Used for ultra-long-haul submarine cables; matches the minimum attenuation window (~0.2 dB/km) and supports EDFA (Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifier) repeaters.
- **Vertical-Cavity Surface-Emitting Lasers (VCSELs) at 850 nm:** Used in short-range multi-mode fiber links (data centers, Gigabit Ethernet).
- **Free-space optical (FSO) communication:** Laser beams carrying modulated data between buildings or to satellites.

**Conclusion (Mark integrated above):** The choice of laser for any application depends critically on the wavelength (which determines material interaction), power, and mode of operation (CW vs. pulsed).

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Attenuation (3 Marks):**

Attenuation is the gradual loss of optical signal power as it propagates through the fiber, quantified in dB/km:

$$\alpha = \frac{10}{L}\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$$

**Causes of Attenuation:**
- **Absorption:** Intrinsic (SiO₂ molecular vibrations at IR wavelengths) and extrinsic (OH⁻ ion impurities at 950 nm, transition metal ions).
- **Rayleigh Scattering:** Caused by microscopic density fluctuations; dominant loss mechanism at $\lambda < 1\;\mu m$; scattering coefficient scales as $\lambda^{-4}$.
- **Bending Losses:** Macro-bends (visible) and micro-bends (sub-millimeter) cause guided modes to radiate out of the core.
- **Coupling Losses:** Misalignment, angular offset, and mode mismatch at connectors and splices.

**Dispersion (3 Marks):**

Dispersion is the temporal broadening of an optical pulse as it travels along the fiber, limiting bandwidth-distance product.

- **Modal (Intermodal) Dispersion:** Different modes travel at different group velocities in multi-mode fibers. Pulse broadening: $\Delta t_{modal} \approx \dfrac{n_1 L \Delta}{c}$. Eliminated in single-mode fibers.
- **Material (Chromatic) Dispersion:** Different wavelengths within the source linewidth travel at different speeds because $n(\lambda)$ varies. Pulse broadening: $\Delta t_{mat} = \dfrac{L\lambda}{c}\left|\dfrac{d^2n}{d\lambda^2}\right|\Delta\lambda$.
- **Waveguide Dispersion:** Caused by wavelength-dependent power distribution between core and cladding in single-mode fibers. By balancing material and waveguide dispersion, total dispersion can be engineered to be zero near specific wavelengths.

**Three Transmission Windows (1 Mark):**

| Window | Wavelength | Attenuation | Dispersion |
|---|---|---|---|
| First | 850 nm | ~ 2–3 dB/km | High |
| Second | 1310 nm | ~ 0.35 dB/km | Near zero |
| **Third (Minimum)** | **1550 nm** | **~ 0.2 dB/km** | Low (with DSF) |

**Wavelength of minimum attenuation: λ ≈ 1550 nm** (Third window). This is why modern long-haul telecom operates at 1550 nm, often with dispersion-shifted fiber (DSF) and erbium-doped fiber amplifiers (EDFAs).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **In Acceptance Angle derivations (most common):** Students often forget the geometric relation $\theta_1 = 90° - \theta$. Always draw the diagram and label both angles explicitly to secure 2 marks upfront.
> 
> 2. **NA Confusion:** Mixing up *acceptance angle* (an angle) with *numerical aperture* (a sine value, dimensionless). Write $\sin\theta_a = NA$ explicitly.
> 
> 3. **V-Number mode count:** Using $V^2$ instead of $V^2/2$. KTU accepts both depending on the textbook; verify the formula table from your module.
> 
> 4. **Attenuation units:** Mixing up dB and dB/km. Always divide by length $L$.
> 
> 5. **Laser applications:** Listing only generic uses without naming the **specific laser type and wavelength** loses the "engineering specificity" marks.
> 
> 6. **TIR Conditions:** Stating only one of the two conditions. Examiners allocate 1 mark to *each* of the two conditions.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision — KTU Module 4 High-Yield Points**

### A. Applications of Laser
- [ ] Industrial: CO₂ (10.6 μm) cutting, Nd:YAG (1.06 μm) welding
- [ ] Medical: Excimer (193 nm) LASIK; Argon-ion (488 nm) retinal surgery
- [ ] Communication: Semiconductor lasers at **1310 nm** and **1550 nm**
- [ ] Military: LIDAR, range-finding using Nd:YAG pulsed lasers
- [ ] Scientific: Holography (He-Ne), spectroscopy (dye lasers), atom cooling
- [ ] Consumer: Barcode scanners, Blu-ray (405 nm), laser printers

### B. Optical Fiber — Core Concepts
- [ ] **Optical fiber = core ($n_1$) + cladding ($n_2 < n_1$) + buffer**
- [ ] **Principle of propagation = Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**
- [ ] **Two TIR conditions:** $n_1 > n_2$ AND $\theta \geq \theta_c$
- [ ] **Critical angle:** $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$
- [ ] **Acceptance angle:** $\sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$
- [ ] **Numerical Aperture (NA):** $NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$
- [ ] **Fractional index change:** $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$
- [ ] **Small-Δ NA approximation:** $NA \approx n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$
- [ ] **V-number:** $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda}\,NA$
- [ ] **Number of modes (step-index MM):** $N_m \approx V^2/2$
- [ ] **Single-mode condition:** $V < 2.405$
- [ ] **Attenuation:** $\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L}\log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$ dB/km
- [ ] **Three transmission windows:** 850, 1310, 1550 nm
- [ ] **Minimum attenuation at:** 1550 nm (~0.2 dB/km)
- [ ] **Dispersion types:** Modal, Material, Waveguide
- [ ] **Zero material dispersion in silica at:** ~1270 nm

### C. Fiber Classification — Quick Reference
- [ ] **Step-index single-mode (SMF):** Core 9 μm, NA 0.10, 1310/1550 nm telecom
- [ ] **Step-index multi-mode:** Core 50–200 μm, NA 0.20–0.40, severe modal dispersion
- [ ] **Graded-index multi-mode:** Parabolic index profile, OM3/OM4 LANs, data centers
- [ ] **Plastic Optical Fiber (POF):** Cheap, large core, short range, automotive

### D. Numerical Problem Triggers (KTU 2024)
- [ ] "Compute NA and acceptance angle" → Use $\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$
- [ ] "Find the V-number / number of modes" → Use $V = 2\pi a\,NA/\lambda$
- [ ] "Single-mode core diameter" → Use $V < 2.405$
- [ ] "Find attenuation" → Use $\alpha = (10/L)\log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$
- [ ] "Material dispersion" → Use $\Delta t_{mat} = L\lambda\left|d^2n/d\lambda^2\right|\Delta\lambda/c$

### E. Engineering Anchors to Remember
- [ ] Standard SMF-28 fiber: core = **9 μm**, cladding = **125 μm**, NA = **0.13**
- [ ] Telecom wavelengths: **1310 nm** (zero dispersion), **1550 nm** (minimum loss)
- [ ] EDFA gain band: **1530–1565 nm** (C-band)
- [ ] V-number cutoff for SMF: **2.405**
- [ ] Acceptance angle of typical SMF ≈ **8°** (very narrow)

### F. Common Pitfalls — Last-Word Reminders
- [ ] NEVER write $\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ (it is $\sin\theta_a$)
- [ ] NEVER use $V^2$ without the factor 1/2 unless specifically stated
- [ ] NEVER mix "Brewster angle" (used in polarization) with "Critical angle" (used in TIR)
- [ ] ALWAYS state both TIR conditions
- [ ] ALWAYS specify wavelength when discussing dispersion
- [ ] ALWAYS label $\theta_1$ from axis and $\theta$ from normal in derivation diagrams

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — GBPHT121 Physics for Electrical Science**
> 
> **Recommended practice:** Solve the 5 numericals in §3.6 twice — once with derivation, once closed-book. Then attempt the Part A and Part B questions in §5.1 and §5.2 under timed conditions (10 minutes per Part A; 25 minutes per Part B sub-part). This is the KTU-recommended exam preparation pattern.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Fibers - Step Index and Graded Index Fibers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GBPHT121 - PHYSICS FOR ELECTRICAL SCIENCE
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types of Fibers - Step Index and Graded Index Fibers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is an Optical Fiber? (Foundation Recap)

An **optical fiber** is a thin, flexible, transparent dielectric cylinder made of high-purity silica glass or plastic, designed to guide **light waves** (electromagnetic signals in the optical/infrared spectrum, typically 850 nm, 1300 nm, or 1550 nm) over long distances with extremely low loss. It operates on the principle of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of fibers into *Step Index* and *Graded Index* is the **foundational taxonomy** from which all further fiber-optic design, mode analysis, and dispersion studies are derived. Every numerical problem in Module 4 ultimately traces back to this classification.

### Structural Anatomy of an Optical Fiber

| Layer | Material / Function | Typical Radius |
|---|---|---|
| **Core** | High refractive index $n_1$ glass; light-guiding region | $a$ = 4 µm to 50 µm |
| **Cladding** | Lower refractive index $n_2$ glass; provides TIR boundary | ~ 62.5 µm to 125 µm |
| **Buffer Coating** | Protective polymer layer (mechanical safety) | ~ 250 µm |
| **Jacket** | Outer PVC/LSZH sheath (environmental safety) | ~ 900 µm |

---

## 1.2 The Two Principal Classifications

### A) Step Index Fiber (SIF)

A **Step Index Fiber** is an optical fiber in which the **refractive index is uniform (constant) within the core** and undergoes a **sharp, discontinuous "step" drop** at the core-cladding interface. There is **no gradual transition**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** "A Step Index Fiber is a fiber in which the refractive index of the core ($n_1$) remains constant throughout its cross-section and drops abruptly to a lower value ($n_2$) at the core-cladding boundary."

**Real-World Analogy — The Highway Analogy 🚗:**
Imagine a multi-lane highway with **rigid, painted lane dividers**. Every car (light ray) entering at a different angle is locked into a single, straight lane. Vehicles injected at steep angles "bounce" violently between the painted boundaries (just like rays zigzagging inside the core). A vehicle injected at a shallow angle travels a longer zigzag path, while a steeply injected one takes a shorter, more direct path. The result? **All vehicles reach the exit at different times** → this timing mismatch is called **intermodal dispersion**, the main weakness of step index fibers.

### B) Graded Index Fiber (GRIN / GIF)

A **Graded Index Fiber** is an optical fiber in which the **refractive index of the core varies continuously** (decreases parabolically) as a function of radial distance from the central axis, reaching its **minimum value at the core-cladding interface**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** "A Graded Index Fiber is a fiber in which the refractive index of the core is maximum ($n_1$) at the center and decreases smoothly (typically parabolically) to a minimum value ($n_2$) at the core-cladding boundary, following a specific radial profile."

**Real-World Analogy — The Onions / Lens Analogy 🧅:**
Picture a long, transparent onion (a stack of concentric glass cylinders, each with slightly different refractive index). Instead of bouncing off walls, light rays travel in **gentle, sinusoidal/helical curves** — they are continuously **refracted** (bent) as they pass through layers of progressively lower refractive index. A ray traveling near the center moves through denser glass (slower speed, shorter path), while a ray traveling near the edge moves through less dense glass (faster speed, longer path). The clever design **equalizes their travel times**, drastically reducing intermodal dispersion.

---

## 1.3 Sub-Classification: Mode Behavior

Both fiber types further subdivide based on the number of optical modes they support:

> [!NOTE]
> **Mode** = a stable, self-replicating spatial distribution of light that propagates down the fiber at a specific angle, satisfying boundary conditions. An optical fiber is essentially a **cylindrical waveguide**.

| Type | Single-Mode | Multi-Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **Step Index** | Single-Mode Step Index Fiber (SM-SIF) | Multi-Mode Step Index Fiber (MM-SIF) |
| **Graded Index** | *(Not common — GRIN is usually multi-mode)* | Multi-Mode Graded Index Fiber (MM-GRIN) |
| Core Diameter | ~ 4–10 µm | ~ 50–100 µm (SIF) / 50–62.5 µm (GRIN) |
| Typical Use | Long-haul telecom, submarine cables | LANs, data centers, medical imaging |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Step Index Fiber — Operational Logic

**Why does light stay confined?**
A ray entering the core at an angle $\theta_i$ (measured from the fiber axis) strikes the core-cladding boundary. If the angle of incidence at this boundary exceeds the **critical angle** $\theta_c$, TIR occurs and the ray is guided. The condition is:

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

Any ray with internal incidence angle $\geq \theta_c$ will be guided.

**Key Structural Logic:**
1. The core has a **uniform** refractive index $n_1$.
2. The cladding has a **uniform** lower refractive index $n_2$.
3. Rays travel in **straight lines** within the core and reflect sharply at the boundary.
4. Different modes take **different geometric path lengths**, leading to arrival-time spread (intermodal dispersion).
5. The number of guided modes depends on the **V-Number** (normalized frequency).

---

## 2.2 Graded Index Fiber — Operational Logic

**Why does light curve smoothly?**
In a graded index fiber, the refractive index follows a **power-law profile**:

$$n(r) = n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta\left(\frac{r}{a}\right)^{\alpha}}$$

where:
- $n_1$ = refractive index at the core center (axial)
- $n_2$ = refractive index at the core-cladding interface ($r = a$)
- $a$ = core radius
- $r$ = radial distance from the center
- $\Delta$ = relative refractive index difference
- $\alpha$ = profile parameter ($\alpha = 2$ → parabolic; $\alpha = \infty$ → step index)

**Operational steps:**
1. The index is **maximum at the center** and decreases parabolically outward.
2. Light rays are **continuously refracted** (not totally reflected) as they traverse layers of decreasing index.
3. Rays follow **sinusoidal (helical) trajectories**, periodically returning to the axis.
4. Rays near the axis travel **slower** (denser medium, shorter path); rays near the cladding travel **faster** (less dense medium, longer path).
5. The optimal $\alpha = 2$ (parabolic profile) **minimizes intermodal dispersion** by making all modes arrive nearly simultaneously.

---

## 2.3 ⚡ KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU NOTE:** All formulas below are board-exam favorites. Memorize the formula AND the physical meaning of each symbol — KTU examiners frequently test *significance*, not just calculation.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Critical Angle | $\sin\theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$ | Minimum angle for TIR at the core-cladding boundary |
| 2 | Relative Refractive Index Difference | $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{2n_1^2} \approx \dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1}$ | Fractional index difference (usually $\sim 0.01$ for SMF) |
| 3 | Numerical Aperture (NA) | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$ | Light-gathering ability of the fiber |
| 4 | Acceptance Angle | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ | Maximum external angle for ray to be guided |
| 5 | Acceptance Cone Half-Angle | $\theta_a$ | Defines the cone of acceptance |
| 6 | V-Number (Normalized Frequency) | $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda}\cdot NA = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda}\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Determines number of modes |
| 7 | Number of Modes — Step Index MMF | $M_{SIF} \approx \dfrac{V^2}{2}$ | Total guided modes in multi-mode step index |
| 8 | Number of Modes — Graded Index MMF | $M_{GRIN} \approx \dfrac{V^2}{4}$ | Total guided modes in multi-mode graded index |
| 9 | Cut-off Condition (Single-Mode) | $V < 2.405$ | Below this V, fiber is single-mode (for $\alpha = \infty$) |
| 10 | GRIN Refractive Index Profile | $n(r) = n_1\sqrt{1 - 2\Delta(r/a)^{\alpha}}$ | General power-law profile |
| 11 | Parabolic GRIN Optimal Profile | $\alpha = 2$ | Minimizes intermodal dispersion |
| 12 | Modal Dispersion in SIF (pulse broadening) | $\Delta t_{SIF} = \dfrac{n_1 L}{c}\cdot\dfrac{1}{\sin\theta_c} - \dfrac{n_1 L}{c}$ | Difference between slowest and fastest mode arrival |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Preferred Fiber | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Submarine / Long-Haul Telecom** | Single-Mode Step Index | Minimal dispersion, highest bandwidth (~THz·km) |
| **LAN / Data Center** | Multi-Mode Graded Index (OM3/OM4) | Easy splicing, large core, lower cost connectors |
| **Medical Endoscopy** | Multi-Mode Step Index / GRIN | Large core for efficient light collection |
| **Fiber Lasers & Amplifiers** | Double-clad GRIN | High power handling |
| **Sensing (Temperature, Strain)** | Specialty GRIN / SMF | Sensitivity to environmental changes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even though graded index fiber was designed primarily to **combat intermodal dispersion**, single-mode step index fiber is still preferred for the longest links because, although it has *zero* intermodal dispersion (only one mode!), its **chromatic dispersion** (from source spectral width) is far easier to compensate using dispersion-shifted fibers (DSF) and non-zero dispersion-shifted fibers (NZ-DSF).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation I: Numerical Aperture (NA) and Acceptance Angle

> **Statement:** Show that the Numerical Aperture of a step index fiber is $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$, and derive the acceptance angle.

### Setup
Consider a step index fiber with core index $n_1$ and cladding index $n_2$. A light ray enters the fiber face from air (refractive index $n_0 = 1$) at an external angle $\theta_a$ (with respect to the fiber axis). Inside the fiber, the ray refracts and travels at angle $\theta_r$ to the axis, then hits the core-cladding interface at an internal incidence angle $\theta_i$.

**Geometric relation (from the right-angled triangle formed by the ray and the axis):**

$$\theta_r + \theta_i = 90^{\circ} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \theta_i = 90^{\circ} - \theta_r$$

### Step 1: Apply Snell's Law at the Air–Core Interface

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_r$$

$$\sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_r \quad (\text{since } n_0 = 1)$$

### Step 2: Apply Snell's Law at the Core–Cladding Interface (Critical Condition)

For TIR, the internal angle of incidence $\theta_i$ must equal the critical angle $\theta_c$ at the acceptance limit:

$$n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 \sin 90^{\circ} = n_2$$

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

### Step 3: Use the Geometric Constraint

From $\theta_i = 90^{\circ} - \theta_r$, taking sine on both sides:

$$\sin\theta_c = \sin(90^{\circ} - \theta_r) = \cos\theta_r$$

$$\therefore \cos\theta_r = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

### Step 4: Convert sin θa to Use sin² + cos² = 1 Identity

$$\sin^2\theta_a = n_1^2 \sin^2\theta_r = n_1^2(1 - \cos^2\theta_r)$$

Substitute $\cos\theta_r = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$:

$$\sin^2\theta_a = n_1^2\left(1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}\right) = n_1^2 - n_2^2$$

### Step 5: Final Result

$$\boxed{\sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = NA}$$

$$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} \quad ; \quad \theta_a = \sin^{-1}\left(\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\right)$$

> **Physical meaning:** $NA$ is simply $\sin\theta_a$, the sine of the **acceptance angle** — the half-angle of the cone of light that can be coupled into the fiber. Higher $NA$ → wider acceptance cone → more light collected.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II: Number of Guided Modes in Step Index Fiber

> **Statement:** Show that the number of guided modes in a step index fiber is $M \approx V^2/2$.

### Setup
The **V-number** (normalized frequency) combines all geometric and optical parameters:

$$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot NA$$

Each guided mode corresponds to a specific solution of the **Helmholtz wave equation** subject to cylindrical boundary conditions (Bessel functions). The mode condition requires:

$$0 \le m \le \sqrt{V^2/2}$$

where $m$ is an integer mode index. The total number of allowed $(l, m)$ mode pairs (where $l$ is the azimuthal number) summed over both polarizations gives:

$$M_{SIF} \approx \frac{V^2}{2}$$

> **Interpretation:** A higher $V$ value (larger core, shorter wavelength, or larger $NA$) supports more modes.

**For Single-Mode Operation** ($M = 1$):

$$V < 2.405$$

This is the first zero of the Bessel function $J_0$, and is the **cutoff condition** for single-mode propagation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III: Number of Modes in Graded Index Fiber

> **Statement:** Show that the number of guided modes in a graded index fiber is $M_{GRIN} \approx V^2/4$.

### Setup
For a graded index fiber with a power-law profile $n(r) = n_1\sqrt{1 - 2\Delta(r/a)^{\alpha}}$, the WKB approximation gives the total number of guided modes as:

$$M_{GRIN} = \frac{V^2}{2} \cdot \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + 2}$$

For the **parabolic profile** ($\alpha = 2$), the optimal case:

$$M_{GRIN} = \frac{V^2}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{4} = \frac{V^2}{4}$$

$$\boxed{M_{GRIN} \approx \frac{V^2}{4} = \frac{1}{2} M_{SIF}}$$

> **Key insight:** A graded index fiber with the same V-number supports **half** the number of modes as a step index fiber. This is one of the main reasons why graded index fibers are typically used as multi-mode fibers, not single-mode.

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Step vs Graded Index)

| Parameter | Step Index Fiber (SIF) | Graded Index Fiber (GRIN) |
|---|---|---|
| Refractive Index Profile | Constant in core; sudden step at boundary | Variable, parabolically decreasing in core |
| Ray Trajectory | Straight line with sharp TIR reflections | Smooth sinusoidal/helical curves |
| Path Length (per mode) | Different for each mode | Compensated by speed variation |
| Intermodal Dispersion | **High** | **Very Low** (minimum at $\alpha=2$) |
| Number of Modes (MMF) | $V^2/2$ | $V^2/4$ |
| Bandwidth–Distance Product | Lower (~10–100 MHz·km) | Higher (~200–2000 MHz·km) |
| NA | Higher (typically 0.2–0.5) | Lower (typically 0.1–0.3) |
| Coupling Efficiency | Lower (more sensitive to alignment) | Higher (larger tolerance) |
| Manufacturing Cost | Lower | Slightly higher (precise doping) |
| Typical Applications | Short-distance LAN, sensors | Medium-distance telecom, LANs, data centers |
| Splicing Difficulty | Easier (large core MMF) | Slightly harder |
| Bandwidth | Low to moderate | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Ray Propagation Comparison

The following Mermaid block-diagram maps the **logical flow of light propagation** in both fiber types, capturing the contrast between straight-line TIR pathing and continuous curved refraction pathing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Light Source at Input Face"] --> B{"Fiber Type Selection"}
    B --> C["STEP INDEX FIBER SIF"]
    B --> D["GRADED INDEX FIBER GRIN"]
    C --> C1["Ray enters straight at angle thetar"]
    C1 --> C2["Constant n1 throughout core"]
    C2 --> C3["Sharp TIR reflection at boundary"]
    C3 --> C4{"Different path lengths per mode"}
    C4 --> C5["HIGH intermodal dispersion"]
    C5 --> C6["Output: pulse broadened"]
    D --> D1["Ray refracts at every radial layer"]
    D1 --> D2["n decreases parabolically with r"]
    D2 --> D3["Sinusoidal curved trajectory"]
    D3 --> D4{"Speed slower near axis, faster near edge"}
    D4 --> D5["Equalized arrival times"]
    D5 --> D6["LOW intermodal dispersion"]
    D6 --> D7["Output: sharp pulse retained"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The two parallel branches (left = SIF, right = GRIN) show the divergent physical processes that lead to dramatically different output pulse quality. The decision node "Fiber Type Selection" is the central design choice governed by bandwidth-distance requirements.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Index Profile and Ray Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["STEP INDEX PROFILE n versus r"] --> Q1["n constant at n1 for r less than a"]
    Q1 --> R1["Sharp drop to n2 at r equals a"]
    R1 --> S1["Ray path: straight line with zigzag TIR"]
    P2["GRADED INDEX PROFILE n versus r"] --> Q2["n maximum n1 at r equals 0"]
    Q2 --> R2["n decreases parabolically to n2 at r equals a"]
    R2 --> S2["Ray path: smooth sinusoidal curve"]
```

> **Visual reading:** The two parallel flow paths contrast the index-versus-radius profile (left side of each block) with the resulting ray geometry (right side of each block).

---

## 4.3 Decision-Tree Diagram — Fiber Selection in Engineering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Choose Optical Fiber for Application"] --> Q1{"Link Distance?"}
    Q1 -->|"Greater than 10 km"| L1["Long Haul"]
    Q1 -->|"Less than 2 km"| L2["Short Reach"]
    Q1 -->|"2 km to 10 km"| L3["Medium Reach"]
    L1 --> S1["Single Mode Step Index SM SIF"]
    L1 --> R1["V less than 2.405 at operating wavelength"]
    L2 --> S2["Multi Mode Step Index MM SIF"]
    L2 --> S3["Cheap connectors OK"]
    L3 --> G1["Multi Mode Graded Index MM GRIN"]
    L3 --> G2["OM3 or OM4 standard"]
    L3 --> G3["Higher bandwidth than MM SIF"]
```

> **Practical use:** Engineers use this decision tree to select fiber type based on link distance. The three dominant use cases (submarine/long-haul, LAN, campus backbone) each map to a specific fiber choice.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern]

**Differentiate between Step Index Fiber and Graded Index Fiber. Mention any three points.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Step Index Fiber (SIF) | Graded Index Fiber (GRIN) |
|---|---|---|
| Refractive index of core | Uniform / constant at $n_1$ | Varies parabolically from $n_1$ (center) to $n_2$ (boundary) |
| Ray path inside core | Straight lines with sudden TIR reflections | Continuous sinusoidal/curved paths |
| Intermodal dispersion | **High** | **Low** (minimum for parabolic profile) |

Any three valid differences earn full marks.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]

**What is meant by the Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fiber? Write its expression in terms of core and cladding refractive indices.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fiber is a dimensionless quantity that measures the **light-gathering ability** of the fiber. It is defined as the **sine of the acceptance angle** $\theta_a$ — i.e., the maximum angle (with respect to the fiber axis) at which an external light ray can enter the fiber and still undergo total internal reflection inside the core.

**Expression:**

$$NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$$

where $n_1$ is the core refractive index, $n_2$ is the cladding refractive index, and $\Delta$ is the relative refractive index difference. A higher NA means a larger acceptance cone and more efficient light coupling.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **KTU Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **Part (a) = 7 marks** and **Part (b) = 7 marks**. KTU provides an internal choice (OR option) between two questions from the same module.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the structure of a **multi-mode step index optical fiber**. Discuss the propagation of light rays through the fiber using the concept of total internal reflection. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **numerical aperture** and the **acceptance angle** of a step index fiber in terms of the core and cladding refractive indices. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Structure of Multi-Mode Step Index Fiber:**

A multi-mode step index fiber consists of:
- A central cylindrical **core** of uniform refractive index $n_1$ and diameter $2a$ (typically 50–100 µm for MMF).
- A surrounding **cladding** of uniform refractive index $n_2 < n_1$ (typically 125 µm outer diameter).
- Protective **buffer coating** and **jacket** for mechanical and environmental safety.

**Propagation mechanism:**

A meridional ray entering the fiber face at an angle $\theta_a$ to the axis refracts into the core at angle $\theta_r$ and travels in a straight line until it strikes the core-cladding interface. At the interface, the angle of incidence is $\theta_i = 90^\circ - \theta_r$. If $\theta_i \ge \theta_c$ (the critical angle), **total internal reflection** occurs and the ray is guided down the fiber. Each TIR reflects the ray back into the core with no loss of energy, and the ray follows a **zig-zag path** along the fiber axis.

Different rays entering at different angles trace **different path lengths**:
- **Axial ray** ($\theta_a = 0$): travels the shortest path straight through the center.
- **Extreme ray** ($\theta_a = \theta_{a,\max}$): travels the longest zig-zag path close to the critical angle.

This path-length difference is the **physical origin of intermodal dispersion**, which is the main limitation of step index multi-mode fibers.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

*Full derivation as in Section 3.1 above.*

**Starting Equations:**

$$n_0 \sin\theta_a = n_1 \sin\theta_r \quad \text{(Snell's law at input face)}$$

$$n_1 \sin\theta_c = n_2 \quad \text{(Critical-angle condition for TIR)}$$

$$\theta_r + \theta_c = 90^\circ$$

**Derivation Steps:**

$$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

$$\cos\theta_r = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$

$$\sin^2\theta_a = n_1^2 \sin^2\theta_r = n_1^2(1 - \cos^2\theta_r) = n_1^2 - n_2^2$$

$$\boxed{NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} \quad;\quad \theta_a = \sin^{-1}\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain the principle of a **graded index fiber** with a suitable refractive index profile. Show that the number of guided modes in a graded index fiber is **half** that in a step index fiber for the same V-number. (7 marks)

**(b)** With neat diagrams, compare the **ray trajectories** in step index and graded index fibers. Explain why graded index fibers exhibit lower intermodal dispersion. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Principle of Graded Index Fiber:**

In a graded index fiber, the refractive index of the core is **maximum at the center** ($n_1$) and decreases continuously (parabolically) to a minimum value ($n_2$) at the core-cladding boundary. The general power-law profile is:

$$n(r) = n_1\sqrt{1 - 2\Delta\left(\frac{r}{a}\right)^{\alpha}}$$

The principle is that light rays entering the fiber are **continuously refracted** as they pass through layers of progressively changing refractive index. A ray entering off-axis is gradually bent back toward the axis, follows a **sinusoidal/helical path**, and is guided down the fiber.

**Mode Count Comparison:**

Using the WKB approximation, the total number of guided modes in:
- **Step index fiber:** $M_{SIF} = V^2/2$
- **Graded index fiber (parabolic, $\alpha = 2$):**

$$M_{GRIN} = \frac{V^2}{2} \cdot \frac{\alpha}{\alpha+2} = \frac{V^2}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{4} = \frac{V^2}{4}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{M_{GRIN}}{M_{SIF}} = \frac{V^2/4}{V^2/2} = \frac{1}{2}}$$

**Conclusion:** For the same V-number, a graded index fiber guides **half** the modes of a step index fiber, leading to lower modal density and consequently **lower intermodal dispersion**.

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Ray Trajectory Comparison:**

**Step Index Fiber (SIF):** Rays travel in **straight line segments**, reflecting at discrete angles via TIR at the core-cladding boundary. The path is a **zig-zag** (meridional rays) or **skew** (helical) trajectory with constant segment length.

**Graded Index Fiber (GRIN):** Rays travel in **smooth, continuous curves** (approximately sinusoidal for parabolic profile) due to continuous refraction in the index gradient. There are no discrete reflection points.

**Why Graded Index has Lower Intermodal Dispersion:**

In a step index fiber, all modes travel at the **same speed** ($c/n_1$) but take **different path lengths** — the longer the path, the later the arrival. This causes pulse spreading proportional to $\Delta t \approx (n_1 L/c)(\Delta n)/n_1$.

In a graded index fiber, the path lengths **differ**, but the **local speed of light also varies**:
- Rays near the axis travel through **denser glass** (high $n$) → **slower** speed → **shorter** path.
- Rays near the cladding travel through **less dense glass** (low $n$) → **faster** speed → **longer** path.

The path-length increase is **exactly compensated** by the speed increase, so all modes arrive **nearly simultaneously** at the output. This is the **equalization principle** that minimizes intermodal dispersion in graded index fibers (best at $\alpha = 2$).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes (Module 4 — Fiber Optics):**
> 1. **Forgetting to state Snell's Law explicitly:** When deriving NA, students often jump from $n_1 \sin\theta_r$ to $\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ without showing the geometric relation $\theta_r + \theta_i = 90°$ — this costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up $n_1$ and $n_2$:** The core index is always $n_1$ (larger), cladding is $n_2$ (smaller). Reverse the symbols and the entire NA expression becomes incorrect.
> 3. **Missing the $V < 2.405$ single-mode condition:** Examiners love testing this number — know it (it is the first zero of $J_0$).
> 4. **Confusing $M = V^2/2$ vs $V^2/4$:** Always state the fiber type. Writing $M = V^2/4$ for a step index fiber = **immediate 1-mark penalty**.
> 5. **Not drawing diagrams with proper labels:** A question on "structure of fiber" without a labeled diagram loses at least 2 marks in Part (a).
> 6. **Ignoring the significance of $\alpha = 2$:** When asked about graded index fibers, always state that the **parabolic profile is optimal for minimum intermodal dispersion**.
> 7. **Units mismatch:** NA is dimensionless; $\theta_a$ is in degrees/radians. Don't write "$NA = 0.5$ degrees."

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Step Index Fiber:** Uniform $n_1$ in core, sudden step to $n_2$ at cladding boundary. Rays travel in **straight-line zig-zag** with **TIR**.
- [x] **Graded Index Fiber:** $n$ varies **parabolically** from $n_1$ (center) to $n_2$ (edge). Rays travel in **smooth sinusoidal/curved** paths.
- [x] **Numerical Aperture (NA):** $NA = \sin\theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_1\sqrt{2\Delta}$ — measures light-gathering ability.
- [x] **Acceptance Angle $\theta_a$:** Maximum external angle for guided propagation; the half-angle of the acceptance cone.
- [x] **V-Number (Normalized Frequency):** $V = (2\pi a / \lambda) \cdot NA$ — controls number of modes.
- [x] **Single-Mode Cutoff:** $V < 2.405$ for step index fiber.
- [x] **Mode Count:** $M_{SIF} = V^2/2$ (step), $M_{GRIN} = V^2/4$ (parabolic graded).
- [x] **Optimal GRIN profile:** $\alpha = 2$ (parabolic) → minimum intermodal dispersion.
- [x] **Intermodal dispersion is HIGH in SIF, LOW in GRIN** — the fundamental reason GRIN is preferred for medium-bandwidth multi-mode links.
- [x] **SM-SIF** is the choice for long-haul, high-bandwidth telecom (zero intermodal dispersion, only chromatic dispersion remains).
- [x] **MM-GRIN (OM3/OM4)** is the choice for LAN, data centers, and campus backbones.
- [x] **NA range:** typically 0.1–0.5 (multi-mode: ~0.2–0.3; single-mode: ~0.1–0.15).
- [x] **Wavelengths used:** 850 nm, 1300 nm, 1550 nm (near-infrared, low-loss silica windows).
- [x] **Mnemonic for NA formula:** "**N**umerical **A**perture = $\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ = **R**oot of **C**ore squared minus **C**ladding squared" (NARC-C²C²).
